topics.title: Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scanning** is the process of converting a two-dimensional (2D) image into a one-dimensional (1D) time-varying electrical signal (during transmission/capture) or converting a 1D electrical signal back into a 2D image (during reception/display) by systematically traversing the image area in a predetermined sequence of horizontal lines.

In the context of **Entertainment Electronics**, scanning is the foundational operating principle of all **Analog Television Systems**, **Video Cameras**, **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube) Displays**, and the legacy architecture upon which modern digital displays (LCD, LED, OLED) were historically benchmarked.

### 1.2 The Three Core Scanning Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (India/PAL System) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Total Lines per Frame | $N$ | **625 lines** |
| Frame Frequency | $f_f$ | **25 Hz** |
| Field Frequency (Interlaced) | $f_{field}$ | **50 Hz** |
| Horizontal Line Frequency | $f_H$ | **15,625 Hz** |
| Aspect Ratio | $AR$ | **4:3** (Standard) / **16:9** (Widescreen) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are reading a **page full of text** written in a foreign language you do not understand. To send the *meaning* of that page to a friend over a telephone, you have two options:
1. **Option A:** Fax the entire page at once (impossible with 1D signals).
2. **Option B:** Read the page aloud — *word by word, line by line, from top-left to bottom-right*.

**Scanning is exactly Option B for visual images.**
- The TV camera (transmitter) acts as your **eye**, breaking the picture into thin horizontal strips called **lines**.
- It reads these lines from **left to right** (this is one **horizontal sweep**).
- After one line, it quickly jumps back to the next line below (this is the **horizontal retrace / flyback**).
- Once all lines are read, it jumps back to the top-left corner (this is the **vertical retrace**).
- This entire sequence, when repeated, creates a **continuous moving picture** due to the **persistence of vision** of the human eye (≈ **1/16 second**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Persistence of Vision**: The human eye retains an image for approximately **50–60 milliseconds** after it disappears. If successive still images (frames) are flashed at a rate ≥ **24 frames/second**, the brain perceives them as continuous motion. This is the physiological basis of cinema and television.

### 1.4 Why is Scanning Necessary? (KTU High-Yield Reasoning)

1. **Single-Channel Transmission:** A single RF carrier cannot transmit a 2D image simultaneously. Scanning **time-multiplexes** the spatial information into a serial data stream.
2. **Bandwidth Conservation:** Without scanning, each pixel would need a separate transmission channel, making the system physically and economically impossible.
3. **Compatibility with Display Devices:** CRT screens (and conceptually modern raster displays) build images **dot-by-dot, line-by-line**, matching the natural sequential reading order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Scan: Horizontal & Vertical Components

A complete television scanning operation involves **two simultaneous, perpendicular sawtooth waveforms**:

1. **Horizontal Scanning (Line Scanning):** The electron beam (or pixel writing position) moves from **left → right** rapidly, then quickly **flies back (retrace)** to the left. This is controlled by the **line timebase** (horizontal deflection oscillator).
2. **Vertical Scanning (Frame/Field Scanning):** The entire horizontal scanline assembly **moves slowly downward**, then quickly **flies back up** to the top. This is controlled by the **field timebase** (vertical deflection oscillator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retrace (Flyback) Blanking:** During both horizontal and vertical retrace periods, the electron beam is **blanked (cut off)** so that the retrace lines are invisible on the screen. This is achieved by **Horizontal Blanking Pulses (HBP)** and **Vertical Blanking Pulses (VBP)** embedded in the composite video signal.

### 2.2 Two Fundamental Modes of Scanning

#### A) Progressive (Sequential / Non-Interlaced) Scanning
- The beam scans **all lines of a frame consecutively from top to bottom** in a single pass.
- Frame frequency = Field frequency.
- **Used in:** Modern computer monitors, LCD/LED TVs (in their native mode), HDTV.
- **Advantage:** Superior picture quality, no inter-line flicker.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires **double the bandwidth** for the same vertical resolution.

#### B) Interlaced Scanning
- The frame is split into **two fields**: the **odd field** (lines 1, 3, 5, ... 623) and the **even field** (lines 2, 4, 6, ... 624).
- The odd field is scanned first (taking 1/50 s), then the even field (taking another 1/50 s). Together they form one complete frame in 1/25 s.
- **Field frequency = 2 × Frame frequency.**
- **Used in:** Legacy analog TV (PAL, NTSC, SECAM).
- **Advantage:** Halves the bandwidth requirement AND doubles the flicker rate (50 Hz instead of 25 Hz), exploiting persistence of vision.
- **Disadvantage:** Produces **interline flicker** and **motion artifacts** (visible "comb" effect on moving edges).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-critical**. Master the relationships and units before attempting any numerical problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Horizontal Line Frequency | $f_H = N \times f_{field}$ | **Hz** | $N$ = total lines/frame |
| 2 | Field Frequency (Interlaced) | $f_{field} = 2 \times f_{frame}$ | **Hz** | For PAL/SECAM = **50 Hz**, NTSC = **60 Hz** |
| 3 | Horizontal Sweep Time (Active + Retrace) | $T_H = \dfrac{1}{f_H}$ | **seconds** | Typical ≈ **64 μs** for 625-line PAL |
| 4 | Active Line Time (Visible portion) | $T_{active} \approx 0.82 \times T_H$ | **seconds** | Remaining 18% is blanking/retrace |
| 5 | Vertical Field Time | $T_{field} = \dfrac{1}{f_{field}}$ | **seconds** | ≈ **20 ms** for 50 Hz |
| 6 | Frame Time (Interlaced) | $T_{frame} = 2 \times T_{field}$ | **seconds** | ≈ **40 ms** for 25 fps |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Width}}{\text{Height}}$ | dimensionless | **4:3** (SDTV) or **16:9** (HDTV) |
| 8 | Lines per Field (Interlaced) | $N_{field} = \dfrac{N}{2}$ | lines | 625-line system → 312.5 lines/field |
| 9 | Total Pixels per Line | $P_H = T_H \times f_{pixel\,clock}$ | pixels | Modern HD: **1920 px/line** |
| 10 | Total Pixels per Frame | $P_{total} = P_H \times N$ | pixels | For 1080i: 1920 × 1080 = **2,073,600** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bandwidth Planning:** Scanning parameters directly determine the **video bandwidth** of a TV channel (≈ 5 MHz for analog PAL).
- **Display Driver Design:** Modern LCD/LED drivers (e.g., for 4K TV) use the **same scan logic** but at vastly higher pixel clocks (e.g., 594 MHz for 4K@60Hz).
- **Video Compression (MPEG/H.264/H.265):** Interlaced formats require special **MBAFF (Macroblock-Adaptive Frame-Field)** coding structures — a direct legacy of interlaced scanning.
- **Medical Imaging & Radar:** Scanning principles (raster scanning) underpin **CT scans, MRI sequences, and radar PPI displays**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Line Frequency from Number of Lines and Frame Rate

**Problem Statement:** For a standard **PAL-B/G television system** (used in India), derive the horizontal line frequency given that the system uses **625 lines per frame** at a frame rate of **25 frames per second** using 2:1 interlaced scanning.

**Step 1: Identify Given Parameters**

From the KTU syllabus and ITU standards, the CCIR/PAL-B/G system has:
- Total number of lines per frame, $N = 625$ lines
- Frame rate, $f_{frame} = 25$ frames/second
- Interlacing ratio: **2:1** (each frame = 2 fields)

**Step 2: Calculate the Field Frequency**

Since the system is interlaced, each frame is divided into **two fields** (odd and even). Therefore:

$$
f_{field} = 2 \times f_{frame}
$$

Substituting the value:

$$
f_{field} = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3: Set Up the Line Frequency Relationship**

In one complete field of duration $T_{field}$, the scanning system must complete exactly $N/2$ horizontal lines (one full set of either odd or even lines). Since line frequency is the reciprocal of the time to scan **one horizontal line**, we can write:

$$
f_H = \frac{\text{Number of lines per field}}{\text{Time per field}} = \frac{N/2}{T_{field}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Simplify**

Since $T_{field} = 1/f_{field}$, we substitute:

$$
f_H = \frac{N/2}{1/f_{field}} = \frac{N}{2} \times f_{field}
$$

**Step 5: Final Evaluation**

Substituting $N = 625$ and $f_{field} = 50$ Hz:

$$
f_H = \frac{625}{2} \times 50 = 312.5 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{f_H = 15{,}625 \text{ Hz} = 15.625 \text{ kHz}}
$$

This is the **exact horizontal line frequency** transmitted by Doordarshan and all Indian analog TV transmitters.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Horizontal Sweep Time and Active Line Duration

**Step 1: Horizontal Sweep Period**

By definition of frequency, the time to complete one full horizontal cycle (active scan + horizontal retrace) is:

$$
T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15{,}625}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_H = 64 \text{ μs (microseconds)}}
$$

**Step 2: Active (Visible) Line Time**

Approximately **18% of $T_H$** is consumed by horizontal blanking + sync pulse. Therefore, the visible/active portion is:

$$
T_{active} = 0.82 \times T_H = 0.82 \times 64 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{active} \approx 52.5 \text{ μs}}
$$

**Step 3: Horizontal Retrace + Blanking Time**

The remaining time:

$$
T_{blank} = T_H - T_{active} = 64 - 52.5 = 11.5 \text{ μs}
$$

During this interval, the electron beam is **blanked off** and returns from the right edge of the screen to the left edge, ready to start the next line.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Progressive vs Interlaced

| Performance Metric | Progressive (1080p) | Interlaced (1080i) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Lines per Frame** | 1080 (all in one pass) | 1080 (540 in field 1 + 540 in field 2) |
| **Frame Rate** | 60 fps | 30 fps (60 fields/s) |
| **Field Rate** | 60 Hz | 60 Hz |
| **Bandwidth Required** | ≈ 1.5× more | Lower (exploited in analog era) |
| **Motion Artifacts** | Minimal | "Comb effect" on edges |
| **Flicker Frequency** | 60 Hz (imperceptible) | 60 Hz field rate (better than 30 Hz frame) |
| **Best Use Case** | Sports, PC monitors, gaming | Legacy broadcast, static scenes |
| **Modern Status** | **Standard for all new displays** | Phased out / "Deinterlaced" by modern TVs |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** A television system uses **525 lines** per frame at a **30 Hz** frame rate with **2:1 interlacing**. Calculate:
- (i) Field frequency
- (ii) Horizontal line frequency
- (iii) Time per line
- (iv) Number of lines per field

**Solution:**

**(i) Field frequency:**

$$
f_{field} = 2 \times f_{frame} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal line frequency:**

$$
f_H = \frac{N}{2} \times f_{field} = \frac{525}{2} \times 60 = 262.5 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{f_H = 15{,}750 \text{ Hz}}
$$

(This is the standard **NTSC** system used in the USA/Japan.)

**(iii) Time per line:**

$$
T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15{,}750} \approx 63.5 \text{ μs}
$$

**(iv) Lines per field:**

$$
N_{field} = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{525}{2} = 262.5 \text{ lines/field}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fractional value (262.5) is **intentional and standard** — it ensures the odd field ends on a half-line, allowing the even field to interlace perfectly between the odd lines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Scanning System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Camera / Video Source 2D Image] --> B[Optical Lens System]
    B --> C[Scanning Sensor: CCD or CMOS]
    C --> D[Horizontal Sweep Generator]
    C --> E[Vertical Sweep Generator]
    D --> F[Sync Pulse Generator]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Composite Video Signal Output]
    G --> H[RF Modulator and Transmitter]
    H --> I[Channel / Transmission Medium]
    I --> J[Receiver RF Front End]
    J --> K[Video Demodulator]
    K --> L[Sync Separator]
    L --> M1[Horizontal Deflection Coils]
    L --> M2[Vertical Deflection Coils]
    K --> N[Video Amplifier]
    N --> O[Display Device: CRT or LCD]
    M1 --> O
    M2 --> O
```

### 4.2 State Diagram: Interlaced Scanning Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Field1_Start
    Field1_Start: Field 1 Start: Top Left Corner Line 1
    Field1_Start --> LineScan1
    LineScan1: Line 1 Scan Left to Right
    LineScan1 --> Retrace1
    Retrace1: H Retrace and Blanking
    Retrace1 --> LineScan3
    LineScan3: Line 3 Scan
    LineScan3 --> Retrace2
    Retrace2: H Retrace
    Retrace2 --> Field1_End
    Field1_End: Field 1 End Line 625 H Retrace V Flyback
    Field1_End --> Field2_Start
    Field2_Start: Field 2 Start Line 2 Interlaced Position
    Field2_Start --> LineScan2
    LineScan2: Line 2 Scan
    LineScan2 --> Field2_End
    Field2_End: Field 2 End Line 624 V Flyback
    Field2_End --> Complete_Frame
    Complete_Frame: Complete Frame 625 Lines Displayed in 40 ms
    Complete_Frame --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Visual Comparison Schematic: Raster Pattern Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROG[Progressive Scanning 625p at 25 Hz]
        P1[Line 1] --> P2[Line 2] --> P3[Line 3] --> P4[Line N 625] --> P5[Frame Complete 40 ms]
    end
    
    subgraph INT[Interlaced Scanning 625i at 50 Hz Fields]
        I1[Field 1 Line 1] --> I2[Field 1 Line 3] --> I3[Field 1 Line 5] --> I4[Field 1 Line 625]
        I4 --> I5[V Retrace Flyback 20 ms]
        I5 --> I6[Field 2 Line 2] --> I7[Field 2 Line 4] --> I8[Field 2 Line 624]
        I8 --> I9[Full Frame Interleaved 40 ms]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In the **Interlaced** path, observe that Field 1 (taking 20 ms) and Field 2 (taking 20 ms) are scanned separately. When temporally overlaid, the even lines of Field 2 fall **exactly in the gaps** between the odd lines of Field 1, producing a single full-resolution 625-line frame. This is the genius of the interlaced technique that **doubled the perceived flicker rate** without doubling bandwidth.

### 4.4 Composite Video Signal Waveform Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Composite Video Signal CVBS] --> B[Video Information 52.5 us]
    A --> C[Horizontal Blanking 11.5 us]
    C --> D[Front Porch 1.5 us]
    D --> E[Horizontal Sync Pulse 4.7 us]
    E --> F[Back Porch 5.3 us]
    F --> G[Start of Next Active Line]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
> **Define the term "Scanning" as applied to television systems. List any two advantages of interlaced scanning over progressive scanning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Scanning is the process of converting a two-dimensional image into a sequence of time-varying electrical signals by traversing the image area in a systematic pattern of horizontal lines, from left to right and top to bottom, in a rapid repetitive sequence. The reverse process reconstructs the image on the display.

**Two advantages of interlaced scanning (1 mark):**
1. **Bandwidth reduction:** Interlacing transmits half the lines per field, effectively halving the required video bandwidth for the same vertical resolution.
2. **Reduced flicker:** By increasing the field rate to 50 Hz (double the 25 Hz frame rate), the perceived flicker is greatly reduced, exploiting the persistence of vision of the human eye, without requiring a higher frame transmission rate.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
> **A TV system uses 625 lines per frame at 25 frames per second with 2:1 interlacing. Calculate the (i) field frequency, (ii) horizontal line frequency, and (iii) time per line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Field frequency:**
$f_{field} = 2 \times f_{frame} = 2 \times 25 = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$

**(ii) Horizontal line frequency:**
$f_H = \dfrac{N}{2} \times f_{field} = \dfrac{625}{2} \times 50 = \mathbf{15{,}625 \text{ Hz}}$

**(iii) Time per line:**
$T_H = \dfrac{1}{f_H} = \dfrac{1}{15{,}625} = \mathbf{64 \text{ μs}}$

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, **explain the functional architecture of a television scanning system** from camera to display. Clearly distinguish between horizontal and vertical scanning operations. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** An **NTSC television system** uses **525 lines** per frame, **30 frames per second**, with **2:1 interlacing**. Calculate:
- (i) Field frequency
- (ii) Horizontal line frequency
- (iii) Time per line
- (iv) Number of lines per field
- (v) If the aspect ratio is 4:3 and the active line time is 52 μs, calculate the **video bandwidth** using the relation $BW = \dfrac{0.8 \times AR}{2 \times T_{active}}$ and state the result in MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (A):**

**(a) Functional Architecture of a TV Scanning System (7 marks):**

A television scanning system performs two simultaneous operations: **transduction** (light → signal) and **reconstruction** (signal → image), synchronized by common timing signals.

1. **Image Capture Section (Transmitter):**
   - The **optical lens** focuses the 2D scene onto the camera's photosensitive target (CCD/CMOS or photoconductive plate in older tube cameras).
   - The **scanning sensor** is read out sequentially under the control of **horizontal and vertical sweep generators** that produce **sawtooth waveforms**.
   - The **sync pulse generator** combines horizontal sync, vertical sync, and equalizing pulses with the video information to form a **composite video signal (CVBS)**.

2. **Signal Processing & Transmission:**
   - The CVBS signal is amplified, **RF modulated** onto a carrier, and transmitted over the air or via cable.

3. **Image Reconstruction Section (Receiver):**
   - The receiver's **RF tuner** selects the channel, and the **video demodulator** recovers the baseband CVBS signal.
   - The **sync separator** circuit extracts horizontal and vertical sync pulses using amplitude discrimination.
   - These pulses drive the **horizontal deflection coils** (fast left-right sweep) and **vertical deflection coils** (slow top-bottom sweep) of the CRT yoke (or the gate drivers of a modern LCD).
   - The **video amplifier** modulates the beam intensity (or pixel brightness) in proportion to the original scene brightness.

4. **Synchronization Principle:**
   - The **critical** requirement is that the receiver's sweep oscillators are **locked in phase and frequency** to the transmitter's sweep. This is achieved by the **sync pulses** traveling with the video signal.

**(b) NTSC Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 525$ lines/frame, $f_{frame} = 30$ fps, 2:1 interlacing, $AR = 4:3$, $T_{active} = 52$ μs

**(i) Field frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_{field} = 2 \times f_{frame} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal line frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_H = \frac{N}{2} \times f_{field} = \frac{525}{2} \times 60 = 262.5 \times 60 = 15{,}750 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(iii) Time per line (1 mark):**

$$
T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15{,}750} \approx 63.49 \text{ μs}
$$

**(iv) Number of lines per field (1 mark):**

$$
N_{field} = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{525}{2} = 262.5 \text{ lines/field}
$$

**(v) Video Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
BW = \frac{0.8 \times AR}{2 \times T_{active}} = \frac{0.8 \times (4/3)}{2 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
BW = \frac{0.8 \times 1.333}{104 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.0667}{104 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{BW \approx 10{,}256 \text{ Hz} \approx 4.2 \text{ MHz}}
$$

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Compare **Progressive Scanning** and **Interlaced Scanning** in detail. Discuss the role of **persistence of vision** and **field frequency** in television display. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Derive the **horizontal line frequency**, **vertical field frequency**, and **active line time** for an Indian broadcast TV system (PAL-B/G) transmitting **625 lines per frame at 25 frames per second** with an aspect ratio of 4:3 and **18% line blanking**. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (B):**

**(a) Progressive vs Interlaced Scanning (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Progressive Scanning | Interlaced Scanning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Method** | All lines scanned sequentially in one pass | Frame split into 2 fields (odd/even lines) |
| **Frame Rate = Field Rate** | Yes | No, field rate = 2 × frame rate |
| **Bandwidth** | Requires higher bandwidth | Bandwidth reduced by ~2:1 |
| **Picture Quality** | Superior, no inter-line artifacts | Slight flicker, "comb effect" on motion |
| **Flicker Perception** | Less flicker at 25 Hz | Better perceived flicker at 50 Hz |
| **Complexity** | Simpler modern implementation | Complex sync, alignment critical |
| **Used in** | PC monitors, modern LCD/LED, HDTV | Legacy analog TV (PAL, NTSC, SECAM) |

**Role of Persistence of Vision (2 marks):**
The human eye retains an image for ~50–60 ms after viewing. If frames are flashed at ≥ 24 fps, motion appears continuous. **Interlacing exploits this by flashing 50 half-resolution fields per second, perceived as 25 full-resolution frames with minimal flicker.**

**Role of Field Frequency (1 mark):**
- A higher field frequency (50 Hz vs 25 Hz) **doubles the flicker rate**, making the picture appear more stable without requiring a higher information rate.
- This is the key engineering reason analog TV chose 2:1 interlacing.

**(b) PAL-B/G Derivation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 625$, $f_{frame} = 25$ fps, 2:1 interlacing, $AR = 4:3$, blanking = 18%

**Step 1: Vertical Field Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_{field} = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Horizontal Line Frequency (2 marks):**

$$
f_H = \frac{N}{2} \times f_{field} = \frac{625}{2} \times 50 = 15{,}625 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3: Time per Line (1 mark):**

$$
T_H = \frac{1}{15{,}625} = 64 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 4: Active Line Time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{active} = (1 - 0.18) \times T_H = 0.82 \times 64 = 52.48 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{active} \approx 52.5 \text{ μs}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for interlacing:** Many students write $f_H = N \times f_{frame}$ instead of $f_H = (N/2) \times f_{field}$. This single error **cascades through the entire problem** and forfeits 4–5 marks. Always begin by stating explicitly: *"The system uses 2:1 interlacing, so $f_{field} = 2 \times f_{frame}$."*
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Writing $15{,}625$ Hz without the "Hz" unit, or confusing μs with ms. Always write **units explicitly** in every line.
> 3. **Aspect Ratio Calculation Mistakes:** Aspect ratio is a **ratio** (4:3 = 1.333), not a fraction of the area. Do not multiply width × height.
> 4. **Confusing Field and Frame:** In interlaced systems, **field** is half a frame. Using them interchangeably is a major conceptual error.
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams in Part A:** KTU examiners expect a **neat labeled block diagram** for full marks (minimum 2–3 marks reserved for diagram quality in 7-mark questions).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- [x] **Scanning** = sequential conversion of 2D image → 1D electrical signal (and vice versa).
- [x] **Two simultaneous motions:** Horizontal (fast, line-by-line) + Vertical (slow, top-to-bottom).
- [x] **Persistence of vision (≈ 50 ms)** is the biological foundation of TV.
- [x] **Interlaced Scanning:** Frame = 2 fields; field rate = 2 × frame rate.
- [x] **Progressive Scanning:** Frame rate = field rate; all lines in one pass.
- [x] **PAL (India):** 625 lines, 25 fps, 50 fields/s, **fH = 15,625 Hz**, TH = 64 μs.
- [x] **NTSC (USA):** 525 lines, 30 fps, 60 fields/s, **fH = 15,750 Hz**, TH ≈ 63.5 μs.
- [x] **Blanking pulses** (HBP, VBP) hide retrace lines from the viewer.
- [x] **Standard Aspect Ratios:** **4:3** (SDTV) and **16:9** (HDTV/Modern).
- [x] **Interlaced advantage:** Halves bandwidth, doubles flicker rate.
- [x] **Interlaced disadvantage:** Inter-line flicker, motion artifacts (comb effect).
- [x] **Active line time ≈ 82% of TH** (rest is blanking/sync).
- [x] **Modern displays** (LCD/LED/OLED) use **progressive scanning** natively; legacy interlaced signals are deinterlaced by internal scalers.
- [x] **Master formulas:** $f_H = (N/2) \times f_{field}$ and $T_H = 1/f_H$.
- [x] **Always state units** (Hz, kHz, μs, ms) explicitly in exam answers.
- [x] **In interlaced systems, the number of lines per field is N/2** (often a fractional value like 312.5, which is **correct and standard**).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal and Vertical Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Horizontal and Vertical Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Synchronization in Video Systems?

**Synchronization** in a video display system is the precise timing mechanism that ensures the **sending end (camera/transmitter)** and the **receiving end (TV/monitor)** scan the image in perfect lockstep, both horizontally (line-by-line) and vertically (frame-by-frame). Without synchronization, the picture would roll, tear, or appear as scrambled noise.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the two fundamental synchronization signals are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Horizontal Synchronization (H-Sync):** A short timing pulse transmitted at the **end of every scan line** that commands the electron beam (in CRTs) or the display controller (in LCD/LED panels) to **fly back to the left side** of the screen to begin drawing the next line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Synchronization (V-Sync):** A longer timing pulse transmitted at the **end of every field/frame** that commands the display to **fly back to the top-left corner** of the screen to begin drawing the next frame/field.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Reading a Book

Imagine you are reading this page to a friend who is **blindfolded** and must copy it onto a blank sheet by listening to your voice:

- **Horizontal Sync** is like you saying *"Next line"* every time you finish reading a row. Your friend finishes the row, lifts the pen, moves it back to the left margin, and starts the new line — all triggered by that one word.
- **Vertical Sync** is like you saying *"Next page"* when you reach the bottom of the page. Your friend lifts the pen, moves it all the way to the **top of a fresh sheet**, and starts again.

If you forget to say *"Next line"*, your friend writes over the same line. If you forget *"Next page"*, the text becomes a tangled mess. **This is exactly what happens in a TV without sync** — the picture tears, rolls, or duplicates.

## 1.3 Where Sync Lives in the Composite Video Signal

In an **analog composite video** signal (e.g., CVBS — Composite Video Blanking Sync), three things are packed into a single wire:

1. **Luminance + Chrominance** (the actual picture information) — occupies the higher voltage levels.
2. **Blanking pulses** — bring the signal down to "black" level during retrace.
3. **Synchronization pulses** — drive the signal **below the black level** (called the *"sync tip"* or pedestal), uniquely positioned so a sync separator circuit can slice them out cleanly.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The H-Sync and V-Sync pulses are deliberately placed at the **lowest voltage level** of the composite video waveform (typically **0 V** to **−0.3 V** relative to a 1 V peak-to-peak signal), so they are easily distinguished from picture data which lies between **0.3 V (black)** and **1.0 V (white)**.

## 1.4 Key Standards at a Glance

| Parameter | NTSC (M) | PAL (B/G) |
|---|---|---|
| Total lines per frame | **525** | **625** |
| Frame rate | **29.97 fps** | **25 fps** |
| Field rate (interlaced) | **59.94 Hz** | **50 Hz** |
| Horizontal line frequency fH | **15,734.26 Hz** | **15,625 Hz** |
| V-Sync standard | **RS-170A** | **CCIR-B** |

Both standards use **interlaced scanning** (2:1), which means **one full frame = 2 fields**, where each field contains half the lines (odd or even).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Composite Video Waveform — Anatomy of a Single Line

A single horizontal line of the composite video signal contains the following time-segments, in order:

1. **Active Video** — the actual picture data for that line (luminance + chrominance).
2. **Front Porch** — a brief blanking period (signal at black level) **before** the sync pulse. Guarantees the sync separator has stabilized.
3. **Horizontal Sync Pulse** — a short negative-going pulse (tip goes *below* black level). Marks the end of the line.
4. **Back Porch** — blanking period **after** sync. In colour systems, the **colour burst** (8–10 cycles of the 3.579545 MHz colour subcarrier) rides on the back porch. This burst synchronizes the receiver's colour decoder.
5. The next **Active Video** starts the cycle again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Horizontal Line Period (TH) = Active Video Time + Horizontal Blanking Interval**

## 2.2 Vertical Synchronization — The Field/Frame Reset

At the end of an entire **field** (half-frame), the system inserts a **Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)**. Inside this VBI:

- **Pre-equalizing pulses** (5–6 short pulses at half-line intervals) — prepare the integrator-based V-sync separator.
- **Serrated V-sync pulses** (5 pulses whose tips are chopped to look like H-syncs at double rate) — this is the actual V-sync; the receiver detects them as V-sync, but the H-sync circuit continues to see regular H-sync pulses within them.
- **Post-equalizing pulses** (mirror of pre-equalizing).
- Then the new field's active video begins.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU 2024 scheme numerical problems on this topic hinge on these relationships. Memorize them as a tightly-coupled set.

$$
f_{\text{H}} \;=\; N_{\text{total}} \;\times\; f_{\text{frame}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{V (field)}} \;=\; 2 \;\times\; f_{\text{frame}} \quad \text{(interlaced)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{H}} \;=\; \frac{1}{f_{\text{H}}}
$$

$$
\text{H-Sync duty cycle} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{HSync}}}{T_{\text{H}}} \;\times\; 100\,\%
$$

$$
f_{\text{pixel clock}} \;=\; f_{\text{H}} \;\times\; N_{\text{pixels per line (total)}}
$$

$$
\text{H-Sync width (NTSC)} \;\approx\; 4.7\;\mu s
$$

$$
\text{V-Sync width (NTSC)} \;\approx\; 3\;T_{\text{H}} \;\approx\; 190.5\;\mu s
$$

| Quantity | Symbol | NTSC Value | PAL Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total lines per frame | $N_{\text{total}}$ | **525** | **625** | lines |
| Frame rate | $f_{\text{frame}}$ | **29.97** | **25** | fps |
| Field rate | $f_{\text{V}}$ | **59.94** | **50** | Hz |
| Line frequency | $f_{\text{H}}$ | **15,734.26** | **15,625** | Hz |
| Line period | $T_{\text{H}}$ | **63.556** | **64.000** | $\mu s$ |
| H-Sync width | $T_{\text{HSync}}$ | **4.7** | **4.7 ± 0.2** | $\mu s$ |
| Active video time | $T_{\text{active}}$ | **52.6** | **52.0** | $\mu s$ |
| H-Blanking interval | $T_{\text{HB}}$ | **10.9** | **12.0** | $\mu s$ |
| Colour subcarrier | $f_{\text{SC}}$ | **3.579545** | **4.433619** | MHz |

## 2.4 Why Synchronization Engineering Matters in the Real World

- **Display Manufacturing:** Every LCD, OLED, and LED panel datasheet lists H-Sync and V-Sync **polarity, frequency, and pulse width** tolerances. Mismatched sync is the #1 reason a new display controller board shows a "No Signal" or "Out of Range" message.
- **Broadcast Engineering:** TV transmitters embed H/V sync in the baseband video. Receivers extract them to drive deflection yokes. A poor sync separator causes **line tearing** (loss of H-sync) or **vertical roll** (loss of V-sync).
- **Video Capture & Streaming:** Frame grabbers and HDMI capture cards must lock onto the H/V sync of the source within microseconds. Modern systems use **Sync-on-Green (SOG)** or **Composite Sync** for legacy compatibility.
- **Gaming & VR:** Variable Refresh Rate (VRR), FreeSync, and G-Sync technologies are *modern digital descendants* of H/V sync — they continuously renegotiate the timing between GPU and display to eliminate stutter and tearing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Line Frequency from Frame Rate

**Given (PAL-B standard):**
- Total lines per frame: $N_{\text{total}} = 625$
- Frame rate: $f_{\text{frame}} = 25$ fps

**Goal:** Derive $f_{\text{H}}$.

**Step 1 — Lines per second.** Since 25 complete frames are transmitted every second, and each frame contains 625 lines, the total number of lines transmitted per second is:

$$
N_{\text{lines/sec}} = N_{\text{total}} \times f_{\text{frame}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting values.**

$$
N_{\text{lines/sec}} = 625 \times 25
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplication.**

$$
N_{\text{lines/sec}} = 15{,}625 \;\text{lines/second}
$$

**Step 4 — Equating to line frequency.** Since one line is drawn per period of the horizontal sync, the line frequency is numerically equal to lines-per-second:

$$
f_{\text{H}} = N_{\text{lines/sec}} = 15{,}625\;\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Concluding.** Therefore $f_{\text{H}} = \mathbf{15.625\;\text{kHz}}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Vertical (Field) Frequency for Interlaced Systems

**Given (NTSC-M):** 525 lines per frame, 30 fps nominal.

**Step 1 — Recognize interlacing.** In 2:1 interlace, each frame is split into 2 fields (odd lines + even lines). Thus fields are transmitted at twice the frame rate:

$$
f_{\text{V (field)}} = 2 \times f_{\text{frame}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
f_{\text{V (field)}} = 2 \times 29.97
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
f_{\text{V (field)}} = 59.94\;\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** This is the **vertical sync frequency** — the rate at which the V-sync pulse fires, i.e., the rate at which a complete odd or even field is laid down. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example: H-Sync Duty Cycle for NTSC

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{H}} = 63.556\;\mu s$
- $T_{\text{HSync}} = 4.7\;\mu s$

**Step 1 — Write the duty cycle definition.**

$$
D_{\text{HSync}} = \frac{T_{\text{HSync}}}{T_{\text{H}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
D_{\text{HSync}} = \frac{4.7}{63.556} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
D_{\text{HSync}} = 0.07395 \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$
D_{\text{HSync}} \approx 7.4\,\%
$$

This means the H-sync pulse is "on" for only about **7.4 %** of every line period — the rest is active video + blanking. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Pixel Clock Derivation for 1080p HD

**Given:** $1920 \times 1080$ @ 60 Hz progressive, with a total of **2200** pixels per line (active + blanking) and **1125** total lines per frame.

**Step 1 — Compute frame rate $f_{\text{frame}}$.**

$$
f_{\text{frame}} = 60\;\text{Hz (progressive, no interlace)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute horizontal line frequency.**

$$
f_{\text{H}} = 1125 \times 60
$$

$$
f_{\text{H}} = 67{,}500\;\text{Hz} = 67.5\;\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute pixel clock using the second formula.**

$$
f_{\text{pixel}} = f_{\text{H}} \times N_{\text{pixels per line}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{pixel}} = 67{,}500 \times 2200
$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$
f_{\text{pixel}} = 148.5\;\text{MHz}
$$

This **148.5 MHz** is the standard pixel clock for 1080p60 — a number every KTU student in Entertainment Electronics must recognize. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sync Signal Parameters Comparison

| Parameter | NTSC-M | PAL-B | 1080p60 (Digital) | 4K UHD 60 (Digital) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{\text{H}}$ (kHz) | 15.734 | 15.625 | 67.5 | 135.0 |
| $f_{\text{V}}$ (Hz) | 59.94 | 50.00 | 60.00 | 60.00 |
| $T_{\text{H}}$ ($\mu s$) | 63.556 | 64.000 | 14.815 | 7.407 |
| Interlaced? | Yes (2:1) | Yes (2:1) | No (progressive) | No (progressive) |
| H-Sync polarity | Negative | Negative | Positive (typically) | Positive (typically) |
| Total lines | 525 | 625 | 1125 | 2250 |
| Total pixels/line | n/a (analog) | n/a (analog) | 2200 | 4400 |
| Pixel clock | n/a | n/a | 148.5 MHz | 594.0 MHz |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Composite Video Line Waveform (Mermaid Block Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid cannot draw analog voltage vs. time waveforms with axis precision. Instead, this block-level architecture shows the **functional structure** of one horizontal line as a sequence of processing blocks, each representing a time-segment of the signal.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef active fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef blank fill:#444,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef sync fill:#FF3030,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef burst fill:#00C8FF,stroke:#000,color:#000

    A1[LINE START]:::blank --> A2[ACTIVE VIDEO LUMA PLUS CHROMA]:::active
    A2 --> A3[FRONT PORCH BLACK LEVEL]:::blank
    A3 --> A4[HORIZONTAL SYNC PULSE]:::sync
    A4 --> A5[BACK PORCH PLUS COLOUR BURST]:::burst
    A5 --> A6[ACTIVE VIDEO NEXT LINE]:::active
    A6 --> A7[LINE END]:::blank

    A7 -.repeats 624 more times.-> A1
```

**Block roles:**
- `ACTIVE VIDEO LUMA PLUS CHROMA` (yellow): picture data, 0.3 V → 1.0 V.
- `FRONT PORCH BLACK LEVEL` (grey): ~1.5 µs at 0.3 V.
- `HORIZONTAL SYNC PULSE` (red): ~4.7 µs at 0 V (tip).
- `BACK PORCH PLUS COLOUR BURST` (blue): colour subcarrier burst at 3.58 MHz / 4.43 MHz.

## 4.2 Sync Separator Block Diagram (Receiver Side)

This is the **functional architecture** of how a TV receiver extracts H-Sync and V-Sync from a composite video input.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef in fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000

    in1[COMPOSITE VIDEO INPUT]:::in --> proc1[DC CLAMP / BIAS RESTORER]:::proc
    proc1 --> proc2[SYNC SLICER COMPARATOR]:::proc
    proc2 --> proc3[LOW PASS FILTER H-PATH]:::proc
    proc2 --> proc4[INTEGRATOR V-PATH]:::proc

    proc3 --> proc5[HORIZONTAL AFC PHASE LOCK LOOP]:::proc
    proc5 --> out1[H-SYNC OUTPUT CLEAN PULSE TRAIN]:::out

    proc4 --> proc6[VERTICAL SYNC DETECTOR THRESHOLD]:::proc
    proc6 --> out2[V-SYNC OUTPUT CLEAN PULSE TRAIN]:::out

    proc5 -.feedback.-> proc3
    proc6 -.timing reset.-> proc5
```

**Why this architecture works:**
- The **DC Clamp** restores the DC reference (sync tip at 0 V) that is lost through AC coupling.
- The **Sync Slicer** is a fast comparator that triggers whenever the input falls *below* the black level — this captures only the sync pulses.
- The **Low-Pass Filter** strips out the H-syncs, while the **Integrator** averages them; during V-sync the serrated pulses keep the integrator output high, triggering the V-detector.

## 4.3 Vertical Blanking Interval — Field Reset Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef act fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pre fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef vsy fill:#FF3030,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef pst fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef burst fill:#00C8FF,stroke:#000,color:#000

    f1[LAST ACTIVE LINE OF FIELD]:::act --> f2[5 PRE EQUALIZING PULSES]:::pre
    f2 --> f3[5 SERRATED V-SYNC PULSES]:::vsy
    f3 --> f4[5 POST EQUALIZING PULSES]:::pst
    f4 --> f5[NEW FIELD ACTIVE VIDEO BEGINS]:::act
```

**Purpose of each block:**
- **Pre-equalizing pulses:** Settle the integrator of the V-sync separator before V-sync arrives (so the threshold is correctly primed).
- **Serrated V-sync:** The actual V-sync — the *serrations* ensure H-sync continues firing at double the line rate, so horizontal lock is maintained during vertical flyback.
- **Post-equalizing pulses:** Mirror of pre-equalizing, settling the integrator for the new field.

## 4.4 Modern Digital Display Timing Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef src fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef gpu fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef panel fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef timing fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000

    gpu1[GPU VIDEO CONTROLLER]:::gpu --> timing1[PIXEL CLOCK GENERATOR]:::timing
    timing1 --> timing2[H-SYNC TIMING BLOCK]:::timing
    timing1 --> timing3[V-SYNC TIMING BLOCK]:::timing
    timing2 --> timing4[PANEL ROW DRIVER]:::panel
    timing3 --> timing5[PANEL FRAME DRIVER]:::panel
    timing4 --> panel1[LCD / OLED / LED MATRIX]:::panel
    timing5 --> panel1
    panel1 --> src1[VIEWER]:::src
```

In modern flat-panel displays, the GPU/TCON emits **discrete H-Sync and V-Sync** signals on dedicated wires (TMDS/HDMI/LVDS). The concept is the **direct digital descendant** of the analog sync pulses in a CRT — the receiver no longer needs an integrator, just an edge detector.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — 3 Marks
**Differentiate between Horizontal Synchronization and Vertical Synchronization in a video signal.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Horizontal Sync (H-Sync) | Vertical Sync (V-Sync) |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Marks the end of one scan line; resets beam to left edge | Marks the end of one field/frame; resets beam to top |
| **Pulse width** | Short (~4.7 µs) | Long (~190 µs ≈ 3 line periods) |
| **Frequency** | ~15.6 kHz (15,625 Hz PAL) | ~50 Hz (PAL) / ~60 Hz (NTSC) |
| **Position in waveform** | Tip below black level, on every line | During Vertical Blanking Interval, once per field |
| **Extraction** | Low-pass / AFC loop | Integrator + threshold detector |

H-Sync operates at a *much higher* frequency and triggers the *horizontal* flyback, whereas V-Sync operates at a *much lower* frequency and triggers the *vertical* flyback. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Why are synchronizing pulses placed below the black level in a composite video signal?**

**Model Answer:**
Synchronizing pulses are deliberately placed **below the black level** (the "sync tip" region, typically 0 V to −0.3 V in a 1 V p-p signal) so that they are **never confused with picture data** (which ranges from 0.3 V black to 1.0 V white). A sync separator circuit uses a **threshold comparator set just above the sync tip** (e.g., at 0.2 V) — any signal crossing this threshold is identified as sync, regardless of picture content. This guarantees **reliable, content-independent sync extraction**. The 0.3 V guard band (between black and sync tip) is called the **setup** or **pedestal**. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **composite sync separator** in a TV receiver. How are H-Sync and V-Sync extracted from the same composite signal? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TV system uses **625 lines per frame**, **25 frames per second** with 2:1 interlace. The H-Sync pulse width is **4.7 µs**. Calculate: (i) the horizontal line frequency, (ii) the total horizontal line period, (iii) the H-Sync duty cycle, and (iv) the field (vertical) frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Need for sync separation.** *(1 mark)*

A composite video signal contains active video, blanking, and sync, all on a single wire. The receiver must **isolate** the sync pulses from the picture information to drive the deflection circuits.

**Step 2 — Block diagram description.** *(3 marks)*

```
[Composite Video] -> [AC Coupling] -> [DC Clamp / Bias Restorer] -> [Sync Slicer / Comparator] -> SPLIT
   -> [Low-Pass Filter] -> [H-AFC PLL] -> [H-Sync Output]
   -> [Integrator] -> [Threshold Detector] -> [V-Sync Output]
```

**Step 3 — Functional explanation of each block.** *(2 marks)*

- **DC Clamp:** AC coupling removes the DC component; the clamp restores the sync tip to a fixed reference (typically 0 V).
- **Sync Slicer:** A high-speed comparator slices the signal at a threshold just above the sync tip, producing a clean digital sync waveform.
- **Low-Pass Filter + H-AFC:** Filters the H-syncs and feeds them to a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) to generate a clean, jitter-free horizontal drive.
- **Integrator + V-Detector:** The integrator averages the sync stream; during V-sync the serrated pulses keep the integrator output high, triggering the V-detector.

**Step 4 — Concluding remark.** *(1 mark)*

The H-sync path exploits the *high frequency* of horizontal pulses; the V-sync path exploits the *long duration* of vertical pulses. Both share the same sliced sync stream, but separate cleanly because their **time constants are vastly different** (H: µs, V: ms).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{total}} = 625$ lines/frame
- $f_{\text{frame}} = 25$ fps
- Interlaced 2:1
- $T_{\text{HSync}} = 4.7\;\mu s$

**(i) Horizontal line frequency $f_{\text{H}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{H}} = N_{\text{total}} \times f_{\text{frame}} = 625 \times 25 = 15{,}625\;\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{\text{H}} = 15.625\;\text{kHz}}
$$ **— (2 marks)**

**(ii) Total horizontal line period $T_{\text{H}}$:**

$$
T_{\text{H}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{H}}} = \frac{1}{15{,}625}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{H}} = 64\;\mu s}
$$ **— (2 marks)**

**(iii) H-Sync duty cycle $D$:**

$$
D = \frac{T_{\text{HSync}}}{T_{\text{H}}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{4.7}{64} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
D = 0.0734 \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\boxed{D \approx 7.34\,\%}
$$ **— (2 marks)**

**(iv) Field (vertical) frequency $f_{\text{V}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{V}} = 2 \times f_{\text{frame}} = 2 \times 25 = 50\;\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{\text{V}} = 50\;\text{Hz}}
$$ **— (1 mark)**

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the structure of a **composite video waveform** for one horizontal line, clearly labelling the front porch, H-Sync, back porch, colour burst, and active video. Why is a back porch needed? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A modern **Full-HD (1920 × 1080)** display runs at **60 fps progressive**, with **2200 total pixels per line** and **1125 total lines per frame**. Calculate: (i) the horizontal line frequency, (ii) the pixel clock, and (iii) the H-Sync period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Draw / describe the line structure.** *(4 marks)*

A single horizontal line of composite video has the following time-segments, left to right:

| Segment | Approx. Duration (NTSC) | Voltage Level | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active Video | ~52.6 µs | 0.3 V → 1.0 V | Picture data (luma + chroma) |
| Front Porch | ~1.5 µs | 0.3 V (black) | Settles the sync separator's comparator |
| **H-Sync Pulse** | **~4.7 µs** | **0 V (sync tip)** | **Triggers horizontal flyback** |
| Back Porch | ~4.7–6.4 µs | 0.3 V (black) | Holds the beam at black during flyback settling; carries **colour burst** |
| (next line starts) | — | — | — |

**Step 2 — Why the back porch is needed.** *(2 marks)*

The back porch serves two critical functions:
1. **Mechanical settling time:** The electron beam (in a CRT) cannot reverse direction instantly. The back porch gives the horizontal deflection yoke time to settle before active video resumes — without it, the first few pixels of each line would be geometrically distorted.
2. **Colour Burst carrier:** In NTSC/PAL, the back porch carries a **8–10 cycle burst of the colour subcarrier**. This burst is sampled by the receiver's colour PLL to recover the exact colour phase, enabling accurate chroma decoding.

**Step 3 — Concluding remark.** *(1 mark)*

The back porch is the *unsung hero* of compatibility — it absorbs flyback transients *and* acts as the colour reference, all in a few microseconds of "black" signal.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{total lines}} = 1125$ lines/frame
- $N_{\text{pixels/line (total)}} = 2200$
- $f_{\text{frame}} = 60$ fps (progressive, so no interlace multiplier)

**(i) Horizontal line frequency $f_{\text{H}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{H}} = N_{\text{total lines}} \times f_{\text{frame}} = 1125 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{\text{H}} = 67{,}500\;\text{Hz} = 67.5\;\text{kHz}}
$$ **— (2 marks)**

**(ii) Pixel clock $f_{\text{pixel}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{pixel}} = f_{\text{H}} \times N_{\text{pixels per line}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{pixel}} = 67{,}500 \times 2200
$$

$$
f_{\text{pixel}} = 148{,}500{,}000\;\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{\text{pixel}} = 148.5\;\text{MHz}}
$$ **— (3 marks)**

**(iii) H-Sync period $T_{\text{H}}$:**

$$
T_{\text{H}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{H}}} = \frac{1}{67{,}500}
$$

$$
T_{\text{H}} = 1.4815 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{H}} = 14.815\;\mu s}
$$ **— (2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the interlace multiplier of 2** when computing field (V) frequency. Vertical sync fires **per field**, not per frame, in interlaced systems. A 25 fps PAL system has a V-sync rate of **50 Hz**, not 25 Hz.
> 2. **Mixing up NTSC and PAL line counts.** NTSC = 525, PAL = 625. These are *not* interchangeable; the H-frequency of 15,625 Hz is uniquely PAL.
> 3. **Skipping the units in the final answer.** KTU examiners *will* deduct a mark if you write 15625 without "Hz" or write 64 without "µs".
> 4. **Using the wrong base formula for digital displays.** For progressive (non-interlaced) systems, $f_{\text{V}} = f_{\text{frame}}$; the ×2 interlace multiplier **does not apply**.
> 5. **Not stating the H/V sync polarity.** Many modern digital displays require *positive* H/V sync, while legacy analog systems used *negative* sync. Always state polarity in timing diagrams.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Horizontal & Vertical Synchronization**

- **H-Sync** = line reset; ~4.7 µs wide; fires at **15.625 kHz (PAL)** or **15.734 kHz (NTSC)**.
- **V-Sync** = frame/field reset; ~190 µs wide; fires at **50 Hz (PAL)** or **60 Hz (NTSC)**.
- **Sync pulses live below the black level** of the composite video (sync tip region, 0 V to −0.3 V), enabling content-independent detection.
- **Front porch** = blanking **before** H-sync (~1.5 µs). **Back porch** = blanking **after** H-sync (~4.7 µs); carries the **colour burst** in colour systems.
- **Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)** contains **5 pre-equalizing + 5 serrated V-sync + 5 post-equalizing** pulses.
- **Master formulas** (memorize in this order):
   - $f_{\text{H}} = N_{\text{total}} \times f_{\text{frame}}$
   - $f_{\text{V (field)}} = 2 \times f_{\text{frame}}$ (interlaced)
   - $T_{\text{H}} = 1 / f_{\text{H}}$
   - $f_{\text{pixel}} = f_{\text{H}} \times N_{\text{pixels/line}}$
- **PAL standard numbers:** 625 lines, 25 fps, 50 Hz V-sync, 15,625 Hz H-sync, 4.43 MHz colour subcarrier.
- **NTSC standard numbers:** 525 lines, 29.97 fps, 59.94 Hz V-sync, 15,734.26 Hz H-sync, 3.579545 MHz colour subcarrier.
- **1080p60 reference:** 1125 total lines × 2200 total pixels/line × 60 fps → **67.5 kHz H-sync, 148.5 MHz pixel clock**.
- **Sync separator blocks (in order):** AC couple → DC clamp → sync slicer → split into H-path (LPF + AFC) and V-path (integrator + threshold detector).
- **Digital descendants:** HDMI/DisplayPort H-Sync and V-Sync are *discrete wire* signals, but the timing relationships derived above apply unchanged.
- **Sync polarity** matters: legacy analog = typically negative; modern digital flat panels = often positive. Always check the panel datasheet.
- **Tearing symptom** = lost H-sync. **Vertical roll symptom** = lost V-sync. (Favourite viva question!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Color Information
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Color Information – Fundamentals

## 1.1 Definition & Scope

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Color Information

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
*Color Information* in entertainment electronics refers to the **scientific representation, encoding, transmission, and reproduction of visible light wavelengths** (≈ **380 nm to 780 nm**) using standardized mathematical models (color spaces) and electronic systems. It is the backbone of television, cinema, gaming, and display technology.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine sunlight passing through a glass prism. What looks like "white" light spreads out into a rainbow — a continuous spectrum of wavelengths. Our eyes act like three different "light meters" that each respond to a band of this rainbow (red, green, blue). The brain combines these three meter readings into every color we perceive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Color is **NOT a property of light alone** — it is the result of *light + the human visual system + context*. A "red" apple is not emitting red; it is reflecting red wavelengths while absorbing the rest.

### Core Physical Constants & Metrics
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Wavelength of visible light:** **380 nm (violet) to 780 nm (red)**
- **Frequency range:** **≈ 430 THz (red) to 750 THz (violet)**
- **Standard reference white:** **D65 (6504 K)** and **Illuminant A (2856 K)** in CIE standards.
- **Luminance standard unit:** **candela per square meter (cd/m²)** or **nit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between **luminance** (physical light intensity), **brightness** (perceived intensity), and **luma** (gamma-corrected luminance used in video signals).

---

## 1.2 The Physics of Color – Light as Electromagnetic Radiation

Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. The relationship between wavelength ($\lambda$), frequency ($f$), and energy ($E$) is:

$$
c = f \lambda
$$

$$
E = h f = \frac{h c}{\lambda}
$$

Where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $c$ = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s

When a photon strikes the retina, its **energy** determines how the cone cells respond, producing the sensation of color.

| Wavelength Band | Color Sensation | Approx. Wavelength |
|---|---|---|
| Violet | Shortest visible | 380–450 nm |
| Blue | Cool | 450–495 nm |
| Green | Mid-spectrum | 495–570 nm |
| Yellow | Warm | 570–590 nm |
| Orange | Warm | 590–620 nm |
| Red | Longest visible | 620–780 nm |

---

## 1.3 Human Visual System – Biological Basis of Color

The human eye contains two types of photoreceptors:

- **Rods (≈ 120 million):** Sensitive to **luminance (brightness)** only — no color. Operate in low light (scotopic vision).
- **Cones (≈ 6–7 million):** Responsible for **color vision** (photopic vision). Three types:
  - **L-cones** (Long wavelength) → peak sensitivity **≈ 564 nm** (Red)
  - **M-cones** (Medium wavelength) → peak sensitivity **≈ 534 nm** (Green)
  - **S-cones** (Short wavelength) → peak sensitivity **≈ 420 nm** (Blue)

> [!NOTE]
> **Trichromatic Theory (Young-Helmholtz):** All perceivable colors can be matched by mixing the outputs of these three cone types. This is the biological basis of the **RGB** color model used in virtually all displays.

**Cone Ratio in Human Retina:** Approximately **L : M : S = 40 : 20 : 1**, meaning the eye is *most* sensitive to red-green information and *least* to blue — a key reason why video compression (e.g., **4:2:0 chroma subsampling**) preserves luma and reduces blue detail.

---

## 1.4 Colorimetry – The Science of Color Measurement

**Colorimetry** is the branch of color science concerned with quantitatively measuring human color perception. The foundation is the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** system established in **1931**.

### CIE 1931 RGB Color Matching Functions
Three reference primaries (R = 700 nm, G = 546.1 nm, B = 435.8 nm) are used to match any test color. The amounts of each primary required are called **tristimulus values**: $R$, $G$, $B$.

For a spectral power distribution $P(\lambda)$:

$$
R = \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{r}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

$$
G = \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{g}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

$$
B = \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{b}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

Where $\bar{r}(\lambda)$, $\bar{g}(\lambda)$, $\bar{b}(\lambda)$ are the **CIE 1931 RGB color matching functions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Point:** Some wavelengths (especially in the blue-green range around 500 nm) require *negative* amounts of one primary to match — this is mathematically inconvenient and led to the development of the **CIE XYZ** model with all-positive matching functions.

---

## 1.5 Color Mixing – Additive vs Subtractive

### Additive Color Mixing (Light Sources)
- **Rule:** Red + Green + Blue = **White**
- Used in: **TVs, monitors, smartphones, stage lighting**
- Primary colors: **R, G, B**
- Secondary colors: Cyan (G+B), Magenta (R+B), Yellow (R+G)

### Subtractive Color Mixing (Pigments/Inks)
- **Rule:** Cyan + Magenta + Yellow = **Black (in theory)**
- Used in: **Printing, photography, paint mixing**
- Primary colors: **C, M, Y** (+ **K = Key/Black** in CMYK)
- Secondary colors: Red, Green, Blue

| Property | Additive (RGB) | Subtractive (CMYK) |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Light emission | Light absorption |
| Total = All Primaries | White | Black |
| Used in | Displays, cameras | Print, painting |
| Channels | 3 | 4 (CMYK) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The CIE XYZ Color Model (1931)

To eliminate negative values, the CIE introduced a mathematically convenient **imaginary** primary set: **X, Y, Z**. The $Y$ value represents **luminance** — making it uniquely important for entertainment electronics.

$$
X = k \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

$$
Y = k \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

$$
Z = k \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

Where $k$ is a normalization constant.

### Normalization to $Y = 100$ (for white):
$$
k = \frac{100}{\int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda}
$$

### Chromaticity Coordinates (2D Representation)
Since $X + Y + Z$ defines intensity and the *ratio* defines color, we reduce to 2D:

$$
x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}
$$

$$
y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}
$$

$$
z = \frac{Z}{X + Y + Z} = 1 - x - y
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Plotting $(x, y)$ for all visible colors produces the famous **CIE Chromaticity Diagram** — a "horseshoe" where the curved boundary is the **spectral locus** (pure wavelengths) and inside is the gamut of all perceivable colors.

---

## 2.2 CIE L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB) – Perceptually Uniform Color Space

The XYZ space is **not perceptually uniform** — equal numerical distances do not represent equal perceived color differences. CIELAB solves this:

$$
L^* = 116 \, f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16
$$

$$
a^* = 500 \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]
$$

$$
b^* = 200 \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]
$$

Where $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ if $t > (6/29)^3$, otherwise $f(t) = \frac{1}{3}(29/6)^2 t + 4/29$.

- $L^*$ → **Lightness (0 to 100)**
- $a^*$ → **Green–Red axis** (negative = green, positive = red)
- $b^*$ → **Blue–Yellow axis** (negative = blue, positive = yellow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Color Difference ($\Delta E$):** Used in display calibration. $\Delta E < 1$ is imperceptible, $\Delta E < 3$ is broadcast-grade. Formula: $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$

---

## 2.3 Video Color Spaces – The Backbone of Entertainment Electronics

### 2.3.1 YUV Color Space (PAL/SECAM Analog TV)

Derived from RGB via a luminance-chrominance split. The eye is **more sensitive to brightness than color**, so chroma can be encoded with less bandwidth.

$$
Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B
$$

$$
U = 0.492 (B - Y)
$$

$$
V = 0.877 (R - Y)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coefficients (0.299, 0.587, 0.114) reflect the human eye's luminance sensitivity to the three primaries — green dominates because the eye is most sensitive to green wavelengths.

### 2.3.2 YIQ Color Space (NTSC Analog TV)

$$
Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B
$$

$$
I = 0.596 R - 0.274 G - 0.322 B
$$

$$
Q = 0.211 R - 0.523 G + 0.312 B
$$

- **I** = In-phase (orange-cyan axis)
- **Q** = Quadrature (magenta-green axis)
- I has wider bandwidth (1.3 MHz) than Q (0.4 MHz) in NTSC.

### 2.3.3 YCbCr (Digital Video, JPEG, MPEG, HDMI)

$$
Y  = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B
$$

$$
Cb = 0.564 (B - Y) + 128
$$

$$
Cr = 0.713 (R - Y) + 128
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> YCbCr is **NOT** an absolute color space — it requires specification of which RGB primaries, transfer function, and matrix coefficients are being used (BT.601, BT.709, BT.2020).

---

## 2.4 HSV / HSL – Human-Friendly Color Models

Designed for **artistic and UI use**, not for hardware.

- **Hue (H):** Color type (0°–360° on color wheel)
- **Saturation (S):** Colorfulness/purity (0% = gray, 100% = pure color)
- **Value (V) / Lightness (L):** Brightness

RGB → HSV conversion (for max and min channel values):

$$
V = \max(R, G, B)
$$

$$
S = \frac{V - \min(R, G, B)}{V} \quad (V \neq 0)
$$

$$
H = \begin{cases}
60^\circ \times \frac{G - B}{V - \min} & \text{if } V = R \\
60^\circ \times \left(2 + \frac{B - R}{V - \min}\right) & \text{if } V = G \\
60^\circ \times \left(4 + \frac{R - G}{V - \min}\right) & \text{if } V = B
\end{cases}
$$

If $H < 0$, add $360°$.

---

## 2.5 Color Temperature & White Balance

**Color Temperature** is measured in **Kelvin (K)** and describes the hue of a light source by comparing it to a **black-body radiator**.

| Temperature | Source | Appearance |
|---|---|---|
| 1000 K | Candle | Deep orange/red |
| 2856 K | Tungsten (Illuminant A) | Warm white |
| 3200 K | Studio tungsten (film) | Slightly warm |
| 5500 K | Mid-day sun | Neutral white |
| 6500 K | D65 (HDTV standard white) | Cool white |
| 10000 K | Heavily overcast sky | Bluish white |

> [!NOTE]
> **D65 (6504 K)** is the reference white for sRGB, BT.709, and most modern HDTV standards.

---

## 2.6 Gamma Correction – Critical for Entertainment Electronics

CRTs (cathode-ray tubes) have a **non-linear voltage-to-luminance** response approximated by a power law:

$$
L = V^{\gamma}
$$

Where typical CRT gamma $\gamma \approx 2.2$ to 2.5. To compensate, camera/video systems apply **gamma encoding** before transmission:

$$
V_{\text{encoded}} = L^{1/\gamma}
$$

This allocates more code values to dark regions (where the eye is more sensitive) and fewer to bright regions — saving bandwidth while preserving perceptual quality.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Luminance (BT.601) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Analog TV, SD video |
| Luminance (BT.709) | $Y = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ | HDTV, sRGB |
| Luminance (BT.2020) | $Y = 0.2627R + 0.6780G + 0.0593B$ | UHD, 4K/8K |
| Chromaticity | $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$ | CIE diagram |
| Tristimulus | $X, Y, Z = k \int P(\lambda)\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}\,d\lambda$ | Colorimetry |
| YUV (PAL) | $U = 0.492(B-Y)$, $V = 0.877(R-Y)$ | Analog broadcast |
| YIQ (NTSC) | $I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B$ | NTSC analog TV |
| YCbCr offset | $Cb = 0.564(B-Y)+128$, $Cr = 0.713(R-Y)+128$ | Digital video |
| Gamma encode | $V_{enc} = L^{1/\gamma}$, $\gamma \approx 2.2$ | Camera/display |
| Color difference | $\Delta E = \sqrt{\Delta L^2 + \Delta a^2 + \Delta b^2}$ | Calibration |
| Hue (HSV) | $H = 60^\circ \cdot \frac{(G-B)}{(V-\min)}$ if $V=R$ | UI design |
| Saturation (HSV) | $S = (V - \min(R,G,B))/V$ | UI design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Luminance Coefficients Are What They Are

The luminance coefficients in $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ are derived from the **photopic luminosity function** $V(\lambda)$ — the human eye's sensitivity curve under normal lighting conditions.

### Step 1 – Define photopic luminosity
The CIE 1924 photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ peaks at **555 nm** (green-yellow) and falls off toward the red and blue ends of the spectrum.

### Step 2 – Express it in terms of RGB primaries
The NTSC primaries are:
- R: 700 nm
- G: 546.1 nm
- B: 435.8 nm

The contribution of each primary to luminance is proportional to the area under $V(\lambda)$ weighted by the spectral power of that primary. The result is:

$$
Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B
$$

The dominance of $G$ reflects the eye's peak sensitivity at green wavelengths.

### Step 3 – Verify with a pure green test color
If a color is pure green ($R = 0, G = 1, B = 0$ in normalized RGB):

$$
Y = 0.299(0) + 0.587(1) + 0.114(0) = 0.587
$$

This is consistent with $V(546 \text{ nm}) \approx 0.95$ being one of the highest in the visible spectrum — but the coefficient accounts for the *spectral spread* of the green primary, not just the peak.

### Step 4 – Sum of coefficients must equal 1 (for white)
For pure white $(R = G = B = 1)$:

$$
Y = 0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114 = 1.000 \;\;\checkmark
$$

This normalization ensures white maps to luminance = 1 (or 100%).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RGB → YCbCr (BT.601) — Full Expansion

Starting from the analog YUV matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y  &= 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B \\
U  &= 0.492 (B - Y) \\
V  &= 0.877 (R - Y)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 – Substitute $Y$ into $U$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 0.492 \, (B - 0.299 R - 0.587 G - 0.114 B) \\
  &= 0.492 B - 0.147 R - 0.289 G - 0.056 B \\
  &= -0.147 R - 0.289 G + 0.436 B
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 – Substitute $Y$ into $V$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 0.877 \, (R - 0.299 R - 0.587 G - 0.114 B) \\
  &= 0.877 R - 0.262 R - 0.515 G - 0.100 B \\
  &= 0.615 R - 0.515 G - 0.100 B
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 – Add the +128 offset for 8-bit digital range
For unsigned 8-bit video, $Y$ ranges [16, 235] and chroma ranges [16, 240] (limited range). The $+128$ offset centers the bipolar chroma around mid-gray:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y  &= 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B \\
Cb &= -0.169 R - 0.331 G + 0.500 B + 128 \\
Cr &= 0.500 R - 0.419 G - 0.081 B + 128
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 – Verify with pure white $(255, 255, 255)$ in 8-bit

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y  &= 16 + (235 - 16) \cdot (0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114) \\
   &= 16 + 219 \cdot 1 = 235 \;\;\checkmark \\
Cb &= 128 + 0 = 128 \;\;\checkmark \\
Cr &= 128 + 0 = 128 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> This confirms white maps to $(Y, Cb, Cr) = (235, 128, 128)$ — a must-remember value in KTU digital video problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse Transform (YCbCr → RGB)

For KTU problems involving decoding, the inverse matrix must be reconstructed.

### Step 1 – Start with the differences
$$
\begin{aligned}
R - Y &= \frac{Cr - 128}{0.500} \\
B - Y &= \frac{Cb - 128}{0.500}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 – Use $Y$ definition
$$
G = \frac{Y - 0.299 R - 0.114 B}{0.587}
$$

### Step 3 – Substitute and simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= Y + 1.402 (Cr - 128) \\
G &= Y - 0.34414 (Cb - 128) - 0.71414 (Cr - 128) \\
B &= Y + 1.772 (Cb - 128)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 – Numerical Example
Given $Y = 76.245$, $Cb = 84.972$, $Cr = 240.436$ (limited range):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 76.245 + 1.402(112.436) = 76.245 + 157.635 = 233.88 \approx 234 \\
G &= 76.245 - 0.34414(-43.028) - 0.71414(112.436) \\
  &= 76.245 + 14.81 - 80.30 = 10.76 \approx 11 \\
B &= 76.245 + 1.772(-43.028) = 76.245 - 76.245 = 0.00
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields **pure red** $(R, G, B) = (234, 11, 0)$ — a known test color in SMPTE color bars! ✓

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: CIE XYZ → Chromaticity for Standard White D65

For the D65 illuminant, the standard values are:
$$
X_n = 95.047, \quad Y_n = 100.000, \quad Z_n = 108.883
$$

### Step 1 – Sum the components
$$
X_n + Y_n + Z_n = 95.047 + 100.000 + 108.883 = 303.930
$$

### Step 2 – Compute chromaticity
$$
x = \frac{95.047}{303.930} = 0.3127
$$

$$
y = \frac{100.000}{303.930} = 0.3290
$$

### Step 3 – Result
$$
(x, y) = (0.3127, \, 0.3290)
$$

> This point is the **standard D65 white point** on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram — a must-know KTU value.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Color Models in Entertainment

| Property | RGB | YUV | YCbCr | HSV | CIE-XYZ | CIELAB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensions | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| Device-dependent? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Perceptually uniform? | No | No | No | Partial | No | **Yes** |
| Luminance separated? | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | No | Partially | No |
| Used in | Displays, cameras | PAL/SECAM | Digital video | UI/graphics | Reference | Calibration |
| Typical bit depth | 8/10/12 | Analog | 8/10/12 | 8/16/float | float | float |
| KTU exam weight | High | High | **Highest** | Medium | Medium | Medium |

---

## 3.6 Chroma Subsampling — The Bandwidth Trick

Since the eye is more sensitive to luma than chroma, video codecs reduce chroma resolution:

| Format | Luma Sample | Chroma (Cb, Cr) Samples | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4:4:4** | Full | Full | Master, post-production |
| **4:2:2** | Full | Half horizontal | Professional SDI, ProRes |
| **4:2:0** | Full | Quarter (½ H × ½ V) | DVD, Blu-ray, streaming, H.264 |
| **4:1:1** | Full | Quarter horizontal | DV, NTSC DV camcorders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse 4:2:0 (quarter chroma) with 4:1:1 (quarter chroma, but different spatial arrangement). In 4:2:0, chroma is shared between adjacent rows; in 4:1:1, chroma is reduced only horizontally.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Color Information Flow in a Modern TV System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scene[Scene Light] --> cam[Camera Sensor RGB]
    cam --> gamma1[Gamma Encoding 1 over gamma]
    gamma1 --> mat1[RGB to YCbCr Matrix]
    mat1 --> sub[Chroma Subsampling 4 2 0]
    sub --> enc[Video Encoder H 264 / HEVC]
    enc --> ch[Transmission Channel]
    ch --> dec[Video Decoder]
    dec --> up[Chroma Upsampling]
    up --> mat2[YCbCr to RGB Matrix]
    mat2 --> gamma2[Gamma Decoding gamma over 1]
    gamma2 --> disp[Display RGB Subpixels]
    disp --> eye[Human Eye L M S Cones]
    eye --> brain[Visual Cortex Color Perception]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: CIE Colorimetry Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    source[Light Source P lambda] --> cmf[Multiply by CIE Matching Functions]
    cmf --> int[Integrate over Visible Spectrum 380 to 780 nm]
    int --> tri[Tristimulus Values X Y Z]
    tri --> chr[Compute Chromaticity x y]
    chr --> diag[CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram]
    tri --> lab[Convert to CIELAB L a b]
    lab --> deltaE[Color Difference Delta E]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: RGB to YCbCr Encoding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[R input] --> mul1[Multiply by 0.299]
    g[G input] --> mul2[Multiply by 0.587]
    b[B input] --> mul3[Multiply by 0.114]
    mul1 --> sumY[Adder]
    mul2 --> sumY
    mul3 --> sumY
    sumY --> outY[Y output]
    r --> diff1[R minus Y]
    diff1 --> mul4[Times 0.500 plus 128]
    mul4 --> outCr[Cr output]
    b --> diff2[B minus Y]
    diff2 --> mul5[Times 0.500 plus 128]
    mul5 --> outCb[Cb output]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Additive vs Subtractive Color Mixing (Gamut Relationship)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Additive RGB] --> A1[Red plus Green equals Yellow]
    A --> A2[Red plus Blue equals Magenta]
    A --> A3[Green plus Blue equals Cyan]
    A --> A4[R plus G plus B equals White]
    S[Subtractive CMY] --> S1[Cyan plus Magenta equals Blue]
    S --> S2[Cyan plus Yellow equals Green]
    S --> S3[Magenta plus Yellow equals Red]
    S --> S4[C plus M plus Y equals Black]
    A -.complementary.-> S
```

---

## 4.5 Conceptual Map: Relationship Between Color Spaces

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CIE[CIE XYZ 1931 Reference Color Space] --> RGB[sRGB BT 709 RGB]
    CIE --> LAB[CIELAB L a b]
    CIE --> UVW[CIE 1960 UCS]
    RGB --> YUV[YUV Analog PAL]
    RGB --> YIQ[YIQ Analog NTSC]
    RGB --> YCb[YCbCr Digital BT 601]
    RGB --> HSV[HSV HSL UI Models]
    YCb --> sub[Chroma Subsampling 4 2 0]
    LAB --> DE[Delta E Color Difference]
    UVW --> CCT[Correlated Color Temperature]
```

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Human Visual System — Color Processing Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Light 380 to 780 nm] --> Lens[Eye Lens]
    Lens --> Retina[Retina]
    Retina --> Rods[Rods Luminance only]
    Retina --> S[S Cones Blue 420 nm]
    Retina --> M[M Cones Green 534 nm]
    Retina --> Lc[L Cones Red 564 nm]
    Rods --> Brain
    S --> Brain
    M --> Brain
    Lc --> Brain
    Brain --> LGN[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus]
    LGN --> V1[Primary Visual Cortex]
    V1 --> V4[Color Processing V4]
    V4 --> perc[Final Color Perception]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
