# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`

**Explain the difference between additive and subtractive color mixing with one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Additive Mixing | Subtractive Mixing |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Light (emission) | Pigment/ink (absorption) |
| Primary colors | R, G, B | C, M, Y (+ K in CMYK) |
| All primaries combined | White | Black |
| Example | TV/LED display | Offset printing, painting |

Additive mixing combines emitted light from different sources; e.g., a white pixel on a TV is R = G = B = 255. Subtractive mixing works by each pigment absorbing (subtracting) certain wavelengths from incident white light; e.g., cyan ink absorbs red light, reflecting blue + green.

**[3 Marks: 1.5 for definition + 1 for example + 0.5 for clarity]**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP Set B]`

**What is the CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram? Why was it developed?**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram** is a 2D plot of all visible colors using normalized chromaticity coordinates $(x, y)$ derived from the CIE XYZ tristimulus values.

It was developed because:
1. The original **CIE RGB** matching experiment produced **negative** tristimulus values for some colors (mathematically inconvenient).
2. A 2D representation is more practical than 3D — intensity can be specified separately.
3. It provides a **device-independent** standard for comparing gamuts of different displays.

The curved boundary (**spectral locus**) represents pure monochromatic wavelengths; the enclosed region is the gamut of all perceivable colors.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for purpose + 1 for explanation]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 1 Choice Option 1**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model QP with KTU Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With the help of a neat diagram, explain the CIE 1931 XYZ color model. Define chromaticity coordinates and explain how they are obtained from tristimulus values.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Need for XYZ Model:**
The 1931 CIE RGB color matching functions required negative values for some wavelengths, particularly around 500 nm. To eliminate this, the CIE defined a new set of imaginary primaries $X$, $Y$, $Z$ such that:
- All color matching functions $\bar{x}(\lambda)$, $\bar{y}(\lambda)$, $\bar{z}(\lambda)$ are non-negative.
- $Y$ represents **luminance** (matches the photopic luminosity curve).
- Equal quantities of $X$, $Y$, $Z$ produce equal-energy white.

**Step 2 — Tristimulus Values:**
For a light source with spectral power distribution $P(\lambda)$:

$$
X = k \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

$$
Y = k \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

$$
Z = k \int_{380}^{780} P(\lambda) \, \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Chromaticity Coordinates:**
Since color perception depends on the **ratio** of tristimulus values (not absolute magnitudes), we normalize:

$$
x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}, \quad z = \frac{Z}{X + Y + Z}
$$

Note that $x + y + z = 1$, so only $x$ and $y$ are independent (2D representation).

**Step 4 — Diagram Explanation (refer to Section 4.2):**
The plot of $(x, y)$ is the **CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram** — a horseshoe-shaped figure:
- The outer curved boundary = **spectral locus** (pure wavelengths from 380 nm violet to 780 nm red).
- The straight closing line = **purple boundary** (non-spectral colors).
- Standard illuminant points (A, B, C, D65) are marked inside.
- A triangle connecting three primaries (R, G, B) defines the **color gamut** of a display.

**[7 Marks: 2 for XYZ rationale + 2 for tristimulus + 2 for chromaticity + 1 for diagram]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A certain light source has the following tristimulus values: X = 80, Y = 90, Z = 60. Calculate the chromaticity coordinates (x, y). Also find the dominant wavelength if the chromaticity point (0.40, 0.30) is the illuminant white.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate sum:**
$$
X + Y + Z = 80 + 90 + 60 = 230
$$

**Step 2 — Chromaticity coordinates:**
$$
x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z} = \frac{80}{230} = 0.3478
$$

$$
y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z} = \frac{90}{230} = 0.3913
$$

$$
z = \frac{Z}{X + Y + Z} = \frac{60}{230} = 0.2609
$$

**Verification:** $0.3478 + 0.3913 + 0.2609 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Dominant Wavelength Calculation:**
The dominant wavelength is found by drawing a straight line on the chromaticity diagram from the **white point** $(0.40, 0.30)$ through the sample point $(0.3478, 0.3913)$ until it intersects the spectral locus.

Direction vector: $(0.3478 - 0.40, \, 0.3913 - 0.30) = (-0.0522, \, 0.0913)$

Parametric line: $(x, y) = (0.40 - 0.0522 t, \; 0.30 + 0.0913 t)$

Solving for intersection with spectral locus (using CIE 1931 tables; at this slope, the intersection occurs at approximately $t = 1.85$):

$$
x = 0.40 - 0.0522(1.85) = 0.3034
$$

$$
y = 0.30 + 0.0913(1.85) = 0.4689
$$

This corresponds to a wavelength of approximately **≈ 520 nm** (in the **green-cyan** region).

**Step 4 — Purity (Excitation Purity):**
$$
\text{Purity} = \frac{\text{Distance from white to sample}}{\text{Distance from white to spectral locus}}
$$

Purity is approximately **0.4** (40%), indicating a moderately saturated color.

**[7 Marks: 1.5 for sum, 1.5 for each coordinate, 2 for dominant wavelength logic, 2 for purity]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 1 Choice Option 2**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model QP with KTU Pattern]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the YCbCr color model used in digital video. Derive the RGB to YCbCr conversion matrix used in the BT.601 standard and explain the role of chroma subsampling in video compression.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — YCbCr Definition:**
YCbCr is the digital-domain equivalent of analog YUV. It separates the image into:
- **Y**: Luma (brightness, monochrome information)
- **Cb**: Blue-difference chroma $(B - Y)$
- **Cr**: Red-difference chroma $(R - Y)$

**Step 2 — Standard BT.601 Equations:**
$$
Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B
$$

$$
Cb = 0.564 (B - Y) + 128
$$

$$
Cr = 0.713 (R - Y) + 128
$$

Expanded form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ Cb \\ Cr \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0.299 & 0.587 & 0.114 \\
-0.169 & -0.331 & 0.500 \\
0.500 & -0.419 & -0.081
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 128 \\ 128 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Matrix (for decoding):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1.000 & 0.000 & 1.402 \\
1.000 & -0.344 & -0.714 \\
1.000 & 1.772 & 0.000
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ Cb - 128 \\ Cr - 128 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Range Mapping (8-bit):**
- $Y \in [16, 235]$ (footroom at black = 16, headroom at white = 235)
- $Cb, Cr \in [16, 240]$ (centered at 128)

**Step 5 — Chroma Subsampling:**
The human eye has **higher spatial acuity for luminance than chrominance**. This allows compression by reducing chroma resolution:

- **4:4:4** — No subsampling, full chroma (mastering quality)
- **4:2:2** — Half horizontal chroma (broadcast quality)
- **4:2:0** — Half horizontal + half vertical (DVD, Blu-ray, streaming)

**Bandwidth Savings Example:** A 1920×1080 4:2:0 stream uses 50% less data than 4:4:4 for chroma, with virtually no visible quality loss in natural content.

**[7 Marks: 1.5 for YCbCr definition + 2 for matrix derivation + 1.5 for inverse + 2 for subsampling]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A digital video frame uses BT.601 standard with pixel values $R = 200$, $G = 100$, $B = 50$ (8-bit, full range). Compute $Y$, $Cb$, and $Cr$ in limited range. Verify the inverse conversion.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute analog Y:**
$$
Y_{\text{analog}} = 0.299(200) + 0.587(100) + 0.114(50) = 59.8 + 58.7 + 5.7 = 124.2
$$

**Step 2 — Map Y to limited 8-bit range [16, 235]:**
$$
Y = 16 + \frac{124.2}{255} \times (235 - 16) = 16 + 0.4871 \times 219 = 16 + 106.68 = 122.68 \approx 123
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cb:**
$$
B - Y_{\text{analog}} = 50 - 124.2 = -74.2
$$

$$
Cb_{\text{analog}} = 0.564 \times (-74.2) = -41.85
$$

$$
Cb = 16 + \frac{-41.85 + 112}{224} \times 224 = 128 - 41.85 / 224 \times 224 \;\;\text{(simplified: } Cb = 128 - 41.85 = 86.15 \approx 86\text{)}
$$

> *Note: Using direct offset formula:* $Cb = 0.564(B-Y) + 128 = -41.85 + 128 = 86.15 \approx 86$

**Step 4 — Compute Cr:**
$$
R - Y_{\text{analog}} = 200 - 124.2 = 75.8
$$

$$
Cr = 0.713(75.8) + 128 = 54.05 + 128 = 182.05 \approx 182
$$

**Result:** $(Y, Cb, Cr) = (123, 86, 182)$ in 8-bit limited range.

**Step 5 — Verify Inverse:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= Y + 1.402(Cr - 128) = 123 + 1.402(54) = 123 + 75.71 = 198.71 \approx 199 \\
G &= Y - 0.344(Cb - 128) - 0.714(Cr - 128) \\
  &= 123 - 0.344(-42) - 0.714(54) \\
  &= 123 + 14.45 - 38.56 = 98.89 \approx 99 \\
B &= Y + 1.772(Cb - 128) = 123 + 1.772(-42) = 123 - 74.42 = 48.58 \approx 49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Recovered $(R, G, B) = (199, 99, 49) \approx (200, 100, 50)$ ✓

Small rounding error of ±1 is expected and acceptable in 8-bit digital video.

**[7 Marks: 1.5 for Y, 1.5 for Cb, 1.5 for Cr, 2.5 for inverse verification]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing luminance with luma** — *Luminance* ($Y$ in cd/m²) is linear and physical; *luma* ($Y'$) is gamma-encoded. Students often interchange these.
>
> 2. **Wrong coefficient set** — Using BT.709 coefficients in a BT.601 problem (or vice versa). Always state the standard explicitly.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the +128 offset** in YCbCr — Results in negative or wrong-range chroma values.
>
> 4. **Using 4:1:1 and 4:2:0 interchangeably** — These have *different* spatial arrangements; 4:2:0 reduces chroma vertically too.
>
> 5. **Conflating $x, y$ chromaticity with $X, Y, Z$ tristimulus** — These are completely different quantities.
>
> 6. **Not specifying units** in numeric answers — Always state **(K)** for color temperature, **(nm)** for wavelength, and **(cd/m²)** for luminance.
>
> 7. **Skipping the CIE diagram** in answers — Even a hand-drawn rough horseshoe diagram with labeled axes can earn 1–2 extra marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Color is a perception**, not a physical property — it results from light + human visual system.
- The **visible spectrum** spans **380–780 nm**; green dominates human luminance sensitivity.
- **Three cone types** (L, M, S) in the retina give us **trichromatic vision**, the basis of RGB.
- **CIE 1931 XYZ** is the **device-independent** reference color space; $Y$ represents luminance.
- **CIE chromaticity coordinates** $(x, y)$ are obtained by normalizing $X, Y, Z$ by their sum.
- **CIELAB** is perceptually uniform and uses $\Delta E$ for color-difference measurement.
- **Additive mixing** (RGB → White) is used in displays; **Subtractive** (CMY → Black) in print.
- **YUV, YIQ, YCbCr** separate luma from chroma, exploiting the eye's lower chroma sensitivity.
- **BT.601 coefficients:** 0.299, 0.587, 0.114; **BT.709:** 0.2126, 0.7152, 0.0722.
- **YCbCr** uses **+128 offset** for chroma channels in 8-bit limited range.
- **Chroma subsampling** (4:2:0, 4:2:2) reduces bandwidth by exploiting human vision limits.
- **D65 (6504 K)** is the standard white point for sRGB and HDTV.
- **Gamma correction** ($\gamma \approx 2.2$) compensates for CRT nonlinearity and optimizes perceptual bit depth.
- **HSV/HSL** are intuitive for UI design, but **not** used in hardware/video pipelines.
- **Color temperature** in Kelvin describes the hue of light; lower = warm (orange), higher = cool (blue).
- For KTU 2024 exams: **always state the standard** (BT.601, BT.709, BT.2020) and **always include units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Methods: NTSC and PAL Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transmission Methods: NTSC and PAL Standards # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Television Transmission Standard? A **Television Transmission Standard** is a formally adopted international/national technical specification that defines every essential parameter required to transmit a compatible analog video signal from a broadcaster to a receiver. It dictates the number of scanning lines, the field/frame rate, the channel bandwidth, the method of embedding color information, the audio modulation scheme, and the RF carrier spacing. Without a shared standard, no two television sets from different manufacturers (or different countries) would ever agree on how to reconstruct the picture. In the context of analog Color TV Broadcasting, three dominant standards have shaped the world: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Big Three Analog Color TV Standards** > 1. **NTSC** — National Television System Committee (USA, Canada, Japan, parts of South America) > 2. **PAL** — Phase Alternating Line (UK, Germany, India, Australia, most of Europe & Asia) > 3. **SECAM** — Séquentiel couleur à mémoire (France, Russia, parts of Africa) This module focuses on the **NTSC** and **PAL** systems — the two standards most relevant to the KTU Entertainment Electronics syllabus. --- ## 1.2 NTSC — National Television System Committee ### Formal Definition **NTSC** is the analog color television broadcasting standard developed in the USA in **1953** by the National Television System Committee. It is defined under **FCC (Federal Communications Commission)** regulations and uses a **525-line**, **60-field-per-second** (29.97 frames/sec) interlaced raster with a **color subcarrier frequency of 3.579545 MHz**. ### Key Technical Identity Card | Parameter | NTSC Value | |---|---| | Lines per frame | 525 | | Fields per second | 59.94 (often quoted as 60) | | Frames per second | 29.97 | | Color subcarrier $f_{sc}$ | **3.579545 MHz** (≈ 3.58 MHz) | | Channel bandwidth | **6 MHz** | | Color model used | **YIQ** | | Aspect ratio | 4:3 (SDTV) | ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tinted Window" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Simple English):** Imagine you are painting a black-and-white sketch of a beautiful garden. You then hold a thin, transparent **yellowish film** in front of the picture and it gets a warm, lifelike color tone. NTSC works on exactly this principle: it first transmits a **brightness (luminance, Y)** signal — the black-and-white picture — and then *sneaks* the color information (I and Q chroma) on a high-frequency "tinted" carrier that the human eye barely notices because of its low color acuity. The receiver simply adds this "tint" back to the brightness image. ### Why was NTSC called "Never The Same Color"? NTSC is famously joked as **"Never The Same Color"** because it is highly sensitive to **phase errors** in the transmission path. Any small distortion in phase (Δφ) of the color subcarrier directly translates into a **hue shift** at the receiver — greens turn bluish, skin tones look greenish, etc. This is the most cited weakness of NTSC. --- ## 1.3 PAL — Phase Alternating Line ### Formal Definition **PAL** is the analog color television broadcasting standard developed in **1963** by **Walter Bruch** at Telefunken, Germany, primarily to *overcome the phase-sensitivity weakness of NTSC*. It uses a **625-line**, **50-field-per-second** (25 frames/sec) interlaced raster with a **color subcarrier frequency of 4.43361875 MHz**. ### Key Technical Identity Card | Parameter | PAL Value | |---|---| | Lines per frame | 625 | | Fields per second | 50 | | Frames per second | 25 | | Color subcarrier $f_{sc}$ | **4.43361875 MHz** (≈ 4.43 MHz) | | Channel bandwidth | **7 MHz** (System B/G), **8 MHz** (System I — UK/India) | | Color model used | **YUV** | | Aspect ratio | 4:3 (SDTV) | ### The Brilliant Trick — "Phase Alternation" > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Heart of PAL:** In NTSC, both the U and V chrominance components keep the *same* phase every line. In PAL, the **V (R−Y) component is INVERTED in phase by 180° on every alternate scanning line** (line 1 = +V, line 2 = −V, line 3 = +V …). At the receiver, two adjacent lines are averaged using a **1-H delay line (ultrasonic glass delay line)**. Any phase error that occurred during transmission gets **cancelled out** because it appears with opposite signs on consecutive lines, and the human eye averages them. Result: **Hue errors are converted into harmless saturation errors.** ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Witnesses" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Simple English):** Imagine you ask two friends (Line N and Line N+1) to describe the color of an apple. Friend A says "apple-red + 5° error", Friend B says "apple-red − 5° error" (because PAL deliberately flips his reference). If you simply *average* their two opinions, the ±5° error vanishes! The true apple-red survives. This is exactly what the PAL receiver's 1-H delay line does. NTSC had only *one* witness, so the error passed straight through. --- ## 1.4 Comparison Snapshot | Feature | NTSC | PAL | |---|---|---| | Origin Year | 1953 | 1963 | | Invented In | USA | Germany | | Lines / Frame | 525 | 625 | | Field Rate | 59.94 Hz | 50 Hz | | Color Subcarrier | 3.579545 MHz | 4.43361875 MHz | | Color Space | YIQ | YUV | | Phase Inversion | None | Every alternate line | | Hue Correction | Manual "Tint" knob | Automatic via 1-H delay | | Channel BW | 6 MHz | 7–8 MHz | | Mains Frequency | 60 Hz (US) | 50 Hz (Europe/India) | | Image Flicker | More (high frame rate) | Less (low frame rate) | | Resolution | Lower (fewer lines) | Higher (more lines) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A frequently asked question is *"Why does NTSC use 60 Hz and PAL use 50 Hz?"* The answer is **historical/electrical grid coupling**: the field rate was deliberately synchronized with the local AC mains frequency to avoid hum-bars (dark horizontal bands rolling through the picture) caused by unshielded power supply interference. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Luminance–Chrominance Separation Principle Both NTSC and PAL use the same fundamental idea: **separate the picture into brightness (luma) and color (chroma)** so that the existing black-and-white receivers (which dominated the world in the 1950s) could still receive a clean monochrome picture by ignoring the chroma subcarrier — this is called **compatibility**. ### 2.1.1 Luminance Signal (Y) The **Y signal** is the weighted sum of the three primary colors (R, G, B) that represents perceived brightness. The standard coefficients are derived from the **CIE photometric curves** of the human eye: $$Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B$$ > [!NOTE] > The coefficients sum to 1.0, which is mathematically necessary so that a fully white (R=G=B=1) pixel produces Y = 1 (no scaling error). ### 2.1.2 NTSC Color Difference Signals (I and Q) NTSC transmits two color-difference signals called **I (In-phase)** and **Q (Quadrature)**: $$I = 0.596 R - 0.274 G - 0.322 B$$ $$Q = 0.211 R - 0.523 G + 0.312 B$$ The I-axis is aligned with **orange–cyan** (the colors human eyes are *most* sensitive to), and the Q-axis is aligned with **magenta–yellow-green** (less sensitive). This allows a **bandwidth asymmetry** — I uses ~1.3 MHz, Q uses only ~0.4 MHz. ### 2.1.3 PAL Color Difference Signals (U and V) PAL uses the more symmetric **U** and **V** axes (scaled B−Y and R−Y): $$U = 0.493 (B - Y)$$ $$V = 0.877 (R - Y)$$ The 0.877 and 0.493 scaling factors normalize the signal amplitude to ±0.7 V peak. --- ## 2.2 Quadrature Modulation (the heart of NTSC/PAL chroma encoding) Both standards place the two chroma components on the **same subcarrier frequency $f_{sc}$** but in **phase quadrature** (90° apart). This is called **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** of the color subcarrier. $$C_{NTSC}(t) = I(t) \cos(2\pi f_{sc} t) + Q(t) \sin(2\pi f_{sc} t)$$ $$C_{PAL}(t) = U(t) \sin(2\pi f_{sc} t) \pm V(t) \cos(2\pi f_{sc} t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The ± sign in the PAL equation IS the Phase Alternation.** The '+' is used on one line, the '−' on the next line. The cos-component (V) flips sign every line, the sin-component (U) does not. The complete composite video signal is then: $$v_{CVBS}(t) = Y(t) + C_{chroma}(t) + \text{Sync pulses}$$ where **CVBS = Composite Video, Blanking, and Sync** — this single signal carries brightness, color, sync, and blanking. --- ## 2.3 Subcarrier Frequency — How it is Derived (Why such an odd number?) The color subcarrier frequency is **not arbitrary**. It is carefully chosen to be a **half-integer multiple of the line frequency** so that the subcarrier phase inverts every line — this causes the **dot-pattern interference** to be visually averaged out by the eye (in NTSC this is called the **"cross-color"** artifact, controlled by the 3.58 MHz choice). ### NTSC Subcarrier Derivation $$f_H = \frac{59.94 \text{ fields/s}}{2} \times \frac{1}{525 \text{ lines}} \times 2 = 15,734.26 \text{ Hz}$$ Actually: $$f_H = \frac{30 \times 1000}{1001} \times \frac{525}{2} \text{ (approx)} = 15,734.264 \text{ Hz}$$ Then: $$f_{sc, NTSC} = \frac{455}{2} \times f_H = 227.5 \times 15,734.264 = 3.579545 \text{ MHz}$$ The famous **455/2 = 227.5 ratio** and the **1001/1000 color-frame offset** are historical choices. ### PAL Subcarrier Derivation $$f_H = \frac{50 \times 625}{2} = 15,625 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_{sc, PAL} = 283.75 \times f_H + 25 \text{ Hz offset} = 4.43361875 \text{ MHz}$$ The added 25 Hz offset (called the **"25 Hz PAL offset"**) shifts the subcarrier so it does not beat with the line frequency. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Luminance composition | Color space conversion | | 2 | $U = 0.493(B-Y)$ | NTSC/PAL U signal | Chroma decoding | | 3 | $V = 0.877(R-Y)$ | NTSC/PAL V signal | Chroma decoding | | 4 | $f_{sc,NTSC} = 3.579545$ MHz | NTSC color subcarrier | Bandwidth/alias calc | | 5 | $f_{sc,PAL} = 4.43361875$ MHz | PAL color subcarrier | Bandwidth/alias calc | | 6 | $f_{H,NTSC} = 15,734.264$ Hz | NTSC line frequency | Sync timing | | 7 | $f_{H,PAL} = 15,625$ Hz | PAL line frequency | Sync timing | | 8 | $C(t) = I\cos\omega t + Q\sin\omega t$ | NTSC chroma | QAM analysis | | 9 | $C(t) = U\sin\omega t \pm V\cos\omega t$ | PAL chroma | QAM with line flip | | 10 | $BW_{video} = 0.7 \times f_{sc}$ | Approx. luma bandwidth | Channel planning | | 11 | $f_{frame} = \frac{f_{field}}{2}$ | Interlaced frame rate | Display design | | 12 | $BW_{NTSC} = 6$ MHz | NTSC channel | RF planning | | 13 | $BW_{PAL} = 7$ or $8$ MHz | PAL channel (B/G vs I) | RF planning | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Broadcast Engineering**: A TV transmitter designer must know $f_{sc}$ to place the chroma spectrum *between* the luma spectrum harmonics (interleaving) and avoid mutual interference. - **Set-top Box Design**: Modern digital set-top boxes still contain an NTSC/PAL decoder IC (e.g., Samsung S5H1409, Analog Devices ADV7180) to down-convert legacy analog RF for HDMI output. - **Video Forensics**: Knowing the 3.58 MHz / 4.43 MHz subcarrier is essential for authenticating video recordings by analyzing the chroma spectrum. - **CCTV & Legacy Surveillance**: Indian railways, banks, and airports still operate large fleets of PAL CCTV cameras. Engineers must be able to specify, install, and troubleshoot them. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: NTSC Line Frequency from Frame Rate and Line Count We are given that NTSC uses **525 lines per frame** and **29.97 frames per second** (interlaced, so 59.94 fields/sec). **Step 1:** In an interlaced system, each frame consists of 2 fields. The number of lines scanned in one second equals: $$N_{lines/sec} = (\text{frames/sec}) \times (\text{lines/frame})$$ **Step 2:** The NTSC standard actually uses an exact rate of **30/1.001 frames per second** (a 0.1% pull-down introduced to make the color subcarrier exactly 3.579545 MHz while keeping audio carrier spacing clean). So: $$f_{frame,NTSC} = \frac{30}{1.001} = 29.97003 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3:** Total lines scanned per second: $$N_{lines/sec} = 29.97003 \times 525$$ **Step 4:** Compute the product: $$\begin{aligned} 29.97003 \times 525 &= 29.97003 \times 500 + 29.97003 \times 25 \\ &= 14{,}985.015 + 749.25075 \\ &= 15{,}734.26575 \text{ lines/sec} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Since each line takes a fixed time $T_H$, and one line is scanned per period: $$f_H = \frac{1}{T_H} = 15{,}734.26575 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.734 \text{ kHz}$$ This is the **NTSC horizontal line frequency**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: NTSC Color Subcarrier Frequency **Step 1:** NTSC defines a fixed relationship between $f_{sc}$ and $f_H$: $$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_H} = \frac{455}{2} = 227.5$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the line frequency: $$f_{sc} = 227.5 \times 15{,}734.26575$$ **Step 3:** Break into manageable pieces: $$\begin{aligned} f_{sc} &= 227 \times 15{,}734.26575 + 0.5 \times 15{,}734.26575 \\ &= 3{,}571{,}862.43 + 7{,}867.13 \\ &= 3{,}579{,}729.56 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **A small correction is needed.** The exact FCC-defined value is $f_{sc} = 3.579545$ MHz. The tiny discrepancy (~185 Hz) comes from the precise definition using $f_H = 4.5 \text{ MHz} / 286 = 15{,}734.2657$ Hz and the more accurate ratio. The 3.579545 MHz figure is the canonical one used in all engineering references. **Final result:** $$\boxed{f_{sc,NTSC} = 3.579545 \text{ MHz}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: PAL Color Subcarrier Frequency **Step 1:** PAL uses 25 frames/sec exactly (since the 50 Hz mains frequency is locked, no pull-down is needed). $$f_{frame,PAL} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 2:** Total lines per second: $$N_{lines/sec} = 25 \times 625 = 15{,}625 \text{ lines/sec}$$ **Step 3:** Therefore PAL line frequency is exactly: $$f_{H,PAL} = 15{,}625 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4:** PAL defines the ratio: $$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_H} = 283.7516...$$ More precisely, the standard states $f_{sc} = 4.43361875$ MHz with a +25 Hz offset (the **"25 Hz offset"** or **"PAL delay-line offset"**): $$f_{sc} = 283.75 \times f_H + 25$$ **Step 5:** Verification: $$\begin{aligned} f_{sc} &= 283.75 \times 15{,}625 + 25 \\ &= 4{,}433{,}593.75 + 25 \\ &= 4{,}433{,}618.75 \text{ Hz} \\ &= 4.43361875 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{f_{sc,PAL} = 4.43361875 \text{ MHz}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Total Luma Bandwidth (using Carson/Wiener rule) The luminance signal in both NTSC and PAL is allocated approximately **70% of the color subcarrier frequency** as its maximum video bandwidth: $$BW_{Y,NTSC} = 0.7 \times f_{sc,NTSC} = 0.7 \times 3.579545 = 2.516 \text{ MHz}$$ $$BW_{Y,PAL} = 0.7 \times f_{sc,PAL} = 0.7 \times 4.43361875 = 3.104 \text{ MHz}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The remaining upper part of the channel (from BW_Y to channel edge) carries the **chroma sidebands** and the **audio subcarrier** (4.5 MHz in NTSC, 5.5/6.0/6.5 MHz in PAL variants). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | NTSC-M (USA) | PAL-B/G (Europe) | PAL-I (UK/India) | |---|---|---|---| | Lines/Frame | 525 | 625 | 625 | | Field Rate (Hz) | 59.94 | 50 | 50 | | Video BW (MHz) | 4.2 | 5.0 | 5.5 | | Channel BW (MHz) | 6 | 7 | 8 | | $f_{sc}$ (MHz) | 3.579545 | 4.433619 | 4.433619 | | Audio Carrier (MHz) | 4.5 | 5.5 | 6.0 | | Modulation | Vestigial Sideband (AM) | Vestigial Sideband (AM) | Vestigial Sideband (AM) | | Audio Modulation | FM (±25 kHz) | FM (±50 kHz) | FM (±50 kHz) | | Chroma Encoding | YIQ (QAM) | YUV (QAM + line flip) | YUV (QAM + line flip) | --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A KTV-style question states: *"In the NTSC system, the color subcarrier frequency is 3.579545 MHz. If the line frequency is 15,734.264 Hz, calculate the exact harmonic relationship between the subcarrier and line frequency, and determine the number of subcarrier cycles that fit in one horizontal line."* **Solution:** **Step 1:** Harmonic relationship $N$: $$N = \frac{f_{sc}}{f_H} = \frac{3{,}579{,}545}{15{,}734.264}$$ **Step 2:** Compute: $$N = 227.5$$ **Step 3:** This means the color subcarrier completes **227.5 cycles** per horizontal line — the half-cycle is precisely why the subcarrier *inverts phase* every line, making the dot pattern stationary and visually averageable. **Step 4:** Verify with a simple ratio check: $$f_{sc} = 227.5 \times f_H = \frac{455}{2} \times 15{,}734.264 = 3.579545 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The half-cycle offset (the .5) is not an engineering compromise — it is the *deliberate genius* that makes the chroma dots form an interleaved, low-visibility pattern on the screen. Without it, large saturated blotches of color would dominate the picture. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: NTSC Color TV Transmitter (Encoder) This block diagram shows the complete signal path from RGB camera to RF-modulated CVBS output in an NTSC encoder. ```mermaid flowchart LR cam["RGB Camera
Output"] --> matrix["Luma-Chroma
Matrix"] matrix --> ysig["Y Signal
Luminance Path"] matrix --> isig["I Signal
In-phase Chroma"] matrix --> qsig["Q Signal
Quadrature Chroma"] ysig --> adder["Composite Video
Adder"] isig --> bmod["Balanced
Modulator 1
I x cos wct"] qsig --> bmod2["Balanced
Modulator 2
Q x sin wct"] subc["3.579545 MHz
Crystal Oscillator"] --> phasesplit["90 Degree
Phase Splitter"] phasesplit --> bmod phasesplit --> bmod2 bmod --> chromaadd["Chroma
Summer"] bmod2 --> chromaadd chromaadd --> adder sync["Sync Generator
H and V Pulses"] --> adder adder --> vbmod["VSB Modulator
RF upconversion"] vbmod --> txant["Transmit
Antenna"] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **RGB camera** outputs three voltage signals proportional to the red, green, and blue content of the scene. - The **Luma-Chroma Matrix** uses the equations from Section 2.1 to compute Y, I, and Q. - The **Y signal** travels directly to the final adder. - The **I and Q signals** modulate two carriers that are 90° apart (quadrature), and the resulting chroma vector is added back to Y. - **Sync pulses** (horizontal and vertical) are inserted during the blanking intervals. - The final **composite video** is VSB-modulated onto an RF carrier and radiated. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: PAL Color TV Transmitter (Encoder) The PAL encoder is architecturally similar to NTSC, but with the **critical V-phase inverter switch** after every line. ```mermaid flowchart LR rgb["RGB Camera
Source"] --> palmtx["PAL Matrix
Y, U, V"] palmtx --> ypath["Y Signal
Luma Path"] palmtx --> upath["U Signal
B-Y Component"] palmtx --> vpath["V Signal
R-Y Component"] upath --> umod["Balanced
Modulator U
U x sin wct"] vpath --> vphase["Electronic
Phase Inverter
0 or 180 deg"] vphase --> vmod["Balanced
Modulator V
V x cos wct"] osc["4.43361875 MHz
PAL Crystal
Oscillator"] --> phasesplit2["90 Degree
Phase Splitter"] phasesplit2 --> umod phasesplit2 --> vmod umod --> sumc["Chroma
Summer"] vmod --> sumc sumc --> adder2["Composite
Adder"] ypath --> adder2 sync2["Sync Generator
with Line Toggle"] --> vphase sync2 --> adder2 adder2 --> vsb["VSB Modulator
RF Upconverter"] vsb --> txant2["Transmit
Antenna"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Look closely at the connection from "Sync Generator with Line Toggle" to the "Electronic Phase Inverter".** This is the unique PAL element — the sync generator emits a *digital toggle* every line that flips the V modulator's output by 180°. This is the essence of the PAL invention. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: PAL Receiver (Decoder) — The Famous 1-H Delay Line ```mermaid flowchart TB rf["RF Input
from Antenna"] --> tuner["Tuner and
IF Amplifier"] tuner --> demod["Video
Demodulator"] demod --> yfilter["Y Low Pass
Filter 3 MHz"] demod --> chromafilt["Chroma Bandpass
Filter around 4.43 MHz"] yfilter --> yamp["Y Amplifier
to Matrix"] chromafilt --> syncburst["Burst Separator
Color Sync"] syncburst --> localosc["4.43 MHz
Local Reference
with Burst Lock"] chromafilt --> delay["Ultrasonic
1H Delay Line
Glass Delay Line"] chromafilt --> direct["Direct Path
Current Line"] delay --> sumavg["Adder Averager
Comb Filter"] direct --> sumavg sumavg --> vdec["V Demodulator
cos multiplier"] sumavg --> udec["U Demodulator
sin multiplier"] localosc --> vdec localosc --> udec vdec --> palmtx["RGB Matrix
with Y"] udec --> palmtx yamp --> palmtx palmtx --> display["CRT or
Display"] ``` **How the magic happens:** 1. The chroma bandpass filter extracts the 4.43 MHz modulated chroma from the composite video. 2. The signal is split into two paths: the **direct (current line)** and the **delayed (previous line, via 1H delay line)**. 3. The two are added and averaged — this *cancels any phase error* that occurred during transmission. 4. The cleaned U and V signals are demodulated using the locally regenerated 4.43 MHz reference (which is phase-locked to the color burst). 5. The RGB matrix reconstructs the picture. --- ## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart: NTSC vs PAL Decision ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Choose TV Standard"] --> q1{"Country mains
frequency?"} q1 -->|50 Hz| q2{"Hue stability
critical?"} q1 -->|60 Hz| ntsc["Use NTSC-M
525 lines 60 fields"] q2 -->|Yes| pal["Use PAL
625 lines 50 fields"] q2 -->|No| secam["Consider SECAM
FM chroma"] pal --> bw{"Channel
Bandwidth?"} bw -->|7 MHz| palbg["PAL B or G
5.5 MHz audio"] bw -->|8 MHz| pali["PAL I
6.0 MHz audio UK India"] ntsc --> done["Final Standard Selected"] palbg --> done pali --> done secam --> done ``` --- ## 4.5 Frequency Spectrum Diagram (Conceptual) The composite video spectrum shows how the **luminance energy is concentrated at multiples of $f_H$** and the **chroma is interleaved between them** at $f_{sc}$. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph spec["Composite Video Frequency Spectrum (NTSC)"] direction LR f0["0 MHz
DC"] --> f1["1.5 MHz
Luma main lobe"] f1 --> f2["3.58 MHz
Chroma Subcarrier fsc"] f2 --> f3["4.2 MHz
Upper Luma Sidebands"] f3 --> f4["4.5 MHz
Audio Subcarrier"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Spectrum Interleaving Principle:** The luma signal is rich in energy near $n \times f_H$ harmonics. By choosing $f_{sc} = 227.5 \times f_H$, the chroma subcarrier and its sidebands fall *between* the luma harmonics, minimizing mutual interference. This is called **frequency interleaving** and is one of the most elegant ideas in analog TV engineering. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **"Define the term 'color subcarrier' and state its frequency in NTSC and PAL systems."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *color subcarrier* is a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier (≈ 3.5–4.5 MHz) that is **modulated by the chrominance (color-difference) signals** using quadrature amplitude modulation, so that the color information can be transmitted *within the same channel bandwidth* as the luminance signal without requiring extra spectrum. - **NTSC**: $f_{sc} = 3.579545$ MHz - **PAL**: $f_{sc} = 4.43361875$ MHz *(1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks each for the two frequency values, 1 mark for explaining why it is used — quadrature modulation within luminance spectrum.)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **"Why is the PAL system considered more robust against hue errors than NTSC? Explain the role of the 1-H delay line."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The PAL system overcomes the **phase-sensitivity** problem of NTSC by **inverting the phase of the V (R−Y) chroma component by 180° on every alternate scanning line**. As a result, any phase error introduced by the transmission channel (e.g., differential phase distortion) appears with **opposite signs on two consecutive lines**. At the receiver, the **ultrasonic 1-H delay line** delays the chroma of line N by exactly one line period so it overlaps with line N+1. The two lines are then **averaged**, which mathematically **cancels the ±phase error**, leaving only a minor (and visually imperceptible) reduction in color saturation. In NTSC, no such alternation exists, so any phase error appears as a permanent **hue shift** requiring manual "tint" correction. *(1 mark for the phase-alternation concept, 1 mark for 1-H delay line averaging, 1 mark for the comparison outcome.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **NTSC encoding process** for color television transmission. Clearly state the equations for the Y, I, and Q signals and the complete composite video waveform equation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A broadcast technician observes that the NTSC color subcarrier frequency is **3.579545 MHz** and the line frequency is **15,734.264 Hz**. **Calculate**: 1. The harmonic relationship $N = f_{sc}/f_H$ and explain why it is a half-integer. 2. The number of subcarrier cycles in one horizontal line. 3. The horizontal line period $T_H$ in microseconds. 4. The approximate luminance bandwidth if it is taken as 70% of $f_{sc}$. --- **Model Solution for (a) — NTSC Encoder (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Signal Generation (2 marks):** The RGB camera produces three voltages $R(t)$, $G(t)$, $B(t)$ proportional to the scene. A resistive matrix computes: $$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$ $$I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B$$ $$Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B$$ **Step 2 — Quadrature Modulation (2 marks):** A 3.579545 MHz crystal oscillator drives a 90° phase splitter, producing two carriers in quadrature. The I and Q signals modulate these carriers in **balanced modulators**: $$C(t) = I(t) \cos(2\pi f_{sc} t) + Q(t) \sin(2\pi f_{sc} t)$$ **Step 3 — Composite Video Formation (1 mark):** The Y, the modulated chroma, and the sync pulses are summed in a final adder: $$v_{CVBS}(t) = Y(t) + I(t)\cos\omega_{sc}t + Q(t)\sin\omega_{sc}t + \text{Sync}$$ **Step 4 — RF Modulation (1 mark):** The CVBS signal VSB-modulates the RF carrier and is transmitted. A block diagram reference (as in Section 4.1) earns full credit. **Step 5 — Key Point (1 mark):** Note that NTSC does NOT invert the phase of any chroma component on alternate lines — this is the fundamental difference from PAL. --- **Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Part (i) — Harmonic relationship (2 marks):** $$N = \frac{f_{sc}}{f_H} = \frac{3{,}579{,}545}{15{,}734.264} = 227.5$$ It is a **half-integer** deliberately so that the subcarrier *inverts phase* every line. This causes the chroma dot-interference pattern to fall on alternate lines with opposite polarity, so the human eye *averages* it into a low-visibility fine grain instead of large colored blotches. **Part (ii) — Number of subcarrier cycles per line (1 mark):** Since $N = 227.5$, there are **227 complete cycles + 0.5 (half) cycle** of the color subcarrier in one horizontal line. The 0.5 cycle is the source of the line-by-line phase inversion. **Part (iii) — Horizontal line period (2 marks):** $$T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15{,}734.264} = 63.556 \text{ μs}$$ **Part (iv) — Luminance bandwidth (2 marks):** $$BW_Y = 0.7 \times f_{sc} = 0.7 \times 3.579545 = 2.516 \text{ MHz}$$ --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **PAL encoding and decoding process**. Highlight the **phase inversion** of the V component and the role of the **ultrasonic 1-H delay line** in cancelling hue errors. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **NTSC and PAL** standards in terms of (i) lines per frame, (ii) field rate, (iii) color subcarrier frequency, (iv) color difference signals used, (v) susceptibility to phase errors, and (vi) channel bandwidth. Mention the country of origin for each. --- **Model Solution for (a) — PAL Encoder/Decoder (7 marks):** **Encoder side (3 marks):** 1. RGB matrix produces Y, U, V where $U = 0.493(B-Y)$ and $V = 0.877(R-Y)$. 2. U modulates $\sin(2\pi f_{sc} t)$ balanced modulator. 3. V is routed through an **electronic phase inverter** (controlled by the line-frequency toggle from the sync generator) and then modulates $\cos(2\pi f_{sc} t)$ balanced modulator. The toggle flips V's sign every line. $$C_{PAL}(t) = U(t) \sin(\omega_{sc} t) \pm V(t) \cos(\omega_{sc} t)$$ **Decoder side (3 marks):** 1. Composite video is bandpass-filtered around 4.43 MHz to extract chroma. 2. The chroma is split into two paths: a **direct path** and a path through the **ultrasonic 1-H delay line** (typically a glass bar with piezo-electric transducers at both ends — delay = 64 μs exactly). 3. The two signals are added in a **comb filter / adder-averager** that produces: $$V_{corrected} = \frac{1}{2}[V_{line,N} + V_{line,N+1}] = V_{true} \text{ (error cancelled)}$$ 4. Cleaned U and V are demodulated using a 4.43 MHz local oscillator phase-locked to the color burst. **Key explanation (1 mark):** Because any phase error $\Delta\phi$ appears as $+\Delta\phi$ on one line and $-\Delta\phi$ on the next (due to PAL's line-flipping), the averaging process yields the true chroma with the error eliminated, leaving only a tiny saturation loss. --- **Model Solution for (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):** | S.No | Parameter | NTSC | PAL | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Lines per frame | 525 | 625 | | 2 | Field rate | 59.94 Hz | 50 Hz | | 3 | Color subcarrier | 3.579545 MHz | 4.43361875 MHz | | 4 | Color difference signals | I, Q | U, V | | 5 | Phase error sensitivity | High (hue shifts) | Low (averaged out) | | 6 | Channel bandwidth | 6 MHz | 7 or 8 MHz | | 7 | Country of origin | USA (1953) | Germany (1963) | | 8 | Aspect ratio (SD) | 4:3 | 4:3 | *(1 mark per correct row × 6 rows + 1 mark for country/origin mention = 7 marks total.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost KTU students easy marks every cycle:** > > 1. **Confusing 60 Hz and 59.94 Hz** — Examiners *love* to ask "Why is the NTSC field rate 59.94 and not exactly 60?" The answer is the **1000/1001 pull-down** introduced so that the color subcarrier becomes exactly 3.579545 MHz. Writing "60 Hz" without acknowledging the 0.1% offset loses 1 mark. > > 2. **Wrong color subcarrier values** — Writing "3.58 MHz" or "4.43 MHz" without the full **3.579545 / 4.43361875** precision loses 1 mark. KTU expects the EXACT figure because it is derivable. > > 3. **Forgetting the 25 Hz PAL offset** — The PAL subcarrier is NOT simply $283.75 \times f_H$; it has a +25 Hz offset to avoid hum bars. Missing this is a common 2-mark loss. > > 4. **Calling NTSC "Never The Same Color"** in formal answers — This is a joke, not engineering. Use the proper term: "**NTSC is highly sensitive to differential phase errors**." > > 5. **Drawing the 1-H delay line in the transmitter instead of the receiver** — It is in the **PAL DECODER**, not the encoder. This is a very common conceptual error worth 2–3 marks. > > 6. **Mixing up YIQ and YUV** — NTSC uses YIQ; PAL uses YUV. Stating "PAL also uses I and Q" loses marks. > > 7. **Skipping the units** — Always write "MHz" or "μs" explicitly. Unanswered units = 0.5 mark deduction per KTU valuation key. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute KTU exam revision list — read this twice on the morning of the exam.** - [x] **NTSC** = 525 lines, 59.94 fields/sec, $f_{sc} = 3.579545$ MHz, **YIQ** color space, **no phase alternation**, originated in **USA 1953**. - [x] **PAL** = 625 lines, 50 fields/sec, $f_{sc} = 4.43361875$ MHz, **YUV** color space, **V alternates 180° every line**, originated in **Germany 1963** by **Walter Bruch**. - [x] **Luminance equation**: $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ — universally true for both NTSC and PAL. - [x] **NTSC color difference**: $I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B$, $Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B$. - [x] **PAL color difference**: $U = 0.493(B-Y)$, $V = 0.877(R-Y)$. - [x] **Chroma equations**: - NTSC: $C(t) = I\cos(\omega_{sc}t) + Q\sin(\omega_{sc}t)$ - PAL: $C(t) = U\sin(\omega_{sc}t) \pm V\cos(\omega_{sc}t)$ ← **the ± is the PAL alternation** - [x] **Line frequency**: NTSC = 15,734.264 Hz; PAL = 15,625 Hz (exact integer). - [x] **Subcarrier-to-line ratio**: NTSC = 227.5 (the .5 is the genius of phase interleaving); PAL ≈ 283.75 + 25 Hz offset. - [x] **The 1-H delay line** is in the **PAL receiver**, made of **ultrasonic glass**, delay = 64 μs; it cancels ±phase errors by line-averaging. - [x] **Channel bandwidth**: NTSC = 6 MHz; PAL-B/G = 7 MHz; PAL-I (India) = 8 MHz. - [x] **Mains-frequency coupling**: 60 Hz in NTSC countries, 50 Hz in PAL countries — to prevent hum bars. - [x] **Why interlacing?** Halves the video bandwidth while preserving perceived flicker rate. - [x] **NTSC's tint knob** is a manual hue correction; PAL doesn't need one because of automatic cancellation. - [x] **Color burst** (8–12 cycles of $f_{sc}$ on the back porch of every line) is the phase reference for the receiver's local subcarrier oscillator. - [x] **Spectrum interleaving**: placing $f_{sc}$ at $227.5 \times f_H$ makes the chroma dots visually averageable — a key invention. - [x] **NTSC = National Television System Committee; PAL = Phase Alternating Line; SECAM = Séquentiel Couleur à Mémoire** (sequential color with memory — France/Russia). --- > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — Transmission Methods: NTSC and PAL Standards** > *KTU 2024 Scheme | OEECT611 Entertainment Electronics*

topics.title: Digital Media Streaming: Packetized Elementary Stream of Audio-Video Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital Media Streaming: Packetized Elementary Stream of Audio-Video Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** is a logical data structure defined within the **MPEG-2 (ISO/IEC 13818-1)** and **DVB (Digital Video Broadcasting)** standard families. It represents a single elementary stream (either compressed video, compressed audio, or auxiliary data) that has been converted into a sequence of variable-length packets suitable for transmission, storage, or further multiplexing.

An **Elementary Stream (ES)** is the raw, compressed output of a single encoder (e.g., MPEG-2 video or AC-3 audio). The process of *packetization* slices this continuous byte stream into discrete **PES packets**. Each PES packet contains a **PES header** followed by **PES payload data** (a fragment of the elementary stream). PES packets are then further fragmented into fixed-length **Transport Stream (TS) packets of 188 bytes** for terrestrial/satellite broadcast, or into **Program Stream (PS) packets of variable length** for storage media like DVDs.

The fundamental purposes of PES are:
1. To provide **time-stamping** (Presentation Time Stamp - PTS, and Decode Time Stamp - DTS) for synchronizing audio with video.
2. To enable **identification** of the stream type and its elementary stream ID.
3. To serve as the **intermediate layer** between the variable-length elementary stream and the fixed-length transport stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PES sits in the **MPEG-2 Systems Layer architecture**, which is a three-layer hierarchy:
> **Elementary Stream (ES) → Packetized Elementary Stream (PES) → Transport Stream (TS) / Program Stream (PS)**.
> Examiners frequently ask students to draw or explain this hierarchy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive **water pipeline** carrying two liquids from a factory — one red (video) and one blue (audio). The liquids arrive in a continuous, never-ending flow. This is the **Elementary Stream**.

Now, suppose a delivery company must load this liquid onto trucks to ship it across the country. The company cannot pour an infinite stream into a truck. So they:
1. **Measure out fixed-size buckets** (e.g., 188 mL each) and pour the liquid into them.
2. **Label each bucket** with: "Red liquid, Bucket 42, Source: Factory A, Tap at exactly 14:30:05.250".
3. **Load the buckets** onto trucks in alternating colors (red, blue, red, blue...).

The *buckets* are the **PES packets**. The *labels* are the **PES headers** (containing PTS/DTS). The *trucks* are the **Transport Stream packets**, and the *truck convoy* is the **Transport Stream** itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The PES is the *packaging layer* — it groups the continuous elementary stream into manageable, addressable, time-stamped units, but it is not yet the final transmission format. PES is the "shipping container", while TS is the "freight truck".

### 1.3 Critical Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **non-negotiable** according to the MPEG-2 / DVB specification and are frequently tested in KTU examinations:

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **TS Packet Length** | **188 bytes** | Fixed length of MPEG-2 Transport Stream packet (4-byte header + 184-byte payload) |
| **Sync Byte Value** | **0x47** | First byte of every TS packet; allows receiver synchronization |
| **PES Header Min Length** | **9 bytes** (for audio) / **9+ bytes** (for video) | Minimal header structure |
| **Elementary Stream ID Range** | **0xC0–0xDF** (audio) / **0xE0–0xEF** (video) | Stream type identifier in PES header |
| **Standard PCR Max Interval** | **≤ 100 ms** | Maximum time between Program Clock References |
| **PTS/DTS Resolution** | **90 kHz** (base) + **27 MHz** (extended) | Time stamp clock frequency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the value 188 bytes.** Almost every KTU question regarding transport streams uses this constant. The **sync byte 0x47 (decimal 71)** is equally important.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MPEG-2 Systems Layer Architecture — Structured Breakdown

The MPEG-2 systems specification defines a **layered architecture** in which each layer serves a specific role. The PES is the **middle layer** and is therefore the *bridge* between application-domain data and transmission-domain data.

**Layer 1 — Elementary Stream (ES):**
- Output of a single audio/video encoder.
- Continuous, variable bit-rate, no inherent packet structure.
- Example: MPEG-2 video elementary stream at 4 Mbps.

**Layer 2 — Packetized Elementary Stream (PES):**
- ES is sliced into PES packets.
- Each PES packet has a **PES header** (variable length) + **PES payload** (a chunk of the ES).
- PES header carries **PTS**, **DTS**, **ES_ID**, and other control fields.
- PES packets are **variable in length** — they can be several thousand bytes long.
- PES by itself is **not used for transmission**; it is a *logical* construct.

**Layer 3 — Transport Stream (TS) or Program Stream (PS):**
- PES packets are further fragmented into fixed **188-byte TS packets**.
- Multiple PES packets (from different programs) are multiplexed into a single TS.
- TS is what is actually transmitted over DVB-T, DVB-S, DVB-C, ISDB, ATSC channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not transmit PES directly?** Because PES packets are variable in length. Transmission channels, error-correction coders, and demodulators all prefer **fixed-length units**. Hence the TS provides the fixed-length "shipping vehicle" while PES provides the logical grouping.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a PES Packet — The Complete Field Map

A PES packet begins with a **3-byte start-code prefix (0x000001)**, followed by a **1-byte stream ID**, a **2-byte PES packet length**, and then optional fields.

**Mandatory fields (always present):**
- **Packet Start Code Prefix (24 bits):** Fixed value `0x000001`. Identifies the start of a PES packet.
- **Stream ID (8 bits):** Identifies the elementary stream type.
  - `0xC0–0xBF` → Audio (32 streams)
  - `0xE0–0xEF` → Video (16 streams)
  - `0xBD` → Private Stream 1 (commonly used for AC-3 audio in DVB)
  - `0xBE` → Padding Stream
  - `0xBF` → Private Stream 2
- **PES Packet Length (16 bits):** Number of bytes remaining in the PES packet after this field. If the value is 0, the PES packet length is unbounded (used for video ES carrying a single access unit).

**Optional fields (present only when flagged in bits):**
- **PES Scrambling Control (2 bits)**
- **PES Priority (1 bit)**
- **Data Alignment Indicator (1 bit)**
- **Copyright (1 bit)**
- **Original or Copy (1 bit)**
- **PTS Flag (1 bit)** — when set, PTS field is present
- **DTS Flag (1 bit)** — when set, DTS field is present
- **ESCR Flag (1 bit)**
- **ES Rate Flag (1 bit)**
- **DSM Trick Mode Flag (1 bit)**
- **Additional Copy Info Flag (1 bit)**
- **PES CRC Flag (1 bit)**
- **PES Extension Flag (1 bit)**
- **PES Header Data Length (8 bits)**
- **PTS (33 bits)** — 90 kHz base + 9-bit extension in marker bits
- **DTS (33 bits)** — present only if DTS flag is set; otherwise PTS = DTS

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MPEG-2 TS packet length | $L_{TS} = 188$ | bytes | Fixed; 4-byte header + 184-byte payload |
| 2 | TS header length | $H_{TS} = 4$ | bytes | Sync byte + 3 control bytes |
| 3 | TS payload length | $P_{TS} = 188 - 4 = 184$ | bytes | Payload available for PES data |
| 4 | PES packet length (general) | $L_{PES} = H_{PES} + N \cdot L_{TS}$ | bytes | $N$ = number of TS packets used |
| 5 | PTS/DTS base clock | $f_{90} = 90\,000$ | Hz | 90 kHz base for time stamps |
| 6 | System clock | $f_{27} = 27\,000\,000$ | Hz | 27 MHz PCR reference |
| 7 | Clock ratio | $f_{27} / f_{90} = 300$ | — | 27 MHz = 300 × 90 kHz |
| 8 | Bit rate of one TS packet | $R_{pkt} = \vert 188 \cdot 8 \cdot f_{pkt} \vert$ | bps | $f_{pkt}$ = TS packet rate |
| 9 | Total TS bit rate | $R_{TS} = 188 \cdot 8 \cdot f_{pkt}$ | bps | Standard DVB: ~ 4–80 Mbps |
| 10 | PES rate field | $R_{ES} = \vert ES\_rate \cdot 50 \vert$ | bytes/s | ES_rate is 22-bit field |
| 11 | Video buffer size (MPEG-2 MP@ML) | $V_{VBV} = 1\,835\,008$ | bits | Standard VBV buffer for Main Profile |
| 12 | PCR interval max | $T_{PCR} \leq 100$ | ms | Mandated by MPEG-2 |
| 13 | Time stamp to seconds | $t = PTS / 90\,000$ | seconds | Convert 90 kHz ticks to seconds |
| 14 | Multiplexed TS rate | $R_{TS} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{ES,i} + R_{overhead}$ | bps | Sum of all elementary stream rates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When using the ES_rate field, the formula is **ES_rate × 50 bytes/second**, NOT × 188. Many KTU answer scripts lose marks here.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The PES architecture is the **backbone of all DVB broadcasting** worldwide. Every digital TV signal that arrives at your home via satellite (DVB-S/S2), cable (DVB-C/C2), or terrestrial (DVB-T/T2) is a Transport Stream containing multiplexed PES packets. The same architecture powers:
- **ISDB** (Japanese / Brazilian digital TV)
- **ATSC** (North American digital TV, with minor differences)
- **DTMB** (Chinese digital TV)
- **Blu-ray Disc** (uses Program Stream, the PES-based DVD sibling)
- **IPTV** (when encapsulated in RTP over UDP/IP)
- **DVB-H** for mobile TV

Engineers working on **set-top box design**, **digital TV middleware**, **STB firmware**, and **broadcast encoders** must understand PES packetization intimately.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit Rate of a Transport Stream from PES Bandwidth

**Problem Statement:** A DVB-T transmitter multiplexes three elementary streams into a single Transport Stream. The elementary stream bit rates are:
- Video PES: 4.0 Mbps
- Audio PES: 192 kbps
- Auxiliary data PES: 128 kbps

The TS overhead (null packets, PSI/SI tables) consumes 400 kbps. Calculate the **total TS bit rate** and the **TS packet rate** (packets per second).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum the elementary stream bit rates.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ES,total} &= R_{video} + R_{audio} + R_{data} \\
R_{ES,total} &= 4.0 \times 10^{6} + 192 \times 10^{3} + 128 \times 10^{3} \\
R_{ES,total} &= 4\,000\,000 + 192\,000 + 128\,000 \\
R_{ES,total} &= 4\,320\,000 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Add the TS overhead bit rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TS} &= R_{ES,total} + R_{overhead} \\
R_{TS} &= 4\,320\,000 + 400\,000 \\
R_{TS} &= 4\,720\,000 \text{ bps} = 4.72 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bytes per second in the TS.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{TS} &= \frac{R_{TS}}{8} \\
B_{TS} &= \frac{4\,720\,000}{8} \\
B_{TS} &= 590\,000 \text{ bytes/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the TS packet rate using the 188-byte packet size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{pkt} &= \frac{B_{TS}}{L_{TS}} = \frac{B_{TS}}{188} \\
f_{pkt} &= \frac{590\,000}{188} \\
f_{pkt} &= 3138.30 \text{ packets/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round to the nearest integer and interpret.**

The transmitter outputs approximately **3138 TS packets per second**. In practice, the system clock (27 MHz) is used to lock the packet rate, so the actual value is governed by the encoder's PCR-driven clock recovery.

**Final Answer:** $R_{TS} = 4.72$ Mbps and $f_{pkt} \approx 3138$ packets/s.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — PTS Calculation and Audio-Video Synchronization

**Problem Statement:** A video frame has a **DTS of 270 000** (in 90 kHz ticks). The video frame is to be displayed **one frame period** after its decode instant. If the frame rate is **25 fps**, compute the **PTS** and the absolute presentation time in **seconds**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the frame period in seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{1}{f_{frame}} = \frac{1}{25} \\
T_{frame} &= 0.04 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the frame period to 90 kHz ticks.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{ticks} &= T_{frame} \cdot f_{90} \\
\Delta_{ticks} &= 0.04 \cdot 90\,000 \\
\Delta_{ticks} &= 3600 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the PTS from the DTS.**

The presentation time equals the decode time plus the frame reordering delay (one frame period for I/P frame reordering).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PTS &= DTS + \Delta_{ticks} \\
PTS &= 270\,000 + 3600 \\
PTS &= 273\,600 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert PTS to absolute time in seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{PTS} &= \frac{PTS}{f_{90}} = \frac{PTS}{90\,000} \\
t_{PTS} &= \frac{273\,600}{90\,000} \\
t_{PTS} &= 3.04 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** PTS = **273 600 ticks**, which corresponds to **3.04 seconds** from the PCR epoch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The PTS is what allows the **STB decoder** to present the video frame at the correct moment, even when B-frames cause the *decode order* to differ from the *presentation order*. Without PTS, audio-video sync (lip-sync) would fail.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — PES Rate Field Decoding

**Problem Statement:** A PES header contains the **ES_rate field = 0x15 7C24** in hexadecimal. Compute the **elementary stream bit rate in kbps** carried by this PES.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert the hex value to decimal.**

The ES_rate field is 22 bits wide, but the field is preceded by marker bits, so the actual value is the lower 22 bits of the 32-bit PES_ES_rate_info field. Treating the entire 24-bit value (since marker bits are in specific positions) is the simplified textbook approach.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES\_rate &= 0x157C24 \\
ES\_rate &= (1 \cdot 16^{5}) + (5 \cdot 16^{4}) + (7 \cdot 16^{3}) + (C \cdot 16^{2}) + (2 \cdot 16^{1}) + (4 \cdot 16^{0}) \\
ES\_rate &= 1\,048\,576 + 327\,680 + 28\,672 + 3072 + 32 + 4 \\
ES\_rate &= 1\,408\,036
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplier to get bytes per second.**

The ES_rate field, when read as a 22-bit integer, must be multiplied by **50** to yield bytes per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{ES} &= ES\_rate \times 50 \\
B_{ES} &= 1\,408\,036 \times 50 \\
B_{ES} &= 70\,401\,800 \text{ bytes/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bits per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ES} &= B_{ES} \times 8 \\
R_{ES} &= 70\,401\,800 \times 8 \\
R_{ES} &= 563\,214\,400 \text{ bps} \\
R_{ES} &\approx 563.2 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kbps.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ES,kbps} &= \frac{R_{ES}}{1000} \\
R_{ES,kbps} &= \frac{563\,214\,400}{1000} \\
R_{ES,kbps} &= 563\,214.4 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R_{ES} \approx 563.2$ Mbps.

> [!NOTE]
> This large value indicates the example value chosen is for a high-bandwidth stream (such as uncompressed HD video). In practice, ES_rate values in the range **4 000–80 000** yield typical SD/HD DVB rates.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Stream Layers

| Property | Elementary Stream (ES) | PES | Transport Stream (TS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packet structure | None (continuous) | Variable length | Fixed **188 bytes** |
| Header size | None | ≥ 9 bytes (typically 14–40) | **4 bytes** |
| Time stamps | No | **Yes (PTS, DTS)** | No (carries PCR instead) |
| Multiplexed | No | No (single ES only) | **Yes (multiple programs)** |
| Error correction | No | No | Yes (FEC, Reed-Solomon) |
| Typical use | Encoder output | Intermediate logical layer | Air interface / cable |
| Sync mechanism | N/A | Start code 0x000001 | Sync byte 0x47 |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MPEG-2 Systems Layer Architecture

This diagram illustrates how the PES sits between the Elementary Stream and the Transport Stream, showing the full data flow from encoder to air interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Video Encoder MPEG-2] --> A1[Elementary Stream ES - Video 4 Mbps]
    B[Audio Encoder AC-3] --> B1[Elementary Stream ES - Audio 192 kbps]
    C[Data Source] --> C1[Elementary Stream ES - Data 128 kbps]

    A1 --> D1[PES Packetizer - Video]
    B1 --> D2[PES Packetizer - Audio]
    C1 --> D3[PES Packetizer - Data]

    D1 --> E1[PES Video - Variable Length Packets with PTS DTS]
    D2 --> E2[PES Audio - Variable Length Packets with PTS]
    D3 --> E3[PES Data - Variable Length Packets]

    E1 --> F[TS Multiplexer]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G1[Transport Stream Packets 188 bytes]
    G1 --> G2[Add PSI SI Tables PAT PMT NIT]
    G2 --> G3[Add Null Packets Padding]
    G3 --> H[Channel Encoder FEC Reed Solomon Viterbi]
    H --> I[Modulator DVB-T DVB-S DVB-C]
    I --> J[Air Interface - RF Transmission]
```

### 4.2 PES Packet Internal Structure (Block-Level View)

The diagram below is a block-level functional architecture flow showing the exact field-by-field structure of a PES packet, including all optional fields.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[PES Packet Start] --> P1[Packet Start Code Prefix - 24 bits - 0x000001]
    P1 --> P2[Stream ID - 8 bits - 0xC0 to 0xEF for AV]
    P2 --> P3[PES Packet Length - 16 bits]
    P3 --> P4{Optional PES Header Present?}

    P4 -- No --> P5[PES Payload - Raw ES Bytes]
    P4 -- Yes --> P6[MPEG-2 PES Header Fields]

    P6 --> P7[PES Scrambling Control - 2 bits]
    P6 --> P8[PES Priority - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P9[Data Alignment Indicator - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P10[Copyright Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P11[Original or Copy - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P12[PTS DTS Flags - 2 bits]
    P6 --> P13[ESCR Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P14[ES Rate Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P15[DSM Trick Mode Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P16[Additional Copy Info Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P17[PES CRC Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P18[PES Extension Flag - 1 bit]
    P6 --> P19[PES Header Data Length - 8 bits]

    P12 --> P20{PTS Present?}
    P20 -- Yes --> P21[PTS - 33 bits - 90 kHz plus 27 MHz]
    P20 -- No --> P22[Skip PTS Field]

    P12 --> P23{DTS Present?}
    P23 -- Yes --> P24[DTS - 33 bits - 90 kHz plus 27 MHz]
    P23 -- No --> P25[Skip DTS Field]

    P21 --> P30[PES Payload Bytes]
    P22 --> P30
    P24 --> P30
    P25 --> P30

    P14 -- Yes --> P26[ES Rate - 22 bits - units of 50 bytes per second]
    P15 -- Yes --> P27[DSM Trick Mode Field - 8 bits]
    P16 -- Yes --> P28[Additional Copy Info - 8 bits]
    P17 -- Yes --> P29[PES CRC - 16 bits]
    P18 -- Yes --> P31[PES Extension Field Length - 8 bits]

    P26 --> P30
    P27 --> P30
    P28 --> P30
    P29 --> P30
    P31 --> P30

    P30 --> P32[End of PES Packet - Aligned to Next Start Code or Stream Boundary]
```

### 4.3 PES-to-TS Fragmentation Process

This diagram shows how a single large PES packet is fragmented into multiple fixed-length TS packets, including the **Payload Unit Start Indicator (PUSI)** mechanism that signals the first fragment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PES1[PES Packet - Video - 4096 bytes] --> TS1[TS Packet 1 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 1]
    PES1 --> TS2[TS Packet 2 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS3[TS Packet 3 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS4[TS Packet 4 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS5[TS Packet 5 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS6[TS Packet 6 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS7[TS Packet 7 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS8[TS Packet 8 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS9[TS Packet 9 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS10[TS Packet 10 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0]
    PES1 --> TS11[TS Packet 11 - 188 bytes - PUSI = 0 - carries Adaptation Field if needed]

    TS1 --> MUX[TS Multiplexer]
    TS2 --> MUX
    TS3 --> MUX
    TS4 --> MUX
    TS5 --> MUX
    TS6 --> MUX
    TS7 --> MUX
    TS8 --> MUX
    TS9 --> MUX
    TS10 --> MUX
    TS11 --> MUX

    MUX --> OUT[Final Transport Stream - Constant Bit Rate Sequence of 188-byte Packets]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight:** Notice that the **PUSI (Payload Unit Start Indicator)** is set to 1 only on the **first** TS packet carrying a new PES packet. This tells the decoder where the PES header begins within the TS payload. The remaining TS packets simply continue the PES payload contiguously.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Packetized Elementary Stream (PES) and list its main components.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** is a logical data structure defined by the MPEG-2 Systems standard (ISO/IEC 13818-1) in which a single elementary stream (ES) — the output of a single audio, video, or data encoder — is sliced into **variable-length packets** suitable for further multiplexing and transmission.

The **main components of a PES packet** are:

1. **Packet Start Code Prefix** — a fixed 24-bit value `0x000001` that marks the beginning of a PES packet.
2. **Stream ID** — an 8-bit identifier specifying the elementary stream type (`0xC0–0xDF` for audio, `0xE0–0xEF` for video).
3. **PES Packet Length** — a 16-bit field indicating the number of remaining bytes in the PES packet.
4. **Optional PES Header** — variable-length fields including PTS, DTS, ES_rate, and flags.
5. **PES Payload** — the actual compressed audio/video data sliced from the elementary stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** A 3-mark question typically expects **2 marks for definition + 1 mark for listing 4–5 components** or for a small diagram.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the difference between PTS and DTS in a PES packet? Why is DTS not required for audio PES?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**PTS (Presentation Time Stamp)** indicates the time instant at which a decoded access unit (e.g., a video frame or an audio frame) is to be **presented** (i.e., displayed to the viewer or fed to the speaker). **DTS (Decode Time Stamp)** indicates the time instant at which an access unit is to be **decoded** by the decoder.

For most elementary streams, **PTS = DTS** because the decode order equals the presentation order. However, when **B-frames (Bi-directional predicted frames)** are used, the decode order differs from the presentation order, requiring both PTS and DTS.

**DTS is not required for audio PES** because audio access units are always presented in the same order as they are decoded. There is no "reordering" required in audio. Therefore, in the PES header, the DTS flag is **not set** for audio streams, and the audio decoder uses only the PTS to schedule playback.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive PES Analysis**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete MPEG-2 systems layer architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of PES in this hierarchy. Compare Elementary Stream (ES), PES, and Transport Stream (TS) in terms of packet length, time-stamping, and error correction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DVB-T multiplexer combines a video PES at 3.5 Mbps and an audio PES at 256 kbps into a single Transport Stream. The TS overhead (null packets, PSI/SI tables) consumes 600 kbps. Calculate:
- (i) The total Transport Stream bit rate in Mbps.
- (ii) The TS packet rate in packets/second.
- (iii) The minimum number of TS packets required to carry one PES video packet of 4 KB.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — MPEG-2 Systems Layer Architecture:**

The MPEG-2 systems layer architecture is a three-tier hierarchy:

- **Tier 1 — Elementary Stream (ES):** The raw, continuous, variable-bit-rate output of a single encoder. It has no packet structure. Example: A 4 Mbps MPEG-2 video elementary stream from an encoder chip.

- **Tier 2 — Packetized Elementary Stream (PES):** The ES is sliced into variable-length PES packets, each prefixed with a PES header containing PTS, DTS, Stream ID, and other control fields. PES provides the **logical grouping** and **time-stamping** that the ES lacks. PES by itself is not used for transmission; it is an intermediate representation.

- **Tier 3 — Transport Stream (TS):** PES packets from multiple elementary streams are fragmented into fixed **188-byte TS packets**, multiplexed, and provided with error-correction codes (Reed-Solomon, convolutional coding, etc.) for over-the-air transmission. The TS is the actual air-interface format used by DVB-T, DVB-S, and DVB-C.

**Role of PES in the hierarchy:** The PES acts as the **bridge** between the encoder-side world (variable-length elementary streams) and the transmission-side world (fixed-length transport packets). It is the layer where time-stamping and stream identification are first introduced.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | ES | PES | TS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Length | None | Variable | Fixed 188 bytes |
| Time-stamps | No | PTS, DTS | PCR (in adaptation field) |
| Error Correction | No | No | Yes (Reed-Solomon, Viterbi) |
| Number of Streams | 1 | 1 | Multiplexed |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total TS bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{ES,total} &= R_{video} + R_{audio} \\
R_{ES,total} &= 3.5 \times 10^{6} + 256 \times 10^{3} \\
R_{ES,total} &= 3\,500\,000 + 256\,000 \\
R_{ES,total} &= 3\,756\,000 \text{ bps} = 3.756 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding overhead:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{TS} &= 3.756 + 0.600 \\
R_{TS} &= 4.356 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) TS packet rate:**

Bytes per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{TS} &= \frac{R_{TS}}{8} = \frac{4\,356\,000}{8} = 544\,500 \text{ bytes/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Packets per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{pkt} &= \frac{B_{TS}}{188} = \frac{544\,500}{188} \approx 2896.28 \text{ packets/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Minimum TS packets for one 4 KB PES packet:**

4 KB = 4096 bytes. The PES packet must be fragmented into TS packets. The first TS packet carries a 4-byte TS header, leaving 184 bytes for PES data. Subsequent TS packets also carry 184 bytes of PES data. Let $N$ be the number of TS packets.

$$
\begin{aligned}
184 \cdot N &\geq 4096 \\
N &\geq \frac{4096}{184} \\
N &\geq 22.26
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, $N = 23$ TS packets are required. The last packet will have unused payload bytes, which may be filled with an **adaptation field** (padding) to maintain the 188-byte fixed length.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $R_{TS} = 4.356$ Mbps
- (ii) $f_{pkt} \approx 2896$ packets/s
- (iii) $N = 23$ TS packets

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — PES Header Deep-Dive**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the complete PES packet header structure and explain the function of each field. What is the significance of the Stream ID field? List the Stream ID values for audio, video, and padding streams.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A video frame has DTS = 810 000 (in 90 kHz ticks). The video frame rate is 30 fps. Compute:
- (i) The PTS of this frame.
- (ii) The absolute presentation time in seconds.
- (iii) The PTS of the next video frame.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — PES Header Structure:**

The PES packet header consists of the following fields in order:

1. **Packet Start Code Prefix (24 bits):** Fixed value `0x000001`. Marks the start of a PES packet.
2. **Stream ID (8 bits):** Identifies the elementary stream. Common values:
   - `0xC0–0xDF` → Audio streams (32 possible audio ES)
   - `0xE0–0xEF` → Video streams (16 possible video ES)
   - `0xBD` → Private Stream 1 (commonly used for AC-3 audio in DVB)
   - `0xBE` → Padding Stream
   - `0xBF` → Private Stream 2
3. **PES Packet Length (16 bits):** Number of bytes following this field in the PES packet. May be 0 (unbounded) for video ES.
4. **PES Scrambling Control (2 bits):** Indicates whether the payload is scrambled.
5. **PES Priority (1 bit):** Indicates priority of the PES packet.
6. **Data Alignment Indicator (1 bit):** Indicates alignment of video access units.
7. **Copyright (1 bit)**
8. **Original or Copy (1 bit)**
9. **PTS DTS Flags (2 bits):** Indicate presence of PTS, DTS, or both.
10. **ESCR Flag (1 bit):** Indicates presence of ESCR field.
11. **ES Rate Flag (1 bit):** Indicates presence of ES_rate field.
12. **DSM Trick Mode Flag (1 bit)**
13. **Additional Copy Info Flag (1 bit)**
14. **PES CRC Flag (1 bit)**
15. **PES Extension Flag (1 bit)**
16. **PES Header Data Length (8 bits):** Length of the remaining optional fields in bytes.
17. **PTS (33 bits):** 90 kHz base + 9-bit extension in marker bits.
18. **DTS (33 bits):** Present only if DTS flag is set.
19. **ESCR (42 bits):** Elementary Stream Clock Reference.
20. **ES_rate (22 bits):** Bit rate in units of 50 bytes/s.
21. **DSM Trick Mode (8 bits)**
22. **Additional Copy Info (8 bits)**
23. **PES CRC (16 bits)**
24. **PES Extension Field**

**Significance of Stream ID:** The Stream ID field allows the **de-multiplexer** at the receiver to identify the type of payload within the PES packet and route it to the correct decoder (video decoder, audio decoder, or data handler). Without it, the receiver would have no way to distinguish between, say, a Spanish-language audio track and an English-language audio track within the same transport stream.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) PTS calculation:**

Frame period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{1}{30} = 0.0\overline{3} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

In 90 kHz ticks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{ticks} &= 0.0\overline{3} \times 90\,000 = 3000 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

PTS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PTS &= DTS + \Delta_{ticks} \\
PTS &= 810\,000 + 3000 \\
PTS &= 813\,000 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Absolute presentation time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{PTS} &= \frac{PTS}{90\,000} = \frac{813\,000}{90\,000} = 9.0\overline{3} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) PTS of the next video frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PTS_{next} &= PTS_{current} + \Delta_{ticks} \\
PTS_{next} &= 813\,000 + 3000 \\
PTS_{next} &= 816\,000 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

In seconds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{PTS,next} &= \frac{816\,000}{90\,000} = 9.0\overline{6} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) PTS = 813 000 ticks
- (ii) $t_{PTS} = 9.0\overline{3}$ s
- (iii) $PTS_{next} = 816\,000$ ticks = $9.0\overline{6}$ s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing PTS with DTS:** A very common mistake is to treat PTS and DTS as the same. Marks are lost when students fail to note that **DTS is optional** and is required only when **B-frames** cause reordering. Always state explicitly why B-frames necessitate both fields.
> 2. **Forgetting the 188-byte constant:** Many students answer questions about TS packet length with the value **192 bytes** (which is the **DVB-S2** physical-layer frame size with 16-byte Reed-Solomon parity, NOT the MPEG-2 TS packet size). The correct MPEG-2 TS packet length is **188 bytes**.
> 3. **Incorrect ES_rate calculation:** Multiplying the ES_rate field by **188** instead of **50** is a frequent error. The ES_rate field, when interpreted as a 22-bit integer, gives bytes/second when multiplied by **50**.
> 4. **PTS-to-seconds conversion:** Students often forget that the 90 kHz base clock requires **division by 90 000**, not by 27 000 000. The 27 MHz value is for the **system clock / PCR**, not for PTS/DTS.
> 5. **Stream ID confusion:** Writing `0xC0` for video (incorrect — that is audio) costs marks. Memorize the ranges: **Audio = 0xC0–0xDF**, **Video = 0xE0–0xEF**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **PES = Packetized Elementary Stream**, defined in **MPEG-2 Systems (ISO/IEC 13818-1)**. It is the **middle layer** between ES and TS.
- **Three-layer MPEG-2 hierarchy:** **ES → PES → TS**. PES is variable-length; TS is fixed **188 bytes**.
- **PES start code** is the fixed 24-bit value **`0x000001`**, followed by **Stream ID** and **PES Packet Length**.
- **Stream ID ranges:** **Audio = 0xC0–0xDF** (32 streams), **Video = 0xE0–0xEF** (16 streams), **Padding = 0xBE**, **Private Stream 1 = 0xBD**.
- **PTS (Presentation Time Stamp)** = when the access unit is **displayed**. **DTS (Decode Time Stamp)** = when the access unit is **decoded**. PTS > DTS when B-frames are used.
- **Audio PES does NOT use DTS** because audio access units are always presented in decode order.
- **PTS/DTS base clock = 90 kHz**. Convert ticks to seconds by dividing by **90 000**.
- **System clock / PCR = 27 MHz**. The ratio is **300 : 1** (27 MHz = 300 × 90 kHz).
- **ES_rate field** is 22 bits wide and is multiplied by **50** to yield bytes/second (NOT 188).
- **TS sync byte** = **`0x47`** (decimal **71**). The first byte of every TS packet.
- **PUSI (Payload Unit Start Indicator)** = **1** on the first TS packet of a new PES packet.
- **PCR maximum interval** ≤ **100 ms** to ensure robust clock recovery at the receiver.
- **TS packet rate** is computed as $f_{pkt} = R_{TS} / (188 \times 8)$ packets/second.
- The PES payload is fragmented into TS packets with **184 bytes** of useful payload each (188 − 4 header).
- The **adaptation field** can be used in any TS packet to carry PCR, padding, or splice countdown information.
- **VBV (Video Buffering Verifier) buffer** for MPEG-2 Main Profile @ Main Level is **1 835 008 bits** — this is the standard buffer the encoder must not overflow or underflow.
- PES is used **logically** in IPTV, RTP/UDP/IP encapsulation, and DVB; the **on-the-air** format is always the **TS**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MPEG Data Stream
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MPEG Data Stream

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
