| Physical size | Smaller | Larger (two memories) |
| Power consumption | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Determinism | Lower (cache misses cause jitter) | Higher (predictable timing) |
| Best for | General-purpose computing | Real-time DSP, embedded control |

**Why Modified Harvard in ARM Cortex-M:**
Modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (used in smart speakers, gaming controllers, set-top boxes) employ a **Modified Harvard Architecture** because:
- It **retains the dual-bus speed advantage** of pure Harvard.
- It allows **unified addressing for flexibility** in loading new firmware (Over-The-Air updates for entertainment devices).
- It supports **tightly-coupled memory (TCM)** for deterministic, jitter-free audio sample delivery — critical for Hi-Fi audio quality.

*(3 marks comparison table + 4 marks justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing PC and IR:** The *Program Counter* holds the *address*; the *Instruction Register* holds the *actual instruction code*. Mixing these up is the single most common mark-losing mistake in FDE questions.
> 2. **Unit Errors in MIPS:** Always express $f_{clk}$ in Hz (not MHz) inside the formula, then divide by $10^6$ at the end. Inverting this gives a 10⁶ error.
> 3. **Forgetting PC Increment:** In the fetch step, students often describe the read but forget $PC \leftarrow PC + N$. This costs 1 full mark.
> 4. **Treating "Modified Harvard" as "Pure Harvard":** Modified Harvard allows unified *addressing space* but separate *physical buses*. Examiners penalise vague answers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Program Access** = Fetch + Decode + Execute, repeated endlessly.
- The **Program Counter (PC)** is the *pointer*; the **Instruction Register (IR)** is the *latched instruction*.
- **Memory architectures:** Von Neumann (single bus, flexible) vs. Harvard (dual bus, fast) vs. Modified Harvard (best of both — used in entertainment MCUs).
- **Addressing modes** determine *where* operands come from: immediate, register, direct, indirect, indexed.
- **Maximum addressable memory** $M_{max} = 2^n$, where $n$ = PC bit-width.
- **Instruction time** $T_{inst} = C / f_{clk}$; **MIPS** $= f_{clk} / (C \times 10^6)$.
- **Indexed addressing** is the workhorse of audio waveform look-up tables and video sprite generation.
- **Modified Harvard** dominates entertainment electronics due to deterministic real-time performance.
- For **branch instructions**, the new PC = current PC + signed offset.
- Always state **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing or wrong units.
- Draw **neat block diagrams** for FDE questions; labelling every block and bus earns full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scrambled Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Scrambled Programs # Scrambled Programs in Entertainment Electronics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **scrambled program** in entertainment electronics refers to a television, radio, or audio-visual signal that has been deliberately encoded, encoded, or encrypted at the transmission end to prevent unauthorized reception, viewing, or listening. The original baseband signal is mathematically transformed using a scrambling algorithm and a secret key, rendering the signal unintelligible to any receiver that does not possess the corresponding descrambling key and decoder circuitry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Scrambling is the process of *systematically altering* the characteristics of a video/audio signal (such as sync pulses, line order, polarity, or digital bitstream) so that the picture becomes unviewable or the audio becomes unintelligible without the proper authorization, typically enforced through a **Conditional Access System (CAS)**. The full ecosystem supporting scrambled programs includes: - **Encoder / Scrambler unit** at the head-end (uplink / cable headend). - **Conditional Access System (CAS)** — manages entitlement and key distribution. - **Subscriber Management System (SMS)** — handles billing and authorization. - **Set-Top Box (STB)** / Integrated Receiver Decoder (IRD) at the consumer end. - **Smart card** or **security module** containing decryption keys. > [!NOTE] > Scrambling is fundamentally different from encryption in scope: scrambling primarily aims to *prevent casual signal interception* and enable subscription control, while encryption provides *cryptographic secrecy*. In entertainment broadcast systems, both are often used in combination. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sending a **puzzle box** through the post to a friend. Before sealing the box, you take the original letter inside, cut it into strips, and randomly shuffle those strips. Anyone who intercepts the box sees only jumbled paper. Your friend, however, has the **decoder sheet** (the correct strip order) and can instantly reassemble the original letter. Scrambled programs work the same way: the broadcaster "cuts and shuffles" the video signal, and only paying subscribers (who own the decoder sheet in the form of a set-top box + smart card) can put the picture back together. **Real-world examples:** | System | Type of Scrambling | |---|---| | Cable TV premium channels (HBO, Star Movies) | RF sync suppression + smart card | | DTH (Direct-to-Home) satellite TV (Tata Sky, Dish TV) | DVB Common Scrambling Algorithm (CSA) | | Pay-per-view sporting events | Addressable impulse scrambling | | Early analog cable premium tier | Video inversion + sync inversion | ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Constants in Scrambling Systems:** > - **Horizontal sync pulse width:** $\mathbf{4.7\ \mu s}$ (NTSC) / $\mathbf{4.7\ \mu s}$ (PAL) — the prime target for sync-suppression scrambling. > - **Color burst frequency:** $\mathbf{3.579545\ MHz}$ (NTSC) / $\mathbf{4.433619\ MHz}$ (PAL) — must be preserved or restored after descrambling. > - **Vertical blanking interval:** $\mathbf{1.6\ ms}$ (NTSC) / $\mathbf{1.6\ ms}$ (PAL) — safe zone to hide in-band descrambling data. > - **DVB CSA key length:** $\mathbf{64\text{-}bit}$ control word (CW) refreshed every **5–10 seconds** (Entitlement Control Message, ECM). > - **Effective security of DVB CSA:** $\mathbf{48\text{-}bit}$ (as of 2024 disclosures). > - **Smart card data rate (ISO 7816):** up to $\mathbf{1.2\ Mbps}$ typical. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Are Programs Scrambled? The primary motivations in the entertainment electronics industry are: 1. **Revenue Protection** — Pay-TV operators monetize premium content (movies, sports, adult channels) by selling subscriptions. 2. **Access Control** — Restrict viewing based on geography (regional sports blackout), age (adult content), or subscription tier. 3. **Intellectual Property (IP) Protection** — Prevent re-distribution and piracy. 4. **Tiered Service Offering** — Basic, Standard, High-Definition, and Premium tiers. 5. **Event-Based Monetization** — Pay-per-view (PPV) and video-on-demand (VoD). ## 2.2 Classification of Scrambling Techniques ### 2.2.1 Analog Domain Scrambling (Legacy Cable Systems) **A. Sync Suppression Scrambling** The amplitude of the horizontal sync pulse is reduced toward the blanking level, causing the receiver's horizontal oscillator to lose lock. The picture rolls horizontally and becomes unviewable. The descrambler reinstates the sync pulse to its full amplitude. **B. Sync Inversion Scrambling** The horizontal sync pulse is inverted in polarity (positive-going instead of negative-going). Receivers using AC-coupled sync separators cannot detect the polarity-inverted pulse, so horizontal synchronization is lost. The descrambler re-inverts the pulse. **C. Line Inversion Scrambling** Alternate video lines are inverted in polarity (multiplied by $-1$). A normal monitor interprets the alternating polarity as an unwanted 50%/60% interference pattern, rendering the picture unwatchable. The descrambler re-inverts the inverted lines in real time. **D. Video Inversion (Full-Frame Inversion)** The entire video waveform polarity is flipped (luminance $Y \rightarrow -Y$). Picture appears as a photographic negative. The descrambler simply inverts the video signal. **E. Trap / Band-Stop Scrambling** A narrow-band notch filter removes a critical frequency (e.g., the color subcarrier) from the signal. The descrambler inserts a corresponding band-pass (notch inverse) filter. ### 2.2.2 Digital Domain Scrambling (Modern DTH, IPTV, OTT) **A. DVB Common Scrambling Algorithm (CSA)** A block cipher standardized by ETSI (ETSI TS 103 197) used in DVB systems. It uses a 64-bit block and a 64-bit secret key (the Control Word, CW). **B. AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) Scrambling** Modern IPTV and OTT systems use 128-bit or 256-bit AES in CBC mode for stream-level encryption. **C. CSS, CPPM, CPRM (DVD / Blu-ray)** Content Scramble System (CSS) for DVD uses a 40-bit key — now considered cryptographically broken. Blu-ray uses AACS (128-bit AES). **D. HDCP (High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection)** Used over HDMI/DVI/DisplayPort links to protect premium content from bit-perfect copying. **E. WMDRM, PlayReady, FairPlay (Streaming)** Software DRM schemes used by Netflix, Amazon Prime, Hotstar, Apple TV+, etc. ## 2.3 Architecture of a Conditional Access System (CAS) The logical flow is: 1. **Content Source** (movie / live event) → MPEG-2 / H.264 / H.265 encoder. 2. **Scrambler** applies a pseudo-random sequence (driven by the Control Word, CW) to the compressed bitstream. 3. **CW Encryption** — the CW is encrypted using a service key and sent in the **Entitlement Control Message (ECM)**. 4. **Entitlement Management Message (EMM)** — subscriber-specific authorization is sent, encrypted using a personal subscriber key. 5. **Smart card** at the subscriber end contains the personal key, decrypts the EMM to recover the service key, then decrypts the ECM to recover the CW. 6. **Descrambler** uses the recovered CW to undo the scrambling in real time. 7. **Output** — clear (viewable) video is sent to the TV. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Scrambled video luminance | $v_{scrambled}(t) = s(t) \cdot v_{baseband}(t)$ | $s(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$ for line inversion | | Line inversion toggle | $s(t) = (-1)^{n}$, $n$ = line number | Dimensionless polarity factor | | Sync suppression factor | $k = \dfrac{A_{sync,scrambled}}{A_{sync,normal}}$ | $0 < k < 1$, typically $k \approx 0.2$ | | DVB CSA block size | $B = 64$ | bits | | DVB CSA key size | $K = 64$ | bits (Control Word) | | Effective CSA security | $S_{eff} = 48$ | bits (post-known attacks) | | AES block size | $B_{AES} = 128$ | bits | | AES key sizes | $K_{AES} \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ | bits | | ECM refresh interval | $T_{ECM} = 5\text{–}10$ | seconds | | Signal-to-Noise after sync suppression | $SNR_{eff} = SNR_{original} - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{k}\right)$ | dB, approximate | | Scrambling gain (security vs. bandwidth) | $G_{s} = \dfrac{\text{Unauthorized viewership reduction}}{\text{Extra bandwidth (kbps)}}$ | dimensionless | | Horizontal line period (PAL) | $T_H = 64\ \mu s$ | $\mu s$ | | Horizontal line period (NTSC) | $T_H = 63.556\ \mu s$ | $\mu s$ | | Color subcarrier (PAL) | $f_{sc} = 4.43361875$ | MHz | | Color subcarrier (NTSC) | $f_{sc} = 3.579545$ | MHz | | Aspect ratio (HDTV) | $R = 16:9 = 1.778$ | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > The **Control Word (CW)** is the heart of any CAS. It is the secret that must never be exposed in clear form outside the secure hardware of the smart card. The CW is rotated every $T_{ECM}$ seconds to defeat brute-force key search. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cable head-end design:** Scramblers are placed between the modulator and the combiner, allowing per-channel authorization. - **DTH uplink stations:** Scrambling occurs after encryption of the MPEG transport stream (TS), before QPSK/8PSK modulation. - **DRM in OTT platforms:** Netflix, Disney+, Amazon Prime, etc., use commercial-grade DRM (Widevine, PlayReady, FairPlay) on top of HTTPS / TLS. - **Blu-ray / UHD players:** Use AACS plus BD+ and HDCP for end-to-end content protection. - **Forensic watermarking:** Modern CAS embeds a unique watermark per subscriber in the descrambled stream, enabling pirate-trace back. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effect of Sync Suppression on Horizontal Hold The horizontal sync pulse of amplitude $A_{sync}$ is reduced to amplitude $k \cdot A_{sync}$, where $0 < k < 1$. A TV receiver's horizontal AFC (Automatic Frequency Control) requires a sync pulse amplitude above a threshold $A_{th} \approx 0.3 A_{sync}$ to maintain lock. **Step 1.** State the condition for receiver lock: $$ A_{sync,received} \geq A_{th} $$ **Step 2.** Substitute the suppressed amplitude: $$ k \cdot A_{sync} \geq A_{th} $$ **Step 3.** Solve for the critical suppression factor $k_{min}$: $$ k_{min} = \frac{A_{th}}{A_{sync}} \approx 0.3 $$ **Step 4.** Therefore, any $k < k_{min}$ will cause the horizontal oscillator to lose lock, and the picture will roll. The descrambler must restore $k = 1$ to recover normal viewing. **Step 5.** Effective SNR penalty on luminance due to sync suppression (in dB): $$ SNR_{penalty} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{k}\right) \ \text{dB} $$ For $k = 0.3$: $$ SNR_{penalty} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.3}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(3.333) = 10.46\ \text{dB} $$ This is the minimum SNR degradation the descrambler must overcome to restore a stable picture. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line Inversion Polarity Sequence For line-inversion scrambling, alternate lines are multiplied by $-1$. The polarity factor $s_n$ for line $n$ is: $$ s_n = (-1)^n $$ **Step 1.** Express the scrambled waveform line-by-line: $$ v_{scrambled}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s_n \cdot v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) $$ **Step 2.** For the descrambler, multiply by the same factor $s_n$: $$ v_{clear}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s_n \cdot v_{scrambled,line,n}(t - nT_H) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s_n^2 \cdot v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) $$ **Step 3.** Since $s_n^2 = (-1)^{2n} = +1$: $$ v_{clear}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) $$ **Step 4.** The picture is perfectly restored line-by-line in real time, with zero quality loss. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DVB CSA Block Scrambling The DVB Common Scrambling Algorithm operates on 64-bit blocks $B_i$ of the MPEG transport stream. **Step 1.** Generate a pseudo-random keystream $K_i$ from the 64-bit Control Word $CW$ and a 64-bit initialization vector $IV$: $$ K_i = F_{CSA}(CW, IV, i) $$ **Step 2.** XOR each 64-bit block with the corresponding keystream block: $$ C_i = B_i \oplus K_i $$ **Step 3.** The descrambler regenerates the same keystream and reverses the XOR: $$ B_i = C_i \oplus K_i $$ **Step 4.** Security assessment — total search space of CW: $$ N_{CW} = 2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19} $$ At $10^9$ CW trials per second, exhaustive search takes: $$ T_{exhaust} = \frac{2^{64}}{10^9} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{10}\ \text{seconds} \approx 584\ \text{years} $$ However, published attacks (e.g., 2012 CSA cryptanalysis) reduce effective security to ~$2^{48}$ operations, which is breakable in days on a parallel cluster. This is why modern systems augment CSA with **AES-128** at the transport layer. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Scrambling Method | Criterion | Sync Suppression | Line Inversion | Video Inversion | DVB CSA | AES-128 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Implementation cost | Very low | Low | Very low | Medium | High | | Security strength | Very low | Very low | Very low | Medium | High | | Pirate difficulty | Low | Low | Low | Medium | High | | Bandwidth overhead | 0 | 0 | 0 | ~2% | ~1% | | Compatible with HD | Yes (analog) | Yes (analog) | Yes (analog) | Yes | Yes | | Smart card needed | No | No | No | Yes | Yes | | Used in modern DTH | No | No | No | Yes (legacy) | Yes (current) | | Latency added | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | 5–10 s (CW refresh) | < 100 ms | | Roll scrambling artifact | Picture roll | 50/60 Hz flicker | Negative image | None | None | ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A cable operator uses sync-suppression scrambling with factor $k = 0.25$. Calculate (a) the SNR penalty in dB, and (b) whether a receiver with sync-detection threshold $A_{th} = 0.30 A_{sync}$ will lose horizontal lock. **Solution:** (a) $$ SNR_{penalty} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{k}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.25}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(4) = 20 \times 0.6021 = 12.04\ \text{dB} $$ (b) The received sync amplitude is: $$ A_{sync,received} = k \cdot A_{sync} = 0.25 \cdot A_{sync} $$ Compare to threshold: $$ 0.25 \cdot A_{sync} < 0.30 \cdot A_{sync} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Horizontal lock is LOST} $$ Therefore, the picture will roll horizontally and become unviewable to unauthorized subscribers. ✅ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Scrambled Program Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Content Source
Studio / Live Feed] --> B[MPEG Encoder
H.264 H.265] B --> C[Scrambler Unit
CW driven] C --> D[Multiplexer
TS MUX] D --> E[Modulator
QPSK 8PSK QAM] E --> F[Transmission Channel
Satellite Cable OTT] F --> G[Subscriber Set Top Box
STB] G --> H[Smart Card
Secure Module] H --> I[Descrambler
Real time] I --> J[Clear TV Picture
HDMI to Display] K[Subscriber Mgmt System
SMS] --> L[Entitlement Mgmt
EMM Generator] L --> G M[CAS Head End] --> N[ECM Generator
CW Encrypted] N --> D style C fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff style I fill:#51cf66,color:#fff style H fill:#ffd43b,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Conditional Access System (CAS) Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Plain MPEG TS] --> B[Scrambler] C[Control Word CW
64 bit] --> B B --> D[Scrambled TS] D --> E[Transmitter] C --> F[ECM Encryptor] G[Service Key] --> F F --> H[Encrypted ECM] H --> E E --> I[Receiver] I --> J[Smart Card] J --> K[Decrypted CW] K --> L[Descrambler] D --> L L --> M[Clear MPEG TS] style B fill:#ff8787,color:#fff style L fill:#69db7c,color:#fff style J fill:#ffd43b,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Scrambling vs. Descrambling Real-Time Dataflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant HEAD as Head End participant SUB as Subscriber STB participant CARD as Smart Card HEAD->>HEAD: Generate CW (random 64 bit) HEAD->>HEAD: Scramble MPEG TS with CW HEAD->>HEAD: Encrypt CW using service key HEAD->>SUB: Send Scrambled TS HEAD->>SUB: Send Encrypted ECM every 5 10 s HEAD->>SUB: Send EMM (subscriber authorization) SUB->>CARD: Forward ECM + EMM CARD->>CARD: Decrypt EMM get service key CARD->>CARD: Decrypt ECM get CW CARD->>SUB: Return CW securely SUB->>SUB: Descramble TS with CW SUB->>SUB: Output clear video to TV ``` ## 4.4 Classification Tree of Scrambling Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Scrambling Techniques] --> B[Analog Domain] A --> C[Digital Domain] B --> B1[Sync Suppression] B --> B2[Sync Inversion] B --> B3[Line Inversion] B --> B4[Video Inversion] B --> B5[Band Stop Trap] C --> C1[DVB CSA] C --> C2[AES 128 256] C --> C3[AACS Blu ray] C --> C4[HDCP HDMI] C --> C5[DRM Widevine PlayReady FairPlay] C1 --> C1a[Legacy DVB S S2 C T] C2 --> C2a[IPTV OTT modern STB] C5 --> C5a[Netflix Prime Disney Plus] style A fill:#4dabf7,color:#fff style B fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff style C fill:#9775fa,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]` **Q: Define a scrambled program. List any two analog-domain scrambling techniques used in cable TV.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A scrambled program is a TV/audio signal that has been intentionally encoded at the transmitter to prevent unauthorized reception, requiring a decoder/descrambler at the receiver for normal viewing. **(1 mark)** It is used by pay-TV operators to enforce subscription-based access to premium content such as movies, sports, and special events. **(1 mark)** Two analog-domain scrambling techniques: **(1 mark)** 1. **Sync Suppression** — amplitude of horizontal sync pulse is reduced, causing the receiver to lose horizontal lock. 2. **Line Inversion** — alternate video lines are inverted in polarity (multiplied by $-1$), producing an unwatchable interference pattern. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: What is a Conditional Access System (CAS)? Mention the role of the Control Word (CW).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Conditional Access System (CAS)** is the complete hardware-and-software framework that controls subscriber authorization, key distribution, and real-time descrambling in a pay-TV network. **(1 mark)** It integrates the **Subscriber Management System (SMS)**, **Entitlement Management Messages (EMM)**, **Entitlement Control Messages (ECM)**, and the **smart card**. **(1 mark)** **Role of the Control Word (CW):** The CW is a 64-bit secret key that drives the pseudo-random scrambling of the MPEG transport stream. It is encrypted inside the ECM, sent to subscribers, decrypted by the smart card, and used by the descrambler to recover the clear video. The CW is rotated every 5–10 seconds for security. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]` **Q: (a)** Explain the various analog-domain scrambling techniques used in cable TV distribution. Discuss their working principle with suitable signal waveforms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Conditional Access System (CAS) in a digital pay-TV network. Explain the roles of EMM, ECM, and the smart card. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks Analog-domain cable TV scrambling techniques are summarized as follows: **1. Sync Suppression Scrambling** *(2 marks)* - The horizontal sync pulse amplitude is reduced by a factor $k$ ($0 < k < 1$, typically $k \approx 0.2$). - The TV receiver's horizontal AFC cannot lock onto the weakened sync, so the picture rolls horizontally and becomes unviewable. - Effective SNR penalty: $SNR_{penalty} = 20\log_{10}(1/k) \approx 14$ dB for $k=0.2$. - The descrambler reinstates the full sync amplitude. **2. Sync Inversion Scrambling** *(1.5 marks)* - The sync pulse polarity is inverted (positive-going instead of negative-going). - AC-coupled sync separators in standard TV receivers cannot detect this polarity, so lock is lost. - The descrambler re-inverts the pulse polarity. **3. Line Inversion Scrambling** *(1.5 marks)* - Alternate video lines are multiplied by $-1$ (polarity factor $s_n = (-1)^n$). - The picture shows a 50/60 Hz interference pattern. - The descrambler multiplies each line by the same $s_n$ to recover the original. **4. Video Inversion / Band-Stop Traps** *(1 mark)* - Full-frame polarity flip → photographic-negative image. - Band-stop filter removes color subcarrier → color loss. - These are easily defeated by the descrambler, hence considered low-security. **5. Comparison & choice** *(1 mark)* Modern cable systems rarely use analog scrambling because digital MPEG-2 / H.264 compression followed by DVB CSA is far more secure and bandwidth-efficient. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Conditional Access System (CAS) Architecture — Block Diagram:** *(3 marks)* The CAS consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **Scrambler** at the head-end — uses a pseudo-random sequence generated from the **Control Word (CW)** to XOR with the MPEG transport stream. 2. **ECM Generator** — encrypts the CW using a **service key** and embeds it in the transport stream. 3. **EMM Generator** — encrypts the service key using a **subscriber-specific personal key** and sends it to each authorized subscriber. 4. **Subscriber Management System (SMS)** — maintains the database of subscribers, billing, and authorization levels. 5. **Smart Card** at the subscriber end — securely stores the personal key, decrypts the EMM to recover the service key, then decrypts the ECM to recover the CW. 6. **Descrambler** in the Set-Top Box — uses the recovered CW to undo the XOR scrambling in real time, producing clear video. **Roles of EMM, ECM, and Smart Card:** *(4 marks)* - **EMM (Entitlement Management Message):** Carries subscriber-specific authorization (which channels, which time-window). It is encrypted with the subscriber's personal key. *(1.3 marks)* - **ECM (Entitlement Control Message):** Carries the encrypted Control Word. It is encrypted with the service key. Refreshed every 5–10 seconds. *(1.3 marks)* - **Smart Card:** A tamper-resistant secure module containing the subscriber's secret key, performing all cryptographic operations internally so the CW is never exposed in plaintext outside the card. *(1.4 marks)* --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]` **Q: (a)** Describe the DVB Common Scrambling Algorithm (CSA) used in modern digital TV broadcasting. Discuss its security strengths and weaknesses. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the operation of line-inversion scrambling in detail. Derive the polarity-factor equation and show that a properly synchronized descrambler perfectly recovers the original signal. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **DVB Common Scrambling Algorithm (CSA):** *(3.5 marks)* - Standardized by **ETSI TS 103 197** (originally ETSI ETR 289). - Used in **DVB-S, DVB-S2, DVB-C, DVB-T, DVB-T2** for pay-TV services. - Operates on **64-bit blocks** of the MPEG-2 transport stream. - Uses a **64-bit Control Word (CW)** as the secret key. - The algorithm generates a pseudo-random keystream from the CW and a 64-bit IV, then XORs each block: $$ C_i = B_i \oplus K_i(CW, IV, i) $$ - The descrambler regenerates the same keystream and XORs again to recover $B_i$. **Security Strengths:** *(2 marks)* - 64-bit CW search space of $2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}$ — exhaustive search is computationally infeasible. - CW rotation every 5–10 seconds limits the time available for any attack. - Smart card stores the CW in hardware-secure memory. **Security Weaknesses:** *(1.5 marks)* - Published cryptanalytic attacks (e.g., 2012 attack by Pouyanne) reduced effective security to $\approx 2^{48}$ operations — breakable in days on a parallel cluster. - Smart card side-channel attacks (power analysis, glitching) have been demonstrated. - Modern systems mitigate this by wrapping the MPEG-TS in an outer **AES-128** layer. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Operation of Line Inversion Scrambling:** *(3 marks)* In line-inversion scrambling, the polarity of the video signal is alternated line-by-line. On odd-numbered lines, the video is passed unchanged; on even-numbered lines, the video is multiplied by $-1$ (inverted). The result is that the descrambler must multiply each line by the *same* polarity factor it was scrambled with, in order to recover the original signal. **Polarity Factor Equation:** *(2 marks)* Define the polarity factor $s_n$ for line number $n$: $$ s_n = (-1)^n $$ Therefore: - $s_0 = +1$ (line 1 unchanged), - $s_1 = -1$ (line 2 inverted), - $s_2 = +1$ (line 3 unchanged), - and so on. **Proof of Perfect Recovery:** *(2 marks)* The scrambled signal over $N$ lines is: $$ v_{scrambled}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s_n \cdot v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) $$ The descrambler multiplies by the same factor $s_n$: $$ v_{clear}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s_n \cdot \big[ s_n \cdot v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) \big] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s_n^2 \cdot v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) $$ Since $s_n^2 = (-1)^{2n} = +1$ for all integer $n$: $$ v_{clear}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} v_{line,n}(t - nT_H) = v_{original}(t) \quad \blacksquare $$ Thus, when the descrambler is perfectly synchronized to the scrambler's line boundary, the original signal is recovered with zero quality loss. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Confusing scrambling with encryption** — Scrambling is a broadcast-level analog/digital signal transformation for *access control*; encryption is *cryptographic secrecy*. Students often mark themselves down by using the terms interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the CW refresh interval** — Examiners expect you to mention that the **Control Word is rotated every 5–10 seconds** via the ECM. Missing this loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Not stating the role of the smart card** — The smart card's *tamper resistance* is the cornerstone of CAS security. A vague answer like "smart card stores keys" loses marks; precise mention of *secure cryptographic element* scores full. > 4. **Wrong formulas in sync suppression** — The SNR penalty is $20\log_{10}(1/k)$ in dB, *not* $10\log_{10}$. Using the wrong factor (10 instead of 20) is a frequent slip. > 5. **Skipping the descrambler block diagram** — In 7-mark theory questions, *always* include a labeled block diagram. A purely textual answer loses at least 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scrambled Programs** > - **Definition:** Scrambling = signal transformation to prevent unauthorized viewing. > - **Why scramble?** Revenue (pay-TV), access control, IP protection, tiered service, PPV. > - **Analog techniques:** Sync suppression ($k < 0.3$), sync inversion, line inversion ($s_n = (-1)^n$), video inversion, band-stop trap. > - **Digital techniques:** DVB CSA (64-bit block, 64-bit CW), AES-128/256, AACS (Blu-ray), HDCP (HDMI), Widevine/PlayReady/FairPlay (OTT). > - **CAS components:** Scrambler, ECM, EMM, SMS, Smart Card, Descrambler. > - **CW:** 64-bit secret, refreshed every 5–10 s, never exposed outside the smart card. > - **ECM** carries the encrypted CW; **EMM** carries the encrypted service key (subscriber authorization). > - **Smart card** is a tamper-resistant secure module that performs all crypto internally. > - **DVB CSA effective security:** $2^{48}$ (post-2012 attack); modern systems layer AES on top. > - **Key equations:** - SNR penalty: $\Delta SNR = 20\log_{10}(1/k)$ dB - Polarity factor: $s_n = (-1)^n$ - Block scrambling: $C_i = B_i \oplus K_i(CW, IV, i)$ > - **Horizontal sync width:** 4.7 µs (NTSC/PAL); **Color subcarrier:** 3.58 MHz NTSC, 4.43 MHz PAL. > - **Real-world systems:** DTH (Tata Sky, Dish TV, Airtel Digital TV) — DVB-S2 + CSA/AES; Cable (Hathway, Den) — DVB-C + CAS; OTT (Netflix, Prime) — Widevine/PlayReady/FairPlay over HTTPS. > - **KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** "Conditional Access System", "Control Word", "Entitlement Control Message", "Entitlement Management Message", "Subscriber Management System", "Set-Top Box", "Smart Card", "MPEG Transport Stream", "DVB Common Scrambling Algorithm".

topics.title: Program Synchronization: PSI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Program Synchronization: PSI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Program Specific Information (PSI)** is a critical component of the **MPEG-2 Systems Layer** (defined by **ISO/IEC 13818-1**) that carries the metadata necessary for a digital television receiver (Set-Top Box / DVB receiver) to **demultiplex, synchronize, and decode** multiple programs within a single **MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS)**.

In any DVB / ATSC / ISDB broadcast, the transport stream is a **multiplex** of several TV channels, radio channels, and data services. The receiver cannot decode anything blindly — it must first understand **"what is being broadcast, where it sits in the bitstream, and how to lock onto it."** This descriptive layer is the **PSI**.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of PSI

| Table | Full Name | Purpose | Default PID |
|:------|:----------|:--------|:------------|
| **PAT** | Program Association Table | Index of all programs in the TS | **0x0000** |
| **PMT** | Program Map Table | Lists elementary streams (A/V/data) of a program | Signalled in PAT |
| **CAT** | Conditional Access Table | Describes CA systems (scrambling keys) | **0x0001** |
| **NIT** | Network Information Table | Physical network & tuning details | **0x0010** |

> [!NOTE]
> The collective term for all metadata tables (PSI + DVB-defined **SI tables** like SDT, EIT, TDT) is sometimes called the **Service Information (SI)** layer, but strictly **PSI** refers only to the four MPEG-defined tables above.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The TV Channel Restaurant Menu"

Imagine a multiplexed transport stream as a **massive restaurant** where one waiter (the 19 Mbps–38 Mbps transport stream) brings 50 different dishes (TV channels) to your table simultaneously. Without a menu, you would be lost.

- The **PAT** is the **"Welcome Card"** at the entrance — it tells you which tables (programs) exist in the restaurant and where the **table-specific menus** are kept.
- The **PMT** is the **"Detailed Menu"** for one specific table — it tells you exactly which dishes (video, audio, subtitles) are on offer and in what order they arrive.
- The **CAT** is the **"VIP / Subscription Card"** — it tells you how to pay / decrypt premium content.
- The **NIT** is the **"Restaurant Network Map"** — the satellite/cable/dish tuning parameters needed to find this restaurant in the first place.

So **PSI is the navigation brain** of every digital TV receiver. Without it, the screen stays black.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Transport Stream Packet Size = 188 bytes** (4-byte header + 184-byte payload)
> - **Sync Byte = 0x47** (decimal 71, every packet starts with this)
> - **Section Max Length = 1021 bytes** (excl. header and CRC)
> - **CRC-32 polynomial = 0x04C11DB7** (MPEG-2 CRC)
> - **Null Packet PID = 0x1FFF** (used for stuffing / constant bitrate padding)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS) — The Carrier of PSI

Every MPEG-2 TS is sliced into **fixed 188-byte packets**. This was deliberately chosen because it aligns perfectly with the **ATM cell payload (48 bytes × 4 ≈ 192)** and provides robust error resilience for noisy broadcast channels.

### TS Packet Structure (188 bytes)

```
+--------------------+----------------------+
|  4-Byte Header     |  184-Byte Payload    |
+--------------------+----------------------+
  Sync (1B)           Adaptation Field
  TEI, PUSI,         (optional, ≤184B)
  Priority (1B)      + PES / PSI Section
  PID (2B = 13 bits) (data, ≤184B)
  TSC, AFC (1B)
  Continuity (1B)
```

### 2.2 Header Field Breakdown

| Field | Size | Function |
|:------|:-----|:---------|
| **Sync Byte** | 8 bits | Always **0x47 (71)**. Receiver uses this byte to lock bit timing. |
| **TEI** (Transport Error Indicator) | 1 bit | Set by hardware if uncorrectable error. |
| **PUSI** (Payload Unit Start Indicator) | 1 bit | Marks start of a PES packet or PSI section. |
| **Transport Priority** | 1 bit | Higher priority packets may be processed first. |
| **PID** (Packet Identifier) | 13 bits | Logical channel address of this packet. |
| **TSC** (Transport Scrambling Control) | 2 bits | Indicates if payload is scrambled (00 = not scrambled). |
| **AFC** (Adaptation Field Control) | 2 bits | `01`=Payload only, `10`=Adaptation only, `11`=Both, `00`=Reserved. |
| **Continuity Counter** | 4 bits | Increments per PID; detects packet loss. |

## 2.3 The PSI Section — Logical Container

Each PSI table (PAT/PMT/CAT/NIT) is logically organized into **sections**:

```
+---------------------------------------+
|  table_id              (8 bits)       |
|  section_syntax_indicator (1 bit)     |
|  '0'                  (1 bit)        |
|  reserved             (2 bits)       |
|  section_length       (12 bits)      |  <-- total bytes after this field
+---------------------------------------+
|  table_id_extension    (16 bits)      |  <-- e.g., program_number (PAT) or
+---------------------------------------+      program_map_PID context (PMT)
|  reserved             (2 bits)       |
|  version_number       (5 bits)       |
|  current_next_indicator (1 bit)      |
|  section_number       (8 bits)       |
|  last_section_number  (8 bits)       |
+---------------------------------------+
|  ... Table-specific data ...         |
+---------------------------------------+
|  CRC_32                (32 bits)     |
+---------------------------------------+
```

## 2.4 Detailed Logic of Each PSI Table

### 2.4.1 PAT — Program Association Table (PID = 0x0000)
- **Always** carried on PID 0x0000. The receiver first looks **only** at this PID after sync acquisition.
- Contains a list of `program_number` → `program_map_PID` pairs.
- `program_number = 0` is reserved for the NIT pointer.
- **How to use:** The receiver decodes the PAT to learn the PID where each program's PMT is being transmitted, then jumps to that PID.

### 2.4.2 PMT — Program Map Table
- PID is taken from the PAT entry for that `program_number`.
- Contains:
  - `PCR_PID` — which packet's adaptation field carries the **Program Clock Reference**.
  - A list of **elementary streams**, each with:
    - `stream_type` (e.g., 0x01=MPEG-1 video, 0x02=MPEG-2 video, 0x03=MP3, 0x04=AAC, 0x06=AC-3, 0x0F=AAC ADTS, 0x1B=AVC/H.264, 0x24=HEVC/H.265).
    - `elementary_PID` — the PID where that stream's PES packets arrive.
    - `ES_info_length` + descriptor loop (language, format, etc.).

### 2.4.3 CAT — Conditional Access Table (PID = 0x0001)
- Describes the **CA_system_ID** of any conditional-access (pay-TV) streams.
- Contains CA descriptors with `CA_PID` for EMM (Entitlement Management Messages).

### 2.4.4 NIT — Network Information Table (PID = 0x0010)
- Carries **physical delivery parameters**: satellite transponder frequency, orbital position, modulation (QPSK/8PSK), symbol rate, FEC, cable modulation (QAM-64/256), etc.
- The receiver reads the NIT **after** the PAT to perform network discovery and tuning.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Address Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Item | Value / Meaning | Units / Notes |
|:--------------|:----------------|:--------------|
| $L_{TS}$ — Transport Stream Packet Length | **188** | bytes |
| $L_{HDR}$ — TS Header Length | **4** | bytes |
| $L_{PL}$ — TS Payload Length | **184** | bytes |
| $L_{SEC,MAX}$ — Max PSI Section Length | **1021** | bytes (excl. table_id, section_length, CRC) |
| $L_{CRC}$ — CRC Length | **4** | bytes (CRC-32 / MPEG-2) |
| $f_{TS}$ — Typical TS Bitrate | **19.39 to 38.81** | Mbps |
| $T_{pack}$ — Packet duration @ 19.39 Mbps | $188 \times 8 / 19.39 \times 10^{6}$ ≈ **77.6 μs** | microseconds |
| $N_{PID,user}$ — User-assignable PIDs | **0x0010 to 0x1FFE** (≈ 8175 usable) | count |
| $R_{PCR}$ — PCR Tick Rate | **27 MHz** | samples/second |
| $G_{PCR}$ — PCR Clock Granularity | **90 kHz** base + 27 MHz extension | derived from $27\ \text{MHz} / 300$ |

## 2.6 The Program Clock Reference (PCR) — Why PSI Needs Synchronization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A digital receiver must reconstruct a **27 MHz system clock** to synchronize audio and video presentation times. The encoder embeds **PCR samples** (42 bits each) into the adaptation field of TS packets whose PID equals the **PCR_PID** declared in the PMT.

The PCR has two parts:
- **PCR_base** (33 bits): the system clock divided by 300, modulo $2^{33}$. It increments every 300 cycles of the 27 MHz clock, i.e., every $\frac{1}{90\ \text{kHz}} \approx 11.11\ \mu s$.
- **PCR_ext** (9 bits): the 27 MHz extension within the current 300-cycle window.

The receiver's PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) uses the **gap** between received PCRs to:
- Adjust its local 27 MHz VCO frequency (lock)
- Adjust its local phase (align)

This continuous re-locking is what gives **lip-sync** between A/V.

> [!NOTE]
> The general timing relationship is:
>
> $$f_{PCR} = 27\ \text{MHz},\quad T_{base} = \frac{300}{27 \times 10^6}\ \text{s} = 11.11\ \mu\text{s}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Packet Duration vs. Bitrate

The **transport-stream packet** is the atomic unit that the receiver locks onto. Knowing its time-domain footprint is essential for buffer (TBV — Transport Buffer) sizing.

### Step 1 — Start with the bit count of one packet
Each TS packet carries exactly **188 bytes**.

$$
N_{bits} = 188\ \text{bytes} \times 8\ \frac{\text{bits}}{\text{byte}} = 1504\ \text{bits}
$$

This is the **fixed payload size in bits** of one packet regardless of bitrate.

### Step 2 — Define the bitrate
Let $R_{TS}$ be the constant bitrate of the transport stream in **bits per second (bps)**.

### Step 3 — Compute packet arrival period
Time between two consecutive packets of the same PID at the receiver:

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_{TS}} = \frac{1504}{R_{TS}}\ \text{seconds}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at the standard DVB bitrate
For **DVB-S**, the most common bitrate is approximately **19.39 Mbps** (after Reed–Solomon/Convolutional coding of a 27.5 Msym/s QPSK stream).

$$
T_{packet} = \frac{1504}{19.39 \times 10^6} \approx 7.756 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s}
$$

Converting to microseconds:

$$
T_{packet} \approx 77.56\ \mu\text{s}
$$

### Step 5 — Express packets-per-second

$$
N_{packets/s} = \frac{R_{TS}}{N_{bits}} = \frac{19.39 \times 10^6}{1504} \approx 12{,}892\ \text{packets/s}
$$

This is the **fundamental cadence** at which a DVB receiver must parse the TS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 188 bytes?** The MPEG-2 working group fixed 188 bytes to:
> (a) Align 4 packets to a single 750-μs ATM cell block (good for ATM-based delivery).
> (b) Provide a payload of 184 bytes that is comfortably larger than the Reed–Solomon block (204, 188) — yielding strong FEC compatibility.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Number of Programs in One TS

Each program requires at least one PMT entry in the PAT. The PAT is itself a single section.

### Step 1 — Section overhead
The PAT header is **8 bytes** (table_id + section_syntax_indicator + section_length + transport_stream_id + flags) and the CRC is **4 bytes**. So:

$$
L_{overhead} = 8 + 4 = 12\ \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Per-program entry size
Each `program_number → program_map_PID` entry is **4 bytes** (16-bit program_number + 3 reserved bits + 13-bit PID).

### Step 3 — Maximum section length constraint

$$
L_{sec,max} = 1021\ \text{bytes (data payload)}
$$

Therefore the number of program entries is bounded by:

$$
N_{prog,max} = \left\lfloor \frac{L_{sec,max}}{4} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1021}{4} \right\rfloor = 255\ \text{programs}
$$

### Step 4 — Net maximum
Subtracting the NIT pointer entry (program_number = 0 reserves 4 bytes), the practical maximum is:

$$
N_{prog,net,max} = 255 - 1 = 254\ \text{programs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real DVB deployments, **6–8 SD programs** or **2–3 HD programs** typically share a TS due to per-program bitrate constraints (~3–6 Mbps for SD, 12–20 Mbps for HD).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PID Allocation Map

| PID Range (Hex) | PID Range (Dec) | Allocation | PSI Relevance |
|:----------------|:----------------|:-----------|:--------------|
| 0x0000 | 0 | **PAT** | Mandatory PSI |
| 0x0001 | 1 | **CAT** | Mandatory PSI |
| 0x0002 – 0x000F | 2 – 15 | Reserved | — |
| 0x0010 | 16 | **NIT / ST** | Mandatory PSI/DVB-SI |
| 0x0011 – 0x1FFE | 17 – 8190 | **Elementary streams, PMT, NIT actual** | Service data |
| 0x1FFF | 8191 | **Null Packets** (stuffing) | Constant bitrate padding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The receiver's **bootstrap process** after sync is **strictly sequential**:
> 1. Acquire sync by scanning every 188 bytes for **0x47** (sync byte).
> 2. Filter PID = **0x0000** → decode **PAT**.
> 3. From PAT, extract the `program_map_PID` of the desired program.
> 4. Filter that PID → decode the **PMT**.
> 5. From PMT, extract the **PCR_PID** and the **elementary_PID**s of each stream.
> 6. Lock 27 MHz PLL using the PCR stream.
> 7. Filter A/V PIDs → collect PES packets → demux and decode.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: PSI Demultiplex Scenario

> **Question:** A DVB-S receiver is tuned to a transponder. The PAT (PID 0x0000) section contains the following programs: `(0x0001, 0x0100), (0x0002, 0x0200), (0x0003, 0x0300)`. The user selects program **0x0002**. Its PMT (PID 0x0200) lists: `PCR_PID = 0x0201`, `Video (0x02 MPEG-2) at PID 0x0202`, `Audio (0x04 AAC) at PID 0x0203`, `Teletext at PID 0x0204`.
>
> **Trace the demultiplexer actions.**

| Step | Demux Action | Output |
|:-----|:-------------|:-------|
| 1 | Sync on 0x47 every 188 B | Bit-clock recovery |
| 2 | Filter PID = 0x0000 | Reassemble PAT section |
| 3 | Locate entry `program_number = 0x0002` | PMT PID = 0x0200 |
| 4 | Re-tune filter to PID = 0x0200 | Reassemble PMT section |
| 5 | Parse PMT → extract `PCR_PID = 0x0201` | Start 27 MHz PLL |
| 6 | Re-tune filter to PID = 0x0202 | Collect MPEG-2 video PES |
| 7 | Re-tune filter to PID = 0x0203 | Collect AAC audio PES |
| 8 | Re-tune filter to PID = 0x0204 (optional) | Collect teletext PES |
| 9 | Forward PES → ES decoders | A/V output |

This is exactly what every DVB set-top box does in milliseconds on every channel change — the **PSI enables channel-zapping latency of typically 0.5–2 seconds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transport Stream Packet — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkt["TS Packet 188 bytes"]
    hdr["Header 4 bytes"]
    payload["Payload 184 bytes"]
    sync["Sync Byte 0x47 8 bits"]
    tei["TEI 1 bit"]
    pusi["PUSI 1 bit"]
    prio["Transport Priority 1 bit"]
    pid["PID 13 bits"]
    tsc["Transport Scrambling 2 bits"]
    afc["Adaptation Field Control 2 bits"]
    cc["Continuity Counter 4 bits"]
    af["Adaptation Field variable"]
    pespsi["PES / PSI Section data"]
    pcr["PCR 42 bits inside AF"]

    pkt --> hdr
    pkt --> payload
    hdr --> sync
    hdr --> tei
    hdr --> pusi
    hdr --> prio
    hdr --> pid
    hdr --> tsc
    hdr --> afc
    hdr --> cc
    payload --> af
    payload --> pespsi
    af --> pcr
```

## 4.2 PSI Table Hierarchy & Bootstrap Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Receiver Power On"]
    syncA["Acquire sync byte 0x47"]
    patA["Filter PID 0x0000 Decode PAT"]
    nitA["Follow program_number 0 PID = 0x0010 Decode NIT Tune parameters"]
    selA["User selects program number N"]
    pmtA["Lookup PAT to find PMT PID for N"]
    pmtdec["Filter that PID Decode PMT"]
    pcrA["Extract PCR_PID Lock 27 MHz PLL"]
    avA["Filter elementary PIDs Decode A/V PES"]
    present["Present synchronized A/V to display"]

    startA --> syncA --> patA
    patA --> nitA
    patA --> selA --> pmtA --> pmtdec --> pcrA --> avA --> present
```

## 4.3 PSI Tables in a Transport Stream — Logical View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ts["MPEG 2 Transport Stream"]
    pat["PAT PID 0x0000 Contains program numbers to PMT PIDs"]
    nit["NIT PID 0x0010 Network and tuning info"]
    cat["CAT PID 0x0001 Conditional access info"]
    pmt1["PMT Program 1001 PID 0x0100"]
    pmt2["PMT Program 1002 PID 0x0200"]
    v1["Video ES PID 0x0101"]
    a1["Audio ES PID 0x0102"]
    v2["Video ES PID 0x0201"]
    a2["Audio ES PID 0x0202"]
    pcr1["PCR Stream PID 0x0101"]
    pcr2["PCR Stream PID 0x0201"]

    ts --> pat
    ts --> nit
    ts --> cat
    pat -->|points to| pmt1
    pat -->|points to| pmt2
    pmt1 --> v1
    pmt1 --> a1
    pmt1 --> pcr1
    pmt2 --> v2
    pmt2 --> a2
    pmt2 --> pcr2
```

## 4.4 PCR-Based Clock Recovery — Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["Encoder 27 MHz VCO"]
    b["PCR Sample inserted in Adaptation Field"]
    c["Receiver Demux extracts PCR_PID packets"]
    d["PCR Timestamps arrive every ~40 ms"]
    e["Local 27 MHz VCO at receiver"]
    f["Phase Frequency Detector PFD"]
    g["Loop Filter LF"]
    h["VCO Control Voltage"]

    a --> b --> c --> d --> f
    e --> f
    f --> g --> h --> e
    f -.-> out["Locked 27 MHz Clock to decoders"]
```

## 4.5 PSI Demux Sequence — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Step 1 Sync acquisition look for 0x47"]
    s2["Step 2 Locate PAT PID 0x0000"]
    s3["Step 3 Reassemble PAT section from packets"]
    s4["Step 4 Validate CRC 32"]
    s5["Step 5 Parse program number to PMT PID table"]
    s6["Step 6 User chooses program"]
    s7["Step 7 Tune to PMT PID"]
    s8["Step 8 Decode PMT get stream types and PIDs"]
    s9["Step 9 Get PCR PID start 27 MHz PLL"]
    s10["Step 10 Tune A/V PIDs demux PES"]
    s11["Step 11 Decode and present"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10 --> s11
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is Program Specific Information (PSI) in MPEG-2? Name the four mandatory PSI tables.**

**Model Answer:**

PSI is the metadata layer of an MPEG-2 Transport Stream (ISO/IEC 13818-1) that allows a receiver to locate, demultiplex, and synchronize all programs and elementary streams within a multi-program TS.

The four mandatory PSI tables are:

1. **PAT** – Program Association Table (PID 0x0000)
2. **PMT** – Program Map Table (PID signalled in PAT)
3. **CAT** – Conditional Access Table (PID 0x0001)
4. **NIT** – Network Information Table (PID 0x0010)

PSI works hand-in-hand with the PCR-based 27 MHz clock for A/V synchronization.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: Why is the MPEG-2 transport packet size fixed at 188 bytes? Mention the role of the sync byte.**

**Model Answer:**

The **188-byte packet length** was chosen by the MPEG-2 committee for two reasons:

1. **ATM compatibility:** Four MPEG-2 packets (4 × 188 = 752 B) plus 4 bytes of ATM overhead align to 768 B, which is a clean multiple of 48-byte ATM cell payloads. This enables clean transport of MPEG-2 over ATM networks.
2. **Reed–Solomon compatibility:** The 188-byte length is the data block size of the standard RS(204,188) FEC code used in DVB, providing strong error correction.

The **sync byte (0x47, decimal 71)** is the first byte of every TS packet. The receiver scans the incoming bitstream for the 0x47 byte at 188-byte intervals to acquire **bit-clock and byte-frame synchronization** before any payload parsing can begin.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the MPEG-2 Transport Stream packet structure. Also explain in detail the role of PSI tables — PAT, PMT, CAT, and NIT — in program demultiplexing and synchronization. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — TS Packet Structure and Header Fields (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

An MPEG-2 Transport Stream is a continuous stream of fixed **188-byte packets**. Each packet consists of a **4-byte header** followed by a **184-byte payload**.

**Header Fields (4 bytes = 32 bits):**

| Field | Width | Meaning |
|:------|:------|:--------|
| Sync byte | 8 bits | Always `0x47`. Allows bit-clock & frame synchronization. |
| Transport Error Indicator (TEI) | 1 bit | Set if hardware detected an uncorrectable error. |
| Payload Unit Start Indicator (PUSI) | 1 bit | If `1`, this packet starts a PES packet or PSI section. |
| Transport Priority | 1 bit | Higher-priority packets are marked. |
| PID (Packet Identifier) | 13 bits | Logical channel address (0 to 8191). |
| Transport Scrambling Control (TSC) | 2 bits | `00` = not scrambled, others indicate CA scrambling. |
| Adaptation Field Control (AFC) | 2 bits | `01`=payload, `10`=adaptation, `11`=both, `00`=reserved. |
| Continuity Counter (CC) | 4 bits | Increments per PID modulo 16; detects packet loss. |

**Payload (184 bytes):**

- Optional **Adaptation Field** (length in 1st byte), which may carry the **PCR** (42 bits), random-access flag, splicing info, etc.
- Remaining bytes carry **PES packets** (audio/video) or **PSI sections** (PAT/PMT/CAT/NIT).

```
+---------------------------------------+
|  47  TEI  PUSI  Pri   |   PID   | TSC AFC CC |
+---------------------------------------+
|         Adaptation Field (optional)   |
+---------------------------------------+
|   PES / PSI Section (≤184 bytes)      |
+---------------------------------------+
                  188 bytes
```

#### Part (b) — PSI Tables and Program Synchronization (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

PSI is the **navigation brain** of an MPEG-2 receiver. It enables the demultiplexer to find, lock, and decode any chosen program.

1. **PAT (Program Association Table) — PID 0x0000**
   - Always carried on PID 0. Lists every program in the TS.
   - Each entry: `program_number (16 bits) → program_map_PID (13 bits)`.
   - The receiver decodes the PAT first; `program_number = 0` is reserved and points to the NIT.

2. **PMT (Program Map Table) — PID signalled by PAT**
   - Lists all elementary streams belonging to a program.
   - Each ES entry has:
     - `stream_type` (e.g., 0x02 MPEG-2 video, 0x04 AAC, 0x1B AVC, 0x24 HEVC).
     - `elementary_PID` (the actual location of the PES data).
     - ES descriptors (language, format).
   - Also carries the `PCR_PID` used for clock recovery.

3. **CAT (Conditional Access Table) — PID 0x0001**
   - Identifies the CA system used for scrambled services.
   - Contains `CA_descriptor`s pointing to EMM PIDs.

4. **NIT (Network Information Table) — PID 0x0010**
   - Carries physical-network information: satellite transponder, frequency, symbol rate, FEC, modulation (QPSK/QAM/8PSK).
   - Required for network discovery and cross-transponder tuning.

**Synchronization role of PSI:**

The PMT declares the `PCR_PID`. The encoder stamps the **27 MHz / 90 kHz** PCR samples in the adaptation fields of that PID. The receiver:

1. Demultiplexes the PCR stream,
2. Drives a PLL that locks the local 27 MHz VCO,
3. Uses the locked clock to present **DTS (Decode Time Stamps)** and **PTS (Presentation Time Stamps)** carried in PES headers.

This is the mechanism that ensures **lip-sync** between audio and video.

**Demultiplexer Bootstrap Order:**

PAT → PMT → PCR lock → A/V PES capture → synchronized decoding.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q: Explain the structure and function of the Program Clock Reference (PCR) in an MPEG-2 Transport Stream. How is it used to achieve audio–video synchronization in a digital TV receiver? Include the relevant equations and timing relationships. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — PCR Structure, Encoding, and Bitrate Relationships (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Program Clock Reference (PCR)** is a **42-bit timestamp** embedded in the **adaptation field** of TS packets whose PID equals the `PCR_PID` declared in the program's PMT. It represents the state of the encoder's 27 MHz system clock at the instant the PCR byte is presented to the TS multiplexer.

**PCR Structure (42 bits):**

| Part | Width | Unit | Tick value |
|:-----|:------|:-----|:-----------|
| PCR_base | 33 bits | 90 kHz (i.e., 27 MHz ÷ 300) | 1 / 90,000 s ≈ 11.11 μs |
| 6 reserved bits | — | — | — |
| PCR_extension | 9 bits | 27 MHz within current base tick | 1 / 27,000,000 s ≈ 37 ns |

**Encoding rule (bit level):**

$$
\text{PCR}(t) = \text{PCR\_base} \times 300 + \text{PCR\_ext}
$$

Where

$$
\text{PCR\_base} = \lfloor f_{sys} \cdot t / 300 \rfloor \mod 2^{33}
$$

$$
\text{PCR\_ext} = (f_{sys} \cdot t) \mod 300
$$

and $f_{sys} = 27 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$ is the encoder's system clock frequency.

**Bitrate justification:** PCRs must arrive **at most every 100 ms** (mandatory upper bound) and **no less frequently than every 40 ms** (recommended) so that the receiver's PLL has enough samples to lock with low jitter.

Number of packets between two consecutive PCRs at a typical TS bitrate $R_{TS}$:

$$
N_{gap} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3} \times R_{TS}}{1504}
$$

For $R_{TS} = 19.39$ Mbps:

$$
N_{gap} = \frac{0.04 \times 19.39 \times 10^{6}}{1504} \approx 515\ \text{packets}
$$

So roughly **one PCR every 515 packets** at 19.39 Mbps.

#### Part (b) — Receiver-Side PCR-Based A/V Synchronization (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The receiver must reconstruct a **stable 27 MHz clock** that is **frequency-locked and phase-aligned** to the encoder's clock, otherwise audio and video presentation timestamps (PTS) drift apart and the famous **lip-sync error** appears.

**Receiver PLL Block Diagram:**

```
+----------+      +-----+      +-------+      +------+
| PCR In   |----->| PFD |----->| Loop  |----->| VCO  |---> 27 MHz to decoders
+----------+      +-----+      | Filter|      +------+
                              +-------+         ^
                                                 |
+----------+                                    |
| Local    |------------------------------------+
| 27 MHz   |
+----------+
```

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **PCR Extraction:** The demultiplexer filters packets of `PCR_PID` and extracts the 42-bit PCR.
2. **Difference computation:** The receiver computes the difference between successive PCR arrivals and compares it to the local 27 MHz clock.
3. **PLL Lock:** A Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD) drives a loop filter that adjusts the VCO control voltage.
4. **Locked Clock Output:** Once locked, the VCO provides a 27 MHz reference to the audio and video decoders.
5. **PTS / DTS Comparison:** Each PES header carries **PTS** (when to present) and **DTS** (when to decode). The decoder compares PTS to the local 27 MHz counter and releases the frame at the precise instant.
6. **Continuous Re-locking:** Every subsequent PCR corrects any small drift in the VCO.

**Result:** A/V presentation is **sample-accurate and lip-sync-perfect**.

**Key Equation — PTS-driven presentation delay:**

$$
t_{present} = \frac{\text{PTS}}{f_{sys}} = \frac{\text{PTS}}{27 \times 10^6}\ \text{seconds}
$$

Where PTS is the 33-bit presentation timestamp in the PES header (units of 90 kHz).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes students make on PSI questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PSI vs. SI.** PSI = the four MPEG-defined tables (PAT, PMT, CAT, NIT). SI = the broader DVB-defined metadata (SDT, EIT, TDT, BAT, RST). Examiners deduct marks if you call SDT/EIT "PSI".
> 2. **Wrong PID values.** PAT is **always** 0x0000, CAT is **always** 0x0001, NIT is **always** 0x0010, null packet is **always** 0x1FFF. Memorize these.
> 3. **Forgetting the sync byte.** The 0x47 sync byte is what bootstraps the receiver; many students describe PAT/PMT but skip this prerequisite.
> 4. **Ignoring PCR.** The PCR is the **only** mechanism for clock recovery. Examiners often give full credit only if you explicitly link PMT's `PCR_PID` to the 27 MHz PLL and A/V sync.
> 5. **Confusing PSI with PS (Program Stream).** PS = MPEG-2 Program Stream (used in DVDs). PSI exists only in **TS** (Transport Stream). Mixing them up costs marks.
> 6. **Not stating units.** When giving bitrates, always state **Mbps**, not just "bits". For PCR, specify **27 MHz / 90 kHz**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **PSI = Program Specific Information** — MPEG-2 (ISO/IEC 13818-1) metadata layer.
- **4 PSI tables:** PAT (0x0000), CAT (0x0001), NIT (0x0010), PMT (PID via PAT).
- **TS packet = 188 bytes = 4 B header + 184 B payload.**
- **Sync byte = 0x47** — first byte of every packet; used for bit-clock lock.
- **PID = 13 bits** — logical channel address; 8192 possible values.
- **Adaptation Field** may carry **PCR (42 bits)** and stuffing.
- **PAT** is the index of all programs; the receiver's first stop after sync.
- **PMT** lists elementary streams (A/V/data) and the **PCR_PID** of a program.
- **CAT** carries CA_system_ID for pay-TV.
- **NIT** carries physical network tuning info.
- **PCR** is sampled from a **27 MHz** encoder clock; receiver's PLL reconstructs this clock.
- **PCR_base = 33 bits @ 90 kHz**, **PCR_ext = 9 bits @ 27 MHz**.
- **Packet period @ 19.39 Mbps ≈ 77.6 μs** → ~12,892 packets/sec.
- **Max programs in one TS ≈ 254** (from section length limit of 1021 B).
- **PCR must arrive at least every 100 ms** (≤ 0.1 s gap) for stable receiver PLL lock.
- **Bootstrap order:** Sync → PAT → (NIT) → PMT → PCR-PID → A/V PIDs → decode.
- **PES headers carry PTS (presentation) and DTS (decode) timestamps** for A/V sync.
- **Null Packet PID = 0x1FFF** is used for constant bitrate stuffing.
- **PSI ≠ SI:** SI (SDT, EIT, TDT, BAT) is the DVB extension; PSI is the MPEG-2 core.
- **PS (Program Stream) is for DVDs; TS (Transport Stream) is for broadcast — and only TS carries PSI.**
- **Stream type codes to remember:** 0x01=MPEG-1 video, 0x02=MPEG-2 video, 0x03=MP3, 0x04=AAC, 0x06=AC-3, 0x1B=AVC (H.264), 0x24=HEVC (H.265).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additional Network Information and Service Description in Data Streams for Set-Top Boxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINEMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Additional Network Information and Service Description in Data Streams for Set-Top Boxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Set-Top Box (STB)** engineering, a digital broadcast stream (DVB / MPEG-2 Transport Stream) does not carry only audio and video. It also embeds a parallel layer of **metadata tables** that describe *what services exist, who provides them, how to tune to them, and what additional network resources are available*. 

The two specific tables that this topic covers are:

- **Additional Network Information (ANI)** → Realised through the **Network Information Table (NIT)**, defined in MPEG-2 ISO/IEC 13818-1 as a *Private Section* and extended by DVB in **EN 300 468**.
- **Service Description** → Realised through the **Service Description Table (SDT)**, also defined in **DVB EN 300 468**, which provides textual names of services and their providers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Interpretation:** *Additional Network Information* refers to the **second loop of the NIT** (the `other` network loop), which contains delivery system descriptors (frequency, orbital position, modulation, FEC, polarisation) for transponders **other than the current one**. *Service Description* refers to the **SDT**, which carries human-readable service names, provider names, and service flags (running, scrambled, etc.).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you land in a foreign country airport. The **NIT is the airport announcement board** — it tells you *which airlines operate here, on which runways, at what frequency they arrive, and where to catch connecting flights* (i.e. tuning parameters for other transponders).

The **SDT is the directory board next to each airline counter** — it tells you *the name of the service ("STAR PLUS HD"), the provider ("STAR TV INDIA"), whether it is currently running, and whether it is scrambled* (CA-protected).

The **STB is the tourist** who, upon arriving, must read **both** boards before deciding which gate (PES stream) to walk to.

> [!NOTE]
> Without the NIT and SDT, an STB would be "blind" — it could decode audio/video of a *known* PID, but it could not build a channel list (Electronic Programme Guide – EPG) or auto-tune to other transponders on the same satellite/cable network.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **TS Packet Size** | **188 bytes** | 4-byte header + 184-byte payload |
| **PID Field Width** | **13 bits** | Allows 8192 unique stream IDs |
| **PAT PID** | **0x0000** | Always PID 0 |
| **CAT PID** | **0x0001** | Conditional Access Table |
| **NIT PID** | **0x0010** | Standardised by DVB |
| **SDT PID** | **0x0011** | Standardised by DVB |
| **Null Packet PID** | **0x1FFF** | Used for stuffing |
| **PSI Repetition Rate (PAT)** | ≤ **100 ms** | Mandatory by MPEG-2 |
| **NIT/SDT Repetition Rate** | ≤ **10 s** | DVB EN 300 468 |
| **CRC32 Polynomial** | **0x04C11DB7** | MPEG-2 section CRC |
| **Section Max Length** | **4096 bytes** | 4 KB excluding header |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS) — Big Picture

A Transport Stream is a **packetised, multiplexed, fixed-length** byte stream of **188-byte packets**. Every packet carries a **4-byte header** containing:

```
sync_byte(8) | TEI(1) | PUSI(1) | Priority(1) | PID(13) | TSC(2) | AFC(2) | CC(4)
```

- **sync_byte** = `0x47` — used by the STB hardware for byte alignment.
- **PID (Packet Identifier)** — 13 bits — acts as a *postal code*; the STB's demux filters packets by PID.
- **PUSI (Payload Unit Start Indicator)** — when set, the first byte of payload is the *pointer_field* indicating the start of a new section.
- **AFC (Adaptation Field Control)** — indicates presence/absence of adaptation field and payload.

## 2.2 Why PSI/SI is needed — The "Why"

An STB after power-on is in a **state of ignorance**:
1. It does not know the bit-rate.
2. It does not know how many services (TV channels) are in the stream.
3. It does not know the audio language, the PCR PID, the CA system, or the text name of each service.
4. It cannot build an EPG.

**PSI (Program Specific Information)** and **SI (Service Information)** solve this problem by embedding structured *tables* in reserved PIDs.

## 2.3 The PSI/SI Family — Operational Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Demuxer captures PID 0x0000 (PAT).**
The STB's tuner locks to the carrier, the MPEG-2 front-end searches for `0x47` sync bytes, and the demux routes PID 0 packets to the section parser.

**Step 2 — PAT (Program Association Table) is decoded.**
PAT contains `program_number → PMT_PID` mappings for every service. It also points to the NIT PID (program_number = 0 is reserved for the NIT).

**Step 3 — NIT (Network Information Table) is decoded.**
The NIT carries two loops:
- **Loop 0 (Network descriptors):** network name, country code, private descriptors.
- **Loop 1 (Transport stream loop):** for *each* TS, the `transport_stream_id`, original_network_id, and a list of **delivery system descriptors** containing:
  - Satellite: `frequency`, `orbital_position`, `polarisation`, `modulation`, `symbol_rate`, `FEC_inner`.
  - Cable: `frequency`, `modulation`, `symbol_rate`, `FEC_outer`.
  - Terrestrial: `centre_frequency`, `bandwidth`, `constellation`, `code_rate`, `guard_interval`, `transmission_mode`.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Additional Network Information"** = Loop 1 of the NIT, which is *not* strictly required to decode the *current* TS but is **mandatory for the STB to build a full channel list** and perform network-wide zapping/auto-tuning.

**Step 4 — PMT (Program Map Table) is decoded for each service.**
For each `program_number` found in PAT, the PMT provides:
- **PCR_PID** (Program Clock Reference PID).
- **Stream type + PID** for each elementary stream (video, audio, subtitling, data).
- **CA_descriptor** if the service is scrambled.

**Step 5 — SDT (Service Description Table) is decoded.**
The SDT carries, for each `service_id` in the current TS:
- `service_descriptor` with `service_name` and `service_provider_name` (the strings shown in the EPG).
- `service_type` (TV, radio, data, etc.).
- `running_status`, `free_CA_mode` (scrambled flag).
- Country, language, and multilingual descriptors.

**Step 6 — CAT (Conditional Access Table) at PID 0x0001.**
Provides CA_system_ID → EMM_PID mappings so the STB knows where to find entitlement messages from the operator.

**Step 7 — EIT (Event Information Table) at PID 0x0012.**
Provides now/next and schedule event data for the EPG. *EIT is not part of this topic* but is logically the next table fetched.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula or Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Packet size $L_p$ | $L_p = 188$ | bytes |
| Header size $L_h$ | $L_h = 4$ | bytes |
| Payload size $L_{pl}$ | $L_{pl} = L_p - L_h = 184$ | bytes |
| TS bit-rate $R_{TS}$ | $R_{TS} = \dfrac{L_p \cdot 8}{T_p}$ where $T_p$ = packet period | **Mbit/s** |
| Effective payload rate | $R_{pl} = R_{TS} \cdot \dfrac{184}{188}$ | Mbit/s |
| Section length (bytes) | $L_{sec} = \text{section_length\_field} + 3$ | bytes |
| Max section | $L_{sec,\max} = 4096$ | bytes |
| Bit-rate per PID | $R_{PID} = \dfrac{N_{pkts} \cdot 188 \cdot 8}{T_{obs}}$ | bit/s |
| Repetition interval NIT | $\Delta t_{NIT} \le 10$ | **seconds** |
| Repetition interval PAT | $\Delta t_{PAT} \le 0.1$ | **seconds** |
| Total service bit-rate | $R_{svc} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{es}} R_{es,i}$ | bit/s |
| Multiplex utilisation $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum R_{svc} + R_{OH}}{R_{TS}}$ | dimensionless, $\eta \le 1$ |
| PSI/SI overhead $R_{OH}$ | $R_{OH} = \dfrac{188 \cdot 8}{\Delta t_{rep}}$ per table | bit/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love the question *"Why 188 bytes?"* — the answer is the ATM cell alignment: 4 ATM cells of 47 bytes = 188 bytes. This is a **frequently repeated 3-mark question**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite (DVB-S/S2) head-ends** must populate the NIT accurately; a single wrong orbital position descriptor breaks auto-installation for millions of STBs.
- **Cable (DVB-C) operators** use the NIT's cable delivery descriptor to push channel-lineup updates to legacy STBs via network download.
- **OTT hybrid STBs (HbbTV)** use SDT service names as a fallback when DVB-IPTV streams lack a clean EPG.
- **Conditional Access Systems (CAS)** such as Irdeto, Nagra, Conax rely on the CAT and PMT CA_descriptors to demux EMM/ECM streams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit-Rate from Packet Repetition

A DVB-S transponder emits one 188-byte TS packet every $T_p$ seconds.

$$
R_{TS} \;=\; \frac{L_p \cdot 8}{T_p} \;\;[\text{bit/s}]
$$

For a **27.5 Mbaud** symbol rate with **3/4 FEC** and **QPSK** (2 bits/symbol):

$$
R_{raw} = 27.5 \times 10^{6} \times 2 = 55 \times 10^{6}\;\text{bit/s}
$$

$$
R_{TS} = 55 \times 10^{6} \times \frac{3}{4} = 41.25\;\text{Mbit/s}
$$

$$
T_p = \frac{188 \times 8}{41.25 \times 10^{6}} = 36.45\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Textual logic:** Each packet carries 188 bytes = 1504 bits. Dividing by the packet period yields the bit-rate; rearranging gives the packet period.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overhead Due to NIT/SDT Repetition

Assume an NIT section of size $L_{NIT} = 600$ bytes repeated every $\Delta t_{NIT} = 10$ s.

$$
R_{NIT} = \frac{L_{NIT} \times 8}{\Delta t_{NIT}} = \frac{600 \times 8}{10} = 480\;\text{bit/s}
$$

Similarly, an SDT of 1.2 kB repeated every 2 s:

$$
R_{SDT} = \frac{1200 \times 8}{2} = 4800\;\text{bit/s}
$$

Total SI overhead:

$$
R_{OH,SI} = R_{NIT} + R_{SDT} = 480 + 4800 = 5280\;\text{bit/s} \approx 5.28\;\text{kbit/s}
$$

**Textual logic:** The bit-rate consumed by a metadata table equals its size in bits divided by its repetition interval. SI tables trade bit-rate for STB install/EPG usability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Section Length Parsing (Worked)

A captured section has the first three bytes:

| Byte | Value (hex) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x40` | `table_id = 0x40` (NIT, actual network) |
| 1 | `0xF0` | high bits: `section_syntax_indicator=1`, `0`, reserved |
| 2 | `0x34` | low 8 bits of section_length |

Combined `section_length` field (12 bits):

$$
L_{sf} = ((0xF0 \; \&\; 0x0F) \ll 8) \;|\; 0x34 = (0x00 \ll 8) \;|\; 0x34 = 52
$$

Total section length:

$$
L_{sec} = L_{sf} + 3 = 52 + 3 = 55\;\text{bytes}
$$

**Textual logic:** The 12-bit `section_length` field counts bytes *after itself but including the CRC*. Adding the 3-byte header (`table_id` + 2-byte length field) yields the full section size. Valid range: $0 \le L_{sf} \le 4093$ (so $L_{sec} \le 4096$).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multiplex Utilisation

A 41.25 Mbit/s transponder carries 8 SD services, each at 4 Mbit/s, plus audio and PSI/SI overhead.

$$
R_{total\,services} = 8 \times (4 + 0.25) = 34\;\text{Mbit/s}
$$

$$
R_{OH,PSI} = 0.2\;\text{Mbit/s (estimated)}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{34 + 0.2}{41.25} = \frac{34.2}{41.25} \approx 0.829 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 82.9\%
$$

**Textual logic:** $\eta$ measures how efficiently the transponder is filled with useful payload. Broadcast engineers target $\eta \approx 0.80 - 0.90$; pushing higher causes PCR jitter and stuffing issues.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How STB Uses Each Table

| Table | PID | table_id | Section Source | STB Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PAT | 0x0000 | 0x00 | MPEG-2 | Build `program_number → PMT_PID` map |
| CAT | 0x0001 | 0x01 | MPEG-2 | Find EMM stream for CA |
| PMT | from PAT | 0x02 | MPEG-2 | Build `service_id → ES_PID[]` map |
| **NIT** | **0x0010** | **0x40/0x41** | **DVB** | **Build network/transponder map** |
| **SDT** | **0x0011** | **0x42/0x46** | **DVB** | **Display service names in EPG** |
| BAT | 0x0011 | 0x4A | DVB | Bouquet grouping (e.g., "Tata Sky Family") |
| EIT | 0x0012 | 0x4E-0x6F | DVB | EPG now/next/schedule |
| TDT/TOT | 0x0014 | 0x70/0x73 | DVB | Set STB clock |
| RST | 0x0013 | 0x71 | DVB | Update running status of events |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that **NIT and SDT share PID 0x0011** in the case of BAT? No — the **NIT is on 0x0010** and the **SDT/BAT share 0x0011** but are distinguished by `table_id` (0x42 = SDT actual, 0x46 = SDT other, 0x4A = BAT). This is a classic KTU "trick" MCQ.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — STB Boot-up Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RF Front-end: Tuner + LNA] --> B[QPSK/QAM Demodulator]
    B --> C[MPEG-2 TS De-multiplexer]
    C --> D{PID Filter}
    D -->|PID 0x0000| E[PAT Parser]
    D -->|PID 0x0010| F[NIT Parser]
    D -->|PID 0x0011| G[SDT Parser]
    D -->|PID from PAT| H[PMT Parser]
    D -->|PID 0x0001| I[CAT Parser]
    D -->|Service ES PIDs| J[Video Decoder H.264/MPEG-2]
    D -->|Service ES PIDs| K[Audio Decoder AC3/MP2/AAC]
    E --> L[Service Database RAM]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> M[CA Engine: EMM/ECM]
    L --> N[EPG / Channel List Builder]
    N --> O[User Interface OSD]
    M --> J
    M --> K
    J --> P[HDMI Output]
    K --> P
```

## 4.2 Transport Stream Packet Structure (ASCII Map)

```
+------------------------------------------------------+
| 0x47  | TEI PUSI Pri |   PID (13 bits)  |TSC|AFC|CC |  <- 4-byte header
+------------------------------------------------------+
|        [ Optional Adaptation Field (AFC=10/11) ]    |  
+------------------------------------------------------+
|        [ Payload (184 bytes if AFC=01) ]            |
|        [  Pointer field (1 byte) if PUSI=1  ]       |
|        [    Section bytes follow              ]     |
+------------------------------------------------------+
```

## 4.3 NIT Section Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[NIT Section] --> B[Table ID 0x40 or 0x41]
    A --> C[Section Syntax Indicator = 1]
    A --> D[Reserved + Section Length 12 bits]
    A --> E[Network ID 16 bits]
    A --> F[Version + Current/Next 8 bits]
    A --> G[Section Number 8 bits]
    A --> H[Last Section Number 8 bits]
    A --> I[Reserved + Network Descriptors Length 12 bits]
    A --> J[Loop 0: Network Descriptors]
    A --> K[Reserved + TS Loop Length 12 bits]
    A --> L[Loop 1: Transport Stream Loop]
    L --> L1[TS ID 16 bits]
    L --> L2[Original Network ID 16 bits]
    L --> L3[Reserved + TS Descriptors Length 12 bits]
    L --> L4[Delivery System Descriptor]
    A --> M[CRC32 32 bits]
```

## 4.4 SDT Section Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SDT Section] --> B[Table ID 0x42 actual or 0x46 other]
    A --> C[Section Length]
    A --> D[Transport Stream ID 16]
    A --> E[Original Network ID 16]
    A --> F[Version Current Next 8]
    A --> G[Section Number 8]
    A --> H[Last Section Number 8]
    A --> I[Original Network ID 16]
    A --> J[Service ID 16]
    A --> K[EIT Schedule Flag 1]
    A --> L[EIT Present Following Flag 1]
    A --> M[Running Status 3]
    A --> N[Free CA Mode 1]
    A --> O[Descriptors Loop Length 12]
    A --> P[Service Descriptor: Name, Provider, Type]
    A --> Q[Country Availability etc.]
    A --> R[CRC32 32]
```

## 4.5 Set-Top Box Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RF[RF Input Satellite/Cable/Terrestrial] --> TN[Tuner]
    TN --> DM[Demodulator QPSK QAM COFDM]
    DM --> TS_DMX[MPEG-2 Transport Demux]
    TS_DMX --> SI_ENG[SI Engine Parser NIT SDT PAT PMT]
    SI_ENG --> CPU[CPU MIPS/ARM]
    CPU --> MEM[Flash + DDR]
    CPU --> VID[MPEG/H.264 Video Decoder]
    CPU --> AUD[AC3/AAC Audio Decoder]
    CPU --> OSD[OSD Generator]
    VID --> HDMI[HDMI Tx]
    AUD --> HDMI
    OSD --> HDMI
    CPU --> CI[CI/CAM Slot]
    CI --> CA[Smart Card Reader]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the MPEG-2 Transport Stream in a set-top box.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The MPEG-2 Transport Stream is a **fixed 188-byte packetised** multiplex carrying compressed video, audio, data, and metadata. The STB tuner locks to the RF carrier; the demodulator outputs a serial bit-stream; the front-end searches for the sync byte **0x47** and aligns to 188-byte packets. The **demultiplexer** routes packets by their 13-bit **PID** to:
- **PID 0x0000** → PAT parser
- **PID 0x0001** → CAT parser
- **PID 0x0010** → NIT parser
- **PID 0x0011** → SDT parser
- **PIDs from PMT** → elementary stream decoders (video, audio, subtitling).

The TS therefore unifies all service data into a single synchronous bit-stream and lets a low-cost demux IC isolate streams by PID. *(3 marks: 1 for TS definition, 1 for 188-byte structure, 1 for PID-based demux.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*List the tables that constitute DVB Service Information (SI) and state one function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Table | PID | Function |
|---|---|---|
| NIT | 0x0010 | Carries network/transponder tuning data (**additional network info**) |
| SDT | 0x0011 | Carries **service description** (name, provider) |
| BAT | 0x0011 | Groups services into bouquets |
| EIT | 0x0012 | Schedule and now/next EPG data |
| TDT | 0x0014 | UTC time reference |
| TOT | 0x0014 | Local time offset |
| RST | 0x0013 | Running status updates |
| ST  | 0x0010-0x0014 | Section stuffing/invalidation |

*(3 marks: 1 per table-function pair × 3 = 3 marks; cite any three.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (Module Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain in detail the structure of the **Network Information Table (NIT)**. How does it provide "additional network information" to a set-top box?*

**Model Solution:**

The NIT is a DVB-defined SI table (DVB EN 300 468) carried on **PID 0x0010** with `table_id = 0x40` (actual network) or `0x41` (other network). Its purpose is to convey:

1. **Identification of the delivery network** (network_id).
2. **Tuning data for the *current* transport stream** (mandatory in the actual_NIT for compliance).
