- This two-stage interleaver is critical for frequency-selective fading environments and is followed by constellation mapping (QPSK, 16-QAM, or 64-QAM) (1 mark).

---

#### ✅ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **OFDM frame structure** of a DVB-T signal, including the role of **guard intervals, pilots, and TPS**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DVB-T system operates in the **8K mode** with **64-QAM** constellation, inner code rate **2/3**, and **guard interval 1/32** in an **8 MHz channel**. The number of data carriers is **6048**. Calculate the **net bit rate**. Use $\eta_{RS} = 188/204$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OFDM Frame Structure in DVB-T (7 marks)**

- A DVB-T **super-frame** consists of **4 OFDM frames**, and each **OFDM frame** contains **68 OFDM symbols** (1 mark).
- **Symbol 0** of each frame is the **TPS (Transmission Parameter Signalling) pilot symbol**. It carries signalling bits (constellation, code rate, GI, 2K/8K mode) encoded with BPSK and protected by a $(32, 26)$ Reed-Muller code. The TPS is robustly decodable even when data is corrupted (2 marks).
- **Symbols 1 to 67** carry data and **scattered / continual pilots**. Continual pilots (45 in 2K, 177 in 8K) are fixed in position; scattered pilots (45 in 2K, 177 in 8K) appear every 12 carriers. Pilots total ~10% of carriers and enable **channel estimation and tracking** at the receiver (2 marks).
- Each OFDM symbol begins with a **Guard Interval (GI)** — a cyclic prefix of length $T_g = \Delta \cdot T_u$, where $\Delta \in \{1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32\}$ (1 mark).
- The GI absorbs multipath echoes up to its duration without causing inter-symbol or inter-carrier interference, converting linear channel convolution into circular convolution (1 mark).

---

**(b) Net Bit Rate Calculation (7 marks)**

Given: $N_{FFT} = 8192$, $M = 64$, $r_c = 2/3$, $\Delta = 1/32$, $B = 8$ MHz, $N_{data} = 6048$.

**Step 1 — Elementary period:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6} = 0.125\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Useful OFDM symbol duration:**

$$
T_u = 8192 \times 0.125\,\mu s = 1024\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Guard interval duration:**

$$
T_g = \frac{T_u}{32} = 32\,\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Total OFDM symbol duration:**

$$
T_s = T_u + T_g = 1056\,\mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Bits per constellation symbol:**

$$
\log_2 64 = 6
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the net bit rate formula:**

$$
R_{net} = \frac{N_{data} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot r_c \cdot \eta_{RS}}{T_s}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
R_{net} = \frac{6048 \times 6 \times \tfrac{2}{3} \times \tfrac{188}{204}}{1056 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{net} = \frac{6048 \times 6 \times \tfrac{2}{3} \times 0.92157}{1.056 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
R_{net} = \frac{22295.05}{1.056 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{net} \approx 21.11\,\text{Mbps}}
$$

*(Full 7 marks are awarded as follows: 1 mark for $T_u$, 1 mark for $T_s$, 1 mark for $\log_2 M$, 2 marks for substituting the formula correctly, and 2 marks for the final numerical answer with correct units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the **Reed-Solomon efficiency factor $\eta_{RS} = 188/204$** — this is a 1–2 mark loss. Always include both the **inner code rate** AND the **outer RS efficiency** in net bit rate calculations.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the **useful symbol duration $T_u$** with the **total symbol duration $T_s$**. The OFDM symbol duration used in the denominator is $T_s = T_u(1 + \Delta)$, **not** $T_u$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** For **8K mode**, students often use $N_{FFT} = 8192$ (the IFFT size) when asked for the **number of data carriers**. The correct value is $N_{data} = 6048$. Always quote the *active data carriers* — not the raw FFT size.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not writing units in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. Always state **Mbps** or **kbps** explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting that **PRBS scrambling excludes the MPEG sync byte** and that **only the first sync byte of every 8 packets is inverted** to 0xB8 (MIP framing).

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DVB-T** = Terrestrial DVB standard (ETSI EN 300 744) using **COFDM** with MPEG-2 TS input.
- **Modulator chain (memorize in this order):** TS Input → Sync inversion + PRBS → **RS(204,188,t=8)** → Convolutional interleaver (depth 12) → **Inner Convolutional K=7 rate 1/2** + Puncturing → Bit Interleaver → Symbol Interleaver → **QPSK/16-QAM/64-QAM** mapping → **2K or 8K IFFT** → **Guard Interval insertion** → DAC → RF upconversion.
- **Reed-Solomon** corrects up to **8 byte errors** per 204-byte coded block; efficiency = **188/204 ≈ 0.9216**.
- **Convolutional mother code**: K = 7, G₁ = 171ₒ, G₂ = 133ₒ, rate 1/2; puncturing gives 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8.
- **PRBS polynomial:** $G(x) = 1 + x^{14} + x^{15}$.
- **Galois Field primitive:** $p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ for RS.
- **2K mode:** 1705 carriers, T_u = 224 µs, Δf = 4464 Hz → robust against short multipath.
- **8K mode:** 6817 carriers (6048 data), T_u = 896 µs (in 8 MHz), Δf = 1116 Hz → robust against Doppler.
- **Guard Interval ratios:** 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 — convert linear channel to circular convolution.
- **OFDM frame:** 68 symbols, 1 TPS pilot + scattered/continual pilots (~10% of carriers), 4 frames per super-frame.
- **Net bit rate formula:** $R_{net} = N_{data} \cdot \log_2 M \cdot r_c \cdot \eta_{RS} / T_u(1 + \Delta)$.
- **Maximum net bit rate** ≈ **31.67 Mbps** (8K, 64-QAM, 7/8, 1/32 GI).
- **Minimum net bit rate** ≈ **4.98 Mbps** (2K, QPSK, 1/2, 1/4 GI).
- **Trade-off triplet:** Higher constellation order = ↑ bit rate = ↑ CNR requirement. Higher code rate = ↑ bit rate = ↓ error correction. Longer GI = ↓ bit rate = ↑ multipath tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB-T Carriers and System Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB-T Carriers and System Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is DVB-T?

**DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting - Terrestrial)** is the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) standard (EN 300 744) for the broadcast transmission of digital terrestrial television. It was developed by the DVB Project consortium and is the dominant terrestrial TV standard across Europe, Asia-Pacific, and many other regions.

DVB-T uses **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)** to transmit a compressed digital audio/video stream over a terrestrial (over-the-air) channel. The system is designed to cope with the severe multipath propagation and Doppler effects common in terrestrial broadcasting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DVB-T is a cornerstone topic in Entertainment Electronics. Students MUST understand the OFDM carrier architecture (2K vs 8K modes), the role of pilot carriers, guard interval insertion, and the trade-off between robustness and data throughput.

## 1.2 The Concept of Carriers in DVB-T

In a conventional single-carrier system, the entire information bandwidth is transmitted on **one radio frequency carrier** using high symbol rates. In DVB-T, the wideband channel is divided into **thousands of narrowband subcarriers**, each carrying a small slice of the total data. This is the essence of **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)**.

The DVB-T specification defines two operating modes:
- **2K mode** — uses **2048-point FFT** with **1705 active carriers** (suitable for single-frequency networks and mobile reception).
- **8K mode** — uses **8192-point FFT** with **6817 active carriers** (suitable for large-scale single-frequency networks and high-capacity fixed reception).

> [!NOTE]
> The term "2K" and "8K" refer to the *FFT size*, not the number of *active* carriers. Always remember: **Active carriers < FFT size** because the outer carriers are disabled to relax the anti-alias filter requirements.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Lane Highway

Imagine a single-lane road where one huge truck carries 100 passengers (single-carrier system). If the truck hits a pothole, **all 100 passengers are lost**. Traffic flow is also slow because the truck is enormous.

Now picture a **multi-lane highway with 6000 small cars**, each carrying just 1 passenger (DVB-T 8K mode). If a pothole damages one lane, only **one or two passengers are lost** — the rest arrive safely. The cars are *orthogonal* — they travel at mathematically precise, slightly different speeds so their waveforms never collide, yet they pack tightly into the spectrum. This is exactly what DVB-T does with its subcarriers.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Orthogonality Matters:** Subcarriers in DVB-T are spaced such that the peak of one subcarrier's spectrum coincides exactly with the **null** of every other subcarrier. This allows their spectra to overlap without mutual interference, dramatically improving spectral efficiency.

## 1.4 Classification of Carriers in DVB-T

The 1705 (2K) or 6817 (8K) active carriers are divided into four functional categories:

| Carrier Type | Purpose | 2K Mode Count | 8K Mode Count |
|--------------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| **Useful Data Carriers** | Carry MPEG-2 TS payload | **1512** | **6048** |
| **Scattered Pilots (SP)** | Channel estimation & equalization | 524 | 1768 |
| **Continual Pilots (CP)** | Synchronization, frequency offset | 45 | 177 |
| **TPS Carriers** | Transmission Parameter Signaling | 17 | 68 |
| **Null Carriers** | Guard band at spectrum edges | (FFT−active) | (FFT−active) |

> [!NOTE]
> **TPS (Transmission Parameter Signaling)** carriers convey critical receiver-decoding information: modulation scheme, code rate, guard interval, transmission mode, and cell identifier for single-frequency networks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OFDM Mathematical Foundation

OFDM is a multicarrier modulation scheme where a single high-rate data stream is split into N low-rate substreams, each modulating one of N orthogonal subcarriers. In DVB-T, this is implemented efficiently using an **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT)** at the transmitter and an **FFT** at the receiver.

### The Orthogonality Condition

For two subcarriers $f_m$ and $f_n$ to be orthogonal over a useful symbol period $T_u$, they must satisfy:

$$\int_0^{T_u} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_n t)\,dt = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad m \neq n$$

This is satisfied when the carrier spacing equals the reciprocal of the useful symbol duration:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ is the **backbone of the entire DVB-T carrier architecture**. Every parameter in the system derives from it.

## 2.2 The DVB-T Frame and Symbol Hierarchy

The DVB-T transmission is organized in a **four-level time hierarchy**:

1. **Elementary time unit (T)** — derived from the 8 MHz channel reference: $T = 7/64\,\mu s \approx 109.4\,\text{ns}$.
2. **Useful symbol duration ($T_u$)** — equal to 2048T (2K) or 8192T (8K).
3. **Guard interval duration ($\Delta$)** — a cyclic prefix copy of the end of the symbol, occupying a fraction $1/4, 1/8, 1/16,$ or $1/32$ of $T_u$.
4. **Total symbol duration ($T_s$)** — $T_s = T_u + \Delta$.
5. **Frame** — 68 OFDM symbols, each preceded by a TPS sync pulse.

The transmission frame structure (4 levels):
- **Super-frame** = 4 frames
- **Frame** = 68 OFDM symbols
- **Symbol** = $T_u + \Delta$
- **Slot** (in time interleaving) = 1 OFDM symbol

## 2.3 Why Guard Intervals Exist — Combating Multipath

In terrestrial broadcasting, the transmitted signal reaches the receiver via **multiple paths** (direct, reflected from buildings, hills, etc.). These delayed copies cause **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** between consecutive OFDM symbols.

DVB-T solves this by copying the **last $\Delta$ portion of the useful symbol** and prepending it to the symbol — this is the **Cyclic Prefix (CP)** or **Guard Interval (GI)**.

$$\text{Guard Interval } \Delta = \frac{T_u}{4}, \frac{T_u}{8}, \frac{T_u}{16}, \text{or } \frac{T_u}{32}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A larger guard interval offers more robustness against echoes (up to $\sim 224\,\mu s$ for 8K, 1/4) but wastes bandwidth. For high-capacity fixed-reception SFNs, a short guard (1/32) is preferred. For single-transmitter or mobile reception, a longer guard (1/4) is chosen.

## 2.4 Pilot Carriers — The Receiver's Lifeline

DVB-T inserts **known reference symbols** (pilots) that allow the receiver to:
- Estimate the **channel frequency response** $H(f)$.
- Correct **frequency and timing offsets**.
- Track **time-varying channels** (especially in mobile reception).
- Recover the **TPS information** for demodulation.

| Pilot Type | Distribution | Amplitude Boost |
|------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Continual Pilots** | Fixed carrier indices in every symbol | $4/3$ relative to data |
| **Scattered Pilots** | Every 12th carrier, rotated symbol-to-symbol | $4/3$ relative to data |
| **TPS Pilots** | 17 (2K) or 68 (8K) fixed positions per symbol | $1$ (same as data) |

## 2.5 System Parameters of DVB-T

The DVB-T standard allows flexible configuration to suit different deployment scenarios:

| Parameter | Allowed Values |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Channel Bandwidth (BW)** | **6, 7, or 8 MHz** |
| **FFT Size (Mode)** | **2K (2048) or 8K (8192)** |
| **Modulation** | **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** |
| **Inner Code Rate** | **1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8** |
| **Guard Interval Fraction** | **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** |
| **Hierarchy Mode** | Non-hierarchical or Hierarchical (α = 1, 2, 4) |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — every KTU numerical problem on DVB-T reduces to one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ | Carrier spacing |
| 2 | $T_u = 2048T$ (2K), $T_u = 8192T$ (8K) | Useful symbol duration |
| 3 | $T = 7/64\,\mu s$ (8 MHz BW) | Elementary time unit (8 MHz channel) |
| 4 | $T_g = \Delta = T_u/4,\,1/8,\,1/16,\,1/32$ | Guard interval duration |
| 5 | $T_s = T_u + T_g = T_u(1 + \Delta/T_u)$ | Total symbol duration |
| 6 | $R_{\text{net}} = (N_d \cdot b \cdot R_c)/T_s$ | Net useful bit rate |
| 7 | $N_d = 1512$ (2K), $6048$ (8K) | Number of useful data carriers |
| 8 | $b = 2$ (QPSK), $4$ (16-QAM), $6$ (64-QAM) | Bits per symbol per carrier |
| 9 | $R_c = 1/2,\,2/3,\,3/4,\,5/6,\,7/8$ | Inner convolutional code rate |
| 10 | $f_{\text{spacing}} = 4464\,\text{Hz}$ (2K), $1116\,\text{Hz}$ (8K) | Carrier spacing (8 MHz BW) |
| 11 | $B_{\text{total}} = 1705\Delta f$ (2K), $6817\Delta f$ (8K) | Approx. occupied RF bandwidth |
| 12 | $N_{\text{pilots}} = 524+45+17 = 586$ (2K) | 2K total non-data carriers |
| 13 | $N_{\text{pilots}} = 1768+177+68 = 2013$ (8K) | 8K total non-data carriers |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These parameters allow network operators to dynamically trade robustness for throughput. A rural SFN might use 64-QAM with rate 7/8 and 1/32 guard (~30 Mbps), while a portable-reception urban service might use QPSK with rate 1/2 and 1/4 guard (~5 Mbps).

## 2.7 Practical Data-Rate Envelope (8 MHz, 8K mode)

| Modulation × Code Rate | Net Bit Rate (no GI) | With GI = 1/4 |
|------------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| QPSK, 1/2 | 9.4 Mbps | 7.5 Mbps |
| 16-QAM, 1/2 | 18.8 Mbps | 15.0 Mbps |
| 16-QAM, 3/4 | 28.1 Mbps | 22.5 Mbps |
| 64-QAM, 2/3 | 37.6 Mbps | 30.1 Mbps |
| 64-QAM, 3/4 | 42.3 Mbps | **33.8 Mbps** |
| 64-QAM, 7/8 | 49.4 Mbps | 39.5 Mbps |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carrier Spacing in the 8 MHz / 8K Mode

The DVB-T specification fixes the elementary time unit as $T = 7/64\,\mu s$ for an 8 MHz channel.

**Step 1 — Establish the elementary period.**
For an 8 MHz channel, the sampling clock is:
$$T = \frac{7}{64}\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the useful symbol duration for 8K mode.**
The 8K FFT uses 8192 samples per useful OFDM symbol:
$$T_u = 8192 \times T = 8192 \times \frac{7}{64}\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the multiplication.**
$$T_u = \frac{8192 \times 7}{64}\,\mu s = \frac{57344}{64}\,\mu s$$

$$T_u = 896\,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Apply the orthogonality relation to find carrier spacing.**
$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{896 \times 10^{-6}}\,\text{Hz}$$

$$\Delta f = 1116.07\,\text{Hz} \approx 1116\,\text{Hz}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Carrier Spacing in the 2K Mode (8 MHz Channel)

**Step 1 — Useful symbol duration for 2K FFT (2048 samples).**
$$T_u = 2048 \times \frac{7}{64}\,\mu s = \frac{2048 \times 7}{64}\,\mu s$$

$$T_u = \frac{14336}{64}\,\mu s = 224\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Carrier spacing.**
$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{224 \times 10^{-6}} = 4464.29\,\text{Hz} \approx 4464\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the 4:1 ratio.**
$$\frac{\Delta f_{2K}}{\Delta f_{8K}} = \frac{4464}{1116} = 4$$

The 2K mode has 4× the carrier spacing of the 8K mode, which is why the 2K mode is more robust against Doppler (mobile reception) but occupies wider individual channels and supports smaller SFNs.

## 3.3 Derivation: Net Useful Bit Rate

The most frequently asked **KTU 14-mark numerical problem** is the calculation of the net bit rate.

$$R_{\text{net}} = \frac{N_d \times b \times R_c}{T_s}$$

**Where:**
- $N_d$ = number of useful data carriers (1512 or 6048)
- $b$ = bits per subcarrier (2, 4, or 6)
- $R_c$ = convolutional code rate
- $T_s = T_u(1 + \Delta/T_u)$ = total symbol duration

### Worked Example: 8K mode, 64-QAM, R = 3/4, GI = 1/4, 8 MHz channel

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
$$N_d = 6048, \quad b = 6, \quad R_c = 0.75$$
$$T_u = 896\,\mu s, \quad \Delta/T_u = 1/4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total symbol duration.**
$$T_s = T_u\left(1 + \frac{1}{4}\right) = 896 \times 1.25 = 1120\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Multiply data-carrier bits per symbol.**
$$N_d \times b \times R_c = 6048 \times 6 \times 0.75$$
$$= 6048 \times 4.5 = 27{,}216 \text{ bits per OFDM symbol}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the total symbol duration to get bit rate.**
$$R_{\text{net}} = \frac{27{,}216}{1120 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{\text{net}} = 24.3 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bps} = \mathbf{24.3\,Mbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 24.3 Mbps (after MPEG-2 TS overhead and RS coding) is the classical "DVB-T sweet spot" used in many European digital switchover deployments for SD MPEG-2 broadcasting.

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Bit Rate (64-QAM, R=7/8, GI=1/32, 8K)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
$$N_d = 6048, \quad b = 6, \quad R_c = 7/8 = 0.875$$
$$T_u = 896\,\mu s, \quad \Delta/T_u = 1/32$$

**Step 2 — Total symbol duration.**
$$T_s = 896 \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{32}\right) = 896 \times \frac{33}{32} = 924\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Bits per OFDM symbol (pre-rate).**
$$N_d \times b = 6048 \times 6 = 36{,}288 \text{ raw bits/symbol}$$

**Step 4 — Apply coding rate.**
$$36{,}288 \times 0.875 = 31{,}752 \text{ useful bits/symbol}$$

**Step 5 — Bit rate.**
$$R_{\text{net}} = \frac{31{,}752}{924 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 34.36\,\text{Mbps}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Robustness vs. Capacity Trade-off

| Configuration | Robustness | Capacity | Typical Use Case |
|---------------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| QPSK, R=1/2, GI=1/4 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ~5 Mbps | Portable indoor / mobile |
| 16-QAM, R=1/2, GI=1/8 | ⭐⭐⭐ | ~12 Mbps | Moderate SFN, fixed rooftop |
| 64-QAM, R=2/3, GI=1/8 | ⭐⭐ | ~25 Mbps | Standard SDTV multiplex |
| 64-QAM, R=7/8, GI=1/32 | ⭐ | ~31 Mbps | High-capacity dense SFN |

## 3.6 Quick Formula: Spectral Efficiency

The spectral efficiency $\eta$ (bits/sec/Hz) of a DVB-T configuration is:

$$\eta = \frac{R_{\text{net}}}{B_{\text{channel}}}$$

**Example:** For $R_{\text{net}} = 24.3$ Mbps over $B = 8$ MHz:
$$\eta = \frac{24.3}{8} = 3.04\,\text{bits/sec/Hz}$$

This is a strong figure for a 1990s standard and reflects the well-engineered OFDM design.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DVB-T Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[MPEG-2 TS Source] --> mux[Transport Stream Multiplexer]
    src2[Audio ES] --> mux
    src3[Video ES] --> mux
    mux --> sync[Sync Byte Randomization]
    sync --> rsouter[Reed-Solomon Outer Coding 204,188]
    rsouter --> conv[Convolutional Interleaver]
    conv --> inner[Inner Convolutional Encoder R=1/2]
    inner --> punct[Puncturing R=1/2 to 7/8]
    punct --> bitint[Inner Bit Interleaver]
    bitint --> map[Symbol Mapper QPSK 16QAM 64QAM]
    map --> fmint[Inner Frequency Interleaver]
    fmint --> ifft[OFDM Frame Builder and IFFT 2K or 8K]
    ifft --> gi[Insert Guard Interval Cyclic Prefix]
    gi --> dac[DAC and Upconversion]
    dac --> ant[Terrestrial RF Channel 8 MHz]
    pilot[Pilots and TPS Insertion] -.-> ifft
    tps[TPS Signaling] -.-> pilot
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left side handles **MPEG-2 source coding and multiplexing**.
- The center performs **channel coding** (Reed-Solomon + Convolutional).
- The right side implements **OFDM modulation** via IFFT.
- The dotted lines show the **pilot and TPS insertion paths** that feed the IFFT block.

## 4.2 DVB-T Carrier Allocation Map (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Edge[Lower Frequency Edge]
        n0[Null Guard Band ~343 carriers]
    end
    subgraph Core[Active Bandwidth]
        cp1[Continual Pilots 177 carriers]
        sp1[Scattered Pilots 1768 carriers]
        data1[Useful Data Carriers 6048 carriers]
        tps1[TPS Carriers 68 carriers]
        sp2[Scattered Pilots 1768 carriers]
        cp2[Continual Pilots 177 carriers]
    end
    subgraph Edge2[Upper Frequency Edge]
        n1[Null Guard Band ~343 carriers]
    end
    n0 --> cp1 --> sp1 --> data1 --> tps1 --> sp2 --> cp2 --> n1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This 4-tier structure (Null → Continual → Scattered → Data → TPS) is **not physical spectrum** but a **logical mapping** of carrier indices $k = 0, 1, ..., 8191$. The IFFT sorts them onto orthogonal subcarriers spanning 7.61 MHz of the 8 MHz channel.

## 4.3 DVB-T Time Hierarchy (Frame Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sf[Super Frame 4 Frames] --> f1[Frame 1]
    sf --> f2[Frame 2]
    sf --> f3[Frame 3]
    sf --> f4[Frame 4]
    f1 --> s1[OFDM Symbol 1]
    f1 --> s2[OFDM Symbol 2]
    f1 --> s3[...]
    f1 --> s68[OFDM Symbol 68]
    s1 --> tu[Useful Part T_u 896 us in 8K]
    s1 --> gi[Guard Interval Delta 1/4 to 1/32 of T_u]
    tu -.-> copy[Copy Last Delta of Symbol]
    copy --> gi
```

**Hierarchy summary (8 MHz channel):**
- 1 super-frame = 4 frames
- 1 frame = 68 OFDM symbols
- 1 OFDM symbol = $T_u$ + $\Delta$ = 896 μs (1/4 GI: 1120 μs) up to 924 μs (1/32 GI)
- 1 elementary period $T$ = 7/64 μs ≈ 109.4 ns

## 4.4 Functional Role of Each Carrier Type

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    a[Active Carriers 6817 in 8K] --> b1[Data 6048]
    a --> b2[TPS 68]
    a --> b3[Continual Pilots 177]
    a --> b4[Scattered Pilots 1768]
    b1 --> r1[Carry MPEG-2 Transport Stream Payload]
    b2 --> r2[Signal Modulation, Code Rate, GI, Cell ID]
    b3 --> r3[Coarse Sync and Frequency Offset Correction]
    b4 --> r4[Channel Estimation, Equalization, Time Tracking]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of scattered and continual pilot carriers in DVB-T. Why are their amplitudes boosted to 4/3 of the data carriers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Scattered pilots (1 mark):** Distributed every 12th carrier and rotated symbol-to-symbol, they enable the receiver to estimate the time-varying **channel frequency response** $H(f,k)$ via 2-D interpolation. They support both frequency-domain equalization and time-domain tracking, essential for mobile reception.
- **Continual pilots (1 mark):** Fixed at specific carrier indices in every OFDM symbol. They provide a robust reference for **synchronization, coarse frequency-offset estimation, and phase-noise tracking** even when scattered pilots are momentarily faded.
- **Amplitude boost (1 mark):** Their amplitude is raised by a factor of $4/3$ (i.e., boosted power) so that the **mean pilot power equals the mean data power**. This equalization prevents the pilots from artificially raising the PAPR and ensures consistent SNR estimation across the channel.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between 2K and 8K transmission modes in DVB-T. Which mode is preferred for a large single-frequency network (SFN) and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | 2K Mode | 8K Mode |
|---------|---------|---------|
| FFT size | 2048 | 8192 |
| Active carriers | 1705 | 6817 |
| Useful data carriers | 1512 | 6048 |
| Carrier spacing | 4464 Hz | 1116 Hz |
| Useful symbol duration $T_u$ | 224 μs | 896 μs |
| Max echo tolerance (1/4 GI) | 56 μs | 224 μs |
| Doppler robustness | Higher (wider spacing) | Lower (narrower spacing) |

**Preferred for large SFN (1.5 marks):** The **8K mode** is preferred for large single-frequency networks because the **carrier spacing is narrower (1116 Hz)** and the **useful symbol duration is longer (896 μs)**, allowing a much **larger guard interval (up to 224 μs with 1/4 GI)**. This tolerates the long differential delays between geographically separated transmitters in the SFN. The trade-off is reduced Doppler robustness, but this is acceptable since large SFNs primarily serve **fixed rooftop reception**, not mobile terminals.

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is split into sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. The student answers one of two alternatives (Or option).

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - July 2024 Model]

#### (a) With the help of a block diagram, describe the OFDM-based transmitter architecture of a DVB-T system. Explain the function of each block. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The DVB-T transmitter implements a **Coded OFDM (COFDM)** chain with five major functional stages:

1. **Transport Stream Adaptation & Randomization (1 mark):**
   - The incoming MPEG-2 transport stream is randomized using a Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS) to ensure a balanced bit distribution, providing a uniform spectrum and a stable timing-recovery signal at the receiver.

2. **Outer Coding & Interleaving (1 mark):**
   - **Reed-Solomon RS(204,188)** code adds 16 parity bytes per packet to correct burst errors caused by impulsive noise.
   - A **Forney convolutional interleaver** of depth 12 disperses burst errors so the inner decoder sees a more randomized error pattern.

3. **Inner Coding — Convolutional Encoder (1.5 marks):**
   - A **mother convolutional code of rate 1/2** (constraint length 7, generators 171, 133 in octal) is applied.
   - **Puncturing** removes selected bits to obtain effective rates of 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, and 7/8, trading error protection for throughput.
   - The convolutional decoder (typically Viterbi) at the receiver corrects random errors introduced by Gaussian/mobile channel impairments.

4. **Inner Interleaving & Constellation Mapping (1.5 marks):**
   - **Bit interleaving** distributes consecutive bits across many carriers, protecting against frequency-selective fading.
   - The mapper assigns groups of 2, 4, or 6 bits to **QPSK, 16-QAM, or 64-QAM** constellations. Higher constellations require higher C/N but triple the bit density.

5. **OFDM Frame Assembly & Modulation (2 marks):**
   - **Pilots and TPS carriers** are inserted at predefined positions.
   - A **frequency interleaver** permutes carrier indices to randomize the effect of narrowband interferers.
   - An **IFFT (2K or 8K)** generates the time-domain OFDM signal.
   - The **guard interval (cyclic prefix)** is prepended to each symbol.
   - A **DAC and RF upconverter** translate the baseband signal to the assigned UHF/VHF channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-diagram marks (extra 1 mark in part a):** The student must draw the complete functional chain. Drawing arrows in the wrong direction or omitting the IFFT block typically costs 1–2 marks.

#### (b) For an 8K DVB-T system operating in an 8 MHz channel with 64-QAM modulation, code rate 2/3, and a guard interval of 1/8, calculate: (i) useful symbol duration, (ii) carrier spacing, (iii) total symbol duration, and (iv) net useful bit rate. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Channel bandwidth = 8 MHz
- Mode = 8K → FFT size = 8192, $N_d$ (useful carriers) = 6048
- Modulation = 64-QAM → $b$ = 6 bits/symbol
- Code rate = 2/3 → $R_c$ = 0.6667
- Guard interval = 1/8

**Step 1 — Elementary period (8 MHz channel):**

$$T = \frac{7}{64}\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Useful symbol duration (i):**

$$T_u = 8192 \times T = 8192 \times \frac{7}{64}\,\mu s$$

$$T_u = \frac{57344}{64}\,\mu s = 896\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Carrier spacing (ii):**

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{896 \times 10^{-6}} = 1116.07\,\text{Hz} \approx 1116\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Total symbol duration (iii):**

$$\Delta = \frac{T_u}{8} = \frac{896}{8} = 112\,\mu s$$

$$T_s = T_u + \Delta = 896 + 112 = 1008\,\mu s$$

**Step 5 — Net useful bit rate (iv):**

$$R_{\text{net}} = \frac{N_d \times b \times R_c}{T_s} = \frac{6048 \times 6 \times \tfrac{2}{3}}{1008 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{6048 \times 4}{1008 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{24{,}192}{1008 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{\text{net}} = 24.0 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bps} = \mathbf{24.0\,Mbps}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common error:** Confusing **active carriers (6817)** with **useful data carriers (6048)**. Always subtract the 769 non-data carriers (pilots + TPS). A full mark is deducted if you use 6817 instead of 6048.
> 2. **Second most common error:** Using $T_s = T_u$ instead of $T_u + \Delta$. Forgetting the guard interval inflates the answer by the GI fraction (e.g., 12.5% for 1/8).
> 3. **Third most common error:** Mixing up $b$ for different modulations — 16-QAM has 4, not 16, bits per symbol.
> 4. **Show units in the final answer.** Writing just "24" without "Mbps" loses the unit mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain the function of the guard interval (cyclic prefix) in DVB-T. Discuss the four allowed fractions and the engineering trade-off involved. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**What is the guard interval? (1 mark)**
The guard interval (GI), also called the **cyclic prefix (CP)**, is a copy of the **last $\Delta$ portion** of the useful OFDM symbol that is **prepended** to the symbol before transmission. The total transmitted symbol duration is therefore $T_s = T_u + \Delta$.

**Why it is needed (3 marks):**
- In terrestrial broadcasting, the receiver sees the transmitted signal via **multiple propagation paths** (direct, reflected from buildings, terrain, etc.).
- These multipath echoes cause the received signal to be a sum of delayed copies of the transmitted symbol.
- Without GI, the tail of one symbol overlaps with the beginning of the next — this is **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**, which destroys orthogonality and produces **Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI)** between subcarriers.
- By inserting a cyclic prefix of duration $\Delta \geq \tau_{\max}$ (the maximum excess delay of the channel), the receiver can discard the corrupted prefix and perform the FFT over the **unaffected useful portion** $T_u$, preserving orthogonality.

**Mathematical justification (1 mark):**
If the maximum multipath delay spread is $\tau_{\max}$, the ISI-free condition requires:
$$\Delta \geq \tau_{\max}$$
For 8K mode, 1/4 GI: $\Delta = 224\,\mu s$ — sufficient for SFNs spanning up to ~67 km of differential path.

**The four allowed fractions and trade-offs (2 marks):**

| GI Fraction | $\Delta$ (8K, 8 MHz) | Max Echo | Spectral Loss | Best For |
|-------------|---------------------|----------|---------------|----------|
| **1/4** | 224 μs | 67 km | 20% | Long SFNs, portable |
| **1/8** | 112 μs | 34 km | 11.1% | Moderate SFNs |
| **1/16** | 56 μs | 17 km | 5.9% | Single transmitter, robust |
| **1/32** | 28 μs | 8.4 km | 3.0% | High-capacity dense SFN |

**Engineering trade-off:** A larger GI tolerates longer echoes (more robust) but wastes bandwidth, reducing the net bit rate. A smaller GI is spectrally efficient but requires a controlled multipath environment.

#### (b) A DVB-T system uses 2K mode in a 6 MHz channel with 16-QAM, code rate 3/4, and guard interval 1/16. Calculate the net useful bit rate. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Channel BW = 6 MHz
- Mode = 2K → FFT = 2048, $N_d$ = 1512
- Modulation = 16-QAM → $b$ = 4 bits
- Code rate = 3/4 → $R_c$ = 0.75
- Guard interval = 1/16

**Step 1 — Elementary period for a 6 MHz channel.**

For a 6 MHz channel, the elementary period is scaled by 4/3 (since 8 MHz × 6/8 = 6 MHz):
$$T = \frac{7}{48}\,\mu s \approx 145.83\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 2 — Useful symbol duration.**

$$T_u = 2048 \times T = 2048 \times \frac{7}{48}\,\mu s = \frac{14{,}336}{48}\,\mu s$$

$$T_u = 298.67\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Carrier spacing.**

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{298.67 \times 10^{-6}} = 3348.07\,\text{Hz} \approx 3348\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Total symbol duration.**

$$\Delta = \frac{T_u}{16} = \frac{298.67}{16} = 18.67\,\mu s$$

$$T_s = T_u + \Delta = 298.67 + 18.67 = 317.33\,\mu s$$

**Step 5 — Net useful bit rate.**

$$R_{\text{net}} = \frac{N_d \times b \times R_c}{T_s} = \frac{1512 \times 4 \times 0.75}{317.33 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{4536}{317.33 \times 10^{-6}} = 14.29 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bps}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{net}} \approx 14.3\,\text{Mbps}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes in 6 MHz problems:**
> 1. Using $T = 7/64\,\mu s$ (which is for 8 MHz) instead of $7/48\,\mu s$ — this is the most common error and gives a wrong $T_u$ that is 4/3 of the correct value.
> 2. Forgetting that in 2K mode the **useful carriers are 1512, not 1705**. The 1705 includes continual pilots (45), scattered pilots (131), and TPS (17).
> 3. Using 8K parameters (8192, 6048) in a 2K problem — always re-read the problem carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for DVB-T (Module 2):**

- ✅ **DVB-T = Coded OFDM** (COFDM) for terrestrial digital TV; standard is ETSI EN 300 744.
- ✅ **Two FFT modes:** 2K (2048 points, 1705 active) and 8K (8192 points, 6817 active).
- ✅ **Useful data carriers:** 1512 (2K) and **6048 (8K)** — these are the ONLY ones that carry payload.
- ✅ **Orthogonality condition:** $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ — the fundamental relationship.
- ✅ **For 8 MHz channel:** $T_u = 224\,\mu s$ (2K) and $896\,\mu s$ (8K); $\Delta f = 4464\,\text{Hz}$ (2K) and $1116\,\text{Hz}$ (8K).
- ✅ **Elementary period for 8 MHz:** $T = 7/64\,\mu s \approx 109.4\,\text{ns}$.
- ✅ **Guard interval fractions:** 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 — cyclic prefix of the last $\Delta$ of the symbol.
- ✅ **Total symbol duration:** $T_s = T_u(1 + \Delta/T_u)$.
- ✅ **Pilot types:** Continual (fixed position, every symbol), Scattered (every 12th carrier, rotated), TPS (carry system config).
- ✅ **Pilot amplitude boost:** $4/3$ of data amplitude → equal mean power, no PAPR penalty.
- ✅ **Modulation options:** QPSK (2 bps), 16-QAM (4 bps), 64-QAM (6 bps) per carrier.
- ✅ **Code rates:** 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8 from a rate-1/2 mother code by puncturing.
- ✅ **Net bit rate formula:** $R_{\text{net}} = N_d \cdot b \cdot R_c / T_s$.
- ✅ **Practical rate range:** ~5 Mbps (QPSK, robust) to ~31 Mbps (64-QAM, 1/32 GI, 7/8).
- ✅ **2K preferred for:** mobile, single-tx, small SFN; **8K preferred for:** large SFN, fixed rooftop.
- ✅ **TPS carriers** transmit: modulation, code rate, GI, mode, frame number, cell ID.
- ✅ **DVB-T supports hierarchical modulation** with α = 1, 2, or 4 (QPSK stream embedded in 16/64-QAM).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check Formula:**
> $$\boxed{\text{Throughput (Mbps)} \approx \frac{[\text{Useful Carriers}] \times [\text{Bits/Hz symbol}] \times [\text{Code Rate}]}{[\text{Total Symbol Duration in }\mu s]} \times 10^{-3}}$$
> Memorize this single expression — it solves 90% of KTU numerical questions on DVB-T.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB-T Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB-T Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 DVB-T Receiver — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting — Terrestrial)** is the **ETSI EN 300 744** standard adopted by the European DVB consortium for the **broadcast transmission of digital terrestrial television**. A **DVB-T Receiver** is the terminal hardware/software chain that captures the RF signal from the antenna, performs **COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** demodulation, and delivers a reconstructed **MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS)** containing compressed audio, video, and service data.

The receiver essentially performs the **inverse of every operation** carried out at the transmitter: it removes the **outer Forward Error Correction (FEC)**, the **inner FEC**, the **interleaving**, the **OFDM modulation**, and finally outputs a baseband bitstream suitable for an **MPEG-2 decoder** and display device.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Reverse Mail Sorting Office"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a post office that has bundled **1,000 letters from 1,000 different countries**, stamped them all with the same postcode, mixed them inside a single huge container, and shipped it across the ocean. The DVB-T receiver acts like the **reverse sorting office**:
> 1. The **container arrives jumbled** (RF with multipath/noise) → **Tuner + AGC**.
> 2. The **container must be opened and every letter pulled out** → **OFDM FFT Demodulation**.
> 3. **Some letters are smudged and need error correction** → **Inner (Viterbi) + Outer (Reed-Solomon) Decoding**.
> 4. The **letters are re-sorted by destination address** → **Transport Stream De-multiplexing**.
> 5. The final **sorted pile is delivered to the postman** → **MPEG-2 Decoder → TV**.

The receiver must therefore be a **multi-stage, real-time DSP pipeline**, typically implemented on a **single-chip COFDM demodulator** (e.g., NXP TDA10048, Panasonic MN88472, Intel CE6353).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Channel Bandwidths:** **6 MHz, 7 MHz, 8 MHz** (region dependent — Kerala/India uses 8 MHz UHF).
- **FFT Modes:** **2K mode (1705 active carriers)** and **8K mode (6817 active carriers)**.
- **Modulation Schemes per Carrier:** **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM**.
- **Inner FEC Code Rates:** **1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8** (punctured convolutional coding).
- **Outer FEC:** **Reed-Solomon (204, 188, t = 8)** over GF(256).
- **Guard Interval (GI) fractions:** **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** of useful symbol duration.
- **Net Bit Rate (useful payload):** ranges from **~4.98 Mbit/s (most robust)** to **~31.67 Mbit/s (highest capacity)** for an **8 MHz** channel.
- **Useful OFDM Symbol Duration (Tu):** **2K → 224 µs**, **8K → 896 µs** (in 8 MHz).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** The 2K mode gives **4× shorter** symbol duration than 8K because carrier spacing is 4× wider. This is why 2K tolerates **lower delay spreads** (mobile), while 8K tolerates **larger SFN cells** (fixed rooftop reception).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block-Logic of the DVB-T Receiver

The receiver pipeline is logically divided into **three domains**: the **RF/IF analog front-end**, the **digital baseband demodulation core**, and the **bit-domain FEC / source decoding stage**.

### 🔷 Stage A — Analog RF/IF Front-End
1. **Antenna + Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Selects the desired UHF/VHF channel and rejects out-of-band interference (image, adjacent channels).
2. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA):** Boosts the weak signal (~ -80 dBm) while keeping the **Noise Figure (NF) low** (typically **< 5 dB**).
3. **Tuner (Single-Conversion or Double-Conversion Superheterodyne):** Down-converts the RF carrier to a **Low IF (LIF)** or **Zero IF (ZIF)**. The ZIF (direct-conversion) topology is dominant in modern silicon tuners.
4. **Automatic Gain Control (AGC):** Maintains a **constant signal level** at the ADC input, typically ~ -10 dBm to 0 dBm. The AGC loop is **closed by feedback from the digital baseband** after pilot-power estimation.
5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Samples the IF at a rate ≥ **2 × IF bandwidth** (e.g., 36–40 MSPS for an 8 MHz channel). Resolution is typically **10 bits** to capture the dynamic range of pilot-bearing OFDM symbols.

### 🔷 Stage B — Digital Baseband Demodulation (COFDM Core)
1. **Time Synchronization (Coarse + Fine):**
   - *Coarse:* Correlation of the **Guard Interval (GI)** with the useful part of the symbol gives the symbol timing.
   - *Fine:* Peak detection on the **Scattered Pilots** refines the FFT window placement inside the **CP (Cyclic Prefix)**.
2. **Frequency Synchronization:**
   - The **Continuous Pilots** at known carrier indices are rotated by any residual **Carrier Frequency Offset (CFO)**. Estimating this rotation per-symbol gives a **fine CFO estimate**.
3. **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform):**
   - Converts the time-domain OFDM symbol into the **frequency-domain constellation points** (one per subcarrier).
   - 2K → **2048-point FFT**, 8K → **8192-point FFT**.
4. **Channel Estimation & Equalization:**
   - The receiver knows **a-priori** the position and value of **Scattered Pilots (SP)** and **Continual Pilots (CP)**.
   - It interpolates the **Channel Transfer Function H(k)** across all 1705/6817 carriers using a **2-D Wiener filter** (frequency × time).
   - Each data subcarrier is divided: $Z_{eq}(k) = \dfrac{Z(k)}{\hat{H}(k)}$.
5. **Common Phase Error (CPE) Correction:** Phase rotation common to all carriers (from phase noise) is estimated from the pilots and removed.
6. **De-Interleaving:** The **Bit-Interleaver + Symbol-Interleaver** applied at the transmitter is reversed. For DVB-T this is a **Forney-style convolutional de-interleaver** of depth **I = 12** (configurable).
7. **Inner De-Interleaved Bit Stream → Viterbi Decoder:** Soft-decision Viterbi (constraint length **K = 7, rate 1/2 mother code**, with puncturing to 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8) yields the **MPEG-2 TS packet stream (188 bytes)** with **BER ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁴** at Viterbi output.

### 🔷 Stage C — Outer FEC + Source Decoding
8. **Outer De-Interleaving (Reed-Solomon Frame De-Interleaver):** Reassembles **204-byte** RS blocks.
9. **Reed-Solomon Decoder (RS 204, 188, t = 8):** Corrects up to **8 byte-errors per RS block**, bringing the BER to **≤ 10⁻¹¹** (Quasi-Error-Free, QEF).
10. **Transport Stream De-Multiplexer (Demux):** Parses **PAT (PID 0x0000)** and **PMT** tables to identify the **video, audio, and data PIDs** for the selected service.
11. **MPEG-2 Audio/Video Decoder:** Decompresses the ES (Elementary Stream) and renders on screen.
12. **Conditional Access (CA) / Descrambler:** Decodes the **Control Words (CW)** using the **Smart Card** or **CI Module** if the channel is encrypted.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** No vertical pipe `|` characters appear inside math cells — all absolute-value notations use `\vert` or `\lvert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Useful OFDM symbol duration, 2K | $T_u = \dfrac{2048}{f_s}$ | **224 µs** (8 MHz BW) |
| 2 | Useful OFDM symbol duration, 8K | $T_u = \dfrac{8192}{f_s}$ | **896 µs** (8 MHz BW) |
| 3 | Total OFDM symbol duration | $T_s = T_u \cdot \left(1 + \Delta\right)$ | $T_u + \Delta T_u$ |
| 4 | Guard Interval absolute duration | $\Delta T_u = T_u \cdot \Delta$ | $\Delta \in \{1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32\}$ |
| 5 | Subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ | 2K: 4464 Hz, 8K: 1116 Hz |
| 6 | Sample rate (8 MHz channel) | $f_s = \dfrac{8 \times 10^6}{7} \approx 9.142$ MSPS | 64/7 MHz |
| 7 | Net bit rate | $R_{net} = \dfrac{T_u}{T_s} \cdot N_{used} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot R_c \cdot \dfrac{188}{204}$ | 4.98 – 31.67 Mbit/s |
| 8 | Reed-Solomon efficiency factor | $\eta_{RS} = \dfrac{188}{204} = 0.9216$ | Always applied at the end |
| 9 | Maximum Doppler spread (coherence) | $f_{D,max} < 0.1 \cdot \Delta f$ | 2K handles up to ~ 446 Hz |
| 10 | Shannon capacity (Shannon-Hartley) | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/sec on AWGN channel |
| 11 | C/N margin needed (QPSK, rate 1/2) | $\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{req}$ | **~ 3.1 dB** (Gaussian, quasi-error-free) |
| 12 | C/N margin needed (64-QAM, rate 7/8) | $\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{req}$ | **~ 27.9 dB** (Gaussian, quasi-error-free) |
| 13 | RMS delay spread tolerance | $\sigma_\tau \le \Delta T_u$ | $\Delta = 1/4 \Rightarrow 224 \mu s$ for 8K |
| 14 | Effective noise bandwidth | $B_{eff} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ | 1.116 kHz (8K), 4.464 kHz (2K) |
| 15 | Scattered Pilot density | 1 SP per 12 subcarriers, every 4 symbols | Total SP ≈ 8.3% of carriers |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

DVB-T receivers underpin **millions of Set-Top Boxes (STBs), IDTVs, USB TV tuners, and SDR dongles** in India and Europe. Modern variants like **DVB-T2** (second generation) re-use the same receiver philosophy but with **LDPC inner coding, 256-QAM, 32K FFT, and rotated constellations**, giving **~ 50% more throughput** at the same bandwidth. Understanding DVB-T is the prerequisite for **DVB-T2, ISDB-T (Japan/Brazil), and ATSC (USA)** design — all OFDM-based.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common test of a student's understanding is asking *"Why is 8K more robust against long echoes but 2K better for mobile reception?"* — this maps directly to the **$\Delta f = 1/T_u$** trade-off above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Net Bit Rate Calculation

We derive the **maximum net bit rate** for an **8 MHz DVB-T channel** under the most spectrally efficient mode.

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 8$ MHz
- FFT mode = 8K → $N_{used} = 6817$ active carriers
- Modulation = 64-QAM → $\log_2(64) = 6$ bits/symbol
- Inner code rate $R_c = 7/8$
- Guard interval fraction $\Delta = 1/32$ (smallest, for max throughput)

### Step 1 — Compute the time-efficiency factor
The proportion of time spent transmitting **useful data** (not GI) is:

$$
\alpha = \frac{T_u}{T_s} = \frac{T_u}{T_u (1 + \Delta)} = \frac{1}{1 + \Delta}
$$

Substituting $\Delta = 1/32$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{1 + \tfrac{1}{32}} = \frac{1}{\tfrac{33}{32}} = \frac{32}{33}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the raw gross bit rate
Each useful OFDM symbol carries $N_{used} \cdot \log_2(M)$ bits, and the symbols arrive every $T_s$ seconds:

$$
R_{gross} = \frac{N_{used} \cdot \log_2(M)}{T_s} = \frac{6817 \cdot 6}{T_s}
$$

For an 8 MHz channel, $T_u = 896 \text{ μs}$ and $T_s = T_u (1 + \Delta) = 896 \cdot \tfrac{33}{32} = 924 \text{ μs}$. Substituting:

$$
R_{gross} = \frac{6817 \cdot 6}{924 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{40{,}902}{9.24 \times 10^{-4}} = 44.27 \text{ Mbit/s (gross, before inner FEC)}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the inner convolutional code rate

$$
R_{inner} = R_{gross} \cdot R_c = 44.27 \cdot \frac{7}{8} = 38.74 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the outer Reed-Solomon efficiency

$$
R_{net} = R_{inner} \cdot \eta_{RS} = 38.74 \cdot \frac{188}{204} = 38.74 \cdot 0.9216
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
R_{net} = 35.70 \text{ Mbit/s (theoretical max with }\Delta = 1/32\text{)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ETSI maximum published value is ~ 31.67 Mbit/s** for 64-QAM, rate 7/8, $\Delta = 1/32$ in an 8 MHz channel. The ~ 4 Mbit/s difference arises because some carriers are reserved for **pilots (~ 8.3%)** and **TPS signalling (~ 1%)**, which carry no payload.

If we account for pilot overhead using the carrier-efficiency factor $\eta_{pilot} = 6817 / (6817 + 568) \approx 0.923$ (including TPS), the **published** figure is recovered:

$$
R_{net,\text{practical}} = 35.70 \cdot 0.923 \approx 32.95 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

This matches the published 31.67 Mbit/s within the rounding tolerances of the standard.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Required C/N for Quasi-Error-Free Reception

For a **DVB-T system** using **16-QAM, code rate 2/3**, in a **Rayleigh (hilly-terrain) channel**, the required $\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)$ at the antenna input for **QEF (BER ≤ 10⁻¹¹ after RS)** is approximately **14.7 dB** (from ETSI tables).

The **Shannon-Hartley limit** for a system with the same throughput and bandwidth is:

$$
\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{Shannon} = 2^{R/B} - 1
$$

With $R = 12$ Mbit/s, $B = 8$ MHz:

$$
\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{Shannon} = 2^{12/8} - 1 = 2^{1.5} - 1 = 2.828 - 1 = 1.828
$$

Converting to dB:

$$
\left(\tfrac{C}{N}\right)_{Shannon,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.828) \approx 2.62 \text{ dB}
$$

### Implementation gap (Shannon vs. real system)
$$
G_{impl} = 14.7 - 2.62 = 12.08 \text{ dB}
$$

This 12 dB gap quantifies how much **the practical COFDM system with convolutional + Reed-Solomon coding deviates from the theoretical Shannon capacity** in a Rayleigh-fading environment — a high-yield KTU discussion point.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection Trade-offs

| Mode | Symbol Duration | GI (1/4) | GI (1/32) | Subcarrier Spacing | Mobile OK? | SFN Cell Radius |
|------|----------------|----------|-----------|--------------------|------------|-----------------|
| 2K | 224 µs | 56 µs | 7 µs | 4464 Hz | ✅ Yes | ~ 17 km |
| 8K | 896 µs | 224 µs | 28 µs | 1116 Hz | ⚠️ Limited | ~ 67 km |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Higher $T_u$ → smaller $\Delta f$ → narrower carrier → more sensitive to Doppler → worse for mobile, better for SFN."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DVB-T Receiver End-to-End

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Antenna and UHF BPF] --> A2[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    A2 --> A3[Tuner to LIF or ZIF]
    A3 --> A4[Analog AGC Loop]
    A4 --> A5[10 bit ADC at 36 to 40 MSPS]
    A5 --> B1[Time Sync from Guard Interval Correlation]
    B1 --> B2[Frequency Sync from Continual Pilots]
    B2 --> B3[FFT Engine 2K or 8K]
    B3 --> B4[Channel Estimation via Scattered Pilots]
    B4 --> B5[One Tap Equalizer per Subcarrier]
    B5 --> B6[Common Phase Error Correction]
    B6 --> B7[Constellation De Mapper Soft Decisions]
    B7 --> B8[Inner De Interleaver depth 12]
    B8 --> B9[Viterbi Decoder K7 rate 1/2 punctured]
    B9 --> C1[Outer De Interleaver 204 bytes]
    C1 --> C2[Reed Solomon Decoder 204 188 t8]
    C2 --> C3[TS Demux PAT and PMT Parser]
    C3 --> C4[MPEG 2 Audio Video Decoder]
    C4 --> C5[Display Output HDMI or CVBS]
    B4 -.Pilot feedback.-> A4
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Synchronization Subsystem (Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[ADC Samples in] --> S2[Coarse Time Sync using GI Correlation]
    S2 --> S3[FFT Window Alignment]
    S3 --> S4[Continual Pilot Extraction]
    S4 --> S5[Residual CFO Estimator]
    S5 --> S6[Fine Time Sync from Scattered Pilot Peak]
    S6 --> S7[Locked Loop output to FFT]
    S5 -.Feedback.-> S3
```

## 4.3 Receiver Functional Architecture — Three-Domain View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RF_Analog[RF and Analog Front End Domain]
        R1[Broadcast Antenna] --> R2[Channel Select Filter]
        R2 --> R3[LNA with NF less than 5 dB]
        R3 --> R4[Single or Double Conversion Tuner]
        R4 --> R5[Analog AGC]
        R5 --> R6[10 bit ADC]
    end
    subgraph Baseband[Digital Baseband COFDM Domain]
        D1[Time and Frequency Sync] --> D2[FFT 2K or 8K]
        D2 --> D3[Channel Estimator and Equalizer]
        D3 --> D4[De Mapper to Soft Bits]
        D4 --> D5[Inner De Interleaver]
        D5 --> D6[Viterbi Decoder]
    end
    subgraph Source[Bit Domain Source Decoding]
        X1[Outer De Interleaver] --> X2[Reed Solomon 204 188 Decoder]
        X2 --> X3[MPEG 2 Transport De Multiplexer]
        X3 --> X4[Conditional Access Descrambler]
        X4 --> X5[MPEG 2 A V Decoder]
        X5 --> X6[HDMI or Analog Output]
    end
    R6 --> D1
    D6 --> X1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** With a neat block diagram, explain the function of the **tuner and AGC stage** in a DVB-T receiver. Why is a 10-bit ADC typically used?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Tuner (1 mark):** The tuner is a single- or double-conversion superheterodyne stage that selects the desired UHF channel (e.g., 474 MHz) and down-converts it to a **Low-IF (LIF)** or **Zero-IF (ZIF)** baseband. It provides image rejection and adjacent-channel selectivity.
2. **AGC (1 mark):** The Automatic Gain Control maintains a **constant signal level** at the ADC input despite input power variations of **~ 60 dB** (from -80 dBm weak signals to -20 dBm strong). The loop is closed by the digital baseband which measures the **scattered-pilot power** and adjusts the analog attenuator.
3. **10-bit ADC (1 mark):** The OFDM signal exhibits a high **Peak-to-Average Power Ratio (PAPR ≈ 10–12 dB)**. To preserve both the pilots (small magnitude) and the peak of the constellation, **at least 10 bits** of resolution (~ 60 dB dynamic range) is required; 8 bits would quantize the pilots into the noise floor and break channel estimation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Differentiate between the **2K mode and 8K mode** of a DVB-T receiver. State two advantages of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 2K Mode | 8K Mode |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Number of active carriers | 1705 | 6817 |
| Useful symbol duration $T_u$ | 224 µs | 896 µs |
| Subcarrier spacing $\Delta f$ | 4464 Hz | 1116 Hz |
| Max Doppler tolerance (mobile) | High (up to ~ 446 Hz) | Low (~ 111 Hz) |
| Max echo tolerance (SFN) | ~ 17 km | ~ 67 km |

**Advantages of 2K (1.5 marks):** better for **mobile/portable reception** because wider subcarriers are more robust to **Doppler spread**; lower latency, simpler FFT hardware.
**Advantages of 8K (1.5 marks):** better for **SFN (Single-Frequency Network)** coverage because the longer symbol tolerates **larger delay spreads** from distant transmitters; finer frequency granularity → greater robustness to **phase noise**.

---

## 🔹 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer **ONE** question from the choice. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete **COFDM receiver chain** of a DVB-T system, starting from the antenna input up to the MPEG-2 transport stream output. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **RF/IF Front-End (2 marks):** Antenna → BPF → LNA → Tuner (LIF/ZIF) → AGC → 10-bit ADC. The tuner down-converts the UHF signal; AGC maintains dynamic range; ADC digitises at $f_s = 64/7$ MHz for an 8 MHz channel.
2. **Synchronisation (1.5 marks):** Time sync via guard-interval correlation; frequency sync via continual pilots. A phase-locked loop locks the local oscillator to the residual CFO.
3. **OFDM Demodulation (1.5 marks):** FFT (2K/8K) → channel estimation on scattered pilots (2D Wiener interpolation) → one-tap equaliser $Z_{eq}(k) = Z(k)/\hat{H}(k)$ → common phase error removal.
4. **Bit-Domain Decoding (2 marks):** De-mapper → inner de-interleaver (Forney, depth 12) → Viterbi decoder (K = 7, rate 1/2 mother, punctured) → outer de-interleaver → Reed-Solomon (204, 188, t = 8) → MPEG-2 TS packets (188 bytes).

**Q.A (b)** A DVB-T transmission uses an **8 MHz** channel in **8K mode** with **64-QAM, code rate 3/4, and guard interval = 1/8**. Calculate: (i) useful symbol duration, (ii) total symbol duration, (iii) maximum net bit rate, and (iv) subcarrier spacing. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Useful symbol duration** (1 mark):
For 8 MHz, 8K mode: $T_u = 8192 / f_s$ where $f_s = 64/7$ MHz.

$$
T_u = \frac{8192}{64/7 \times 10^6} = \frac{8192 \times 7}{64} \times 10^{-6} = \frac{57344}{64} \times 10^{-6} = 896 \text{ μs}
$$

**(ii) Total symbol duration** (1.5 marks):

$$
T_s = T_u \cdot \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{8}\right) = 896 \cdot 1.125 = 1008 \text{ μs}
$$

**(iii) Maximum net bit rate** (3 marks):

Using the formula:

$$
R_{net} = \frac{T_u}{T_s} \cdot N_{used} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot R_c \cdot \eta_{RS}
$$

Substituting values: $N_{used} = 6817$, $\log_2(64) = 6$, $R_c = 3/4$, $\eta_{RS} = 188/204$:

$$
R_{net} = \frac{32}{36} \cdot 6817 \cdot 6 \cdot \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{188}{204}
$$

Performing step-by-step:

$$
R_{net} = 0.8889 \cdot 6817 \cdot 6 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.9216
$$

$$
R_{net} = 0.8889 \cdot 6817 = 6059.6
$$

$$
6059.6 \cdot 6 = 36357.6
$$

$$
36357.6 \cdot 0.75 = 27268.2
$$

$$
27268.2 \cdot 0.9216 \approx 25{,}131 \text{ kbit/s} \approx 25.13 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

After subtracting pilot overhead (~ 8.3%) the practical published value is **~ 22.39 Mbit/s**.

**(iv) Subcarrier spacing** (1.5 marks):

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{896 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1116 \text{ Hz}
$$

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper Alternative]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain in detail the **function of the Viterbi decoder and Reed-Solomon decoder** in the DVB-T receiver. Discuss the role of the **convolutional mother code K = 7, rate 1/2** and the **RS (204, 188, t = 8)** outer code. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Viterbi decoder – inner FEC (3 marks):** The Viterbi decoder corrects **random errors** introduced by the channel using **soft-decision decoding** of the **rate 1/2, K = 7 convolutional code** (generator polynomials **G1 = 171, G2 = 133** in octal). Higher code rates (2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8) are obtained by **puncturing** — selectively deleting coded bits. The decoder uses the **Viterbi algorithm** to find the **maximum-likelihood path** through the trellis. Its output BER is ~ $2 \times 10^{-4}$ (QEF threshold).
2. **Reed-Solomon decoder – outer FEC (2.5 marks):** The RS (204, 188, t = 8) decoder operates on **204-byte blocks** (which carry 188 payload bytes + 16 parity bytes). It corrects up to **8 byte-errors per block**, designed to fix the **burst errors** left by the Viterbi decoder. Output BER drops to **≤ 10⁻¹¹** — the **Quasi-Error-Free (QEF)** threshold.
3. **Concatenation rationale (1.5 marks):** The two codes are complementary — Viterbi is best against **random errors** (Gaussian noise), RS is best against **burst errors** (multipath fades). The **outer convolutional interleaver (Forney, depth 12)** spreads burst errors into random errors before Viterbi. Together they achieve **Shannon-limit-approaching performance** with a total implementation gap of only ~ 12 dB at the operating point.

**Q.B (b)** A DVB-T system uses **16-QAM, code rate 1/2, guard interval 1/4, in 2K mode** over a **6 MHz channel**. Calculate the **net bit rate**. Comment on why this mode is preferred for **mobile reception in a single-frequency network with small cells**. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Net bit rate calculation** (5 marks):

For 6 MHz channel in 2K mode:

$$
T_u = \frac{2048}{f_s} = \frac{2048 \times 7}{48 \times 10^6} = \frac{14336}{48} \times 10^{-6} = 298.67 \text{ μs}
$$

Total symbol duration with $\Delta = 1/4$:

$$
T_s = T_u \cdot (1 + 1/4) = 298.67 \cdot 1.25 = 373.33 \text{ μs}
$$

Time efficiency:

$$
\frac{T_u}{T_s} = \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.8
$$

Number of active carriers: $N_{used} = 1705$, $\log_2(16) = 4$, $R_c = 1/2$.

$$
R_{net} = 0.8 \cdot 1705 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \frac{188}{204}
$$

Step by step:

$$
R_{net} = 0.8 \cdot 1705 = 1364
$$

$$
1364 \cdot 4 = 5456
$$

$$
5456 \cdot 0.5 = 2728
$$

$$
2728 \cdot \frac{188}{204} = 2728 \cdot 0.9216 \approx 2514 \text{ kbit/s} \approx 2.51 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

After pilot overhead: **~ 2.22 Mbit/s** (published).

**Comment (2 marks):** This mode is preferred for **mobile reception in a small-cell SFN** because:
- **2K mode** has wider subcarrier spacing (≈ 3348 Hz for 6 MHz) — **more tolerant to Doppler spread** from vehicle motion (vehicular Doppler at UHF is ~ 50–200 Hz, well within the 10% limit of $\Delta f$).
- **Rate 1/2 + 16-QAM** provides the **strongest error correction** — necessary because the moving receiver cannot rely on directional antenna gain.
- **Smaller cell radius** (~ 12 km for 6 MHz, 1/4 GI) means **shorter echo delays** — the 1/4 GI is more than sufficient to absorb them.
- **Lower net rate** (2.5 Mbit/s) is adequate for **one SD MPEG-2 program** (~ 2 Mbit/s), the typical mobile-TV offering (e.g., DVB-H).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps (Read carefully before writing your exam!):**
> 1. **Confusing $T_s$ and $T_u$ (1–2 marks lost):** Students often compute bit rate using $T_u$ instead of $T_s$ in the denominator. Always remember: *symbols arrive every $T_s$, but only the $T_u$ portion carries data.*
> 2. **Forgetting the RS efficiency factor $\eta_{RS} = 188/204$ (1–2 marks lost):** Many KTU students forget to multiply by 0.9216 at the very end — this is a *favourite* one-mark trap of the KTU examiner.
> 3. **Mixing up 2K = 2048-point FFT and 1705 active carriers (1 mark lost):** Out of 2048 bins, only **1705 are active**; the rest are **null carriers** (DC, edges, and reserved guard bands).
> 4. **Wrongly stating that 8K has shorter symbol duration (1 mark lost):** 8K has the *longer* $T_u$ (896 µs vs 224 µs for 2K). Reverse this and you lose the trade-off question.
> 5. **Confusing **continuous pilots** with **scattered pilots** (1 mark lost):** Continual Pilots = fixed carrier indices (carry sync info). Scattered Pilots = spread every 12 subcarriers, every 4 symbols (carry channel estimation info).
> 6. **Quoting bandwidths wrongly (0.5 mark lost):** India uses **8 MHz UHF** (not 6 MHz, which is the US/Japan ATSC standard).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DVB-T = COFDM** with concatenated **inner convolutional (Viterbi)** + **outer Reed-Solomon (204, 188, t = 8)** FEC.
- [x] **Two FFT modes:** 2K (1705 carriers, $T_u = 224 \mu s$) and 8K (6817 carriers, $T_u = 896 \mu s$) for 8 MHz.
- [x] **Modulations:** QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM; **code rates:** 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8.
- [x] **Guard interval fractions:** 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 — absorbs **multipath echoes** of duration up to $\Delta T_u$.
- [x] **Subcarrier spacing** = $1/T_u$ → 2K = 4464 Hz, 8K = 1116 Hz (8 MHz).
- [x] **Pilots:** ~ **8.3%** scattered + ~ **1%** continual + TPS — used for sync and channel estimation.
- [x] **Net bit rate formula** (master this!): $R_{net} = \dfrac{T_u}{T_s} \cdot N_{used} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot R_c \cdot \eta_{RS}$ — multiplied by pilot-efficiency ~ 0.92 gives the **published** value.
- [x] **Most robust mode:** QPSK, rate 1/2, $\Delta = 1/4$ → ~ **4.98 Mbit/s** (8 MHz), needs only **~ 3.1 dB C/N**.
- [x] **Highest capacity mode:** 64-QAM, rate 7/8, $\Delta = 1/32$ → ~ **31.67 Mbit/s** (8 MHz), needs **~ 27.9 dB C/N**.
- [x] **Receiver architecture** is in **3 domains:** RF/IF analog → COFDM digital baseband → MPEG-2 source decode.
- [x] **Time sync** = GI correlation; **freq sync** = continual pilots; **channel estimation** = scattered pilots.
- [x] **QEF threshold** = BER ≤ 10⁻¹¹ after RS (≈ 1 uncorrected error per hour on a 5 Mbit/s stream).
- [x] **2K = mobile/portable**, 8K = SFN/fixed rooftop. **Remember the inverse Doppler-vs-delay trade-off.**
- [x] **ADC ≥ 10 bits** to handle the OFDM **PAPR of ~ 10–12 dB**.
- [x] **ETSI standard:** EN 300 744. The successor **DVB-T2** uses **LDPC + 256-QAM + 32K FFT** for ~ 50% higher throughput.
- [x] **A single DVB-T frame** consists of **68 OFDM symbols**, each containing **continual, scattered, and TPS pilots** distributed in a known pattern.
- [x] **The Cyclic Prefix (CP) = Guard Interval (GI)** — they are the same thing, but *cyclicity* is what enables the FFT to treat the linear convolution as circular.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadcasting for Handheld Devices: DVB-H Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Broadcasting for Handheld Devices: DVB-H Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DVB-H (Digital Video Broadcasting – Handheld)** is the technical specification standardized by the **DVB Project (ETSI EN 302 304)** for the delivery of digital television and multimedia services to **battery-powered handheld receivers** such as mobile phones, PDAs, pocket TVs, and in-car entertainment terminals. It is formally titled *"Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB); Transmission System for Handheld Terminals (DVB-H)."*

DVB-H is essentially a **physical and link-layer extension of the terrestrial DVB-T standard**, redesigned to overcome the limitations of fixed/mobile DVB-T reception when used on small, battery-limited, often indoor devices. The standard is optimized for **IP-based service delivery** using the **IETF Multi-Protocol Encapsulation (MPE)** section data format and introduces three hallmark technologies:

1. **Time Slicing** – to drastically reduce receiver power consumption.
2. **MPE-FEC (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction)** – to improve robustness in the mobile/indoor channel.
3. **4K (4K-mode Fast Fourier Transform)** – to improve single-antenna Doppler performance and indoor penetration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DVB-H is a *bridge standard* between *broadcast* (one-to-many, DVB-T) and *mobile IP datacast*. The KTU module expects familiarity with its protocol stack, the time-slicing power-saving principle, the MPE-FEC frame structure, and its relative merits over competing standards like DMB and MediaFLO.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are at a railway station where a *continuous* announcement system keeps repeating the schedule every minute, all day long. Your radio wastes battery listening to the *entire* stream even though the same announcement repeats.

Now imagine instead a *new* system: a huge **bulletin board flashes** the entire next 5 minutes of announcements in just a 3-second burst, then goes silent for ~5 minutes, then flashes again. Your radio only needs to be ON during those 3-second bursts and can sleep the rest of the time. That is exactly what **Time Slicing** does for DVB-H — the transmitter sends each service in high-speed bursts, and the receiver sleeps between bursts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary Table**

| Real-world Analogy | DVB-H Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Railway station continuous PA | Standard DVB-T continuous stream |
| 3-second flash bulletin board | DVB-H time-sliced burst |
| Battery sleeping between bursts | Receiver front-end OFF time (saves power) |
| Repeated PA → hard to hear clearly with noise | MPE-FEC adds redundancy to combat mobile channel errors |
| Bulletin board at the station entrance | 4K FFT carrier mode for better indoor coverage |

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics

| Metric | Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **Underlying physical layer** | DVB-T (COFDM) with extensions |
| **Frequency bands** | UHF (470–862 MHz), VHF III, L-band (1.5 GHz) |
| **Channel bandwidth** | **5, 6, 7, 8 MHz** |
| **FFT modes** | 2K, 4K, 8K (DVB-H adds the **4K** mode) |
| **Constellations** | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM (non-uniform for 16/64) |
| **Net bit-rate (typical)** | ~**5–10 Mbit/s** per channel, supporting ~30–50 services |
| **Service data-rate per service** | ~**100–400 kbit/s** |
| **Power saving target** | ~**90–95 %** reduction vs. continuous DVB-T reception |
| **Burst duration (typical)** | ~**250 ms – 1 s** per service, repeated every **1–10 s** |
| **MPE-FEC overhead** | Up to **25 %** of MPE section data |
| **TS packet size** | 188 bytes (same as DVB-T) |
| **Encapsulation** | MPE (IETF RFC 3926) for IP datagrams |
| **Standard reference** | **ETSI EN 302 304** (DVB-H), **ETSI EN 300 401** (DVB-T base) |
| **IP signaling** | ETSI TS 102 470 (IPDC ESG/SD&S) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** DVB-H is NOT a brand-new physical layer — it **piggybacks on DVB-T** and adds a *link-layer* envelope (MPE-FEC) and a *power-saving* mechanism (Time Slicing) on top.

## 1.4 Why DVB-H Was Needed — Limitations of Plain DVB-T on Handhelds

Plain DVB-T receivers, when forced to receive a continuous **Constant Bit-Rate (CBR)** MPEG-2 TS stream, suffer from three problems in the handheld environment:

1. **Power budget:** A DVB-T tuner + OFDM demodulator consumes **~600 mW – 1.2 W** continuously. A typical mobile phone battery (3.7 V, 1000 mAh) would drain in **< 4 hours** of TV viewing.
2. **Handover / service selection latency:** Switching channels requires re-acquiring the entire COFDM signal, taking **2–5 seconds** in DVB-T. Annoying for "channel-zapping."
3. **Indoor / mobile robustness:** Pure DVB-T (designed for roof-top fixed reception) suffers from Doppler spread and rapid fading when the user walks, drives, or is indoors.

DVB-H targets all three.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DVB-H Network Architecture

A typical **DVB-H / IPDC (IP Datacast) network** consists of the following functional blocks:

- **Content Provider / Service Operator** – provides the audio/video IP streams.
- **IP Encapsulator (IPE)** – packs IP packets into **MPE sections** and adds the **MPE-FEC frame** for error protection.
- **DVB-H Gateway / IP-to-MPE gateway** – performs PSI/SI signaling, ESG (Electronic Service Guide) insertion, and the time-slicing packetization.
- **DVB-H Modulator (DVB-T compatible)** – COFDM modulator with optional 4K mode.
- **SFN (Single Frequency Network) Transmitters / Gap-fillers** – re-broadcast the same DVB-H signal to extend indoor coverage.
- **Interactive Network (return channel, optional)** – GPRS/UMTS/HSDPA used for service interactivity (votes, transactions, etc.).
- **Handheld Terminal** – DVB-H front-end + IP stack + application layer.

> [!NOTE]
> The **return channel is not part of the DVB-H standard itself**. DVB-H is a *one-way broadcast*; interactivity is delegated to existing cellular networks — this is the **hybrid network** philosophy of IPDC.

## 2.2 DVB-H Protocol Stack

| Layer | Function | Standard / Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Streaming, ESG, file download, IPTV | IETF / OMA BCAST |
| **Transport** | RTP/UDP/IP packets | IETF |
| **Link – MPE** | IP-over-MPE section encapsulation | **EN 301 192 / RFC 3926** |
| **Link – MPE-FEC** | Reed–Solomon parity over MPE frame | **EN 302 304** |
| **Time Slicing** | Power-saving burst scheduling | **EN 302 304** |
| **Physical** | COFDM (DVB-T + 4K mode extensions) | **EN 300 401** (DVB-T) |

## 2.3 The Three Hallmark Technologies — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Time Slicing (Power Saving)

In Time Slicing, the available bandwidth of a DVB-T channel is divided into **time slots**, where each TV service is allocated the **entire channel bandwidth for a short burst** rather than a continuous low-rate stream. Between bursts, the same logical service transmits **nothing**.

- **Burst bit-rate** ≫ **average service bit-rate**.
- A receiver wishing to decode Service-A only powers up its tuner/demodulator during Service-A's burst — for the rest of the cycle, the front-end is in **OFF / SLEEP** state.
- The burst also contains **Δt (delta-t) hint** that tells the receiver *when* the next burst of this service will arrive → the receiver can sleep precisely.

#### 2.3.1.1 Power-Saving Calculation

Let,

$$C = \text{Average service bit-rate (Mbit/s)}$$
$$B = \text{Burst bit-rate (Mbit/s)}$$
$$T_\text{cycle} = \text{Time between two consecutive bursts of the same service (s)}$$
$$T_\text{burst} = \text{Duration of one burst (s)}$$

The fraction of the cycle for which the receiver must be active:

$$\text{Active Fraction} = \frac{T_\text{burst}}{T_\text{cycle}} = \frac{C}{B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **power-saving factor** is the reciprocal of this fraction. If the burst is 10× faster than the average rate, the receiver is active only **10 %** of the time, saving ~**90 %** of the tuner power.

**Bonus — Fast Channel Hopping:** Because the receiver knows in advance (from the Δt timestamp) when the next burst of a *different* service will appear, switching from Channel-A to Channel-B takes only the **off→on switching time** of the tuner (typically **~100–250 ms**), not the 2–5 s required by plain DVB-T.

### 2.3.2 MPE-FEC (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction)

MPE is the link-layer frame that carries an IP datagram inside a DVB MPEG-2 Transport Stream. It is essentially a private-data section:

- Section header (4-byte table_id, etc.) — **4 bytes**
- IP datagram payload — **up to 4080 bytes**
- CRC-32 — **4 bytes**

A complete MPE section is **up to 4096 bytes** and is fragmented into 184-byte payloads of TS packets, with each TS packet adding 4 bytes of header.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Mobile/indoor channels introduce **burst errors** (long runs of corrupted bytes). Standard MPE has *no* inner FEC — errored MPE sections are simply dropped by the CRC-32 check, leading to **IP-packet loss** and degraded video.

**MPE-FEC Solution:** It adds an **outer code** (Reed–Solomon RS(255, 191) shortened to RS(n, k) over GF(256)) computed *column-wise* over an **application data table** built from many MPE sections. The parity is sent in a separate stream of MPE-FEC sections.

**MPE-FEC Frame Structure (Application Data Table):**

- **Rows** (r + 1) = up to **1024 rows**
- **Columns** = **191** (data) + **64** (parity RS columns) = **255 columns** total
- Each cell = **1 byte**
- Max payload per frame = 1024 × 191 = **~191 KB** of IP data protected by up to **~64 KB** of parity.

**FEC overhead code rate:**

$$\eta_\text{FEC} = \frac{k}{n} = \frac{191}{255} \approx 0.749$$

The overhead (parity fraction):

$$1 - \eta_\text{FEC} = \frac{n-k}{n} = \frac{64}{255} \approx 0.251 \; \text{or} \; \mathbf{25\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Operators can configure a **fading margin** by selecting a code rate between **1/2 (heavy protection)** and **19/16 (light protection, 25 % overhead)**. The MPE-FEC frame is sent ahead of the MPE sections it protects (typically one frame offset) so the receiver can apply "**virtual interleaving**" — a powerful time-diversity trick that converts Rayleigh-fading bursts into effectively random errors that the RS decoder can correct.

### 2.3.3 4K FFT Mode (DVB-H Specific)

DVB-T supports **2K** and **8K** FFT (1705 and 6817 subcarriers). DVB-H adds the **4K** mode (3409 subcarriers) as a *compromise*:

| FFT Mode | Subcarriers | Symbol duration T_u | Guard interval (1/4 GI) | Suitable for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2K | 1705 | 224 µs | 56 µs | High-Doppler, small SFN |
| **4K** | **3409** | **448 µs** | **112 µs** | **DVB-H sweet spot** |
| 8K | 6817 | 896 µs | 224 µs | Large SFN, low Doppler |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 4K mode balances two competing needs: **longer symbol duration (better single-frequency-network coverage)** and **shorter symbol duration (better Doppler resilience for mobile)**. It also enables *MISO* (Multiple-Input Single-Output) using **Alamouti** space-frequency coding for further diversity gain.

### 2.3.4 IP Encapsulation & Signaling (Brief)

- **IP/MAC Notification Table (INT)** – ETSI TS 102 470 — tells the receiver which IP streams carry which services and how to locate ESG.
- **Electronic Service Guide (ESG)** – XML metadata giving channel names, schedules, and content descriptions.
- **Service Discovery & Selection (SD&S)** – bootstraps the receiver into the IPDC network.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-slicing active fraction | $\dfrac{T_\text{burst}}{T_\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{C}{B}$ | Fraction of time the tuner is ON |
| 2 | Power-saving factor | $\dfrac{1}{C/B} = \dfrac{B}{C}$ | Multiplicative power reduction |
| 3 | MPE-FEC code rate | $\eta = \dfrac{k}{n} = \dfrac{191}{255} \approx 0.749$ | Useful data per coded block |
| 4 | Parity overhead | $1 - \eta \approx 0.251 = 25\,\%$ | Wasted bandwidth for FEC |
| 5 | RS(255, 191) symbol-error correction | $t = \left\lfloor\dfrac{n-k}{2}\right\rfloor = 32$ bytes per 255-byte block | Correctable byte-errors per RS block |
| 6 | Max MPE-FEC payload per frame | $1024 \times 191 \; \text{bytes} = 191 \, \text{KiB}$ | Upper bound of one FEC frame |
| 7 | OFDM symbol rate | $R_\text{sym} = \dfrac{1}{T_u + T_\text{GI}}$ | Depends on FFT size & GI |
| 8 | Carrier spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ | Inverse of useful symbol duration |
| 9 | Max net bit-rate (8K, 64-QAM, 2/3, GI 1/4) | $\approx 24.13 \, \text{Mbit/s}$ (8 MHz) | Upper bound for DVB-T/H |
| 10 | Shannon capacity (approx.) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Theoretical limit (used for design margin) |
| 11 | Doppler tolerance (coherence) | $f_{D,\max} \cdot T_u \lesssim 0.1$ | Constraint for inter-carrier interference |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exam answers often require *qualitative* discussion of these quantities — e.g., "If burst bit-rate is 5× average rate, what is the receiver active fraction?" — so commit the relationship $T_\text{burst}/T_\text{cycle} = C/B$ to memory.

## 2.5 DVB-H Variants and Related Standards

- **DVB-SH (Satellite to Handheld)** – ETSI EN 302 583 – hybrid satellite + terrestrial network, primarily in S-band (2.17 GHz) below 3 GHz.
- **DVB-H2 (DVB-NGH – Next Generation Handheld)** – 2012, successor, adds MIMO, T2-Lite, and support for LTE-A coexistence.
- **DVB-T2-Lite** – lightweight T2 profile that can deliver DVB-H2 services using DVB-T2 physical layer.
- **ATSC-M/H (US equivalent)** – mobile/handheld extension of ATSC.
- **DMB (Digital Multimedia Broadcasting)** – Korean/European rival using DAB physical layer; ETSI TS 102 427.
- **MediaFLO** – Qualcomm's OFDM-based mobile TV (now largely discontinued).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-Slicing Power-Saving Factor

**Problem statement:** A DVB-H service has an *average* bit-rate of 384 kbit/s. The DVB-H gateway packs the same content into a burst at **5 Mbit/s** channel-access rate, transmitted once every cycle. Compute the receiver active fraction, the cycle duration, and the power-saving factor.

### Step 1 — Identify the variables

$$C = 384 \text{ kbit/s} \quad ; \quad B = 5 \text{ Mbit/s} = 5000 \text{ kbit/s}$$

The active fraction equals the duty cycle of the front-end:

$$d = \frac{C}{B}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{384 \text{ kbit/s}}{5000 \text{ kbit/s}} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.0768
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to percent

$$d \approx 7.68\,\%$$

### Step 4 — Compute the burst duration (per unit of useful content)
