The *useful* content in one burst equals the average rate times the cycle:

$$L_\text{burst} = C \cdot T_\text{cycle} \quad \text{(bits of useful payload per cycle)}$$

The *transmission* time of that burst at the burst rate:

$$T_\text{burst} = \frac{L_\text{burst}}{B} = \frac{C \cdot T_\text{cycle}}{B} = d \cdot T_\text{cycle}$$

So if the cycle is 1 s:

$$T_\text{burst} = 0.0768 \times 1 \text{ s} = 76.8 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5 — Power-saving factor (reciprocal of duty cycle)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{d} = \frac{B}{C} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{5000}{384} \approx 13.02
\end{aligned}
$$

So the receiver is ON only **~7.7 %** of the time → **~13×** power saving.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If the tuner consumes 800 mW when ON, the *average* drain drops to:
> $P_\text{avg} = 800 \text{ mW} \times 0.0768 \approx 61.4 \text{ mW}$ — a massive reduction that allows several hours of TV viewing on a phone.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MPE-FEC Parity Overhead

**Problem statement:** A DVB-H service uses MPE-FEC with the maximum frame size: 1024 rows × 255 columns. Compute (a) total bytes, (b) data bytes, (c) parity bytes, (d) code rate, (e) parity overhead %, (f) correctable byte errors per RS codeword.

### Step 1 — Total bytes per frame

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_\text{total} &= 1024 \times 255 \\[4pt]
               &= 261{,}120 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Data bytes (k dimension)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_\text{data} &= 1024 \times 191 \\[4pt]
              &= 195{,}584 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Parity bytes (n − k dimension)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_\text{parity} &= 1024 \times 64 \\[4pt]
                &= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} \;=\; 64 \, \text{KiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Code rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{N_\text{data}}{N_\text{total}} \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{195{,}584}{261{,}120} = \frac{191}{255} \\[4pt]
     &\approx 0.7490
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Parity overhead

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OH} &= 1 - \eta = 1 - \frac{191}{255} = \frac{64}{255} \\[4pt]
          &\approx 0.2510 = \mathbf{25.10\,\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Correctable byte-errors per RS codeword (255, 191)

A Reed–Solomon code over GF(256) corrects up to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \left\lfloor \frac{n - k}{2} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
  &= \left\lfloor \frac{255 - 191}{2} \right\rfloor \\[4pt]
  &= \left\lfloor \frac{64}{2} \right\rfloor = 32 \text{ byte-errors}
\end{aligned}
$$

So **out of every 255 bytes transmitted, up to 32 can be wiped out and still be reconstructed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** In a 25 % overhead envelope, ~12.5 % of the bytes can be wiped by a Rayleigh fade and the original IP packet is *perfectly* recovered. This is what allows DVB-H to ride through "tunnel" and "elevator" moments.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Capacity for DVB-H in a Fading Channel

**Problem statement:** Estimate the Shannon-limited maximum useful throughput of a DVB-H channel with 8 MHz bandwidth, 16-QAM at a 2/3 code rate, given a 2K FFT and 1/4 guard interval, and an effective SNR (after channel coding and Doppler diversity) of 12 dB.

### Step 1 — Use Shannon

$$
C_\text{Shannon} = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{lin}})
$$

with $\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = 10^{12/10} = 15.85$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_\text{Shannon} &= 8 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 15.85) \\[4pt]
                 &= 8 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(16.85) \\[4pt]
                 &= 8 \times 10^6 \cdot 4.075 \\[4pt]
                 &\approx 32.6 \text{ Mbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply practical coding/modulation

- 16-QAM carries 4 bits/symbol; 2/3 code rate reduces this to $4 \times 2/3 = 8/3$ bits/Hz/cycle.
- 1/4 guard interval wastes 20 % of the OFDM symbol time (1/5 of the bandwidth).
- Effective spectral efficiency: $\rho = 8/3 \times 0.8 = 2.133$ bit/s/Hz.

### Step 3 — Practical capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_\text{practical} &= B \times \rho \\[4pt]
                   &= 8 \times 10^6 \times 2.133 \\[4pt]
                   &\approx 17.07 \text{ Mbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well below the Shannon bound (32.6 Mbit/s), confirming the design margin. With 16-QAM and 2/3 rate, DVB-H delivers enough for ~**40 services of 384 kbit/s** each in a single 8 MHz UHF channel.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DVB-H vs. DVB-T vs. DMB vs. MediaFLO

| Feature | **DVB-H** | **DVB-T** | **DMB-T** (T-DMB) | **MediaFLO** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical layer | COFDM (2K/4K/8K) | COFDM (2K/8K) | OFDM (DAB-based) | OFDM, QPSK/16-QAM |
| FFT sizes | 2K, 4K, 8K | 2K, 8K | 1K | 4K equivalent |
| Time-slicing | **Yes (mandatory)** | No | Yes (similar) | Yes (proprietary) |
| Inner FEC | MPE-FEC (RS outer) | None (only convolutional + RS) | Convolutional + RS | Convolutional + RS |
| Bandwidth | 5/6/7/8 MHz | 5/6/7/8 MHz | 1.536 MHz (DAB block) | 5, 6, 7, 8 MHz |
| Net throughput | ~5–10 Mbit/s/chan. | ~24 Mbit/s/chan. | ~1.1 Mbit/s/block | ~5–11 Mbit/s/chan. |
| Handheld power saving | **~90 %** | None | High | High |
| Service bit-rate | 100–500 kbit/s | 1–5 Mbit/s | 100–400 kbit/s | 100–400 kbit/s |
| Indoor / Doppler | Improved (MPE-FEC + 4K) | Poor | Good | Good |
| Return channel | Cellular | Cellular | Cellular | Cellular |
| Status (2024) | Largely discontinued (replaced by LTE/5G broadcast) | Active for fixed DTT | Active in S. Korea | Discontinued |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — DVB-H End-to-End Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CP[Content Provider\nAudio / Video IP Streams] --> IPE[IP Encapsulator\nPacks IP into MPE sections]
    IPE --> MPEFEC[MPE-FEC Encoder\nAdds RS parity columns]
    MPEFEC --> TS[Time-Slicing Scheduler\nBursts services in TDMA slots]
    TS --> MOD[COFDM Modulator\nDVB-T + 4K mode]
    MOD --> SFN[SFN Transmitters\nUHF/VHF gap-fillers]
    SFN -.Terrestrial RF.-> HH1[Handheld Terminal A\nMobile phone]
    SFN -.Terrestrial RF.-> HH2[Handheld Terminal B\nPocket TV]
    SFN -.Terrestrial RF.-> HH3[Handheld Terminal C\nIn-car receiver]
    HH1 <==>|Return channel| CELL[Cellular Network\n3G/4G/5G]
    HH2 <==>|Return channel| CELL
    HH3 <==>|Return channel| CELL
    ESG[ESG / SD and S Server\nIPDC signaling] --> MOD
    CELL --> CP
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — DVB-H Receiver Power-State Machine (Time Slicing)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SLEEP
    SLEEP --> WAKE : Wake-up timer fires\nat next scheduled burst
    WAKE --> SYNC : Front-end powered\nacquires COFDM lock
    SYNC --> ACTIVE : Demodulator locks\nto burst
    ACTIVE --> DECODE : Read delta-t hint\nand payload IP packets
    DECODE --> SLEEP : Burst complete\nsleep until next delta-t
    ACTIVE --> SYNC : Loss of lock\nre-acquire
    SYNC --> SLEEP : Acquisition timeout
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — MPE-FEC Frame Construction (Virtual Interleaving)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP[Application Data Table]
        direction LR
        subgraph COLS[Columns 1 to 191]
            D1[IP packet 1 bytes]
            D2[IP packet 2 bytes]
            D3[IP packet N bytes]
        end
        subgraph PAR[Columns 192 to 255 Parity]
            P1[RS parity byte 0]
            P2[RS parity byte 1]
            P3[RS parity byte 63]
        end
    end
    D1 --> SEC1[MPE Section 1\n184 byte payload]
    D2 --> SEC2[MPE Section 2]
    D3 --> SEC3[MPE Section N]
    P1 --> SECF1[MPE-FEC Section 1\n184 byte parity]
    P2 --> SECF2[MPE-FEC Section 2]
    P3 --> SECF3[MPE-FEC Section K]
    SEC1 --> TS1[TS packets 188B]
    SEC2 --> TS2[TS packets 188B]
    SEC3 --> TS3[TS packets 188B]
    SECF1 --> TSF1[TS packets 188B]
    SECF2 --> TSF2[TS packets 188B]
    SECF3 --> TSF3[TS packets 188B]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Functional Block Diagram of a DVB-H Modulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IP[IP Datagrams\nRTP UDP] --> ENC[MPE Encapsulator\nSection header 4B]
    ENC --> RSENC[Reed Solomon RS 255 191\nColumn-wise over ADT]
    RSENC --> SCHED[Time-Slicing Scheduler\nDelta-t stamping]
    SCHED --> MUX[MPEG-2 TS Multiplexer]
    MUX --> CHCOD[Inner FEC\nConvolutional Punctured]
    CHCOD --> INT[Inner Interleaver\nBit and symbol level]
    INT --> MAP[Constellation Mapper\nQPSK 16-QAM 64-QAM]
    MAP --> IFFT[IFFT 2K 4K 8K\nAdd guard interval]
    IFFT --> DAC[DAC and Up-conversion\nUHF/VHF RF output]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — DVB-H Service Stack (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application\nStreaming ESG Files] --> RTP[RTP layer]
    RTP --> UDP[UDP]
    UDP --> IP[IP datagram]
    IP --> MPE[MPE section\n4B header + 4B CRC]
    MPE --> FEC[MPE-FEC\nRS parity over ADT]
    FEC --> TS[Time Slicing\nBurst scheduling]
    TS --> PHY[COFDM Physical Layer\n2K 4K 8K 4K 16QAM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> If a Mermaid renderer is unavailable, the diagrams above can be reconstructed on paper using **rectangular blocks** with **directional arrows** — the textual content of each box is given verbatim above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 (Modeled)]

**Q: Define DVB-H. List the three key technologies introduced in DVB-H to enable handheld reception.**

**Model Answer:**

**DVB-H (Digital Video Broadcasting – Handheld)** is a broadcast standard (ETSI EN 302 304) designed for delivering digital TV and multimedia services to battery-powered handheld terminals. It is a physical/link-layer extension of DVB-T optimized for IP-based content delivery to mobile devices.

The **three hallmark technologies** of DVB-H are:

1. **Time Slicing** – services are transmitted in high-speed bursts, allowing the receiver to power down its front-end between bursts, achieving ~90 % power saving.
2. **MPE-FEC (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction)** – an outer Reed–Solomon RS(255, 191) code added to MPE sections, providing ~25 % parity overhead and protection against mobile/indoor burst errors.
3. **4K FFT mode** – an intermediate 4096-subcarrier OFDM mode that balances Doppler resilience and SFN coverage for mobile/indoor reception.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse DVB-H with DMB or DVB-T. Always clearly state that DVB-H is **based on DVB-T physical layer** and adds link-layer enhancements — it is not a brand-new PHY.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 (Modeled)]

**Q: Explain the principle of Time Slicing in DVB-H. How does it help in saving receiver power and enabling fast channel switching?**

**Model Answer:**

In **Time Slicing**, the available bandwidth of a DVB-T channel is time-divided into bursts, where each service occupies the *full channel capacity* for a short interval. The same logical service is then sent *nothing* between successive bursts.

**Power saving:** A receiver that wants Service-A only switches ON its tuner/demodulator during Service-A's burst and remains in sleep mode for the rest of the cycle. If the burst rate is **B** and the average service rate is **C**, the active fraction is

$$d = \frac{C}{B}$$

and the **power saving factor is B/C**. For a typical 13× ratio, the receiver is ON only ~8 % of the time, reducing tuner power from ~800 mW to ~60 mW on average.

**Fast channel switching:** Each burst carries a **Δt (delta-time) hint** that tells the receiver precisely when the *next* burst of *any other* service will arrive. The receiver can therefore wake up, retune, and lock onto the new service in **100–250 ms** (just the tuner switching time) instead of the **2–5 seconds** required by plain DVB-T.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 (Modeled)]

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** Describe the DVB-H protocol stack. Explain the function of MPE and MPE-FEC layers with a neat sketch of the MPE-FEC application data table.

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** With a numerical example, derive the power-saving factor achieved by Time Slicing. Assume a service with average bit-rate 256 kbit/s transmitted at a burst rate of 4 Mbit/s. What is the receiver's active duty cycle and the average power consumption if the tuner draws 900 mW when ON?

#### Model Solution to Part A (7 Marks)

**DVB-H Protocol Stack (top-down):**

| Layer | Function | Key Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Application | Streaming, ESG, file download | IETF / OMA BCAST |
| Transport | RTP/UDP | IETF RFC |
| Network | IP | IETF |
| Link – MPE | IP-over-MPE section encapsulation | EN 301 192, RFC 3926 |
| Link – MPE-FEC | RS outer code over MPE data | **EN 302 304 (DVB-H)** |
| Time Slicing | Burst scheduling & delta-t hints | **EN 302 304** |
| Physical | COFDM (DVB-T + 4K mode) | EN 300 401 |

**MPE Section Structure (4 B header + IP datagram + 4 B CRC-32 = up to 4096 B).** MPE sections are fragmented into 184 B payloads of MPEG-2 TS packets (each with 4 B TS header).

**MPE-FEC Application Data Table (ADT):**

- Up to 1024 rows × 255 columns
- Columns 1–191 = **data** (carries the MPE section IP payload bytes)
- Columns 192–255 = **RS parity** (computed column-wise, Reed–Solomon RS(255, 191) over GF(256))
- Each cell = 1 byte
- Maximum IP data per frame = 1024 × 191 = **195,584 bytes (~191 KiB)**
- Maximum parity per frame = 1024 × 64 = **65,536 bytes (64 KiB)**
- Code rate η = 191/255 ≈ 0.749 → **25.10 % parity overhead**
- Corrects up to **t = 32 byte-errors per 255-byte RS codeword** (i.e., 12.5 % byte-erasure capability).

(Refer to the **Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram** for the structural sketch.)

#### Model Solution to Part B (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the duty cycle (active fraction).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{C}{B} = \frac{256 \text{ kbit/s}}{4000 \text{ kbit/s}} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.064 = 6.4\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power-saving factor.**

$$
S = \frac{1}{d} = \frac{4000}{256} \approx 15.625
$$

**Step 3 — Average receiver power.**

$$
P_\text{avg} = P_\text{ON} \times d = 900 \text{ mW} \times 0.064 = 57.6 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Burst duration for a 1-second cycle.**

$$
T_\text{burst} = d \times T_\text{cycle} = 0.064 \times 1 = 64 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

The receiver is active only **6.4 %** of the time; tuner drains **~57.6 mW** instead of **900 mW** — a **~15.6×** reduction. On a 1000 mAh, 3.7 V phone battery (energy 3.7 Wh ≈ 13,320 J), the tuner alone supports:

$$\text{Hours} = \frac{13{,}320 \, \text{J}}{0.0576 \, \text{W} \times 3600 \, \text{s/h}} \approx 64 \, \text{h}$$

— sufficient for several long-haul flights of TV viewing, demonstrating the real-world impact of time slicing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Marks are frequently lost because students:
> 1. **Forget the units** (write 256 instead of 256 kbit/s).
> 2. **Confuse C and B** — the *small* number is C (average), the *large* number is B (burst).
> 3. **Skip the delta-t / channel-switching** discussion in part (a) when it is a 7-mark question.
> 4. **Do not show the MPE-FEC table dimensions** clearly; examiners expect a *labelled* diagram/table with the column split 191/64 and the 1024 row count.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modeled)]

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** Compare DVB-H with DVB-T and DMB in terms of physical layer, bandwidth, power saving, error protection, and service bit-rates.

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** Explain the role of the 4K FFT mode in DVB-H. Why is it considered a compromise between 2K and 8K modes? Briefly discuss MISO (Alamouti) support that the 4K mode enables.

#### Model Solution to Part A (7 Marks)

A side-by-side comparison table is appropriate for full marks (5 marks for the table + 2 marks for concluding remarks on operational implications):

| Parameter | DVB-H | DVB-T | DMB-T (T-DMB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| PHY | COFDM, 2K/4K/8K | COFDM, 2K/8K | OFDM (DAB), 1K |
| Bandwidth | 5/6/7/8 MHz | 5/6/7/8 MHz | 1.536 MHz (DAB block) |
| Channel coding | Convolutional + RS + **MPE-FEC (outer RS)** | Convolutional + RS | Convolutional + RS |
| Time slicing | **Yes (mandatory)** | No | Yes |
| Power saving | **~90 %** | None | ~80–90 % |
| Net throughput | 5–10 Mbit/s/chan. | up to 24 Mbit/s/chan. | ~1.1 Mbit/s/block |
| Service rate | 100–500 kbit/s | 1–5 Mbit/s | 100–400 kbit/s |
| Indoor / mobile | Strong (MPE-FEC, 4K) | Weak (fixed) | Good |
| Return channel | Cellular | Cellular | Cellular |
| Status | Largely retired | Active DTT | Active in Korea |

**Concluding note:** DVB-H sits between the high-capacity, fixed-reception DVB-T and the low-bandwidth, single-block DMB. It uniquely combines DVB-T's high spectral efficiency with handheld-specific enhancements (Time Slicing + MPE-FEC + 4K).

#### Model Solution to Part B (7 Marks)

**The 4K FFT mode (3409 subcarriers)** is a DVB-H-specific innovation that occupies the middle ground between DVB-T's 2K (1705 subcarriers) and 8K (6817 subcarriers) modes.

**Trade-off analysis:**

| Parameter | 2K | **4K** | 8K |
|---|---|---|---|
| Useful symbol duration T_u | 224 µs | **448 µs** | 896 µs |
| Guard interval (1/4) | 56 µs | **112 µs** | 224 µs |
| Carrier spacing Δf | 4.464 kHz | **2.232 kHz** | 1.116 kHz |
| Max SFN size | ~17 km | **~34 km** | ~67 km |
| Doppler tolerance | **Best** (short T_u) | Medium | Worst |

**Why a compromise?**

- **2K** has *short* symbols → strong against Doppler (good for high-speed trains) but the short guard interval limits the size of the single-frequency network, leading to poor SFN coverage in indoor cells.
- **8K** has *long* symbols (longer guard interval) → excellent for large SFNs but very fragile under Doppler — a fast-moving receiver sees inter-carrier interference.
- **4K** doubles the symbol duration (and guard interval) compared to 2K, **doubling the maximum SFN cell radius** while still halving the symbol duration of 8K, **doubling the Doppler tolerance**. This is the *sweet spot* for handheld mobile-TV reception, which is rarely at > 200 km/h and often needs medium-sized SFN cells for indoor coverage.

**MISO support:** DVB-H's 4K mode was the first DVB variant designed in parallel with **MISO (Alamouti) transmit diversity**. Two co-located or geographically separated transmitters send **space-frequency-block-coded** versions of the same OFDM symbols, using the *Alamouti code* on adjacent subcarrier pairs. The receiver (single antenna) decodes them as a 2×1 MISO system, gaining up to **3 dB of effective SNR** without any extra receiver complexity — a powerful technique to compensate for handheld "rubber-duck" antenna losses in deep fades.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. Examiners expect you to write the *numbers* — 1705/3409/6817 subcarriers, 224/448/896 µs. A qualitative-only answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. MISO discussion must mention **Alamouti** by name and the **3 dB diversity gain** claim; do not just say "MISO is used."
> 3. The *concluding* remark should connect the 4K mode to the *handheld* use case (mid-size SFN, moderate Doppler) — this is the KTU-style "engineering reasoning" line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DVB-H Standard (Module 2)**

- **DVB-H = DVB-T + 3 add-ons** → Time Slicing, MPE-FEC, 4K FFT.
- **Standard reference:** ETSI **EN 302 304** (DVB-H), built on EN 300 401 (DVB-T).
- **Purpose:** Deliver IP-based digital TV and multimedia to *battery-powered handheld terminals* with low power drain and good mobile/indoor robustness.
- **Time Slicing duty cycle:** $d = C/B$, **power-saving factor** = $B/C$. Typical saving: ~**90 %** (B ≫ C).
- **Time Slicing also enables fast channel zapping** (~100–250 ms) by reading the *delta-t* hint from the current burst.
- **MPE-FEC = RS(255, 191) over GF(256)** applied **column-wise** to the *Application Data Table* (up to 1024 rows × 255 columns).
  - Code rate **η = 191/255 ≈ 0.749** → ~**25 % parity overhead**.
  - Corrects up to **32 byte-errors per 255-byte RS codeword**.
  - Provides *virtual time interleaving* — defeats Rayleigh burst fading.
- **4K FFT mode:** 3409 subcarriers, T_u = 448 µs, **sweet-spot** for mobile + medium SFN. Supports **Alamouti MISO** for 3 dB diversity gain.
- **Channel bandwidth:** 5 / 6 / 7 / 8 MHz (UHF, VHF III, L-band).
- **Constellations:** QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM.
- **Encapsulation:** IP datagrams inside MPE sections (RFC 3926), which become MPEG-2 TS packets of 188 B.
- **Return channel:** NOT part of DVB-H itself; uses 3G/4G/5G cellular for interactivity (IPDC = hybrid broadcast + cellular).
- **Related standards:** **DVB-SH** (satellite-to-handheld, S-band), **DVB-NGH / DVB-H2 / DVB-T2-Lite** (successor), **DMB / T-DMB**, **ATSC-M/H**, **MediaFLO**.
- **Typical service bit-rate:** 100 – 500 kbit/s; ~30–50 services per DVB-T channel.
- **Status (2024):** Largely displaced by **LTE/5G broadcast** (eMBMS / FeMBMS / 5G Multicast), but remains the *textbook reference* for the principles of mobile digital broadcasting.

### Memory Tricks for the Exam

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **"3 T's of DVB-H"** | **T**ime Slicing, **T**urbo (RS) FEC (MPE-FEC), **T**housand-and-something (4K FFT ≈ 3 thousand + 409) |
| **"B beats C by 10"** | Bursts are 10× the average rate → 90 % power saving |
| **"191-64 of 255"** | MPE-FEC: 191 data + 64 parity = 255 total; RS(255,191) |
| **"1024 rows, 191 columns of data, 64 of parity"** | MPE-FEC ADT max dimensions |
| **"DVB-H = DVB-T + Mobile"** | Same PHY, link-layer enhancements for mobility |

### Likely 2-Mark KTU Quick-Fire Topics (from Past Pattern)

1. Name the three DVB-H technologies and one-line purpose of each. *(2 M)*
2. What is the duty cycle and power saving if $B = 12 \times C$? *(2 M)*
3. State the MPE-FEC code rate and parity overhead. *(1–2 M)*
4. Why is the 4K mode preferred for DVB-H? *(2 M)*
5. What is the function of the *delta-t* field in Time Slicing? *(1–2 M)*
6. List two advantages of DVB-H over DVB-T. *(2 M)*
7. What is the role of MISO / Alamouti coding in DVB-H? *(2 M)*
8. State the role of INT (IP/MAC Notification Table) in DVB-H. *(2 M)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB Teletext
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB Teletext

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**DVB Teletext** is a digital text-based information delivery service standardized under the **Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB)** family of specifications, primarily defined by the **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)**. It is the digital successor to the analog **VBI (Vertical Blanking Interval) teletext** systems such as Ceefax (BBC), Oracle (ITV), and Antiope (France).

The governing specifications are:

- **ETSI EN 300 706** — *Enhanced Teletext Specification* (the actual data format of the pages, hierarchical/magazine structure, page headers, packet types X/0, X/1…X/25, X/26, X/27, X/28, X/29, X/30, M/29, M/30, etc.)
- **ETSI EN 300 472** — *Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB); Specification for conveying ITU-R System B Teletext in DVB bitstreams* (the transport mechanism that places EN 300 706 teletext inside an **MPEG-2 Transport Stream**).
- **ETSI TS 101 154** — the *Implementation Guidelines* describing how a DVB receiver must decode it.
- **ISO/IEC 13818-1 (MPEG-2 Systems)** — provides the PES/TS container structure in which DVB teletext rides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** DVB Teletext = **EN 300 706 (content format)** carried via **EN 300 472 (transport in MPEG-2 TS as PES packets)**. Examiners frequently test this two-layer model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a Malayalam news channel. Beside the video, a hidden **digital newspaper** is being streamed — share prices, cricket scores, subtitles, subtitles for the hearing-impaired, program guides, even downloadable software updates. You do not "see" it as pixels on screen; instead, your **Set-Top Box (STB)** decodes it and either renders it as a black-text-on-white page (when you press TEXT on the remote) or overlays it as **DVB Subtitles**.

**The real-world analogy:**

| Analogy Element | DVB Teletext Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Newspaper printing press | DVB encoder at the broadcaster head-end |
| Newsstand delivery truck | MPEG-2 Transport Stream (188-byte TS packets over DVB-T/S/C) |
| Newspaper pages in truck | Teletext PES packets (data_PES = EBU teletext) |
| News reader at home | STB / IDTV with teletext decoder (Page=100, Magazine=1) |
| "Press the page number" | Row/Column addressing inside a teletext magazine |

So DVB Teletext is essentially **a text-publishing service riding on top of a digital TV broadcast**, instead of being squeezed into the analog overscan lines as in 1970s Ceefax.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant to remember:** DVB Teletext runs at the **DVB MPEG-2 TS packet rate** of approximately one PES every 40 ms (25 Hz) or 33.3 ms (30 Hz), with each **teletext PES packet carrying 45 data bytes**, corresponding to a fixed bit-rate envelope of **36 kbit/s per active stream** (standard, non-subtitle use).

### 1.3 Evolution from Analog to Digital Teletext

The historical lineage is critical for KTU questions:

1. **1970s — Analog Teletext (ITU-R System B / World System Teletext):** Teletext bits placed in **lines 6–22 and 319–335** of the 625-line analog video signal (the VBI). Bit rate ≈ **6.9375 Mbit/s NRZ** on a 625-line system, but only one row of 360 bits per TV line.
2. **1990s — Enhanced Teletext (EN 300 706):** Increased character repertoire, parallel magazine transmission, **Packet X/26** for Object Display, **Packet X/28** for FLOF (Full-Level One-Floor navigation), **Packet X/29** for magazine organization.
3. **1997 — DVB Teletext (EN 300 472):** Teletext transported as a **PES stream** in MPEG-2 TS. No more VBI dependency; the teletext is a *program element* alongside video, audio, and subtitles.
4. **2000s onward — DVB Subtitling (EN 300 743):** A derivative that uses the same PES/TS plumbing but for **bit-map graphics subtitles** (DVB subtitles) — this is *not* teletext but is often confused with it in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Layer Architecture (Why and How)

DVB Teletext must be understood as **two independent layers**, each defined by its own ETSI standard. The reason for this two-layer design is **backward compatibility** with 20+ years of existing teletext pages and decoders.

**Layer 1 — Content Format (EN 300 706):**
Defines the *magazine* (max 8 magazines per service, hex-addressed 1–8, where 8 is treated as 0), *page* (00–FF hex = 256 pages per magazine, but only 0x00–0x99 typically used = 100 user pages per magazine), *sub-page* (up to 0x3F7F), and the *packet* structure (rows of 45 bytes prefixed by a 5-byte header, total 360 bits per teletext "row").

**Layer 2 — Transport (EN 300 472):**
Defines how those EN 300 706 rows are packed into a **PES (Packetized Elementary Stream)** of stream_id = **0xBD (Private Stream 1)**. The PES is then sliced into **188-byte MPEG-2 TS packets** and multiplexed with video, audio, and other services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five-stage "Why" chain that examiners love:
> Page → Magazine → Packets (X/0…X/30) → PES data_bytes → TS packet payload (184 bytes) → RF modulation (DVB-T COFDM / DVB-S QPSK / DVB-C QAM).

### 2.2 Teletext Packet Types (EN 300 706) — Memory Aid

| Packet Type | Function | Use in DVB Teletext? |
|---|---|---|
| **X/0** | Page header (page number, control bits, charset) | Yes |
| **X/1 … X/25** | Normal text rows (24 rows of 40 characters = 960 chars/page) | Yes |
| **X/26** | Object Display (geometric primitives, DRCS characters) | Yes |
| **X/27** | Linked Pages (FLOF top-level navigation) | Yes |
| **X/28** | FLOF navigation data | Yes |
| **X/29** | Magazine Organization Table (MOT) | Yes |
| **X/30** | Magazine Inventory Page (MIP) — used for fast page discovery | Yes |
| **M/29** | Multipage / Extension for packet 28 | Yes |
| **M/30** | Magazine Inventory (private) | Yes |

Where the **Magazine number X** is a hex digit 1–8 (always excluding 0 in the high nibble of the page tens digit).

### 2.3 The PES Structure (EN 300 472)

The critical EN 300 472 clause states that teletext PES packets carry, after the standard PES header, a **data_identifier = 0x10 … 0x1F** indicating the teletext stream component. Each PES_payload begins with:

$$ \text{pes\_payload} = \underbrace{0x10\text{ to }0x1F}_{\text{data\_identifier}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{0x02}_{\text{EBU teletext sub-id}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{45 \text{ bytes of EN 300 706 data}}_{\text{one teletext packet}} $$

This is the **"46-byte block"** that DVB decoder chips know how to parse. Multiple 46-byte blocks can appear in a single PES, allowing several teletext packets per PES frame.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Teletext row size (EN 300 706) | $N_{row}$ | $45$ bytes $= 360$ bits | bits | One teletext "row" (line equivalent) |
| PES payload per teletext packet | $L_{pes}$ | $46$ bytes | bytes | data_id + sub_id + 45 data bytes |
| Bytes per teletext page | $B_{page}$ | $26 \times 45 = 1170$ bytes | bytes | 1 header row + 24 text rows + 1 termination |
| Pages per magazine | $N_{mag}$ | $100$ (hex 00–99) | pages | Hex page numbers 00 to 99 |
| Magazines per service | $M$ | $8$ | — | Hex 1 to 8, where 8 ≡ 0 |
| Teletext bit rate (full page, 25 Hz) | $R_{tt}$ | $B_{page} \times 8 \times 25 = 234\,000$ | bit/s | Worst case full-page refresh |
| Typical carried bit rate | $R_{typ}$ | $\approx 36\,000$ | bit/s | One PES every ~40 ms with 45 B |
| MPEG-2 TS packet size | $L_{ts}$ | $188$ | bytes | 4-byte header + 184-byte payload |
| TS packet rate (DVB) | $f_{ts}$ | depends on mux; e.g. $27.5 \times 10^6$ | pkt/s | Set by broadcaster |
| DVB-T FFT size (2K mode) | $N_{FFT}$ | $2048$ | carriers | 8K = 8192 |
| DVB-T carrier spacing (8K, 8 MHz) | $\Delta f$ | $\dfrac{8 \times 10^6}{8192} \approx 1116$ | Hz | OFDM sub-carrier spacing |
| DVB-T guard interval (1/4) | $T_g$ | $\dfrac{T_u}{4} \approx 224\ \mu s$ | s | Useful symbol time $T_u = 896\ \mu s$ (8K) |
| Hamming parity per row | — | $2$ per byte | bits | EN 300 706 8/4 Hamming protection |
| VBI teletext bit rate (legacy) | $R_{vbi}$ | $6.9375 \times 10^6$ | bit/s | MUX of one 360-bit row per TV line |
| Page acquisition time (worst) | $t_{acq}$ | $\approx 0.6$ to $4$ | s | Magazine inventory search time |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX safety rule** for markdown tables: never write absolute value \vert x \vert directly. Use \lvert x \rvert or simply state "absolute value of x". Above, the vertical bar has been replaced with the word "per" or the symbol \Vert to keep the markdown table parser intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DVB Teletext still survives in 2024–2025 because of three non-negotiable roles:

1. **Subtitling for the deaf and hard-of-hearing (SDH):** The most-used feature in Europe and increasingly required by EU **Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD)**.
2. **Electronic Program Guide (EPG) supplement:** DVB EPG uses DVB-SI (Service Information, EN 300 469), but teletext often carries parallel plain-text now/next.
3. **Emergency Warning (EWBS) and MHEG-5 interactivity layers** historically ran on top of X/26/X/27 teletext packets in UK/Ireland.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU's "entertainment electronics" context, the examiner expects you to **link DVB Teletext → Set-Top Box architecture → MPEG-2 demux → OSD rendering**, not just recite the ETSI numbers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bytes Required to Carry a Single Teletext Page

A teletext page is, by EN 300 706, composed of **26 packets** (rows): one **X/0** (header) + twenty-four **X/1…X/24** (text body rows) + one **X/30** (magazine inventory termination marker), although the spec allows up to X/25. We derive the total bytes below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of rows per page} &= N_{rows} = 1\ (\text{header}) + 24\ (\text{text}) + 1\ (\text{terminator}) \\
&= 26\ \text{rows} \\
\text{Bytes per row} &= 45\ \text{bytes} \\
\therefore B_{page} &= N_{rows} \times 45 = 26 \times 45 \\
&= 1170\ \text{bytes} \\
\text{In bits:} \quad B_{page}^{bit} &= 1170 \times 8 = 9360\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

The next step is to determine the **time taken to transmit a complete page** at the standard DVB carousel rate of **25 page-equivalents per second** (i.e. one PES per 40 ms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{page} &= B_{page}^{bit} \times 25 \\
&= 9360 \times 25 \\
&= 234\,000\ \text{bit/s} \\
&= 234\ \text{kbit/s (worst-case instantaneous page rate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **peak** bit rate if a broadcaster were dedicating 25 full pages per second to teletext. In practice, a DVB service allocates a much smaller slice — see Derivation 2.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Practical Carried Bit-Rate of a DVB Teletext PES

EN 300 472 says a DVB teletext PES typically carries **one 46-byte block per PES** (the minimum useful payload), and PES packets for a given stream arrive at the **DVB frame rate** of $25\ \text{Hz}$ (PAL countries) or $29.97\ \text{Hz}$ (NTSC). The carried data rate is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{typ} &= (L_{pes\ block} \times 8) \times f_{PES} \\
&= 46 \times 8 \times 25 \\
&= 9200\ \text{bit/s (one block per PES)}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, EN 300 472 also allows **multiple 46-byte blocks per PES**, and the typical broadcaster-allocated envelope is **36 kbit/s** so that several packets can be packed in:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{blocks\ per\ PES} &= \dfrac{R_{target}}{L_{pes\ block} \times 8 \times f_{PES}} \\
&= \dfrac{36\,000}{46 \times 8 \times 25} \\
&= \dfrac{36\,000}{9200} \\
&\approx 3.91 \Rightarrow \text{3 full blocks per PES in practice}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a real DVB teletext stream for a single magazine typically sends **3–4 teletext packets per PES frame**, and the magazine inventory (X/30) cycles through the entire set of pages every 20–30 s.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — OFDM Sub-Carrier Spacing (DVB-T 8K, 8 MHz)

Although not strictly "teletext maths", the KTU 2024 examiner often blends OFDM parameters with DVB-Teletext transport. Deriving the sub-carrier spacing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bandwidth } B &= 8\ \text{MHz} = 8 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz} \\
N_{FFT} &= 8192\ \text{(8K mode)} \\
\Delta f &= \dfrac{B}{N_{FFT}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^6}{8192} \\
&= 976.5625\ \text{Hz} \quad \text{(commonly rounded to } 1116\ \text{ Hz when including useful + guard overheads)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A more precise textbook value (with the useful symbol period $T_u = 1/\Delta f$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \dfrac{1}{976.5625} \approx 1.024\ \text{ms} \\
T_g &= \dfrac{T_u}{4} = 256\ \mu s \\
T_{symbol} &= T_u + T_g = 1.024\ \text{ms} + 0.256\ \text{ms} = 1.28\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hamming (8,4) Error Protection of a Teletext Row

EN 300 706 uses an **8/4 Hamming code** to protect the 5-byte address/control of every row (magazine, row, page-tens, page-units) plus parity. The useful data per byte is 4 bits, the protection overhead is 4 bits, giving the 8/4 ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Hamming} &= \dfrac{4}{8} = 0.5 \\
\text{Protected bits per row} &= 5 \times 4 = 20\ \text{bits (page address)} \\
\text{Unprotected bits per row} &= 40 \times 8 = 320\ \text{bits (the 40 displayable text characters)} \\
\text{Total per row} &= 20 + 320 = 360\ \text{bits} = 45\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The error-correction capability of the (8,4) Hamming code is **single-bit error correction + double-bit error detection (SEC-DED)** per protected byte, which is exactly the noise immunity profile of cable/satellite paths where a single bit-flip is the most common failure mode.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Total Storage of a Teletext Service

The maximum theoretical content size of a single DVB teletext service is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total\ pages} &= M \times N_{mag} \times N_{subpages} \\
&= 8 \times 100 \times (0x3F7F + 1) \\
&= 8 \times 100 \times 16\,256 \\
&= 13\,004\,800\ \text{pages (theoretical maximum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, a service carries **< 5000 pages** and uses a magazine inventory (X/30) to signal which ones actually exist.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set B]**
> *Define DVB Teletext. State the two ETSI specifications that jointly describe the DVB Teletext system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DVB Teletext is the digital successor of analog VBI teletext and is a text-based information service carried inside an MPEG-2 Transport Stream of a DVB broadcast. It is jointly defined by:
1. **ETSI EN 300 706** — the *Enhanced Teletext Specification* that defines the page, magazine, packet, and character format (the *content layer*).
2. **ETSI EN 300 472** — the *Specification for conveying ITU-R System B Teletext in DVB bitstreams* that defines how EN 300 706 rows are wrapped as a **Private Stream 1 PES (stream_id 0xBD)** inside the MPEG-2 TS (the *transport layer*).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]**
> *What is the role of the X/30 packet in a DVB Teletext magazine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Packet X/30** is the *Magazine Inventory Page (MIP)*. Its role is to enable **fast page acquisition** in the receiver. Every time the X/30 packet is broadcast, it lists all the pages currently available in its magazine along with their **sub-code** identifiers. The STB teletext decoder can scan the X/30 stream of all 8 magazines and build a **page inventory cache**, so that when the user requests a page (e.g. 412), the decoder already knows whether the page exists, which sub-page to fetch next, and how to jump directly to it. This avoids the slow 20–30 s linear scan of the older analog Ceefax decoders.

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end architecture of a DVB Teletext system from the broadcaster head-end to the receiver. Clearly identify the role of **EN 300 706** and **EN 300 472** at each stage.
> **(b) (7 marks)** Derive the bit rate required to carry a full DVB Teletext page of 26 rows continuously at 25 Hz, and compare it with the practical carried bit rate of ≈ 36 kbit/s typically allocated in a DVB service. Comment on the engineering significance of this gap.

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Stage 1 — Content authoring (head-end):** Teletext pages are produced by an editorial system (offline PC-based teletext authoring) and fed as raw text + graphic commands to the **EN 300 706 encoder** (3 marks).

**Stage 2 — Content layer (EN 300 706):** The encoder organises pages into **8 magazines × 100 pages = 800 user pages**, generates the magazine inventory (X/30), and emits **360-bit teletext rows** (header + 24 text rows + FLOF/DRCS extensions). The output is a stream of 45-byte EN 300 706 packets (1 mark).

**Stage 3 — Transport layer (EN 300 472):** The EN 300 706 stream is wrapped inside a **PES** with `stream_id = 0xBD` (Private Stream 1) and `data_identifier = 0x10 to 0x1F`, `sub_id = 0x02`. This PES is the actual DVB Teletext elementary stream (1 mark).

**Stage 4 — Multiplex and modulate:** The PES is sliced into 188-byte MPEG-2 TS packets and multiplexed with video, audio, and other services in the **DVB MPEG-2 TS multiplexer**. The TS is then modulated — DVB-T (COFDM), DVB-S (QPSK), or DVB-C (QAM) — and broadcast over the RF channel (1 mark).

**Stage 5 — Receiver (STB):** The STB tuner, channel decoder, and TS demux extract the PES, the **EN 300 706 decoder** reconstructs pages from the 360-bit rows, builds the page cache (with X/30-driven inventory), and the **OSD compositor** either renders a full-screen text page or overlays the data as **DVB subtitles** on the video (1 mark).

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation — bit rate for continuous full page at 25 Hz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{page} &= 26\ \text{rows} \times 45\ \text{bytes} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} \\
&= 26 \times 45 \times 8 \\
&= 9360\ \text{bits} \\
R_{full} &= 9360 \times 25 = 234\,000\ \text{bit/s} = 234\ \text{kbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare with 36 kbit/s practical allocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gap} &= 234 - 36 = 198\ \text{kbit/s} \\
\text{Ratio} &= \dfrac{234}{36} \approx 6.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering significance (3 marks):**
1. The 36 kbit/s allocation is far below the worst-case 234 kbit/s because **browsers do not need every page to be transmitted continuously**; they are *carouselled* (cyclically repeated). With a 25 s carousel, only 1 of 25 page-equivalents is needed per second, giving roughly $\dfrac{234}{25} \approx 9.4\ \text{kbit/s}$ for one magazine — and the 36 kbit/s comfortably covers **3–4 magazines of 100 pages each**.
2. DVB Teletext is **bandwidth-efficient** compared to a dedicated TV sub-channel: 36 kbit/s is less than 0.04 % of a 100 Mbit/s DVB-T multiplex, yet delivers hundreds of pages.
3. The receiver uses the **X/30 magazine inventory** to avoid blind scanning, so the user-perceived latency remains under 1 s despite the low bit rate.

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a) (7 marks)** Explain the hierarchical structure of a DVB Teletext service: Service → Magazine → Page → Sub-page → Packet. For each level, give the addressing range as per EN 300 706.
> **(b) (7 marks)** Describe the internal structure of a single 45-byte EN 300 706 teletext packet. Explain the role of the Hamming (8,4) protection bits and the difference between **Packet X/0** (page header) and **Packet X/26** (Object Display).

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks

The hierarchy defined by EN 300 706 is as follows (1 mark per level):

| Level | Addressing | Range | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Service** | `original_network_id + transport_stream_id + service_id` (DVB-SI) | One per PMT entry | The DVB Teletext elementary stream listed in the PMT with `stream_type = 0x06` (private PES) |
| **Magazine** | Hex digit 1–8 (where 8 ≡ 0), present in **every packet address** | **8 magazines** | A logical container of up to 100 user pages. Signalled by the top 4 bits of the page tens digit. |
| **Page** | Two hex digits — page tens (PT) and page units (PU) | **0x00 to 0x99 = 100 user pages per magazine** (0x9A–0xFF reserved for system/hidden) | The basic addressable unit; the user types page number (e.g. 412) on the remote |
| **Sub-page** | Two bytes — sub-code tens and units, 0x0000 to 0x3F7F | **16 256 sub-pages per page** | Used for **rolling news**, sports tickers, and time-varying data; receivers can auto-cycle |
| **Packet** | Magazine + row number (X/0…X/30) | **Up to 31 packets per page** | The 360-bit (45-byte) atomic transport unit |

Note the **8,4 Hamming code** on the 5-byte address portion guarantees that even a single bit-flip in the page address does not result in a wrong page display.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Internal structure of a 45-byte EN 300 706 packet (3 marks):**

| Bytes | Field | Length | Hamming-protected? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1, 2 | **Magazine + row address** (3-bit mag, 5-bit row, with run-in and clock run-in replaced in DVB) | 16 bits | Yes (8/4) |
| 3, 4, 5 | **Page number + sub-code + control bits** (PT, PU, CU, CUF, etc.) | 24 bits | Yes (8/4) |
| 6–45 | **Data payload** — 40 text characters (8 bits each) for X/1…X/25; structured fields for X/26, X/27, X/28, X/29, X/30 | 320 bits | No Hamming; optional CRC for some packets |

**Hamming (8,4) protection (2 marks):** The first 5 bytes of the row use **4 data bits + 4 parity bits per byte** (8/4 Hamming). This provides **single-bit error correction and double-bit error detection (SEC-DED)** on the address/control portion. The protection is deliberately limited to the address/control because if a page is mis-addressed, the entire page is rendered wrong; whereas a single character error in the body is visually tolerable and can be handled by the decoder's character ROM substitution.

**Packet X/0 vs Packet X/26 (2 marks):**
- **Packet X/0 (Page Header):** Carries the page number, **control bits C4–C14** (erase page, news flash, subtitle, suppressed header, update indicator, serial/parallel magazine transmission, character set selection), and a 32-bit **status display row** for the top of the page (channel name, time, date). It is always the first packet of a page and is *not* displayed as part of the page body.
- **Packet X/26 (Object Display):** Carries *geometric* data — **Active Objects** and **Adaptive Objects** — used to draw filled rectangles, lines, and **Dynamically Re-definable Characters (DRCS)**. Unlike X/1…X/25 (which carry fixed 8-bit character codes from the G0/G1 character ROM), X/26 carries raw bit-map data that the STB downloads into a DRCS RAM and uses to render **logos, language-specific glyphs, or simple graphics** (e.g. the BBC "Ceefax" ball logo or Indian-language matras).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **DVB Teletext** with **DVB Subtitles (EN 300 743)**. DVB Subtitles use *bit-map graphics PES* (`stream_type = 0x06` with `data_id = 0x20`), are pixel-accurate, and support multiple regions, colours, and anti-aliasing. DVB Teletext is character-based, fixed-resolution, and limited to 40 × 24 characters. Examiners specifically look for this distinction in 14-mark answers. Also remember: **DVB Teletext is NOT a VBI system** — the analog-VBI bit rate of 6.9375 Mbit/s does *not* apply to DVB Teletext, which uses the MPEG-2 PES envelope.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- DVB Teletext = **EN 300 706 (content)** + **EN 300 472 (transport over MPEG-2 TS)**.
- Carried as a **PES with stream_id = 0xBD (Private Stream 1)** and a **data_identifier of 0x10 to 0x1F**, sub_id **0x02**.
- One teletext row = **45 bytes = 360 bits**; one full page = **26 rows = 1170 bytes**.
- **8 magazines × 100 user pages × up to 16 256 sub-pages** per DVB Teletext service.
- **X/0** = page header, **X/1…X/25** = 40-character text rows, **X/26** = objects/DRCS, **X/27** = linked pages, **X/28** = FLOF navigation, **X/29** = magazine organisation, **X/30** = magazine inventory (fast page discovery).
- **8/4 Hamming (SEC-DED)** on the 5-byte address/control portion; data payload is unprotected.
- Practical carried bit rate ≈ **36 kbit/s**; worst-case full-page-at-25-Hz rate ≈ **234 kbit/s**; the gap is bridged by **carousel transmission** using the X/30 inventory.
- It is **NOT the same** as DVB Subtitles (EN 300 743, data_id 0x20) or DVB-SI EPG (EN 300 469).
- DVB Teletext is also **not** tied to the analog VBI lines — it lives in the digital MPEG-2 PES.
- Receiver functions: TS demux → PES parser (0xBD, 0x10–0x1F, 0x02) → EN 300 706 decoder → OSD compositor.
- Key physical constants to memorise: MPEG-2 TS packet = **188 bytes**; DVB-T 8K sub-carrier spacing ≈ **1116 Hz**; useful symbol period $T_u \approx 896\ \mu s$ (8K, 8 MHz); guard interval $T_g = T_u/4 \approx 224\ \mu s$.
- For KTU 14-mark answers, always **draw the head-end to STB block diagram** and **show at least one numeric derivation** (typically the bit-rate or page-acquisition calculation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB Subtitling System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB Subtitling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — DVB SUBTITLING SYSTEM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **DVB Subtitling System** is a digital television closed-captioning and teletext-replacement framework standardized under **ETSI EN 300 743** (originally published by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute in 1997, with subsequent revisions). It specifies the carriage, signalling, and presentation of graphical and textual subtitle data within a **MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS)** alongside compressed video and audio elementary streams.

Formally, it is a **bitmapped, region-based graphical overlay system** in which pre-rendered text and graphics are transmitted as **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES) packets** carrying *Display Definition Segments*, *Page Composition Segments*, *Region Composition Segments*, *CLUT Definition Segments*, and *Object Data Segments*. These segments are interpreted by the Set-Top Box (STB) / Integrated Digital Television (iDTV) decoder to reconstruct pixel-accurate subtitle overlays on top of the decoded video framebuffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DVB Subtitling System is the de-facto European standard for digital TV subtitles, replacing the legacy analog teletext (WST – World System Teletext, ETSI EN 300 706) on digital broadcasts because teletext is poorly suited to MPEG-2 packetized transmission.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Multilingual Stickynote on a Photo"

Imagine you receive a printed **photograph (the compressed TV picture)** in the mail every 1/25th of a second. Along with the photograph comes a set of **transparent stickynote instructions (the DVB subtitle segments)** that say:

- "Take this region of the photo (top-third, middle)."
- "Use these colours (red text, black border)."
- "Write this word in that region, in this font style, at this time."

You (the **IRD — Integrated Receiver Decoder**) peel the stickynotes off, paste them onto the correct spatial region of the photo, and show the combined picture to the viewer. A new batch of stickynotes arrives for the next scene, replacing or modifying the previous ones. **The stickynotes carry NO information about the photo itself** — they are purely an *overlay instruction set*. That, in essence, is the DVB Subtitling System.

### 1.3 Why the Industry Migrated from Teletext to DVB Subtitles

| Aspect | Analog Teletext (WST) | DVB Subtitling (EN 300 743) |
|---|---|---|
| **Carriage** | VBI (Vertical Blanking Interval) of analog CVBS | PES packets inside MPEG-2 TS |
| **Resolution** | Fixed 480 × 250 (UK system) | Up to 1920 × 1080 (HD) |
| **Colour depth** | Limited CLUT (8–16 colours) | 32-bit ARGB with 256-entry CLUT |
| **Region freedom** | Strict row-column character grid | Arbitrary rectangles anywhere on screen |
| **Anti-aliasing** | None (blocky) | Sub-pixel rendered graphics |
| **Language** | Single per page | Multiple concurrent subtitle streams |
| **HD support** | No | Native |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants and Boundaries (per ETSI EN 300 743 v1.6.1):**
> - Maximum **subtitle stream count per program**: theoretically limited by PSI/SI bandwidth, practically **up to 32–64 streams** (e.g., 32 language audio + 32 subtitle tracks).
> - Display size signalled in **Display Definition Segment (DDS)**: width ∈ [1, 4096] pixels, height ∈ [1, 4096] pixels (encoded as 16-bit values).
> - Number of regions per composition page: **up to 64**.
> - Number of objects per region: **up to 16**.
> - CLUT entries: **256 entries per CLUT segment**, multiple CLUTs allowed.
> - Page Composition timeout: default **5 seconds**; if no PCS received, the page is erased.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layering of the DVB Subtitle Stack

The DVB Subtitling System is hierarchically organized into **five logical layers**, each carried as a syntactically distinct PES packet segment type. A higher layer *references* lower layers — for example, a Page Composition Segment *points to* Region Composition Segments, which in turn *point to* Object Data Segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Logical Layers (in dependency order):**
> 1. **Display Definition Segment (DDS)** — *defines the virtual display canvas (width × height) on which subtitle coordinates are computed.*
> 2. **Page Composition Segment (PCS)** — *defines a "page" (one full screen of subtitles), its timeout, and the list of regions with their screen positions.*
> 3. **Region Composition Segment (RCS)** — *defines a region (a rectangular area) and lists the objects (graphic icons / text glyphs) within it, with their offsets.*
> 4. **CLUT Definition Segment (CDS)** — *carries the Colour Look-Up Table mapping 8-bit indices to actual ARGB colours.*
> 5. **Object Data Segment (ODS)** — *carries the actual run-length-encoded pixel data of each graphic object.*

## 2.2 Operational Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — PES Packet Carriage
The broadcaster **encodes each segment type** as a *private_stream_1* PES packet (or *private_stream_2* in some implementations) with the **stream_id = 0xBD** (private stream 1) and a private_data field whose first byte is the **subtitling_descriptor** data. Each PES packet is identified via the **PMT (Program Map Table)** entry, which contains a *subtitling_descriptor* (tag **0x59**) listing the **composition_page_id** and **ancillary_page_id** plus ISO 639 language code.

### Step 2 — Display Definition Handshake
Upon channel change, the IRD receives the **DDS first**. This sets the *display_width* and *display_height* parameters (in pixels). All subsequent region/object coordinates are interpreted relative to this display rectangle. This indirection allows the same logical subtitle to scale gracefully from SD (720×576) to HD (1920×1080) or UHD (3840×2160) by simply re-issuing a new DDS.

### Step 3 — Page Composition
A **PCS** declares a *composition_page_id* (16-bit, 0x0001–0x0FFF for normal subtitles; 0x1000+ for teletext-emulated subtitles) and a *page_composition_state* (normal or acquisition point). It contains a list of **regions**, each with:
- *region_id* (8-bit, 0–255)
- *region_horizontal_address* (16-bit, 0–4095)
- *region_vertical_address* (16-bit, 0–4095)
- *region_width* and *region_height* (16-bit each)

### Step 4 — Region Composition
An **RCS** references a *region_id* and lists **objects** within it. Each object entry contains:
- *object_id* (8-bit, 0–255)
- *object_horizontal_offset* (16-bit)
- *object_vertical_offset* (16-bit)
- *object_foreground_pixel_index* (8-bit) → CLUT index for non-zero pixels
- *object_background_pixel_index* (8-bit) → CLUT index for transparent/cut-out pixels

### Step 5 — CLUT Definition
Each **CDS** provides up to 256 ARGB entries. With **8-bit CLUT indices**, a region can use 2-, 4-, 16-, or 256-colour depth, selected at region-creation time. The CDS *id* binds it to specific region/object references.

### Step 6 — Object Data Decoding (RLE)
Each **ODS** contains the **pixel data of one object** compressed via a simple **2-D run-length encoding** algorithm. The algorithm exploits the fact that subtitles are mostly background-colour (cut-out) with sparse foreground glyphs. The decoder walks the pixel grid left-to-right, top-to-bottom, emitting 8-bit *next_pixel_code* values that reference the CLUT.

> [!NOTE]
> **RLE Decoding Pseudologic (verbal):** For each pixel row, repeat: read an 8-bit *number_of_pixels* (N); if N == 0 → end of row; if N != 0 → read *N* pixels, each 2/4/8/16-bit depending on *object_coding_method*; toggle the colour state if *object_coding_method* indicates 2-colour (foreground ↔ background).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Display canvas width | $D_W \in \mathbb{Z}^+,\ 1 \le D_W \le 4096$ | pixels | Signalled in **DDS** as 16-bit unsigned |
| Display canvas height | $D_H \in \mathbb{Z}^+,\ 1 \le D_H \le 4096$ | pixels | Signalled in **DDS** as 16-bit unsigned |
| Number of regions per page | $N_R \le 64$ | count | ETSI EN 300 743 cap |
| Number of objects per region | $N_O \le 16$ | count | ETSI EN 300 743 cap |
| Number of CLUT entries | $N_C \le 256$ | count | 8-bit index space |
| Pixel rate of subtitle | $f_{px} = D_W \cdot D_H \cdot f_{frame}$ | pixels/sec | where $f_{frame} \in \{24,25,30,50,60\}$ |
| Aspect-ratio-scaled region X | $x_{screen} = x_{logical} \cdot \dfrac{W_{screen}}{D_W}$ | pixels | Used when DDS ≠ screen resolution |
| Aspect-ratio-scaled region Y | $y_{screen} = y_{logical} \cdot \dfrac{H_{screen}}{D_H}$ | pixels | Used when DDS ≠ screen resolution |
| Bit-rate per CLUT (ARGB-32) | $R_{CLUT} = 256 \cdot 32 = 8192$ | bits = 1 KiB | Static unless palette changes |
| Subtitle stream PID | Signalled in **PMT** as elementary_PID | 13-bit | Reused with stream_id = 0xBD |
| Language code | ISO 639-1 / 639-2, 3-byte | bytes | Carried in subtitling_descriptor |
| Composition page state | $S_{PCS} \in \{\text{normal, acquisition point}\}$ | enum | Determines decoder behaviour |
| Default page timeout | $T_{page} = 5$ s (typical broadcaster default) | seconds | Erasing timeout after last PCS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The DVB subtitling system is used not only for human-language subtitles but also for **closed captioning for the deaf/hard-of-hearing (SDH)**, **audio description channels**, **emergency alerts (EAS via subtitling streams)**, **interactive MHEG applications**, and **HbbTV red-button** trigger overlays in modern Hybrid Broadcast Broadband TV receivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — How a Single Subtitle Line Reaches the Screen

This derivation shows the *complete data path* from broadcaster studio to display pixels, including every numerical scaling and address translation. We assume **one subtitle line of "Hello"** in English on an HD DVB-T2 broadcast at **1920 × 1080** with display_definition of **720 × 576** (broadcast in SD coordinates, scaled by the IRD).

---

### Step 1 — PMT Signalling (PSI Parsing)

The broadcaster's MPEG-2 **Transport Stream** carries a PMT for the current service. The PMT contains one elementary stream entry with *stream_type* = **0x06** (PES private data) and a descriptor loop including a **subtitling_descriptor** with tag **0x59**:

$$
\text{TAG} = 0x59,\quad \text{length}=8,\quad \text{ISO\_639\_lang} = \text{"eng"},\quad \text{type}=0x10,\quad \text{composition\_page\_id}=0x0001,\quad \text{ancillary\_page\_id}=0x0001
$$

This tells the IRD: "The PES packets on PID = **0x0118** carrying private_stream_1 (stream_id 0xBD) contain the English subtitle stream referenced by composition page 0x0001."

### Step 2 — DDS Interpretation

The first PES payload contains a Display Definition Segment. The IRD reads:

$$
D_W = 720 \text{ pixels},\quad D_H = 576 \text{ pixels}
$$

It then computes the **display_window_definition_flags** which control whether subtitles respect the full frame or are inset within a "safe area." For our example, *display_window_flag* = 0 → subtitles may use the full 720×576 logical canvas.

### Step 3 — PCS Translation to Screen Pixels

The PCS declares one region (region_id = 0x01) at logical position:

$$
x_{logical} = 100,\quad y_{logical} = 450,\quad W_R = 320,\quad H_R = 60
$$

The IRD applies aspect-ratio scaling because the actual screen is 1920×1080 but the DDS specifies 720×576. Assuming the broadcaster intends the subtitle to occupy the same *fractional* position on a 16:9 display:

$$
x_{screen} = x_{logical} \cdot \frac{W_{screen}}{D_W} = 100 \cdot \frac{1920}{720} = 100 \cdot 2.6667 = 266.67 \text{ px}
$$

$$
y_{screen} = y_{logical} \cdot \frac{H_{screen}}{D_H} = 450 \cdot \frac{1080}{576} = 450 \cdot 1.875 = 843.75 \text{ px}
$$

$$
W_{R,screen} = 320 \cdot 2.6667 = 853.33 \text{ px},\quad H_{R,screen} = 60 \cdot 1.875 = 112.5 \text{ px}
$$

The IRD rounds these to integer pixel boundaries for the on-screen framebuffer.

### Step 4 — RCS Object Layout

The Region Composition Segment for region 0x01 declares one object (object_id = 0x01) at offset (0, 0) within the region — i.e., the object occupies the full region. The *object_coding_method* = 0x00 (bitmap), and *object_type* indicates a graphical glyph run.

### Step 5 — CLUT Resolution

The CDS binds CLUT_id = 0 to this region. It contains, for example:

$$
\text{CLUT[0]} = (0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0) \quad\text{(fully transparent — cut-out / background)}
$$

$$
\text{CLUT[1]} = (255,\ 255,\ 255,\ 255) \quad\text{(opaque white — glyph fill)}
$$

$$
\text{CLUT[2]} = (0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 255) \quad\text{(opaque black — outline)}
$$

These are 32-bit ARGB tuples, byte-ordered **A, R, G, B** in the bitstream.

### Step 6 — ODS RLE Decoding

The ODS payload is a sequence of *runs*. For a 2-bit-per-pixel object (4-colour glyph), the RLE stream for the letter "H" might be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:}&\quad \text{codes} = [0,2,2,0,0,0] \\
&\quad \text{→ "draw 2 background pixels, 2 foreground pixels, then 0 (end-of-row)"} \\
\text{Row 1:}&\quad \text{codes} = [1,1,0,2,2,0,0] \\
&\quad \text{→ "1 background, 1 foreground, 2 background, 2 foreground, end-of-row"} \\
\vdots&\quad \text{(repeats for full glyph height)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The decoder fills a bitmap of size $W_{obj} \times H_{obj}$, then blits it into the framebuffer at the region's screen position computed in Step 3, with each pixel's 8-bit CLUT index being translated to the corresponding ARGB tuple.

### Step 7 — Composition & Blending

The IRD's compositor blends the subtitle bitmap on top of the decoded video YUV framebuffer using the alpha channel from CLUT. Pixels with alpha = 0 are **not** drawn (true transparency), so the original video shows through. The result is presented on screen at 60 Hz progressive, with subtitle pages updated whenever a new PCS/ODS triplet arrives.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Object Coding Methods

| Code | Method | Bits/pixel | Colours | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | 2-bit indexed | 2 | 2 | Simple text glyphs (white on black) |
| 0x01 | 4-bit indexed | 4 | 4 | Coloured text with antialiasing |
| 0x02 | 8-bit indexed | 8 | 256 | Anti-aliased glyphs, icons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *object_coding_method* is signalled in the RCS. It is **not** per-pixel — a single object uses one method throughout, so an entire glyph must be rendered with the same bit-depth. For high-quality anti-aliasing (e.g., 256 grey levels of font edge smoothing), 8-bit CLUT entries with a graduated alpha ramp are used.

---

## 3.3 Subtitle Stream Identification Equation

Given a *subtitling_descriptor* in the PMT, the complete stream identification is the 4-tuple:

$$
S_{subtitle} = \langle \text{PID},\ \text{ISO\_639\_lang},\ \text{composition\_page\_id},\ \text{ancillary\_page\_id}\rangle
$$

A single PID can carry **multiple** logical subtitle streams distinguished by *composition_page_id*. The IRD picks the one matching the user's selected language.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the DVB subtitling system from broadcaster encoder to display pixels, showing all five segment types, their logical relationships, and the MPEG-2 transport wrapper.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Broadcast Encoder / Subtitle Authoring Tool] --> B[Subtitle ES Muxer]
    B --> C[MPEG-2 PES Packets stream_id 0xBD]
    C --> D[MPEG-2 Transport Stream Multiplexer]
    D --> E[Modulator DVB-T or DVB-S2 or DVB-C]
    E --> F[RF Channel]
    F --> G[IRD or iDTV Tuner and Demodulator]
    G --> H[MPEG-2 TS Demux]
    H --> I[PMT Parser]
    I --> J[Subtitle PES Filter by PID]
    J --> K[Segment Type Dispatcher]
    K --> L1[DDS Display Definition Segment]
    K --> L2[PCS Page Composition Segment]
    K --> L3[RCS Region Composition Segment]
    K --> L4[CLUT Definition Segment]
    K --> L5[ODS Object Data Segment]
    L1 --> M[Display Canvas Register D_W D_H]
    L2 --> N[Page Composition State Machine]
    L3 --> O[Region Object List]
    L4 --> P[Colour Look Up Table Memory]
    L5 --> Q[RLE Bitmap Decoder]
    M --> R[Compositor and Blender]
    N --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Subtitle Bitmap Overlay]
    S --> T[Video Framebuffer YUV]
    T --> U[Display Panel HDMI or LVDS]
    U --> V((Viewer))
```

## 4.2 Segment Dependency Graph

This shows the strict hierarchical referencing: DDS → PCS → RCS → CDS & ODS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DDS[Display Definition Segment DDS] --> PCS1[Page Composition Segment PCS page 1]
    DDS --> PCS2[Page Composition Segment PCS page 2]
    PCS1 --> RCS1[Region Composition Segment RCS region 1]
    PCS1 --> RCS2[Region Composition Segment RCS region 2]
    RCS1 --> ODS1[Object Data Segment ODS object 1]
    RCS1 --> ODS2[Object Data Segment ODS object 2]
    RCS2 --> ODS3[Object Data Segment ODS object 3]
    RCS1 --> CLUT[CLUT Definition Segment CDS]
    RCS2 --> CLUT
    PCS2 --> RCS3[Region Composition Segment RCS region 3]
    RCS3 --> ODS4[Object Data Segment ODS object 4]
    RCS3 --> CLUT
```

## 4.3 PES Packet Structure (Logical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PES1[MPEG-2 PES Packet Header] --> PES2[stream_id 0xBD]
    PES2 --> PES3[PES_packet_length]
    PES3 --> PES4[PES_header_data_length]
    PES4 --> PES5[Optional PES_extension]
    PES5 --> PES6[Private Data Payload]
    PES6 --> SEG1[Segment 1: DDS or PCS or RCS or CDS or ODS]
    SEG1 --> SEG2[Segment 2: another type]
    SEG2 --> SEG3[Segment N: end of PES]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtitle descriptor** is a **PMT-level** descriptor (tag 0x59), NOT a PES-level descriptor. It signals the language and page IDs of the subtitle stream referenced by that PID. Students commonly confuse these two locations in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the DVB Subtitling System and list the five segment types that constitute a DVB subtitle elementary stream.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DVB Subtitling System, standardized under **ETSI EN 300 743**, is a digital television overlay system for carrying textual and graphical subtitle data as **Packetized Elementary Streams (PES)** within an MPEG-2 Transport Stream. It is the digital replacement for analog teletext (WST) and supports **multi-language closed captioning** in SD, HD, and UHD broadcasts.

The five segment types carried in the PES payload are:

1. **DDS — Display Definition Segment:** Specifies the virtual display canvas dimensions (width and height) in pixels.
2. **PCS — Page Composition Segment:** Declares a subtitle page, its timeout, and the list of regions with screen coordinates.
3. **RCS — Region Composition Segment:** Defines a region and lists the objects within it with their pixel offsets.
4. **CDS — CLUT Definition Segment:** Carries the Colour Look-Up Table mapping 8-bit pixel indices to 32-bit ARGB colours.
5. **ODS — Object Data Segment:** Carries the run-length-encoded pixel bitmap of a single graphic object (text glyph or icon).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently write "teltext segments" or omit **CDS** because it is conceptually optional if a default palette is assumed. ETSI EN 300 743 mandates that all five types *may* be present, and the CDS is required whenever non-default colours are used. Marks are deducted for missing CDS.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain how the DVB subtitling descriptor (tag 0x59) in the PMT is used to identify a subtitle stream and its language.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **subtitling_descriptor** is carried inside the descriptor loop of an elementary stream entry in the **Program Map Table (PMT)** of the MPEG-2 Transport Stream. Its fields are:

- **descriptor_tag = 0x59** (identifies the descriptor type)
- **descriptor_length** (8-bit)
- **ISO_639_language_code** (24 bits, e.g., "eng" for English, "mal" for Malayalam, "hin" for Hindi)
- **subtitling_type** (8 bits, e.g., 0x10 = DVB subtitles for hearing-impaired, 0x20 = teletext-based)
- **composition_page_id** (16 bits, identifies the page to be displayed)
- **ancillary_page_id** (16 bits, identifies auxiliary data pages, e.g., for teletext-emulated subtitles)

The combination of the **elementary_PID** (carrying the PES packets) plus the **composition_page_id** uniquely identifies a single logical subtitle stream. The IRD's demux filters PES packets by PID and then dispatches the segments to the subtitle decoder, which selects the appropriate *composition_page_id* matching the user's chosen language.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### Q.A. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Module 2 Internal Choice A (14 marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the DVB Subtitling System. Describe the role of the MPEG-2 PES layer and the subtitling descriptor in the PMT.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the structure of a Page Composition Segment (PCS) and a Region Composition Segment (RCS). Show how region coordinates are translated to actual screen pixels when the display definition does not match the physical screen resolution.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** above for the full Mermaid block diagram.)

**Architecture Description:**

The DVB Subtitling System operates in **three tiers**:

**Tier 1 — MPEG-2 Transport Layer:** The subtitle elementary stream is carried in a **Transport Stream (TS)** packetized into 188-byte TS packets. The TS is identified by a unique **PID** in the PMT. The PMT descriptor loop contains the **subtitling_descriptor (tag 0x59)** which binds the PID to a specific language and composition page.

**Tier 2 — PES Layer:** The TS payload is reassembled into **PES packets** with **stream_id = 0xBD** (private_stream_1). Each PES packet may contain one or more **subtitle segments** (DDS, PCS, RCS, CDS, ODS).

**Tier 3 — Segment Layer:** The IRD parses the segments, builds a display canvas of size $D_W \times D_H$ (from DDS), composes a page (from PCS), and renders the regions and objects (from RCS/ODS) using the CLUT (from CDS) to overlay the decoded video.

**Role of MPEG-2 PES Layer:** PES provides **synchronization** via Presentation Time Stamps (PTS) so that each subtitle page is displayed at the correct video frame. The PES also allows **private data insertion** via the private_data field, which is exactly where the subtitle segments reside.

**Role of Subtitling Descriptor:** The descriptor is the **control signalling** that tells the IRD: "This PID carries DVB subtitles, in this language, on this page." Without it, the IRD cannot distinguish a subtitle stream from any other private PES stream.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Page Composition Segment (PCS) Structure:**

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| *sync_byte* | 8 bits | 0x0F — identifies DVB subtitle segment |
| *segment_type* | 8 bits | 0x10 for PCS |
| *page_id* | 16 bits | Identifies the composition page (0x0001–0x0FFF) |
| *segment_length* | 16 bits | Bytes remaining in segment |
| *page_timeout* | 8 bits | Seconds before page is erased if no new PCS |
| *page_version_number* | 4 bits | Increments on PCS update |
| *page_state* | 2 bits | Normal or acquisition point |
| *region_count* | 8 bits | Number of regions (0–64) |
| For each region: | | |
| *region_id* | 8 bits | 0–255 |
| *region_horizontal_address* | 16 bits | X in DDS coordinates |
| *region_vertical_address* | 16 bits | Y in DDS coordinates |

**Region Composition Segment (RCS) Structure:**

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| *sync_byte* | 8 bits | 0x0F |
| *segment_type* | 8 bits | 0x11 for RCS |
| *page_id* | 16 bits | Matches PCS |
| *segment_length* | 16 bits | |
| *region_id* | 8 bits | Matches PCS region |
| *region_version_number* | 4 bits | |
| *region_fill_flag* | 1 bit | Fills with background CLUT index? |
| *region_width* | 16 bits | In pixels |
| *region_height* | 16 bits | In pixels |
| *object_count* | 8 bits | 0–16 |
| For each object: | | |
| *object_id* | 8 bits | |
| *object_horizontal_offset* | 16 bits | |
| *object_vertical_offset* | 16 bits | |
| *object_foreground_pixel_index* | 8 bits | |
| *object_background_pixel_index* | 8 bits | |

**Coordinate Translation to Screen Pixels:**

The DDS declares a logical canvas of $(D_W, D_H) = (720, 576)$ for an SD broadcast. The IRD's physical panel is $(W_s, H_s) = (1920, 1080)$. To preserve the *fractional* position of the subtitle, the IRD applies a uniform scaling:

$$
x_{screen} = \text{round}\!\left( x_{logical} \cdot \frac{W_s}{D_W} \right)
$$

$$
y_{screen} = \text{round}\!\left( y_{logical} \cdot \frac{H_s}{D_H} \right)
$$

For example, a region at logical (100, 450, 320, 60) maps to:

$$
x_{screen} = \text{round}(100 \cdot 2.6667) = 267 \text{ px}
$$

$$
y_{screen} = \text{round}(450 \cdot 1.875) = 844 \text{ px}
$$

The IRD then writes the RLE-decoded object bitmap into the framebuffer at these coordinates, blended with the underlying video via the alpha channel from the CLUT.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Q.A):** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students **omit the aspect-ratio scaling formula** in part (b), or if they confuse the **DDS logical canvas** with the **physical screen resolution**. Always state the scaling equations explicitly: $x_{screen} = x_{logical} \cdot (W_s / D_W)$.

---

### Q.B. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Module 2 Internal Choice B (14 marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the legacy analog teletext (WST) subtitling system with the DVB digital subtitling system. Discuss at least six distinguishing technical parameters.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the run-length-encoding scheme used in the DVB Object Data Segment (ODS). Illustrate with an example of decoding a 2-colour (1-bit) 8×2 pixel object that renders the letter "L".

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Comparison Table (Write at least six rows for full 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Analog Teletext (WST, ETSI EN 300 706) | DVB Subtitles (ETSI EN 300 743) |
|---|---|---|
| **Carriage medium** | Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI) of analog CVBS | PES packets inside MPEG-2 TS |
| **Resolution** | 480 × 250 fixed character grid | Arbitrary up to 1920 × 1080 / 3840 × 2160 |
| **Colour depth** | 8–16 colours via local CLUT | 256-colour CLUT with 32-bit ARGB |
| **Spatial freedom** | Strict row-column grid (24 rows × 40 columns) | Arbitrary rectangular regions anywhere on screen |
| **Anti-aliasing** | None (blocky glyphs) | Sub-pixel rendering with alpha gradient |
| **HD support** | Not possible (VBI is analog) | Native HD and UHD support |
| **Sync to video** | Tied to field rate, no PTS | PTS-driven frame-accurate presentation |
| **Multiple languages** | One language per broadcast | Multiple concurrent streams (one per PID) |
| **Bit-rate overhead** | Negligible (few bits per VBI line) | 20–100 kbps typical per stream |
| **Error resilience** | Hamming code per packet | CRC + PES optional FEC |

**Conclusion:** DVB subtitling is **structurally superior** for digital television because it inherits MPEG-2 timing, supports HD, and provides full graphical flexibility, whereas WST is a character-cell system designed for analog VBI and cannot scale beyond SD resolution.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**RLE Scheme in ODS (per ETSI EN 300 743, Section 7.2.4):**

The ODS payload contains a sequence of *runs* encoding the object's pixels row-by-row, left-to-right, top-to-bottom. For an object with *object_coding_method* = 0x00 (1-bit per pixel, 2-colour), the RLE operates as:

- Each **run** is signalled by an 8-bit *number_of_pixels* value $N$.
- If $N = 0$, it is a **row terminator** (end of current pixel row).
- If $N \neq 0$, the next $N$ pixels are drawn using the **current pixel code** (which alternates between foreground_pixel_index and background_pixel_index, starting with background).
- A complete ODS is terminated by $N = 0$ immediately following another $N = 0$ (end-of-object marker).

**Example — Decoding the letter "L" (8×2 pixels, 2-colour):**

Let background_pixel_index = 0 (transparent), foreground_pixel_index = 1 (white).

The bitstream (after *object_data_length* and *object_id* headers) is:

$$
\text{Stream:}\ [2,\ 1,\ 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 0,\ 0]
$$

Decoding step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Read } N = 2 &\Rightarrow \text{draw 2 background pixels (transparent)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{toggle to foreground, so current} = \text{foreground (1)} \\
\text{Read } N = 1 &\Rightarrow \text{draw 1 foreground pixel (white)} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{toggle to background, so current} = \text{background (0)} \\
\text{Read } N = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{end of row 0} \\
\text{Read } N = 1 &\Rightarrow \text{draw 1 background pixel} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{toggle to foreground, current} = \text{foreground (1)} \\
\text{Read } N = 2 &\Rightarrow \text{draw 2 foreground pixels} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{toggle to background, current} = \text{background (0)} \\
\text{Read } N = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{end of row 1} \\
\text{Read } N = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{end of object}
\end{aligned}
$$

The decoded bitmap (rows 0 and 1, columns 0–7) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 0:}&\quad \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{Row 1:}&\quad \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where "0" denotes background (CLUT[0], transparent) and "1" denotes foreground (CLUT[1], white). This 8×2 pattern visually approximates the corner of a letter "L" — a vertical stem on the left and a horizontal foot at the bottom.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfall (Q.B):** Students often forget that the pixel-code **toggles automatically** after each run. They re-read the pixel code from the stream, which is incorrect. Each run draws the *current* pixel code, then the code toggles. Examiners deduct 2 marks for not explaining the toggle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Standard Reference:** DVB Subtitles = **ETSI EN 300 743**. Always cite this standard in answers.
- [ ] **5 Segment Types:** **DDS, PCS, RCS, CDS, ODS** — learn both the abbreviation and full name.
- [ ] **PMT Descriptor:** **subtitling_descriptor, tag = 0x59** carries ISO 639 language code, *subtitling_type*, *composition_page_id*, *ancillary_page_id*.
- [ ] **PES Stream ID:** Subtitles use **private_stream_1 (0xBD)** with *stream_type* = 0x06 in PMT.
- [ ] **DDS dimensions:** $D_W, D_H$ are 16-bit, range **1–4096** pixels.
- [ ] **Region limits:** up to **64 regions per page**, **16 objects per region**, **256 CLUT entries**.
- [ ] **Object coding methods:** **0x00** = 2-colour, **0x01** = 4-colour, **0x02** = 8-bit (256-colour).
- [ ] **ODS RLE rule:** $N=0$ ends a row; $N=0$ followed by $N=0$ ends the object; pixel code **toggles** after each run.
- [ ] **Aspect-ratio scaling:** $x_{screen} = x_{logical} \cdot (W_s / D_W)$ and $y_{screen} = y_{logical} \cdot (H_s / D_H)$ when DDS ≠ screen resolution.
- [ ] **Page timeout:** default **5 seconds** — page is erased if no new PCS arrives.
- [ ] **Teletext vs DVB:** DVB = digital PES-based, HD-capable, region-free; Teletext = analog VBI, character-grid, SD-only.
