- [ ] **Subtitling_type values:** 0x10 = DVB subtitles (normal), 0x20 = DVB subtitles (for hearing-impaired), 0x30 = teletext-based, 0x40 = teletext subtitles for hearing-impaired.
- [ ] **Practical use cases:** **SDH** (subtitles for the deaf and hard-of-hearing), **multiple language tracks**, **emergency alert overlays**, **HbbTV interactive triggers**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB): Comparison of DAB with DVB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB): Comparison of DAB with DVB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition**
> **Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB)** is a digital radio standard (Eureka 147) developed in the late 1980s to replace traditional analog AM/FM broadcasting. It uses **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)** in the **VHF Band III (174–240 MHz)** and **L-Band (1452–1492 MHz)**, delivering near CD-quality audio, robust mobile reception, and the ability to multiplex multiple audio/data services within a single ~**1.536 MHz** wide frequency block.

**DAB+** is the upgraded second-generation variant that replaces the legacy MPEG-1 Layer II audio codec with the far more efficient **HE-AAC v2** (High-Efficiency Advanced Audio Coding, version 2) and adds **Reed–Solomon** outer error correction.

## 1.2 Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> **Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB)** is a family of open standards for digital television and data services. Key variants include:
> - **DVB-T / DVB-T2** — Terrestrial (over-the-air) TV
> - **DVB-S / DVB-S2** — Satellite TV
> - **DVB-C / DVB-C2** — Cable TV
> - **DVB-H** — Handheld / mobile TV
>
> DVB uses **COFDM** in the UHF/VHF bands (typically **470–862 MHz** for terrestrial) and is primarily engineered to carry **MPEG-2 / H.264 / HEVC** compressed video along with multi-channel audio.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

| Concept | Analogy |
|---|---|
| **DAB** | Imagine a **multi-lane highway** where each lane is a separate radio station, and the entire highway fits inside a single 1.5 MHz block. Cars (data) can change lanes (channels) instantly without retuning — that is the **Ensemble** concept. |
| **DVB** | Think of a **giant 4K movie screen delivered through a fibre bundle**. Each fibre is a subcarrier, and the whole bundle (COFDM) is wide enough (6/7/8 MHz) to carry full-motion video with surround sound. |
| **COFDM in both** | Splitting a fast, narrow stream into thousands of slow, parallel sub-streams — like **converting one fast expressway into many small village lanes**, so a small pothole (multipath fade) only damages a tiny village instead of the whole road. |

## 1.4 Why the Comparison Matters in KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"How is DAB different from DVB if both use COFDM?"* The answer lies in the **purpose, bandwidth, subcarrier count, audio/video codecs, and bit rate** — these are direct syllabus targets in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### A. The DAB Transmission Chain
1. **Audio sources** (stereo/surround) are digitally encoded (MPEG-1 Layer II or HE-AAC v2).
2. **Programme Associated Data (PAD)** such as song titles, traffic info, and slides are multiplexed.
3. All services are packed into a **Main Service Channel (MSC)** and a **Fast Information Channel (FIC)**.
4. The combined bit stream is **COFDM-modulated** with **DQPSK** (DAB) or **DAPSK** (DAB+).
5. The radio frequency block is **1.536 MHz** wide, carrying up to **~1.5 Mbps**.

### B. The DVB Transmission Chain (Terrestrial)
1. **Video** is encoded (MPEG-2, H.264, or HEVC).
2. **Audio** is encoded (MPEG-1 Layer II, AC-3, or AAC).
3. The MPEG-2 **Transport Stream (TS)** carries packetized video, audio, and SI/PSI tables.
4. **Outer RS (Reed–Solomon)** + **Inner Convolutional / LDPC** coding is applied.
5. The stream is **COFDM-modulated** with **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** (DVB-T) or up to **256-QAM** (DVB-T2).
6. The transmitted channel is **6, 7, or 8 MHz** wide, carrying up to **~50 Mbps** (DVB-T2).

### C. Why Both Use COFDM
> [!NOTE]
> **COFDM = Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing**
> It combats **multipath fading** and supports **Single Frequency Networks (SFN)**. The long symbol duration with a cyclic prefix absorbs delayed echoes and converts them into useful energy.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | DAB / DAB+ | DVB-T | DVB-T2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Channel Bandwidth (BW) | **1.536 MHz** | **6 / 7 / 8 MHz** | **1.7 / 5 / 6 / 7 / 8 / 10 MHz** |
| 2 | FFT size (N) | 1536 (Mode I) / 384 / 768 | 2K (1705) / 8K (6817) | 1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K |
| 3 | Subcarrier spacing (Δf) | **1 kHz** | ~1116 Hz (8K) / 4464 Hz (2K) | ~167 Hz (32K) – 8370 Hz (1K) |
| 4 | Useful symbol duration (T_u) | **1 ms** | 224 μs (2K) / 896 μs (8K) | 119 μs – 3.57 ms |
| 5 | Guard interval (T_g) | 0.246 ms (¼ of T_u) | ¼, ⅛, ⅛, ⅟32 of T_u | 1/4 to 1/128 of T_u |
| 6 | Constellation | **DQPSK** (DAB) / **DAPSK** (DAB+) | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM | QPSK, 16, 64, 256-QAM |
| 7 | Channel coding | Convolutional (DAB), RS+Convolutional (DAB+) | RS + Convolutional | LDPC + BCH |
| 8 | Net bit rate (approx.) | **0.6 – 1.5 Mbps** | 5 – 32 Mbps | up to **50+ Mbps** |
| 9 | Frequency band | VHF III / L-band | VHF / UHF | VHF / UHF |
| 10 | Audio codec | MPEG-1 L-II / **HE-AAC v2** | MPEG-1 L-II / AC-3 / AAC | HE-AAC, AC-3, MPEG Surround |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden rule for KTU numericals:** Subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ and total symbol duration $T_s = T_u + T_g$.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Engineering Limits

- **DAB Guard Interval = 0.246 ms** corresponds to a maximum echo delay of ~**75 km** — perfect for dense urban multipath.
- **DVB-T 8K with ¼ GI** tolerates echoes up to ~**67 km**.
- **SFN gain**: All transmitters in a DAB/DVB SFN transmit on the **same frequency** and **same time**, providing **diversity gain** instead of interference.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility
- **DAB** powers national digital radio in Europe, Australia, and parts of Asia.
- **DVB-T/T2** is the default terrestrial TV standard across **Europe, India (DD Free Dish hybrid), Australia, and parts of Africa**.
- Both standards share the **COFDM physical layer philosophy**, making them the two pillars of consumer digital broadcasting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Subcarrier Spacing and Symbol Duration

### DAB Mode I Calculation

**Given (KTU 2024 standard data):**
- Channel bandwidth $BW = 1.536$ MHz
- Number of active subcarriers $N = 1536$
- Useful symbol duration $T_u = 1$ ms
- Guard interval fraction $= 1/4$

**Step 1 — Useful symbol duration and subcarrier spacing**
The relationship is governed by OFDM orthogonality:
$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting DAB values**
$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}
$$

> This means DAB uses **1 kHz** subcarrier spacing.

**Step 3 — Total occupied bandwidth (theoretical)**
$$
BW_{occ} = N \cdot \Delta f = 1536 \times 1000 = 1.536 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
BW_{occ} = 1.536 \text{ MHz} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Guard interval duration**
$$
T_g = \frac{T_u}{4} = \frac{1 \text{ ms}}{4} = 0.246 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Total symbol duration**
$$
T_s = T_u + T_g = 1.000 + 0.246 = 1.246 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum echo tolerance distance**
Using speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:
$$
d_{max} = c \cdot T_g = 3 \times 10^{8} \times 0.246 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
d_{max} = 73{,}800 \text{ m} \approx 73.8 \text{ km}
$$

### DVB-T 8K Mode Calculation (8 MHz channel)

**Given:**
- $N = 6817$ active subcarriers
- Guard interval $= 1/4$

**Step 1 — Useful symbol duration**
$$
T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{1116} \approx 896 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2 — Guard interval**
$$
T_g = \frac{896}{4} = 224 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum echo distance**
$$
d_{max} = 3 \times 10^{8} \times 224 \times 10^{-6} = 67{,}200 \text{ m} \approx 67.2 \text{ km}
$$

## 3.2 Bit-Rate Estimation

For an OFDM system:
$$
R_b = N \cdot m \cdot \frac{R_c}{T_s}
$$

where $m$ = bits per symbol, $R_c$ = code rate, $T_s$ = total symbol duration.

**DAB example (DQPSK, R_c = 0.5):**
$$
R_b = 1536 \times 2 \times \frac{0.5}{1.246 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
R_b = 1536 \times 0.8027 \times 10^{3} \approx 1.23 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**DVB-T 8K example (64-QAM, R_c = 2/3, 8 MHz, 1/4 GI):**
$$
R_b = 6817 \times 6 \times \frac{2/3}{1120 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_b \approx 6817 \times 4 \times 892.86 \approx 24.3 \text{ Mbps}
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | DAB / DAB+ | DVB-T | DVB-T2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary payload | Audio + data | Video + audio + data | Video + audio + data |
| Typical net bit rate | **~1.2 Mbps** | **~24 Mbps** | **~40–50 Mbps** |
| Subcarrier count (Mode I / 8K / 32K) | 1536 | 6817 | 27841 |
| Subcarrier spacing | 1 kHz | 1.116 kHz | 167 Hz |
| Echo tolerance (¼ GI) | 73.8 km | 67.2 km | 50.4 km (16K, 1/4 GI) |
| Modulation efficiency | 2 bits/symbol | 6 bits/symbol | 8 bits/symbol |
| Service capacity per block | ~6–10 audio | 4–6 SDTV | 1 HD + several SD |
| Mobility support | **Excellent** | Good | Good (better with rotated constellations) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** The lower subcarrier count in DAB (~1500) gives a **longer symbol duration (1 ms)**, which is precisely why DAB is so robust against **Doppler effects at high vehicle speeds** — a frequent KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DAB Transmitter & Receiver Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Audio Source 1] --> MUX1[Service Multiplexer]
    A2[Audio Source 2] --> MUX1
    A3[PAD Data Service] --> MUX1
    MUX1 --> ENS[Main Service Channel MSC]
    ENS --> FIC[Fast Information Channel FIC]
    FIC --> ENC[Convolutional Encoder]
    ENS --> ENC
    ENC --> INT[Time and Frequency Interleaver]
    INT --> MOD[DQPSK or DAPSK Modulator]
    MOD --> IFFT[COFDM 1536-point IFFT]
    IFFT --> GI[Add Guard Interval 0.246 ms]
    GI --> DAC[DAC and RF Up-conversion]
    DAC --> ANT1[Transmit Antenna VHF Band III]
    ANT1 --> CH[Channel Multipath and Noise]
    CH --> ANT2[Receive Antenna]
    ANT2 --> RX1[RF Front End and ADC]
    RX1 --> SYNC[Time and Frequency Synchronization]
    SYNC --> FFT[1536-point FFT]
    FFT --> DEM[DQPSK or DAPSK Demodulator]
    DEM --> DEINT[De-interleaver]
    DEINT --> DEC[Viterbi Decoder]
    DEC --> DEMUX2[Service Demultiplexer]
    DEMUX2 --> A1OUT[Loudspeaker Audio]
    DEMUX2 --> PADOUT[PAD Display]
```

## 4.2 DVB-T / DVB-T2 Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Video H.264 or HEVC] --> MUX2[MPEG-2 Transport Stream]
    AUD[Audio AC-3 or AAC] --> MUX2
    DATA[SI PSI Tables] --> MUX2
    MUX2 --> RS[Outer Reed Solomon Encoder]
    RS --> INT2[Outer Interleaver]
    INT2 --> CONV[Inner Convolutional or LDPC Encoder]
    CONV --> IMOD[Bit Interleaver]
    IMOD --> MAP[Constellation Mapper QPSK 16 64 256 QAM]
    MAP --> IFFT2[COFDM FFT 2K 8K or 32K]
    IFFT2 --> GI2[Insert Guard Interval]
    GI2 --> RF2[RF Up-conversion UHF]
    RF2 --> CH2[Channel]
    CH2 --> RX2[Receiver Tuner]
    RX2 --> SYNC2[Sync Equalizer]
    SYNC2 --> FFT3[FFT Demodulation]
    FFT3 --> DMAP[Demapper]
    DMAP --> DINT[De-interleaver]
    DINT --> VDEC[Viterbi or LDPC Decoder]
    VDEC --> RS2[Reed Solomon Decoder]
    RS2 --> TS2[Transport Stream Output]
    TS2 --> TV[Video Audio Display]
```

## 4.3 DAB vs DVB — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DAB["DAB SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        D1[Audio MPEG or HE-AAC] --> D2[COFDM 1536 subcarriers]
        D2 --> D3[DQPSK or DAPSK]
        D3 --> D4[1.536 MHz BW VHF III]
    end
    subgraph DVB["DVB-T/T2 SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        V1[Video and Audio MPEG Transport Stream] --> V2[COFDM 2K 8K 32K]
        V2 --> V3[QPSK 16 64 256 QAM]
        V3 --> V4[6 7 8 MHz BW UHF]
    end
    DAB --> CP{Common Physical Layer}
    DVB --> CP
    CP --> SFN[Single Frequency Network Support]
    CP --> MUX[Multiplexing of Services]
    CP --> MIMO[Optional MIMO in T2]
```

## 4.4 Frequency Spectrum Allocation (DAB vs DVB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Band I 47 to 68 MHz FM] --> B[Band II 87.5 to 108 MHz Analog FM]
    B --> C[Band III 174 to 240 MHz DAB]
    C --> D[UHF 470 to 862 MHz DVB-T and DVB-T2]
    D --> E[L-band 1452 to 1492 MHz DAB Satellite]
    E --> F[Ku band DVB-S and DVB-S2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define DAB. List any two advantages of DAB over conventional analog FM broadcasting.**

**Model Answer:**
> Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) is a digital radio standard based on the Eureka 147 specification that uses COFDM modulation to deliver CD-quality audio in the VHF Band III and L-band spectrum.
>
> **Advantages over analog FM:**
> 1. **Superior audio quality** — near CD quality due to digital encoding, free from multipath hiss and fading.
> 2. **Higher spectrum efficiency** — multiple services share a single 1.536 MHz block (an *Ensemble*).
> 3. **Robust SFN operation** — same frequency can be reused by all transmitters in a network.
> 4. **Data services** — PAD such as song titles, traffic, and slide shows are embedded in the same stream.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Mention the audio codecs used in DAB and DAB+. What is the role of Reed–Solomon coding in DAB+?**

**Model Answer:**
> - **DAB (original)** uses **MPEG-1 Layer II** audio codec.
> - **DAB+** uses **HE-AAC v2 (High-Efficiency Advanced Audio Coding, version 2)** for ~3× higher compression efficiency.
>
> **Role of Reed–Solomon (RS) coding in DAB+:**
> RS coding is added as an **outer error-correction layer** over the convolutional inner code. It provides powerful **burst-error correction** (especially for long fades in mobile reception) and is the reason DAB+ is significantly more robust than legacy DAB at the same bit rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - Many students confuse **FFT size** with **subcarrier count** — remember only *active* subcarriers carry data; the FFT size may be larger (e.g., 2048 FFT → 1705 active in 2K DVB-T).
> - Forgetting to add the **guard interval** to total symbol duration is a common 2-mark loss.
> - Always quote **DQPSK for DAB** and **QPSK/16/64-QAM for DVB-T**; mixing them up loses marks.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of a DAB transmitter and receiver with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of COFDM in DAB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A DAB system transmits multiple digital audio programs plus data services within a single 1.536 MHz frequency block called an **Ensemble**.

**Transmitter side:**
1. **Audio encoders** — each audio program is digitally compressed using MPEG-1 Layer II (DAB) or HE-AAC v2 (DAB+).
2. **Service multiplexer** — combines all audio streams and Programme Associated Data (PAD) into a Main Service Channel (MSC).
3. **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — carries Multiplex Configuration Information (MCI) and Service Information (SI).
4. **Channel coding** — convolutional coding (and RS in DAB+) for forward error correction.
5. **Time and frequency interleaver** — spreads burst errors in time and frequency.
6. **COFDM modulator** — distributes data across 1536 orthogonal subcarriers (Mode I) with DQPSK mapping.
7. **IFFT + Guard Interval insertion** — produces the time-domain OFDM symbol of 1.246 ms duration.
8. **RF up-conversion** to VHF Band III / L-band and transmission.

**Receiver side:**
1. RF tuner + ADC.
2. Time and frequency synchronisation to lock onto the COFDM frame.
3. FFT to recover individual subcarriers.
4. De-interleaving and Viterbi decoding (plus RS decoding in DAB+).
5. Service demultiplexer separates the chosen audio stream from the MSC.
6. Audio decoder + DAC + loudspeaker.

**Role of COFDM in DAB:**
- Splits the high bit-rate stream into **1536 low-rate parallel subcarriers** (1 kHz spacing each).
- The **long symbol duration (1 ms)** plus a 0.246 ms **guard interval** makes DAB immune to severe multipath echoes and Doppler spread.
- Enables **Single Frequency Network (SFN)** — multiple transmitters across a country use the same frequency with no co-channel interference.
- Provides excellent **mobile reception** at speeds up to 200 km/h.

**Q. (b)** With neat diagrams, compare DAB and DVB-T in terms of bandwidth, modulation, audio/video coding, subcarrier spacing, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DAB | DVB-T |
|---|---|---|
| Primary use | Audio broadcasting | Television broadcasting |
| Channel bandwidth | 1.536 MHz | 6 / 7 / 8 MHz |
| FFT size (Mode I / 2K / 8K) | 1536 subcarriers | 1705 / 6817 subcarriers |
| Subcarrier spacing | 1 kHz | ~4.46 kHz (2K) / ~1.116 kHz (8K) |
| Symbol duration | 1 ms | 224 μs (2K) / 896 μs (8K) |
| Modulation | DQPSK (DAB) / DAPSK (DAB+) | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM |
| Channel coding | Convolutional (± RS) | RS + Convolutional |
| Audio codec | MPEG-1 L-II / HE-AAC v2 | MPEG-1 L-II / AC-3 / AAC |
| Video codec | Not used | MPEG-2 / H.264 |
| Frequency band | VHF III / L-band | VHF / UHF |
| Net bit rate | ~1.2 Mbps | 5 – 32 Mbps |
| SFN support | Native | Native |
| Applications | Radio, multimedia, traffic info | SDTV / HDTV terrestrial TV |

**Inference:** DAB is **bandwidth-narrow but highly mobile-robust**, optimised for audio. DVB-T is **bandwidth-wide and bit-rate-heavy**, optimised for video. Both share the **COFDM physical-layer philosophy** to combat multipath and to enable SFNs.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the COFDM technique used in DAB. Derive the subcarrier spacing, total symbol duration, and maximum echo distance for DAB Mode I. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**COFDM technique:**
Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing distributes the data over many **orthogonal** narrowband subcarriers. The orthogonality condition is:
$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u}
$$

where $T_u$ is the useful OFDM symbol duration. A **cyclic prefix (guard interval)** of duration $T_g$ is added to absorb multipath echoes.

**DAB Mode I parameters:**
- Active subcarriers $N = 1536$
- Useful symbol duration $T_u = 1$ ms
- Guard interval fraction = 1/4

**Step 1 — Subcarrier spacing**
$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Total bandwidth check**
$$
BW = N \cdot \Delta f = 1536 \times 1000 = 1.536 \text{ MHz} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Guard interval duration**
$$
T_g = \frac{T_u}{4} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.246 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total symbol duration**
$$
T_s = T_u + T_g = 1.000 + 0.246 = 1.246 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum echo distance**
$$
d_{max} = c \cdot T_g = 3 \times 10^{8} \times 0.246 \times 10^{-3} \approx 73.8 \text{ km}
$$

**Conclusion:** DAB can tolerate echoes up to **73.8 km**, which is the basis for designing large-gap SFNs.

**Q. (b)** Describe the DVB-T2 standard and highlight its improvements over DVB-T. Compare the bit rate of a DAB ensemble with that of a DVB-T 8K, 64-QAM, 2/3 code rate system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**DVB-T2 features:**
- FFT sizes: 1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K.
- Up to **256-QAM** constellation (vs. 64-QAM in DVB-T).
- **LDPC + BCH** channel coding (replacing convolutional), gaining ~30% capacity.
- **Rotated constellations** for better robustness.
- Optional **MISO / MIMO** antenna diversity.
- **Multiple PLPs (Physical Layer Pipes)** for service-specific robustness.
- Up to **~50 Mbps** net throughput per 8 MHz channel.

**Bit-rate comparison:**

For DVB-T (8K, 64-QAM, code rate 2/3, GI 1/4, 8 MHz):
$$
R_b = N \cdot m \cdot \frac{R_c}{T_u + T_g}
$$
$$
R_b = 6817 \times 6 \times \frac{2/3}{1120 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 24.3 \text{ Mbps}
$$

For DAB Mode I (DQPSK, code rate 0.5, 1.536 MHz):
$$
R_b = 1536 \times 2 \times \frac{0.5}{1.246 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1.23 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Comparison:**
$$
\frac{R_{b, DVB-T}}{R_{b, DAB}} \approx \frac{24.3}{1.23} \approx 19.8
$$

**DVB-T carries ~20× more data** than DAB, but DAB has **~10× longer symbol duration**, making it far more robust to fast fading and Doppler effects at high vehicle speeds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Checklist**

- **DAB** = Eureka 147 digital radio; **DVB** = family of digital TV standards (T, T2, S, S2, C, C2, H).
- **Common DNA:** Both use **COFDM** and support **SFNs**.
- **Bandwidth:** DAB = **1.536 MHz**; DVB-T = **6/7/8 MHz**; DVB-T2 = up to **10 MHz**.
- **Subcarriers:** DAB Mode I = **1536** with **1 kHz** spacing; DVB-T 8K = **6817** with **~1.116 kHz** spacing.
- **Useful symbol duration:** DAB = **1 ms**; DVB-T 8K = **896 μs**; DVB-T 32K = **~3.57 ms**.
- **Guard interval:** DAB = **0.246 ms (¼)**; DVB-T = **¼, ⅛, ⅛, ⅟32** of T_u; DVB-T2 = up to **1/128** of T_u.
- **Maximum echo distance for DAB Mode I (¼ GI):** $\approx$ **73.8 km**.
- **Modulation:** DAB → **DQPSK / DAPSK**; DVB-T → **QPSK / 16 / 64-QAM**; DVB-T2 → adds **256-QAM**.
- **Channel coding:** DAB → Convolutional (+ RS in DAB+); DVB-T → **RS + Convolutional**; DVB-T2 → **LDPC + BCH**.
- **Audio codec:** DAB → MPEG-1 L-II; DAB+ → **HE-AAC v2**; DVB → AC-3, AAC, MPEG-1 L-II.
- **Bit rate:** DAB ≈ **1.2 Mbps**; DVB-T ≈ **24 Mbps**; DVB-T2 ≈ **40–50 Mbps**.
- **Frequency band:** DAB → **VHF Band III / L-band**; DVB-T → **UHF (470–862 MHz)**; DVB-S → **Ku/Ka band**.
- **Mobility:** DAB is **best-in-class** due to its 1 ms symbol; DVB-T2 is improved by rotated constellations and longer FFT modes.
- **Bit-rate formula:** $R_b = N \cdot m \cdot R_c / (T_u + T_g)$.
- **Golden rule:** $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ and **always** add $T_g$ when asked for *total* symbol duration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer of DAB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer of DAB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Physical Layer of DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting)** — formally designated as **Eureka 147 / ETSI EN 300 401** — is the lowest layer of the DAB protocol stack responsible for the **bit-level transport, channel coding, modulation, and radio-frequency transmission** of digital audio and associated data services over terrestrial VHF Band III (174–240 MHz) and L-Band (1452–1492 MHz).

In strict OSI terminology adopted by KTU 2024, the DAB physical layer is defined by **four orthogonal functional blocks**:
1. **Energy Dispersal Scrambling** (Randomization)
2. **Convolutional Channel Coding** (Forward Error Correction)
3. **Time–Frequency Interleaving** (Robustness against burst and selective fading)
4. **COFDM Modulation** (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing with π/4-DQPSK)

The DAB physical layer transmits a **continuously repeating Transmission Frame** of fixed duration, each frame containing a **Null Symbol**, a **Phase Reference Symbol**, and a sequence of **Data Symbols** carrying the Fast Information Channel (FIC) and Main Service Channel (MSC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** The Physical Layer of DAB is the foundation for understanding DAB's hallmark immunity to multipath fading. Every parameter (1536 carriers, 1 ms frame, 76 µs guard interval in Mode I) is specifically engineered to defeat echoes typical of mobile reception in cities, tunnels, and hilly terrain.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are shouting a long story to a friend across a noisy, echoing marketplace. To make sure they hear every word, you would:

| Real-World Problem | DAB Solution | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Echoes overlap your voice | **Guard Interval** | Pause briefly between each word so echoes die down |
| A loud horn drowns out one word | **Interleaving + FEC** | Rearrange the order of words; repeat important ones |
| The market is loud overall | **Spread across many frequencies (OFDM)** | Speak softly but on many pitches simultaneously |
| Receiver must know when you start | **Null Symbol + Phase Reference Symbol** | Ring a bell (sync) before telling the story |
| Wireless channel adds random noise | **Energy Dispersal Scrambling** | Use a known "code-word" to XOR your message |

The genius of DAB is that instead of transmitting a high-rate bitstream on **one** carrier (as in FM), it spreads the data over **hundreds or thousands of narrow sub-carriers** transmitted in parallel, each modulated at a very low symbol rate. This makes each sub-carrier's symbol duration much **longer than the maximum echo delay**, virtually eliminating intersymbol interference (ISI) caused by multipath.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — COFDM (Coded OFDM):** COFDM = OFDM (multi-carrier transmission) + Channel Coding (FEC) + Interleaving (time & frequency). DAB is the first mass-market broadcast system to deploy COFDM in the consumer domain.

---

## 1.3 Standardized Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for Memory)

The KTU board examiner expects familiarity with these **Mode I** (the default terrestrial VHF mode) parameters:

- **Total number of sub-carriers (K):** **1536**
- **Frame duration (T_F):** **96 ms**
- **Number of OFDM symbols per frame:** **76**
- **Null symbol duration (T_N):** **1.297 ms**
- **Useful symbol duration (T_S):** **1 ms**
- **Guard interval duration (T_G):** **0.246 ms**
- **Sub-carrier spacing (Δf):** **1 kHz**
- **Channel spacing (occupied bandwidth):** **1.536 MHz**
- **Modulation:** **π/4-DQPSK** (Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying, π/4 rotated)
- **Channel coding:** **Convolutional code, mother rate = 1/4**, constraint length K = 7, punctured to 1/2, 1/3, or used directly
- **Audio source coding:** **MPEG-1 Audio Layer II (MP2)** at 32–256 kbps per sub-channel

> [!TIP]
> The number **1536 = 192 × 8** is no coincidence — it arises from the underlying FFT length of 2048, where 1536 active sub-carriers occupy the inner band, and the remaining 512 are virtual/side carriers that define the spectrum mask.

---

## 1.4 Operational Modes of DAB (Brief)

KTU Module 2 expects awareness of the four transmission modes:

| Mode | Frequency Band | Frame Duration | K (Carriers) | Δf | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode I** | VHF Band III (174–240 MHz) | **96 ms** | **1536** | 1 kHz | Terrestrial DAB (most common) |
| Mode II | VHF Band I & L-Band | 24 ms | 384 | 4 kHz | Cable / Satellite distribution |
| Mode III | L-Band (≤ 3 GHz) | 24 ms | 192 | 8 kHz | Satellite-to-cable |
| Mode IV | L-Band (≤ 1.5 GHz) | 48 ms | 768 | 2 kHz | Hybrid satellite/terrestrial |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of the DAB Physical Layer (Transmitter Side)

The DAB transmitter pipeline is a textbook cascade of **four sequential stages**, each addressing a specific channel impairment. The order is **NOT arbitrary** — it is dictated by the Eureka 147 specification.

### Stage 1 — Energy Dispersal Scrambling (Randomization)
- **Purpose:** Prevent long runs of 0s or 1s that would degrade receiver synchronization (bit-clock recovery) and cause strong DC spectral lines.
- **Method:** The bitstream is XORed with a **9-bit Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS)** generated by the polynomial:
  
$$G(x) = x^9 + x^5 + 1$$
  
- The PRBS is initialized at the start of every **FIC** with the all-ones state and reset at the beginning of each Common Interleaved Frame (CIF).
- **Output:** A pseudo-random bitstream with roughly 50% ones and 50% zeros regardless of source statistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** In a fully-loaded DAB multiplex, the audio bit-rate is variable (MPEG Layer II uses a constant bit-rate per sub-channel, but with bit-allocation tables per frame). Without scrambling, the spectrum would have deep nulls and peaks. Scrambling ensures a flat spectral envelope, simplifying the receiver's AGC design.

---

### Stage 2 — Convolutional Channel Coding (FEC)
- **Purpose:** Add controlled redundancy so the receiver can correct random bit errors caused by thermal noise and interference.
- **Mother code:** Rate $R_c = 1/4$, constraint length $K_c = 7$, generator polynomials in octal: $G_1 = 133, \ G_2 = 171, \ G_3 = 145, \ G_4 = 133$.
- **Puncturing:** To trade robustness for capacity, the mother code is **punctured** to obtain higher rates:
  - **Rate 1/2** (used for FIC — needs maximum protection, control data must be flawless)
  - **Rate 1/3** (high protection for audio)
  - **Rate 2/3, 3/4** (lower protection for less critical data)
- **Viterbi decoding** is used at the receiver (soft-decision Viterbi, 64-state trellis).

---

### Stage 3 — Time and Frequency Interleaving
- **Purpose:** Distribute burst errors across the time-frequency plane so the Viterbi decoder sees *random* errors, which it corrects efficiently.
- **Time interleaving depth:** Up to **16 CIFs** (~ 384 ms) — long enough to decorrelate vehicular fading bursts.
- **Frequency interleaving depth:** Spreads consecutive bits over the **full 1.536 MHz bandwidth** so a narrowband interferer (e.g., a single FM carrier) only destroys a small fraction of the sub-carriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The combination "Convolutional Code + Interleaver" is what makes DAB virtually immune to *frequency-selective fading*. A deep null in the spectrum (e.g., at 1.2 MHz offset) destroys perhaps 2–3 sub-carriers out of 1536, which the FEC then corrects. The deinterleaver is the "magic" that reorders the symbols back before decoding.

---

### Stage 4 — COFDM Modulation with π/4-DQPSK
- **Purpose:** Map the interleaved bit pairs to **complex constellation points** and modulate each of the 1536 sub-carriers.
- **Mapping:** π/4-shifted DQPSK — pairs of bits select one of 4 differential phase shifts {0, π/2, π, 3π/2} between consecutive OFDM symbols on the same sub-carrier.
- **Why differential?** Coherent detection of 1536 carriers is impractical in a mobile receiver due to phase noise and frequency drift. Differential detection only requires a **previous-symbol reference** — a much simpler hardware implementation.
- **Why π/4 shift?** Prevents the constellation from ever passing through the origin, which would cause **envelope dropouts** and degrade the RF power amplifier's efficiency.

---

## 2.2 Transmission Frame Structure (Mode I — The Default)

Each **96 ms DAB transmission frame** consists of:

| Symbol | Count | Duration | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Null Symbol** | 1 | **1.297 ms** | Tx OFF — receiver uses it for AGC, A/D range alignment, coarse synchronization |
| **Phase Reference Symbol (PRS)** | 1 | **1.246 ms** | All 1536 sub-carriers transmit a known fixed pattern (QPSK reference) — receiver uses it for fine synchronization, channel estimation |
| **Data Symbols** | 74 | **1.246 ms each** | Carry the FIC and MSC information |
| **Total OFDM symbols** | **76** (including null) | — | One full frame |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state the null symbol duration as "1 ms" because the guard interval is 0.246 ms and the useful symbol is 1 ms. **The null symbol has NO guard interval** (the transmitter is OFF, so a guard would be redundant) and its total duration is **T_N = T_G + T_S = 1.246 + 0.051? No — actually T_N = 1297 µs is a SPEC-defined special value** to allow AGC settling. **Do not confuse** the null symbol with a data symbol of 1246 µs.

---

## 2.3 The Fast Information Channel (FIC) vs Main Service Channel (MSC)

| Aspect | FIC | MSC |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Control & signalling (MCI, SI, CA) | Audio + data payload |
| **Coding rate** | Always **1/2** (no puncturing) | Variable: 1/3, 2/3, 1/2 |
| **Interleaving** | Frequency only (no time) | Both time and frequency |
| **Position in frame** | First 3 data symbols of every frame | Remaining data symbols |
| **Capacity** | ~ 32 kbps (Mode I) | Up to ~ 1.5 Mbps (Mode I) |

The FIC carries the **Multiplex Configuration Information (MCI)** — without it, the receiver cannot locate, decode, or assemble the audio sub-channels.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize Mode I values; derive others using the same relationships.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Mode I Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of sub-carriers (K) | $K = N_{FFT} \cdot \tfrac{3}{4}$ (Mode I) | **1536** | sub-carriers |
| 2 | Sub-carrier spacing (Δf) | $\Delta f = \tfrac{1}{T_S}$ | **1 kHz** | Hz |
| 3 | Useful symbol duration (T_S) | $T_S = \tfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | **1.000** | ms |
| 4 | Guard interval (T_G) | $T_G = \tfrac{T_S}{4}$ (Mode I) | **0.246** | ms |
| 5 | Total OFDM symbol duration (T) | $T = T_S + T_G$ | **1.246** | ms |
| 6 | Null symbol duration (T_N) | ETSI spec value | **1.297** | ms |
| 7 | Frame duration (T_F) | $T_F = T_N + 76 \cdot T$ | **96** | ms |
| 8 | Occupied bandwidth (B) | $B = K \cdot \Delta f$ | **1.536** | MHz |
| 9 | Channel spacing (allocation) | ETSI raster | **1.712** | MHz |
| 10 | OFDM symbol rate | $R_{OFDM} = \tfrac{1}{T_S}$ | **1000** | sym/s |
| 11 | Bit per DQPSK symbol | $\log_2 4 = 2$ | **2** | bits/sym |
| 12 | Raw bit rate (1 carrier) | $R_c = 2 \cdot \Delta f$ | **2 kbps** | bps |
| 13 | Gross MSC bit rate (Mode I) | $R_{MSC} = K \cdot 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{T_S} \cdot \tfrac{74}{76}$ (approx) | ~ 2.4 Mbps | bps |
| 14 | Net audio bit rate (after 1/3 code) | $R_{net} = R_{gross} \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}$ | ~ 0.8 Mbps | bps |
| 15 | PRBS polynomial | $G(x) = x^9 + x^5 + 1$ | — | — |
| 16 | Convolutional code rate (mother) | $R_c = \tfrac{1}{4}, \ K_c = 7$ | — | — |
| 17 | Audio codec | MPEG-1 Layer II | — | — |
| 18 | DQPSK phases | $\{0, \tfrac{\pi}{2}, \pi, \tfrac{3\pi}{2}\}$ + π/4 rotation | — | rad |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Notation Rule:** All absolute values / norms in derivations are written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. NEVER use bare vertical bars inside a `|...|` table cell.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive infotainment:** DAB's COFDM lets a car moving at 200 km/h receive CD-quality audio without dropouts — a feat impossible for analog FM.
- **Single-Frequency Networks (SFN):** All transmitters in a region can broadcast on the **same frequency** because the guard interval absorbs the inter-transmitter delay. SFNs save spectrum — a critical enabler for the Kerala DAB rollout.
- **DAB+ upgrade:** Adds **AAC+ (HE-AAC v2)** audio coding and **Reed-Solomon outer FEC** to the same physical layer, demonstrating the modularity of the Eureka 147 design.
- **Cognitive radio research:** DAB's flexible multiplex is a useful benchmark for Software Defined Radio (SDR) implementations using USRP / RTL-SDR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Number of Sub-Carriers in Mode I (K = 1536)

The DAB specification chooses a useful symbol duration $T_S$ such that the **coherence bandwidth** of the typical urban multipath channel is much wider than the sub-carrier spacing, ensuring each sub-carrier experiences **flat fading**.

**Step 1:** Choose useful symbol duration equal to **1 ms** to keep Doppler spread negligible at vehicular speeds (max Doppler $f_d \approx 200$ Hz at 200 km/h, VHF Band III).

$$T_S = 1 \, \text{ms} = 10^{-3} \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Set sub-carrier spacing equal to the inverse of the useful symbol duration (this is the **orthogonality condition** of OFDM):

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_S} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000 \, \text{Hz} = 1 \, \text{kHz}$$

**Step 3:** Decide the channel bandwidth. Eureka 147 fixes the **occupied bandwidth** at:

$$B_{occ} = 1.536 \, \text{MHz}$$

**Step 4:** Number of active sub-carriers = bandwidth / sub-carrier spacing:

$$K = \frac{B_{occ}}{\Delta f} = \frac{1.536 \times 10^6}{1000} = 1536$$

> **Sanity check:** 1536 sub-carriers × 1 kHz spacing = 1.536 MHz ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Guard Interval Duration

**Step 1:** The maximum tolerable echo delay in a dense urban SFN is approximately **T_G/3** to maintain a guard margin. For Mode I, target max delay = 200 µs.

**Step 2:** Empirical DAB design sets:

$$T_G = \frac{T_S}{4} = \frac{1000 \, \mu s}{4} = 246 \, \mu s$$

> **Interpretation:** Echoes of duration up to **~ 246 µs** (corresponding to path-length differences of ~ 73.8 km) are absorbed within the guard interval and do not cause intersymbol interference.

**Step 3:** Total OFDM symbol duration:

$$T_{sym} = T_S + T_G = 1000 + 246 = 1246 \, \mu s = 1.246 \, \text{ms}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Frame Duration (T_F = 96 ms)

**Step 1:** Total OFDM symbols per frame in Mode I = **76** (1 null + 1 PRS + 74 data).

**Step 2:** Sum of all symbol durations:

$$T_F = T_N + (N_{sym} - 1) \cdot T_{sym}$$

$$T_F = 1297 \, \mu s + 75 \cdot 1246 \, \mu s$$

$$T_F = 1297 + 93450 = 94747 \, \mu s \approx 96 \, \text{ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The null symbol has the special duration of **1297 µs** (≈ 1 ms useful + 246 µs "guard-like" interval to allow the transmitter PA to ramp down and the receiver AGC to settle). Hence the (N − 1) in the formula. **Do not** use $N_{sym} = 76$ directly with $T_{sym} = 1246$ µs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D: Gross Bit Rate of the MSC (Mode I, 1/3 puncturing)

**Step 1:** Each sub-carrier carries one DQPSK symbol per OFDM symbol period $T_S$. One DQPSK symbol = 2 bits.

**Step 2:** Each OFDM data symbol carries:

$$N_{bits/sym} = K \cdot 2 = 1536 \cdot 2 = 3072 \, \text{bits}$$

**Step 3:** OFDM data symbol rate:

$$R_{OFDM} = \frac{1}{T_S} = 1000 \, \text{sym/s}$$

**Step 4:** Gross data-carrying bit rate (excluding null and PRS, 74 data symbols per frame out of 76 total):

$$R_{gross} = 3072 \cdot 1000 \cdot \frac{74}{76}$$

$$R_{gross} = 3.072 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.9737 \approx 2.99 \, \text{Mbps}$$

> Wait — that overstates; let us re-derive carefully accounting for the frame structure:
>
> The CIF (Common Interleaved Frame) is defined as a sub-multiple of the transmission frame. In Mode I, **1 CIF = 55,296 bits** ≈ 24 ms of MSC capacity. Over 96 ms (full frame):
>
> $$R_{MSC, gross} = \frac{55296 \, \text{bits}}{24 \, \text{ms}} = 2.304 \, \text{Mbps}$$
>
> This is the **canonical Mode I MSC gross bit rate**.

**Step 5:** Net useful bit rate after convolutional code of rate $R_c = 1/3$:

$$R_{net} = R_{MSC, gross} \cdot R_c = 2.304 \times \frac{1}{3} = 0.768 \, \text{Mbps}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A Mode I DAB ensemble can deliver up to ~ 768 kbps of error-protected payload. With MPEG-1 Layer II at 192 kbps per stereo sub-channel, this allows ≈ **4 stereo programmes** plus auxiliary data — exactly the capacity of the All India Radio DAB pilot in Delhi/Mumbai.

---

## 3.5 Derivation E: DQPSK Phase Mapping

**Step 1:** Two incoming bits $(b_1, b_0)$ select a **differential phase increment** $\Delta\phi$:

$$(0,0) \rightarrow \Delta\phi = 0, \quad (0,1) \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}, \quad (1,1) \rightarrow \pi, \quad (1,0) \rightarrow \frac{3\pi}{2}$$

**Step 2:** The absolute transmitted phase of sub-carrier $k$ in OFDM symbol $n$ is:

$$\phi_k[n] = \phi_k[n-1] + \Delta\phi_k[n] + \frac{\pi}{4}$$

**Step 3:** The complex baseband symbol:

$$s_k[n] = e^{j\phi_k[n]}$$

**Step 4:** Receiver performs non-coherent detection by computing the **product** of the current symbol with the complex conjugate of the previous symbol (per sub-carrier):

$$z_k[n] = s_k[n] \cdot s_k^{*}[n-1] = e^{j\Delta\phi_k[n]}$$

**Step 5:** Argument of $z_k[n]$ recovers the data bits via 4-QAM decision regions. **Crucially, no channel estimation is required** — the differential encoding carries the information in phase *differences*.

---

## 3.6 Derivation F: Convolutional Code Puncturing (Rate 1/2 for FIC)

**Step 1:** Mother code rate $R_0 = 1/4$ produces 4 output bits for every 1 input bit:

$$\text{output} = (c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4)$$

**Step 2:** To obtain rate 1/2, **discard** (puncture) outputs $c_3$ and $c_4$:

$$\text{transmitted} = (c_1, c_2)$$

**Step 3:** Effective code rate:

$$R_c = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4:** At the receiver, a Viterbi decoder operates on the **punctured trellis** — it knows the puncturing pattern and treats deleted bits as erasures (LLR = 0).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Puncturing reduces robustness by ~ 3 dB compared to the rate-1/4 mother code but doubles the throughput. The FIC accepts this trade-off because the FIC carries only ~ 32 kbps — a small portion of the ensemble — and must be received with near-zero error.

---

## 3.7 Derivation G: Interleaving Depth and Diversity Gain

**Step 1:** A frequency-selective fade of coherence bandwidth $B_c = 200$ kHz at a given instant destroys:

$$N_{fade} = B_c / \Delta f = 200{,}000 / 1000 = 200 \, \text{sub-carriers}$$

**Step 2:** The frequency interleaver permutes consecutive data bits across **all 1536** sub-carriers, so the 200 destroyed bits are spread over 200 different *logical* positions.

**Step 3:** In the time domain, a fading burst of duration $T_{burst} = 10$ ms destroys:

$$N_{t-burst} = \frac{T_{burst}}{T_S} = \frac{10 \, ms}{1 \, ms} = 10 \, \text{successive OFDM symbols}$$

**Step 4:** The time interleaver (depth 16 CIFs ≈ 384 ms) spreads the destroyed bits across a much longer time window, so Viterbi sees them as *random independent errors* of probability:

$$P_e \approx \frac{200 \cdot 10}{1536 \cdot 384 \cdot 1000} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

> Such a low post-deinterleaving error rate is well within the Viterbi decoder's correction capability at typical SNR.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of DAB Physical Layer Parameters Across Modes

| Parameter | Mode I | Mode II | Mode III | Mode IV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| K (sub-carriers) | 1536 | 384 | 192 | 768 |
| T_S (µs) | 1000 | 250 | 125 | 500 |
| Δf (kHz) | 1 | 4 | 8 | 2 |
| T_G (µs) | 246 | 62 | 31 | 123 |
| T_sym (µs) | 1246 | 312 | 156 | 623 |
| Frame (ms) | 96 | 24 | 24 | 48 |
| Bandwidth (MHz) | 1.536 | 1.536 | 1.536 | 1.536 |

> **Universal rule across all modes:** $K \cdot \Delta f = 1.536$ MHz and $\Delta f = 1/T_S$ (orthogonality) hold in every case.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DAB Transmitter Physical Layer (Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Audio Sources<br/>MPEG Layer II Encoders] --> MUX[Service Multiplexer<br/>CIF Builder]
    MUX --> FICbuild[FIC Builder<br/>MCI and SI]
    FICbuild --> SCR1[Energy Dispersal<br/>Scrambler PRBS x9+x5+1]
    MUX --> SCR2[Energy Dispersal<br/>Scrambler PRBS x9+x5+1]
    SCR1 --> CC1[Convolutional Encoder<br/>Rate 1/2 K=7]
    SCR2 --> CC2[Convolutional Encoder<br/>Rate 1/3 K=7]
    CC1 --> TI1[Time and Frequency<br/>Interleaver FIC]
    CC2 --> TI2[Time and Frequency<br/>Interleaver MSC]
    TI1 --> MAP1[pi over 4 DQPSK<br/>Mapper FIC]
    TI2 --> MAP2[pi over 4 DQPSK<br/>Mapper MSC]
    MAP1 --> OFDM[COFDM Modulator<br/>IFFT 2048 pt]
    MAP2 --> OFDM
    OFDM --> GI[Add Guard Interval<br/>246 us cyclic prefix]
    GI --> FRM[Frame Builder<br/>Null + PRS + 74 Data]
    FRM --> RF[RF Upconverter<br/>VHF Band III]
    RF --> ANT[TX Antenna]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Audio encoders produce a multiplexed Common Interleaved Frame (CIF). The FIC and MSC paths are coded, interleaved, and mapped separately, then merged in the OFDM modulator. The frame builder prepends the null and phase-reference symbols before RF upconversion.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — DAB Receiver Physical Layer (Mirror Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ANT[RX Antenna] --> TUN[RF Tuner<br/>VHF Band III]
    TUN --> AGC[AGC Settling<br/>uses Null Symbol]
    AGC --> SYNC[Synchronizer<br/>uses PRS]
    SYNC --> FFT[OFDM Demodulator<br/>FFT 2048 pt]
    FFT --> REM[Remove Guard Interval]
    REM --> DMAP[DQPSK Differential<br/>Demapper]
    DMAP --> DTI[Deinterleaver<br/>Time and Frequency]
    DTI --> V1[Viterbi Decoder<br/>Rate 1/2]
    DTI --> V2[Viterbi Decoder<br/>Rate 1/3]
    V1 --> DSCR1[Descrambler<br/>PRBS x9+x5+1]
    V2 --> DSCR2[Descrambler<br/>PRBS x9+x5+1]
    DSCR1 --> FICout[FIC Decoder<br/>MCI SI Tables]
    DSCR2 --> AUDIO[MPEG Layer II<br/>Audio Decoder]
    FICout --> AUDIO
    AUDIO --> SPK[Loudspeaker]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — DAB Transmission Frame (Mode I, 96 ms)

```mermaid
gantt
    title DAB Mode I Transmission Frame Structure 96 ms
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section Frame
    Null Symbol (1297 us)       :a1, 0, 1297
    Phase Reference Symbol 1    :a2, after a1, 1246
    Data Symbol 1  FIC          :a3, after a2, 1246
    Data Symbol 2  FIC          :a4, after a3, 1246
    Data Symbol 3  FIC          :a5, after a4, 1246
    Data Symbols 4 to 74  MSC   :a6, after a5, 84000
```

> **Visual interpretation:** The first 4 data symbols carry the FIC; the remaining 71 carry the MSC payload. Each data symbol is 1246 µs. The full frame fits into 96 ms.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Single Sub-Carrier DQPSK Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[b1 b0] --> DIFF[Differential Encoder]
    DIFF --> ROT[pi over 4 Rotator]
    ROT --> SC[Sub carrier Modulator<br/>e to j 2 pi fc t]
    SC --> CH[Multipath Channel<br/>h tau]
    CH --> RX[Coherent Removal<br/>or Differential Detect]
    RX --> DEC[4 QAM Decision<br/>Device]
    DEC --> BOUT[Recovered b1 b0]
```

> **Note on differential detection:** The receiver multiplies the current received symbol by the complex conjugate of the previous OFDM symbol *on the same sub-carrier*, eliminating the need to estimate the channel's phase rotation.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — DAB Single Frequency Network Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SFN[Single Frequency Network]
        TX1[TX Site 1<br/>VHF Ch 9A] 
        TX2[TX Site 2<br/>VHF Ch 9A]
        TX3[TX Site 3<br/>VHF Ch 9A]
    end
    SFN --> RX[Mobile Receiver<br/>in Car]
    RX --> AUDIO[Audio Output]
    TX1 -.Delay less than 246 us.-> RX
    TX2 -.Delay less than 246 us.-> RX
    TX3 -.Delay less than 246 us.-> RX
```

> **SFN principle:** All transmitters use the **same frequency** and **synchronized bit-clock**. The guard interval absorbs inter-transmitter delays up to 246 µs (≈ 73 km of path difference). The receiver treats the multiple transmitters as **constructive multipath** rather than interference — a striking property unique to COFDM.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define COFDM. Why is it preferred over conventional OFDM in DAB?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** is the combination of standard OFDM with **forward error correction (convolutional coding)** and **time–frequency interleaving** applied to the data stream before transmission.
> 
> Conventional OFDM alone offers no protection against **frequency-selective fading** — a deep null in the spectrum would erase the information on those sub-carriers. COFDM adds:
> 1. **Convolutional coding** with Viterbi decoding — recovers errors.
> 2. **Interleaving** in time and frequency — converts burst errors into random errors, exploiting the FEC's strength against random errors.
> 
> Hence COFDM is **preferred in DAB** because the mobile VHF channel exhibits both multipath (frequency-selective) and Doppler (time-selective) fading, and COFDM handles both robustly. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for OFDM limitation, 1 for the role of coding + interleaving.)**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the function of the Phase Reference Symbol (PRS) in a DAB transmission frame?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> The Phase Reference Symbol is the **second symbol** in every 96 ms DAB transmission frame (Mode I) and has a duration of **1.246 ms**. Its functions are:
> 1. **Coarse frequency synchronization** — the receiver detects the known fixed QPSK pattern on all 1536 sub-carriers and locks its local oscillator.
> 2. **Channel estimation** — by comparing the received PRS to the known transmitted pattern, the receiver estimates the complex channel transfer function $\hat{H}_k$ for each sub-carrier.
> 3. **Frame timing reference** — marks the boundary between frames for deinterleaving and CIF alignment.
> 
> Without the PRS, the receiver could not perform coherent combining of sub-carriers or initialize the FFT window accurately. **(3 marks — 1 mark per point.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Student answers ONE.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the functional blocks of the DAB **transmitter physical layer**. State the purpose of each block. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
> 
> The DAB transmitter physical layer consists of four sequential functional blocks operating on the bitstream:
> 
> 1. **Energy Dispersal Scrambler (1 mark):** XORs the input bitstream with a PRBS generated by polynomial $G(x) = x^9 + x^5 + 1$. Purpose — eliminate long runs of identical bits that would otherwise produce DC and low-frequency spectral components, ensuring a flat spectrum and reliable bit-clock recovery at the receiver.
> 
> 2. **Convolutional Encoder (1.5 marks):** Applies a rate-1/4, constraint-length-7 convolutional code (mother code) using generator polynomials $G_1=133, G_2=171, G_3=145, G_4=133$ in octal. The code is punctured to obtain rate 1/2 (for the FIC) or rate 1/3 (for the MSC). Purpose — add controlled redundancy to enable Viterbi-decoder error correction at the receiver.
> 
> 3. **Time–Frequency Interleaver (1.5 marks):** Reorders the coded bits in two dimensions — across time (depth up to 16 CIFs ≈ 384 ms) and across the 1536 sub-carriers. Purpose — disperse burst and frequency-selective errors so the Viterbi decoder sees pseudo-random errors.
> 
> 4. **COFDM Modulator with π/4-DQPSK Mapping (2 marks):** Groups bits into pairs, applies differential encoding with a π/4 phase rotation, and modulates each pair onto one of 1536 orthogonal sub-carriers using an IFFT of length 2048. A guard interval of 246 µs is appended to every symbol. Purpose — parallel low-rate transmission that is robust to multipath and Doppler, with simple non-coherent differential detection at the receiver.
> 
> **Block diagram (1 mark):** [As shown in Section 4.1 above.]

**(b)** A DAB system operates in **Mode I** with $T_S = 1$ ms, $T_G = 246$ µs, and $K = 1536$ sub-carriers using DQPSK. Calculate:
   1. The sub-carrier spacing $\Delta f$.
   2. The total OFDM symbol duration $T_{sym}$.
   3. The gross MSC bit rate (assuming 74 of 76 OFDM symbols per frame carry MSC/FIC data).
   4. The net bit rate after a rate-1/3 punctured convolutional code. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (7 marks — 1.75 each):**
> 
> **(1)** Sub-carrier spacing — orthogonality condition:
> 
> $$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_S} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000 \, \text{Hz} = 1 \, \text{kHz}$$
> 
> **(2)** Total OFDM symbol duration:
> 
> $$T_{sym} = T_S + T_G = 1000 + 246 = 1246 \, \mu s$$
> 
> **(3)** Gross MSC bit rate — each sub-carrier carries 2 bits per OFDM symbol (DQPSK); per OFDM symbol:
> 
> $$N_{bits/sym} = K \cdot 2 = 1536 \cdot 2 = 3072 \, \text{bits}$$
> 
> Symbol rate is $1/T_S = 1000$ sym/s. Effective data symbols per frame = 74 of 76:
> 
> $$R_{gross} = 3072 \cdot 1000 \cdot \frac{74}{76} \approx 2.99 \, \text{Mbps}$$
> 
> (A more accurate CIF-based calculation gives **2.304 Mbps**; either is acceptable with proper justification — 1 mark for the correct approach and 0.75 for arithmetic.)
> 
> **(4)** Net bit rate after rate-1/3 convolutional code:
> 
> $$R_{net} = R_{gross} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 2.304 \times 0.333 \approx 0.768 \, \text{Mbps}$$

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **DAB transmission frame structure** in Mode I. Sketch the frame, label all symbols with their durations, and state the role of the Null Symbol and the Phase Reference Symbol. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
> 
> A DAB Mode I transmission frame has a **total duration of 96 ms** and consists of **76 OFDM symbols**, each of duration **1.246 ms** (1 ms useful + 0.246 ms guard interval), with the **exception of the first symbol (Null)** which has a special duration of **1.297 ms**. The frame structure is:
> 
> | Position | Symbol | Duration | Function |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Null Symbol** | 1.297 ms | Tx OFF; receiver settles AGC, aligns A/D range |
> | 2 | **Phase Reference Symbol (PRS)** | 1.246 ms | All 1536 sub-carriers transmit known QPSK reference; enables frequency synchronization and channel estimation |
> | 3–76 | **Data Symbols (74 total)** | 1.246 ms each | Carry FIC (first 3) and MSC (next 71) data |
> 
> **Null Symbol (2 marks):** Transmitter is OFF for 1.297 ms, allowing the receiver to:
> - Settle automatic gain control (AGC) without interference.
> - Perform coarse symbol timing and frequency-offset estimation.
> - Align its A/D converter dynamic range to the upcoming signal.
> 
> **Phase Reference Symbol (2 marks):** A predetermined complex pattern is transmitted on all 1536 sub-carriers. The receiver:
> - Acquires fine carrier-frequency offset.
> - Estimates the channel frequency response $H_k$ for each sub-carrier.
> - Marks the start of the data section.
> 
> **Frame sketch (1 mark):** [As shown in Section 4.3 Gantt chart.]
> 
> **Mode I summary (1 mark):** $K = 1536$, $T_S = 1$ ms, $T_G = 246$ µs, $\Delta f = 1$ kHz, $B = 1.536$ MHz.

**(b)** Describe the **convolutional encoding and puncturing scheme** used in DAB. Why is the FIC always protected with rate-1/2 coding while the MSC may use 1/3, 2/3, or 3/4? **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
> 
> **Mother code (2 marks):** DAB uses a **rate-1/4 convolutional code** with constraint length $K_c = 7$. The four generator polynomials in octal are:
> 
> $$G_1 = 133, \quad G_2 = 171, \quad G_3 = 145, \quad G_4 = 133$$
> 
> For every 1 input bit, the encoder produces 4 output bits $(c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4)$, giving a coding gain of approximately **5 dB** at BER = $10^{-4}$ with Viterbi soft-decision decoding.
> 
> **Puncturing (2 marks):** To trade robustness for capacity, the mother code is **punctured** by deleting specific output bits according to a periodic pattern:
> - **Rate 1/2** — transmit $c_1, c_2$ only; delete $c_3, c_4$.
> - **Rate 1/3** — transmit $c_1, c_2, c_3$; delete $c_4$.
> - **Rate 2/3, 3/4** — transmit fewer bits per input; provides less protection but doubles or triples the throughput.
> 
> The receiver is informed of the puncturing pattern via the FIC and applies Viterbi decoding on the de-punctured sequence.
> 
> **Why FIC uses rate 1/2 (1.5 marks):** The FIC carries the **Multiplex Configuration Information (MCI)** — without it, the receiver cannot locate services or decode any audio. Loss of FIC would be catastrophic (loss of the entire ensemble), so the highest protection (rate 1/2, with the entire mother code family still recoverable) is mandatory. The capacity penalty is acceptable because FIC data is small (~ 32 kbps).
> 
> **Why MSC uses variable rates (1.5 marks):** The MSC carries user data (audio + PAD). Different sub-channels have different error-tolerance requirements:
> - **Rate 1/3** — for low-bit-rate stereo audio where robust reception is paramount.
> - **Rate 2/3 or 3/4** — for high-bit-rate audio or non-critical data services where capacity matters more.
> 
> The ensemble operator chooses the code rate per sub-channel to balance **audio quality vs. robustness** in the target coverage area.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose 2–3 marks):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the π/4 offset in DQPSK** — naive QPSK mapping (with constellation points at {0°, 90°, 180°, 270°}) is wrong; DAB uses {45°, 135°, 225°, 315°} rotated by π/4 per symbol to avoid the origin.
> 2. **Confusing "carrier spacing" and "channel spacing"** — DAB Mode I has $\Delta f = 1$ kHz but channel allocation raster is 1.712 MHz. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Writing null symbol duration as 1 ms** — it is **1.297 ms** (special AGC-settling value).
> 4. **Coding rate arithmetic** — the rate-1/4 mother code has effective coding gain ~ 5 dB; rate-1/2 punctured gives ~ 3.7 dB. Don't claim "more bits = less protection" without justification.
> 5. **Omitting the guard interval** in frame duration calculation — 75 × 1246 + 1297 = 94747 µs ≈ 96 ms, **not** 76 × 1246 = 94696 µs (this is a common off-by-one mistake).
> 6. **Forgetting to mention Viterbi decoding** — the convolutional encoder is useless without specifying the decoder at the receiver end.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this to your revision wall — every KTU question on DAB Physical Layer touches at least three of these points.**

- ⭐ **DAB Physical Layer = 4 blocks:** Scrambling → Convolutional Coding → Interleaving → COFDM (π/4-DQPSK).
- ⭐ **Mode I golden numbers:** $K = 1536$, $T_S = 1$ ms, $T_G = 246$ µs, $T_{sym} = 1246$ µs, $T_N = 1297$ µs, $T_F = 96$ ms, $B = 1.536$ MHz, $\Delta f = 1$ kHz.
- ⭐ **Frame = 1 Null + 1 PRS + 74 Data symbols.**
- ⭐ **Guard interval (T_G) absorbs multipath echoes** up to ≈ 73 km path difference (for Mode I).
- ⭐ **Null Symbol** is transmitter-OFF → receiver AGC, coarse synchronization.
- ⭐ **Phase Reference Symbol** is a known QPSK pattern on all 1536 sub-carriers → fine sync, channel estimation.
- ⭐ **FIC** = control plane, always rate 1/2, frequency-only interleaving, 32 kbps.
- ⭐ **MSC** = user plane, rate 1/3 to 3/4, time + frequency interleaving, up to ~ 768 kbps net (Mode I).
- ⭐ **Mother convolutional code:** Rate 1/4, $K_c = 7$, polynomials $133, 171, 145, 133$ (octal).
- ⭐ **PRBS polynomial:** $G(x) = x^9 + x^5 + 1$, 9-bit LFSR.
- ⭐ **π/4-DQPSK** uses differential detection — no channel estimation required at symbol level; PI/4 rotation prevents origin crossing.
- ⭐ **SFN** is enabled by the guard interval — all transmitters on the same frequency.
- ⭐ **Audio codec** = MPEG-1 Layer II (MP2), 32–256 kbps per sub-channel.
- ⭐ **DAB+ upgrade** uses HE-AAC v2 + Reed–Solomon outer code; physical layer unchanged.
- ⭐ **Interleaving depth:** Time = 16 CIFs (384 ms); Frequency = full 1.536 MHz bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Useful bit rate formula:** $R_{net} = K \cdot 2 \cdot (1/T_S) \cdot (N_{data}/N_{total}) \cdot R_c$.
- ⭐ **FFT size in Mode I = 2048** (of which 1536 are active sub-carriers, 512 are virtual side-carriers).
- ⭐ **DAB operates in VHF Band III (174–240 MHz) and L-Band (1452–1492 MHz)** — Kerala's DAB pilot is in Band III.
- ⭐ **COFDM** = OFDM + Coding + Interleaving — the "C" is what makes DAB robust to multipath.

> **End of Module 2 — Physical Layer of DAB Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAB Modulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DAB Modulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
The **DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting) Modulator** is the transmission-side signal processing subsystem defined under the **Eureka-147 / ETSI EN 300 401** standard that converts compressed digital audio (and ancillary data) into a robust, multipath-resistant **COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** waveform suitable for terrestrial VHF Band III or L-Band transmission. It performs audio source coding, channel coding, interleaving, differential QPSK mapping, OFDM modulation via IFFT, and guard interval insertion to combat intersymbol interference (ISI) caused by long multipath echoes typical in mobile reception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> DAB modulation is built on the COFDM principle. Unlike analog FM, DAB distributes information across thousands of closely spaced orthogonal subcarriers, each carrying a low-bit-rate DQPSK symbol. This is the *core* of why DAB works flawlessly in moving vehicles.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have a long, important letter (your audio program) and you want to send it across a noisy, echo-filled stadium. Instead of giving the whole letter to **one messenger** (analog FM) who might trip and lose half of it, you **tear the letter into 1,536 tiny slips of paper** and hand each slip to a different runner. They all walk out at exactly the right moment (orthogonal timing) so they don't bump into each other. If a few runners fall (multipath fading), you still reconstruct 99% of the letter from the others. This is exactly what a DAB modulator does:

- **Tearing the letter** = Serial-to-Parallel conversion (splitting data into subcarrier streams)
- **Different runners** = 1,536 orthogonal subcarriers (COFDM carriers)
- **All walking at the right moment** = IFFT operation ensures orthogonality
- **Protection against falls** = Convolutional coding + time/frequency interleaving
- **Letter in your hand** = Reconstructed audio at the receiver

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **FFT size (Mode I)** | **2,048 points** | Generates 1,536 useful subcarriers |
| **Carrier spacing (Mode I)** | **1 kHz** | Maintains orthogonality |
| **Total RF bandwidth** | **1.536 MHz** | ETSI spectral mask |
| **Guard interval (Mode I)** | **246 µs** | Absorbs echoes up to ~73 km |
| **Bit rate (net)** | **~1.2 – 1.5 Mbit/s** | Capacity for ~6 CD-quality programs |
| **Modulation per carrier** | **DQPSK (π/4-DQPSK)** | Differential, no channel estimation needed |
| **Operating band** | **VHF Band III (174–240 MHz)** & **L-Band (1.452–1.492 GHz)** | International DAB allocations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DQPSK and not QPSK?**
> DAB receivers in cars experience rapid channel variations (Doppler shift). Tracking absolute phase is nearly impossible. With *Differential* QPSK, the receiver only compares the **phase change between two successive OFDM symbols** on the same carrier — no pilot-based channel estimation is required, which dramatically simplifies the receiver.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Block-Level Operation of a DAB Modulator

The DAB modulator performs a *strictly defined* sequence of operations, which must be followed in this order according to the Eureka-147 specification:

1. **Audio Source Coding (MPEG-1 Audio Layer II)**
   - Each audio program is compressed at typically **128 kbit/s (stereo)** or **192 kbit/s (stereo)**.
   - Produces a *Main Service Channel* (MSC) stream.
   - Auxiliary data (PAD — Program Associated Data) is embedded within the Layer II bitstream.

2. **Energy Dispersal Scrambling**
   - A **Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS)** of length $2^{15}-1$ is XORed with the bitstream.
   - Purpose: To "whiten" the spectrum, preventing long strings of 0s or 1s that would produce strong discrete spectral lines and impair clock recovery at the receiver.

3. **Convolutional Channel Coding**
   - A **mother code of rate $1/4$** with constraint length $K = 7$ is the baseline.
   - Through *puncturing* (deleting certain output bits), the effective code rate is increased to $3/8$, $1/2$, or $3/4$.
   - The choice of rate depends on the **Equal Error Protection (EEP)** or **Unequal Error Protection (UEP)** profile selected for the service.

4. **Time Interleaving**
   - The convolutional code output is **convolutional time-interleaved** with depth typically equal to 16 frames.
   - Spreads burst errors (caused by brief fades) across multiple time slots so that the Viterbi decoder can correct them.

5. **Frequency Interleaving (DAB-specific, not present in DVB-T)**
   - Within each OFDM symbol, the QPSK symbols are **re-ordered across the 1,536 subcarriers** according to a fixed permutation rule.
   - Ensures that adjacent frequency-domain fades do not wipe out adjacent logical bits.

6. **QPSK Mapping → DQPSK**
   - Pairs of bits are mapped to one of four constellation points: **$(1,1), (-1,1), (-1,-1), (1,-1)$**.
   - The *differential* encoding causes the current OFDM symbol's phase to be the **sum** of the previous symbol's phase and the new QPSK phase.

7. **OFDM Modulation (IFFT)**
   - The complex frequency-domain samples $X_k$ are fed into a **2,048-point Inverse FFT**.
   - The output $x_n$ is a time-domain signal of duration **1 ms** (in Mode I).

8. **Guard Interval Insertion (Cyclic Prefix)**
   - A copy of the **last 504 samples** (in Mode I) is prepended to the IFFT output.
   - Creates a **cyclic prefix of 246 µs** that absorbs multipath echoes.

9. **DAB Transmission Frame Assembly**
   - The stream is organized into **transmission frames of 96 ms** (Mode I), each containing:
     - **Null symbol** (1.297 ms) — for coarse synchronization.
     - **Phase Reference Symbol** (1.297 ms) — for fine synchronization & channel estimation.
     - **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — 3 OFDM symbols carrying multiplex configuration.
     - **Main Service Channel (MSC)** — 72 OFDM symbols carrying audio + data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Hook — Why cyclic prefix, not zero padding?**
> A cyclic prefix is a *copy* of the end of the symbol, not zeros. This makes the linear convolution of the transmitted signal with the channel impulse response equivalent to a **circular convolution** in the time domain. That is precisely the property that allows the receiver to use a simple FFT to recover each subcarrier's data with only a single-tap equalizer per carrier.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of carriers (Mode I)** | $N = 1536$ | Useful subcarriers |
| **FFT size** | $N_{FFT} = 2N + 2N_{null} = 2048$ | Includes virtual + null carriers |
| **Carrier spacing** | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ | $T_u$ = useful symbol duration |
| **Useful symbol duration (Mode I)** | $T_u = 1\,\text{ms}$ | $1/1000$ second |
| **Guard interval duration** | $T_g = \dfrac{T_u}{4} = 246\,\mu s$ | Mode I default |
| **Total symbol duration** | $T_s = T_u + T_g = 1.246\,\text{ms}$ | |
| **OFDM equation (transmitter)** | $x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j2\pi f_k t}$ | Sum of subcarriers |
| **DQPSK encoding rule** | $Y_k = X_k \cdot Y_{k-1}$ | Differential mapping |
| **Maximum Doppler tolerance (3 GHz)** | $f_d = \dfrac{v}{\lambda}$ | Determines upper speed limit |
| **Maximum echo tolerance** | $\tau_{max} = T_g$ | Guard interval length |
| **Convolutional code rate (with puncturing)** | $R = \dfrac{k}{n}$ | e.g., $1/2, 3/4$ |
| **Coding gain (rate 1/2, K=7)** | $G_c \approx 5\,\text{dB}$ | At BER $= 10^{-5}$ |
| **Net bit rate (Mode I, EEP-3A, $T_g = T_u/4$)** | $\approx 1.152\,\text{Mbit/s}$ | MSC capacity |
| **Interleaving depth** | $D = 16$ frames | Time interleaving depth |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
The DAB modulator is the *heart* of every national digital radio network (e.g., All India Radio's DAB+ rollout, BBC in UK, Deutschlandradio in Germany). Its design lets a single RF channel of 1.536 MHz carry a bouquet of 6–9 CD-quality audio programs, an Electronic Programme Guide (EPG), traffic announcements (TPEG), and even slideshow images — all with **single-frequency network (SFN)** capability, where all transmitters across a country broadcast on the **same frequency** with **perfect synchronization**, turning the network itself into a *distributed antenna* with constructive macro-diversity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — The OFDM Equation from First Principles
The DAB modulator must generate a multi-carrier signal. We start from the requirement that $N$ orthogonal subcarriers carry $N$ independent QPSK symbols within one useful OFDM symbol of duration $T_u$.

**Step 1: Orthogonality condition.**
Two subcarriers $f_m$ and $f_n$ are orthogonal over the interval $[0, T_u]$ if and only if:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_u} \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_n t)\,dt = 0, \quad m \neq n
$$

**Step 2: The minimum carrier spacing that satisfies orthogonality.**
The integral evaluates to zero if $\Delta f = f_m - f_n$ is a non-zero integer multiple of $1/T_u$. The **minimum** such spacing is:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u}
$$

**Step 3: Build the OFDM signal.**
For $N$ subcarriers centered at frequencies $f_k = f_0 + k\Delta f$, the composite signal is:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi f_k t}, \quad 0 \leq t < T_u
$$

**Step 4: Sample the signal at $t = nT_u/N$.**
Substituting $f_k = f_0 + k/T_u$ and $t = nT_u/N$:

$$
x[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi(f_0 + k/T_u) \cdot nT_u/N}
$$

**Step 5: Drop the common factor $e^{j2\pi f_0 nT_u/N}$** (handled by the RF upconverter), giving:

$$
x[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \cdot e^{j2\pi kn/N}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is *exactly* the definition of the **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)**. The DAB modulator therefore implements the OFDM modulator using an **IFFT** algorithm.

**Step 6: Number of FFT points needed for Mode I.**
DAB Mode I requires $N = 1536$ useful carriers. The 2,048-point FFT/IFFT is chosen because:

- $2048 = 2^{11}$ → highly efficient **radix-2 FFT** implementation.
- 1,536 carriers map to indices $k = -768$ to $+768$ (excluding DC and edges).
- The remaining samples are virtual/null carriers for spectral shaping.

### Derivation 2 — DQPSK Differential Encoding Rule
Let $X_k$ be the *current* QPSK symbol to be transmitted, drawn from the constellation $\left\{e^{j\pi/4}, e^{j3\pi/4}, e^{j5\pi/4}, e^{j7\pi/4}\right\}$. Let $Y_{k-1}$ be the *previous* transmitted symbol (which the receiver already knows from its previous decision).

**Step 1:** Differential phase shift.
The transmitted symbol is:

$$
Y_k = Y_{k-1} \cdot X_k
$$

**Step 2:** At the receiver, the recovered QPSK symbol is obtained by:

$$
\hat{X}_k = Y_k \cdot Y_{k-1}^{*}
$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude check — the noise on $Y_{k-1}$ appears in *both* numerator and denominator, so differential detection has a ~2–3 dB SNR penalty compared to coherent QPSK, but requires **zero channel estimation**.

### Derivation 3 — Guard Interval Length for SFN Operation
In a Single Frequency Network, two transmitters at distance $d$ km apart broadcast the same bit. The differential delay is:

$$
\Delta \tau = \frac{d}{c}, \quad c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}
$$

To avoid ISI, the guard interval $T_g$ must satisfy $T_g \geq \Delta \tau_{max}$. For Mode I:

$$
T_g = 246\,\mu s \implies \Delta \tau_{max} = 246 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
d_{max} = c \cdot T_g = 3 \times 10^8 \times 246 \times 10^{-6} \approx 73.8 \text{ km}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is why DAB transmitters in an SFN must be spaced **no more than ~70 km apart** in Mode I. Mode II has a longer guard interval (~$623\,\mu s$) but proportionally fewer carriers.

### Derivation 4 — Net MSC Bit Rate
The MSC consists of 72 OFDM symbols per 96 ms frame. Each OFDM symbol carries 1,536 QPSK symbols = 3,072 bits.

**Step 1:** Raw bit count per frame.

$$
\text{Bits}_{\text{raw}} = 72 \times 3072 = 221{,}184 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply code rate $R = 1/2$ (typical for EEP-3A).

$$
\text{Bits}_{\text{net}} = 221{,}184 \times 0.5 = 110{,}592 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by frame duration $T_f = 96 \text{ ms}$.

$$
R_{b} = \frac{110{,}592}{0.096} = 1{,}152{,}000 \text{ bit/s} = 1.152 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

This 1.152 Mbit/s is shared among all services in the DAB ensemble. With Layer II at 128 kbit/s per stereo program, the ensemble can carry ~6–9 audio programs simultaneously.

### Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which DAB Mode

| Criterion | Mode I | Mode II | Mode III | Mode IV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ (carriers) | 1,536 | 384 | 192 | 768 |
| $T_u$ | 1 ms | 250 µs | 125 µs | 500 µs |
| $T_g$ | 246 µs | 62 µs | 31 µs | 123 µs |
| $T_g/T_u$ | 1/4 | 1/4 | 1/4 | 1/4 |
| Max SFN distance | ~74 km | ~18 km | ~9 km | ~37 km |
| Max Doppler (mobile) | Best | Excellent | Moderate | Good |
| Typical use | **Terrestrial VHF SFN** | **L-band local** | Satellite / hybrid | **DAB+ / Hybrid** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Complete DAB Modulator Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Audio Source 1\nLayer II Encoder] --> mux[Service Multiplexer]
    srcB[Audio Source 2\nLayer II Encoder] --> mux
    srcC[Audio Source 3\nLayer II Encoder] --> mux
    srcD[Data Service\nPAD] --> mux
    pad[Programme Associated\nData PAD] --> mux
    
    mux --> fic[Fast Information\nChannel FIC Generator]
    
    fic --> scram[Energy Dispersal\nPRBS Scrambler]
    mux --> scram
    
    scram --> cc[Convolutional Encoder\nMother Rate 1/4 K=7]
    cc --> punct[Puncturing\nto rate 1/2, 3/8, 3/4]
    punct --> ti[Time Interleaver\nDepth 16 frames]
    
    ti --> fi[Frequency Interleaver\nDAB-specific permutation]
    fi --> qpsk[DQPSK Mapper\nDifferential Phase Encoder]
    
    qpsk --> ifft[2048-point IFFT\nOFDM Modulator]
    ifft --> gi[Guard Interval Insertion\nCyclic Prefix 246 us]
    
    gi --> frame[DAB Frame Assembler\n96 ms Superframe Structure]
    frame --> dac[DAC and RF Upconverter\nVHF Band III or L-Band]
    dac --> ant[TX Antenna]
    
    ant -.-> air((Multipath Channel\nSFN Network))
```

### Diagram 2 — DAB Transmission Frame Structure (Mode I)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    frame["DAB Transmission Frame\nDuration 96 ms Mode I"] --> null[Null Symbol\n1297 us coarse sync]
    frame --> prs[Phase Reference Symbol\n1297 us fine sync + channel estimate]
    frame --> fic[FIC OFDM Symbols\nMultiplex Configuration Data]
    frame --> msc[MSC OFDM Symbols 1 to 72\nAudio + Data Services]
    
    null --> prs
    prs --> fic
    fic --> msc
    msc --> sf[Convolutional Time Interleaving spans 16 frames]
    
    style null fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#7ad9ff,color:#ffffff
    style prs fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ff9d4d,color:#ffffff
    style fic fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#a3ff7a,color:#000000
    style msc fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ff7ad9,color:#000000
```

### Diagram 3 — DQPSK Constellation and Differential Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in[Bit Pair b0 b1] --> enc[Differential Phase Logic]
    prev[Previous Symbol Y sub k-1] --> enc
    enc --> sym[New Symbol Y sub k]
    
    sym --> const[Constellation Points:\n  11 maps to plus 1 plus j\n  01 maps to minus 1 plus j\n  00 maps to minus 1 minus j\n  10 maps to plus 1 minus j]
    
    style in fill:#2d3a1f,stroke:#7ad9ff,color:#ffffff
    style enc fill:#3a1f2d,stroke:#ff9d4d,color:#ffffff
    style const fill:#1f3a3a,stroke:#a3ff7a,color:#ffffff
```

### Diagram 4 — Multipath Echo Absorption by Cyclic Prefix

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TX as DAB Modulator
    participant CH as Multipath Channel
    participant RX as DAB Receiver
    TX->>CH: Transmit OFDM symbol of length 1246 us
    Note over CH: Direct path arrives at t=0
    CH->>RX: Direct signal
    Note over CH: Echo from building arrives 100 us later
    CH->>RX: Echoed signal delayed 100 us
    Note over RX: FFT window opens AFTER 246 us guard interval
    RX->>RX: Both direct and echo fall inside the useful 1000 us window
    RX->>RX: Single-tap equalizer per subcarrier recovers the data
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Caption:** The cyclic prefix makes the time-domain received signal equivalent to a **circular convolution** of the transmitted symbol with the channel impulse response. Therefore, the receiver's FFT diagonalizes the channel into $N$ independent flat-fading sub-channels, each corrected by a single complex multiplication.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 1]**
*List any three salient features of DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting) compared to conventional analog FM broadcasting.*

**Model Answer (3 points, 1 mark each):**
1. **Multiplexed bouquet:** A single DAB frequency block of 1.536 MHz can carry 6–9 CD-quality audio programs, whereas one FM channel carries only one mono program.
2. **Multipath and mobility robustness:** COFDM with 1,536 carriers and DQPSK modulation eliminates the multipath distortion and "picket-fencing" typical of FM in moving vehicles.
3. **Single Frequency Network (SFN) capability:** Multiple transmitters across a region can broadcast the same program on the *same* frequency, dramatically improving spectral efficiency and coverage uniformity.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam Reference]**
*Why does DAB use DQPSK modulation instead of coherent QPSK?*

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. The mobile DAB channel exhibits rapid time-varying fading due to Doppler shift, making absolute phase tracking very difficult.
2. **Differential** detection compares the phase between two *consecutive* OFDM symbols, requiring **no pilot-based channel estimation** — only the *difference* in phase is decoded.
3. This greatly simplifies the receiver's digital signal processing, reduces cost, and improves robustness in vehicular reception, at the cost of a ~2–3 dB SNR penalty.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)
> *As per KTU ESE pattern, answer any ONE of the following.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional blocks of a DAB modulator** for terrestrial transmission. Clearly mention the role of the **convolutional encoder**, **time interleaver**, **frequency interleaver**, and **DQPSK mapper**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **net bit rate** of the Main Service Channel (MSC) of a DAB system operating in **Mode I** with a guard interval of $T_g = T_u/4$ and a convolutional code rate of $R = 1/2$. Assume 72 OFDM symbols per frame and a frame duration of 96 ms. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram explanation — Key Points for 7 marks:**

| # | Block | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | MPEG-1 Layer II encoder | Compresses each audio program to ~128–192 kbit/s |
| 2 | Service multiplexer | Combines multiple audio + data into MSC; control data into FIC |
