| 3 | Energy dispersal (PRBS) | Whitens the bitstream to prevent spectral line accumulation |
| 4 | Convolutional encoder | Mother code rate 1/4, constraint length K=7; provides ~5 dB coding gain |
| 5 | Puncturing | Raises effective rate to 1/2, 3/8, or 3/4 to trade capacity vs. robustness |
| 6 | Time interleaver | Spreads burst errors across 16 frames; depth 16 |
| 7 | Frequency interleaver | Reorders subcarriers within each OFDM symbol (DAB-specific) |
| 8 | DQPSK mapper | Maps bit pairs to differential phase shifts $\in \lbrace \pm\pi/4, \pm 3\pi/4 \rbrace$ |
| 9 | 2048-point IFFT | Generates the OFDM baseband signal (orthogonal multiplexing) |
| 10 | Guard interval insertion | Prepends cyclic prefix of 246 µs (Mode I) to absorb echoes |
| 11 | Frame assembler | Builds 96 ms superframe with Null, PRS, FIC, MSC symbols |
| 12 | RF upconverter + antenna | Shifts to VHF Band III or L-Band |

**(b) Net MSC bit rate derivation — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Mode I: $N = 1536$ subcarriers
- 72 OFDM symbols per frame for MSC
- Frame duration $T_f = 96$ ms
- Code rate $R = 1/2$
- QPSK → 2 bits per symbol

**Step 1: Bits per OFDM symbol (raw).**

$$
\text{Bits per symbol} = N \times \log_2(M) = 1536 \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Bits per frame in MSC (raw, before coding).**

$$
\text{Bits}_{\text{raw/frame}} = 72 \times 3072 = 221{,}184 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Apply convolutional code rate $R = 1/2$.**

$$
\text{Bits}_{\text{net/frame}} = 221{,}184 \times \frac{1}{2} = 110{,}592 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Divide by frame duration.**

$$
R_b = \frac{110{,}592 \text{ bits}}{0.096 \text{ s}} = 1{,}152{,}000 \text{ bit/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_b = 1.152 \text{ Mbit/s}}
$$

This capacity supports ~6 stereo Layer II programs at 192 kbit/s each (with 64 kbit/s overhead for PAD/FIC).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **COFDM modulation principle** used in DAB. Show mathematically that the **minimum carrier spacing** required to maintain orthogonality between adjacent subcarriers over a useful symbol duration $T_u$ is $\Delta f = 1/T_u$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a clear diagram, describe the **DAB transmission frame structure in Mode I**. What is the function of the **Phase Reference Symbol (PRS)** and the **Fast Information Channel (FIC)**? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) COFDM orthogonality derivation — 7 marks:**

Two subcarriers $\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and $\cos(2\pi f_n t)$ are orthogonal over $[0, T_u]$ iff:

$$
\int_0^{T_u} \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_n t) \, dt = 0, \quad m \neq n
$$

Using the product-to-sum identity:

$$
= \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{T_u} \left[ \cos(2\pi(f_m-f_n)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_m+f_n)t) \right] dt
$$

Both terms integrate to zero if and only if:

$$
(f_m - f_n)T_u \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}
$$

The minimum non-zero integer is 1, giving:

$$
\boxed{\Delta f_{min} = \frac{1}{T_u}}
$$

**Conclusion:** For Mode I with $T_u = 1$ ms, $\Delta f = 1$ kHz. The 1,536 carriers span $1.536$ MHz, which is exactly the ETSI-defined DAB channel bandwidth.

**Interpretation:** Because each subcarrier spectrum has a $\text{sinc}(f)$ shape whose first null sits at the *neighbouring* subcarrier's center frequency, the carriers do not interfere even though their spectra overlap heavily — this is the essence of *orthogonal* multiplexing.

**(b) DAB Frame Structure in Mode I — 7 marks:**

A DAB transmission frame in Mode I has total duration **96 ms** and consists of the following OFDM symbols in sequence:

| # | Component | Duration | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Null Symbol** | 1.297 ms | Carries no power; coarse symbol timing & AGC |
| 2 | **Phase Reference Symbol (PRS)** | 1.297 ms | Pilot cells in fixed positions for fine sync & channel estimation |
| 3 | **FIC** | 3 OFDM symbols | Multiplex configuration, service info, fast signalling |
| 4 | **MSC** | 72 OFDM symbols | Audio programs and data services |
| 5 | (Total) | 96 ms | One complete DAB frame |

**Phase Reference Symbol (PRS):** The PRS contains *known* DQPSK symbols on a fixed subcarrier pattern (called *scattered pilots* and *continual pilots* spread across all 1,536 carriers). The receiver uses this **known reference** to:
- Estimate the channel frequency response $H(f)$ for each subcarrier.
- Apply a single-tap equalizer to correct channel distortion.
- Achieve frame synchronization.

**Fast Information Channel (FIC):** The FIC is a low-data-rate, highly protected signalling channel that tells the receiver *how* the rest of the frame is organized. It carries:
- **MCI (Multiplex Configuration Information):** which services are in the MSC, their bit rates, protection profiles, and start addresses.
- **SI (Service Information):** Service labels, program types, language codes, EPG data, and announcements (traffic, news).

> Because the FIC is protected with the strongest code rate (typically 1/4 punctured to 1/3), it is decoded first and is essential for the receiver to "find" the audio services in the MSC.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DAB Modulator Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing QPSK with DQPSK:** Many students write "QPSK" when they mean differential. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this error because differential encoding is a *defining* DAB feature.
> 2. **Forgetting the IFFT step:** Stating that "data is sent on 1,536 subcarriers" without mentioning the **Inverse FFT** as the implementation mechanism loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong guard interval formula:** Writing $T_g = T_u/4$ without specifying the **Mode** is incomplete. Mode I has $T_u = 1$ ms; Mode II has $T_u = 250\,\mu s$. Always state both.
> 4. **Net bit-rate arithmetic:** Forgetting to apply the **coding rate** when computing net throughput loses 2 marks. Raw bits = 221,184/frame; *net* bits = 110,592/frame at $R = 1/2$.
> 5. **DAB vs. DVB-T confusion:** DAB has **frequency interleaving** and uses **DQPSK**; DVB-T does *not* have these. Mixing up the two standards costs marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DAB = Eureka-147 / ETSI EN 300 401**, using **COFDM + DQPSK**.
- **1536 carriers (Mode I)**, 1 kHz spacing, **1.246 ms total symbol** (1 ms useful + 246 µs guard).
- **DQPSK** chosen for **simplicity of receiver** (no channel estimation).
- **Convolutional code** mother rate **1/4, K=7**, punctured to 1/3, 3/8, 1/2, or 3/4.
- **Time interleaving** depth = 16 frames; **frequency interleaving** is DAB-specific.
- **DAB Frame = Null + PRS + FIC + MSC**, total **96 ms (Mode I)**.
- **PRS** = scattered/continual pilots for channel estimation and synchronization.
- **FIC** = signalling channel (MCI + SI), most heavily protected.
- **MSC** carries compressed audio (MPEG-1 Layer II at ~128–192 kbit/s).
- **IFFF of size 2048** implements the OFDM modulation; cyclic prefix = guard interval.
- **Net MSC bit rate** at $R = 1/2$ in Mode I = **1.152 Mbit/s**.
- **Single Frequency Network (SFN)** possible because guard interval absorbs inter-transmitter echoes up to **~74 km (Mode I)**.
- **Four modes (I–IV)** for VHF, L-band, satellite, and hybrid scenarios.
- **Energy dispersal** PRBS scrambler prevents DC bias and spectral line accumulation.
- **DAB+** uses **HE-AAC v2** instead of MPEG-1 Layer II for better compression, but the modulator chain is *unchanged*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAB Data Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DAB Data Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of DAB Data Structure

The **DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting) Data Structure** defines the systematic organization of digital audio, associated data, and control information into a hierarchical transmission frame format, as standardized under **ETSI EN 300 401** (the European standard for DAB). It describes how multiplexed audio services and ancillary data are packed, interleaved, and protected before being modulated onto multiple orthogonal sub-carriers using **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The DAB data structure is the backbone of the Eureka-147 DAB system. For KTU examinations, students must be able to draw the transmission frame, explain the role of FIC and MSC, and describe the logical organization of services, sub-channels, and capacity units.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Organization of DAB

The DAB system organizes data at multiple levels. From top to bottom, the hierarchy is:

| Level | Entity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ensemble** | A complete DAB transmission from one frequency block, containing several services |
| 2 | **Service** | A user-selectable audio stream (e.g., Radio Mirchi, BBC Radio 1) |
| 3 | **Service Component** | The actual audio or data element within a service |
| 4 | **Sub-channel** | The logical data pipe inside the Main Service Channel |
| 5 | **Capacity Unit (CU)** | The smallest addressable data block, **1 CU = 8 bytes = 64 bits** |
| 6 | **Common Interleaved Frame (CIF)** | The fundamental data unit of MSC, contains **864 CUs** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Delivery Truck"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — How DAB Packs Data**
> Imagine a newspaper printing press:
> - The **full newspaper (DAB Transmission Frame)** is delivered every 24 ms.
> - The **front page (FIC — Fast Information Channel)** contains the *table of contents* — it tells the receiver *which page has which article*.
> - The **inner pages (MSC — Main Service Channel)** contain the actual *news articles* (audio services).
> - Each article is divided into **chunks (Capacity Units)** so the printing press (multiplexer) can efficiently interleave them.
> - The **delivery truck (OFDM modulator)** then broadcasts the entire newspaper simultaneously on thousands of sub-frequencies to avoid being jammed by echoes in the city.

This analogy captures the essence of the DAB data structure: **multiplexed, time-interleaved, frequency-diverse transmission** of digital audio.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

- **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing):** Modulation scheme using thousands of closely spaced orthogonal sub-carriers.
- **Eureka-147:** The original European research project that defined DAB.
- **ETSI EN 300 401:** The official DAB system standard.
- **DAB+:** The upgraded version using HE-AAC v2 audio coding.
- **TII (Transmitter Identification Information):** Identifies which transmitter is broadcasting, enabling seamless handover.
- **FIG (Fast Information Group):** A logical grouping of control information in the FIC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DAB Transmission Frame — Top-Level Architecture

A DAB transmission is organized into a **repeating Transmission Frame** of duration **96 ms**, regardless of the DAB mode (I, II, III, IV). The frame is composed of:

1. **Null Symbol (T_null)** — Duration: ~1.297 ms (Mode I)
2. **Synchronization Channel (SC)** — Carried by the **Synchronization Symbol (T_sync)**
3. **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — Carried by the remaining OFDM symbols
4. **Main Service Channel (MSC)** — Carried by the same OFDM symbols, logically separated

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Null Symbol?**
> The Null Symbol creates a "silent period" that the receiver uses to:
> - Perform **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** adjustment
> - Detect the start of a new frame
> - Measure the radio environment (e.g., for TII decoding)

## 2.2 The OFDM Symbol Structure

Each OFDM symbol contains multiple carriers, classified into:

| Carrier Type | Function | Quantity (Mode I) |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Pilots** | Frequency synchronization & channel estimation | Fixed positions, reused as TPS |
| **Time Pilots** | Time synchronization | Spread across the symbol |
| **TII Carriers** | Transmitter Identification | Rotating pattern |
| **Data Carriers** | Carry actual MSC + FIC data | Majority of carriers |
| **Control Cells** | Carry FIC data exclusively | Reserved positions |

### Operation of TII:
- In consecutive OFDM symbols, the TII pattern **rotates**, so each transmitter gets a unique signature.
- The receiver decodes this to know which transmitter it is connected to, enabling **seamless handover** while driving.

## 2.3 Fast Information Channel (FIC) — The Control Channel

The FIC is **non-interleaved, highly protected** data. It carries:

- **MCI — Multiplex Configuration Information (FIG 0)**
  - Ensemble configuration, sub-channel organization, service definitions
- **SI — Service Information (FIG 1)**
  - Service labels, program type, language
- **CA — Conditional Access Information (FIG 2)**
  - Entitlement messages for encrypted services
- **FIDC — Fast Information Data Channel (used for paging, EPG, TMC)**

**Structure:** FIC is divided into **Fast Information Blocks (FIB)**, each containing multiple **Fast Information Groups (FIGs)**.

## 2.4 Main Service Channel (MSC) — The Payload Channel

The MSC carries the actual audio (PAD — Program Associated Data) and data services.

- Organized as a sequence of **Common Interleaved Frames (CIFs)**.
- **1 CIF = 55296 bits = 864 Capacity Units (CUs)**
- **1 CU = 64 bits = 8 bytes** (the atomic data unit)
- Sub-channels are formed by **consecutive CUs** within the CIF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-channel Bitrate Equation:**
> Given a sub-channel of size S CUs and a protection profile with rate R, the audio bitrate is:
> $$\text{Bitrate} = \frac{S \times 64 \text{ bits}}{24 \text{ ms}}$$
> This gives a baseline of **~2.667 kbps per CU**. For example, a 128 CU sub-channel yields **128 × 2.667 ≈ 341 kbps**, suitable for DAB+ with HE-AAC v2.

## 2.5 Time Interleaving — Burst Error Protection

To combat time-selective fading (common in mobile reception), the bits of one logical frame are **spread across multiple CIFs**:

- A single logical frame spans **16 CIFs** (over 16 × 24 ms = 384 ms in Mode I).
- This **convolutional time interleaver** ensures that a deep fade of 10 ms affects only a small portion of the audio frame, allowing error concealment to mask the loss.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission Frame Duration | $T_f = 96$ | ms |
| OFDM Symbol Duration (Mode I) | $T_s \approx 1.246$ | ms |
| Null Symbol Duration | $T_{null} \approx 1.297$ | ms |
| Number of OFDM Symbols per Frame | 76 (Mode I) | — |
| CIF Duration | $T_{CIF} = 24$ | ms |
| CIF Size | 55296 bits = 864 CU | — |
| Capacity Unit (CU) | 1 CU = 64 bits = 8 bytes | — |
| Bitrate per CU | $\text{BR} = 64/0.024 \approx 2.667$ | kbps |
| Sub-channel bitrate | $R_s = S \cdot 2.667$ | kbps |
| Time Interleaving Depth | 16 CIFs | — |
| Number of Carriers (Mode I) | 1536 | — |
| Carrier Spacing (Mode I) | 1 kHz | kHz |
| Total Bandwidth | ~1.536 MHz | MHz |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DAB data structure enables:
- **SFN (Single Frequency Network) operation** — Multiple transmitters on the same frequency due to OFDM's robustness to echoes.
- **Seamless mobility** — Time interleaving + TII + error correction.
- **Spectrum efficiency** — Multiple services share one frequency block.
- **Cross-receiver compatibility** — Standardized CIF format means any DAB receiver can decode any compliant broadcast worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bitrate of a Sub-channel in DAB

**Given:**
- 1 Capacity Unit (CU) = 64 bits
- CIF duration = 24 ms
- Sub-channel size = S CUs

**Find:** The audio bitrate carried by the sub-channel.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The number of useful bits delivered per CIF by the sub-channel is:

$$B = S \times 64 \text{ bits}$$

Since the CIF is repeated every 24 ms, the bitrate in kbps is:

$$R_s = \frac{S \times 64 \text{ bits}}{24 \text{ ms}}$$

Convert denominator to seconds:

$$R_s = \frac{S \times 64}{24 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ bits/sec}$$

Convert to kilobits per second:

$$R_s = \frac{S \times 64 \times 10^3}{24} \text{ kbps}$$

Simplify the constant:

$$\frac{64 \times 10^3}{24} = \frac{64}{0.024} = 2666.67$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_s \approx 2.667 \times S \text{ kbps}}$$

### Worked Example (KTU-style)
A DAB service uses 96 CUs. What is the sub-channel bitrate?

$$R_s = 2.667 \times 96 = 256 \text{ kbps}$$

This is typical for **DAB+ (HE-AAC v2)** with error correction overhead.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Payload of the MSC in One Frame

The MSC consists of 4 CIFs per 96 ms frame (since 96 / 24 = 4).

$$B_{MSC} = 4 \text{ CIFs} \times 864 \text{ CUs/CIF} \times 64 \text{ bits/CU}$$

$$B_{MSC} = 4 \times 864 \times 64 = 221184 \text{ bits per 96 ms}$$

Converting to throughput:

$$R_{MSC} = \frac{221184 \text{ bits}}{0.096 \text{ sec}} = 2.304 \text{ Mbps}$$

This is the **gross capacity** of the MSC, before convolutional coding and time interleaving overhead.

## 3.3 Derivation: Time Interleaver Effective Delay

DAB applies a **convolutional time interleaving** with constraint length $L = 16$. The effective delay introduced is:

$$D = (L-1) \times T_{CIF} = 15 \times 24 \text{ ms} = 360 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This delay is acceptable for audio broadcasting (one-way) but is the reason DAB is unsuitable for two-way voice communication. The 360 ms delay is the trade-off for mobile robustness.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protection Levels and Code Rates

| Protection Level | Puncturing Rate (R) | Useful Bitrate per CU | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1/4 | 0.667 kbps | Very robust (DAB, mobile) |
| 2 | 3/8 | 1.000 kbps | Robust |
| 3 | 1/2 | 1.333 kbps | Standard |
| 4 | 3/4 | 2.000 kbps | High bitrate, strong signal |
| 5 (EEP) | 4/7 | ~1.524 kbps | Equal Error Protection |

**Logic for protection choice:** The trade-off is between **robustness** (lower code rate = more redundancy) and **audio quality** (higher useful bitrate). A car receiver in hilly terrain uses Protection Level 1, while a stationary home receiver can use Level 3 or 4.

## 3.5 Analytical Reasoning — Why 864 CUs per CIF?

The CIF size of 864 CUs is not arbitrary:
- The OFDM symbol in Mode I has **1536 carriers** for 1 ms.
- Over 24 ms, the MSC data carriers contribute: approximately **2304 carriers/ms × 24 ms** (after subtracting FIC).
- The result is **55296 bits per CIF** = **864 CUs × 64 bits/CU**.

The CIF is also **common interleaved** — even if a particular sub-channel is meant for one service, its CUs are *interleaved* with others across the CIF. This is the **interleaving depth** of the physical layer that prevents long burst errors from corrupting a single sub-channel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DAB Transmission Frame — Top-Level Mermaid Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["NULL SYMBOL Tnull 1.297 ms - AGC - Frame Start - TII Detection"]
    nodeB["SYNC SYMBOL TSC - Frequency Pilots - Time Pilots - Frame Sync"]
    nodeC["OFDM SYMBOL 3 - FIC Block 1 - MSC CIF Start"]
    nodeD["OFDM SYMBOL 4 - FIC Block 2 - MSC CIF Body"]
    nodeE["OFDM SYMBOL N - FIC Block - MSC CIF Body"]
    nodeF["OFDM SYMBOL 76 - Final MSC CIF - Final FIB"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF

    nodeC -.-> noteM1["FIC carries FIG 0, FIG 1, FIG 2 - Service List - Sub-channel Map"]
    nodeD -.-> noteM2["MSC carries 4 CIFs in 96 ms frame - Audio Services - Data Services"]
    nodeE -.-> noteM3["Time Interleaving spans 16 CIFs for burst protection"]

    classDef sync fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fic fill:#b4d4ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef msc fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class nodeA,nodeB sync
    class nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF msc
```

## 4.3 OFDM Carrier Layout (Mode I) — Block Functional View


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exam:**
> When drawing the DAB frame, always start with **Null Symbol → Sync → OFDM symbols (FIC + MSC)**, and clearly mark the boundary between the **logical channels (FIC, MSC)** and the **physical structure (symbols, carriers)**. Examiners reward students who distinguish between "logical" and "physical" organization.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Capacity Unit (CU) and Common Interleaved Frame (CIF) in the DAB data structure.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Capacity Unit (CU)** is the smallest addressable data block in the DAB Main Service Channel, defined as:
$$1 \text{ CU} = 64 \text{ bits} = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

A **Common Interleaved Frame (CIF)** is the fundamental logical data unit of the MSC, comprising exactly:
$$1 \text{ CIF} = 864 \text{ CUs} = 55296 \text{ bits}$$

The CIF spans **24 ms** and is the basis upon which all sub-channels and services are organized within the DAB transmission frame.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between FIC and MSC in the DAB system.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FIC (Fast Information Channel) | MSC (Main Service Channel) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Control & service information | Actual audio/data payload |
| Data content | FIGs (MCI, SI, CA) | Audio + PAD + data services |
| Time interleaving | **No** time interleaving | **Yes** (16 CIFs deep) |
| Protection | High (more robust) | Configurable (Protection Level 1–5) |
| Used by receiver | First, to build service map | After FIC is decoded |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **DAB transmission frame structure**, clearly indicating the Null symbol, Synchronization symbol, FIC, and MSC. (7 marks)

**(b)** A DAB sub-channel is allocated **72 Capacity Units** with **Protection Level 3 (code rate 1/2)**. Calculate:
- (i) Useful audio bitrate
- (ii) Bitrate of the encoded stream
- (iii) Net throughput per 24 ms CIF for this sub-channel (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

The DAB transmission frame has a **fixed duration of 96 ms** and consists of the following:

1. **Null Symbol** (~1.297 ms) — Provides a quiet period for the receiver to:
   - Adjust AGC (Automatic Gain Control)
   - Detect frame start
   - Decode TII (Transmitter Identification Information)

2. **Synchronization Channel (SC)** — A single OFDM symbol that contains only:
   - Frequency pilots (for carrier synchronization)
   - Time pilots (for symbol timing)
   - The receiver locks onto this symbol

3. **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — Carried in the remaining OFDM symbols, but logically independent of MSC. Contains:
   - FIG 0 (Multiplex Configuration Info)
   - FIG 1 (Service Info)
   - FIG 2 (Conditional Access)
   - FIDC (announcements, EPG)

4. **Main Service Channel (MSC)** — Carried in the same OFDM symbols as FIC, but logically separated. Contains:
   - 4 Common Interleaved Frames (CIFs) per 96 ms frame
   - Sub-channels made up of consecutive CUs
   - Audio services + data services + PAD

**Block Diagram:**

```
+------+----------+--------+---------+---------+-----+
| NULL | SYNC SC  | OFDM 3 | OFDM 4  |  ...    |OFDM 76|
| Tnull| T_SC     | (FIC+  | (FIC+   |         |       |
|      |          |  MSC)  |  MSC)   |         |       |
+------+----------+--------+---------+---------+-----+
| 1.3ms|  ~1.2ms  |<--------- 93.5 ms (74 symbols) ----->|
+------+----------+--------------------------------------+
|        Total Frame Duration = 96 ms                   |
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- Sub-channel size, S = 72 CUs
- Protection Level 3 → Code rate, R = 1/2
- CIF duration = 24 ms
- 1 CU = 64 bits

**(i) Useful audio bitrate (after error correction overhead is removed):**

$$R_{useful} = S \times 2.667 \text{ kbps}$$

$$R_{useful} = 72 \times 2.667 = 192 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Bitrate of the encoded stream (raw bitrate before puncturing):**

The encoded stream before puncturing is twice the useful bitrate (since R = 1/2):

$$R_{encoded} = \frac{R_{useful}}{R} = \frac{192}{0.5} = 384 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Net throughput per 24 ms CIF for this sub-channel:**

Number of useful bits delivered per CIF:

$$B = 72 \text{ CUs} \times 64 \text{ bits/CU} = 4608 \text{ bits}$$

This confirms:

$$R_{useful} = \frac{4608 \text{ bits}}{0.024 \text{ sec}} = 192000 \text{ bps} = 192 \text{ kbps} \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]**

**(a)** Explain the **logical hierarchy of DAB data**: Ensemble → Service → Service Component → Sub-channel → CU → CIF. Use a diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of the DAB **time interleaving structure**, explain how DAB provides robust mobile reception. If a logical frame spans **16 CIFs**, calculate the effective time de-interleaving delay introduced. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Logical Hierarchy (from top to bottom):**

1. **Ensemble** — A complete DAB transmission on one frequency block. Can contain many services.
2. **Service** — A user-selectable program (e.g., a radio station).
3. **Service Component** — The actual audio (or data) inside a service. A service may have multiple components (audio + PAD).
4. **Sub-channel** — A logical data pipe that carries one service component. Sized in CUs.
5. **Capacity Unit (CU)** — The atomic data block, 8 bytes.
6. **CIF** — The fundamental interleaved frame, contains 864 CUs over 24 ms.

**Diagram:**

```
ENSEMBLE (1 freq block)
   │
   ├── SERVICE A (Radio Station)
   │     ├── Service Comp: Audio (192 kbps, 72 CU)
   │     └── Service Comp: PAD  (8 kbps, 3 CU)
   │
   ├── SERVICE B (Data EPG)
   │     └── Service Comp: Data  (32 kbps, 12 CU)
   │
   └── ...
         ↓
   All components multiplexed into CIF (864 CU, 24 ms)
         ↓
   Transmitted across OFDM carriers in MSC
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Time Interleaving for Mobile Reception:**

DAB's time interleaver spreads the bits of one logical audio frame across **16 successive CIFs** using a convolutional interleaver. This means a single burst error of up to **384 ms** (16 × 24 ms) will only affect 1/16 of the audio frame.

- The receiver applies the inverse (de-interleaving) to reconstruct the original frame.
- Errors are spread out in time, allowing the **Reed-Solomon decoder** + **concealment** to mask the errors.
- This is the **fundamental reason DAB works in cars, tunnels, and hilly terrain** where fast fading occurs.

**Effective Time De-interleaving Delay:**

The constraint length of the convolutional interleaver is L = 16.

$$D = (L - 1) \times T_{CIF}$$

$$D = 15 \times 24 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{D = 360 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing physical and logical channels** — The OFDM symbols *physically* contain both FIC and MSC together, but they are *logically* distinct. Always clarify this in the answer.
> 2. **Wrong CU size** — 1 CU = 64 bits = 8 bytes. Many students write 32 or 128 bits. Memorize this!
> 3. **Forgetting time interleaving is in the MSC only** — The FIC is *not* time-interleaved because it must be decoded quickly for control.
> 4. **Writing "DAB frame = 24 ms"** — The CIF is 24 ms; the **full transmission frame is 96 ms**.
> 5. **Missing the TII role** — TII is critical for SFN (Single Frequency Network) operation. Examiners often ask "How does DAB identify the transmitter?" Answer: **TII pattern rotation in successive OFDM symbols.**
> 6. **Confusing protection level with code rate** — Protection Level 1 has the *lowest* useful bitrate but the *highest* robustness. State the relationship clearly: **lower code rate = more redundancy = more robust = lower useful bitrate.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DAB Data Structure**
>
> ✅ **Transmission Frame = 96 ms** (fixed for all DAB modes)
>
> ✅ **Components of the Frame:**
>   - Null Symbol (AGC, frame start, TII)
>   - Synchronization Channel (pilots only)
>   - FIC (control, not interleaved)
>   - MSC (payload, time-interleaved)
>
> ✅ **CIF = 864 CU = 55296 bits = 24 ms** — the MSC's fundamental unit
>
> ✅ **1 CU = 8 bytes = 64 bits** — smallest addressable block
>
> ✅ **Bitrate formula:** $R = 2.667 \times S$ kbps, where S = sub-channel size in CUs
>
> ✅ **Logical hierarchy:** Ensemble → Service → Service Component → Sub-channel → CU → CIF
>
> ✅ **FIC contains FIGs:** FIG 0 (MCI), FIG 1 (SI), FIG 2 (CA)
>
> ✅ **Time interleaving depth = 16 CIFs** → delay = **360 ms**
>
> ✅ **OFDM (Mode I) = 1536 carriers, 1 kHz spacing, ~1.536 MHz bandwidth**
>
> ✅ **TII** identifies the transmitter for SFN and seamless handover
>
> ✅ **Protection Levels 1–4** (UEP) and **Level 5** (EEP) trade robustness vs. bitrate
>
> ✅ **DAB+** uses HE-AAC v2 instead of MPEG-1 Layer II for better efficiency
>
> ✅ **4 CIFs per 96 ms frame** → gross MSC capacity ≈ **2.304 Mbps**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAB Single Frequency Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DAB Single Frequency Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DAB Single Frequency Network (SFN)** is a network configuration in Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB / DAB+) where a large geographical service area is covered by **multiple synchronized transmitters radiating the same DAB ensemble (frequency block) on the identical carrier frequency, at the same time, with bit-identical content.** All transmitters in the SFN must broadcast the **same OFDM signal synchronized to a common reference (typically GPS-derived 1 pps and 10 MHz clock)** so that the receiver perceives the combined emissions as a single, coherently reinforced wavefield.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Key Definition Snapshot**
> An SFN exploits the **COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** modulation of DAB together with a **Guard Interval (GI)** deliberately inserted between useful OFDM symbols. The guard interval allows the receiver to absorb echoes (multipath, including *artificial* echoes created by the neighbouring transmitters) **without inter-symbol interference (ISI)**, provided the differential path delay from any transmitter falls inside the guard interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **DAB Standard Backbone**
> DAB is defined by **EN 300 401 (Eureka-147 / DAB family)**. The relevant SFN timing and synchronisation clauses are given in **ETSI EN 300 401, ETSI TS 102 563 (DAB+) and ETSI TS 103 461 (audio)**, while the SFN synchronisation mechanism itself is standardised in **ETSI TS 101 191 (DAB SFN synchronisation - the "TII" and "MCI" streams)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, dark highway. A **single streetlamp** only illuminates one short stretch. You could put up **many streetlamps**, but if you simply turned them on *at different times and on different switches*, the result would be confusing glare. Now imagine that **all the streetlamps are choreographed** to switch on and off in perfect unison, projecting the **same light pattern**: a passenger standing anywhere along the road sees **one continuous, uniform light field**, indistinguishable from a single, very long lamp.

- The **streetlamps** = the **SFN transmitters**.
- The **choreographed switching** = the **synchronised OFDM frame timing** (locked to GPS).
- The **continuous light field** = the **uniform field-strength coverage** of the SFN.
- The **"blending zone" where two lamps overlap** = the area where signals from two transmitters add, and where the **guard interval** must absorb the differential delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – Stadium Cheer**
> In a stadium, if a *synchronized* audience claps together, the sound is louder and feels like a single source — this is analogous to SFN **constructive addition**. If the same audience claps with even a small delay, the result is muddled echo — this is what happens when transmitters are **outside the guard interval window** and ISI occurs.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **system constants** govern every DAB SFN design and must be memorised:

| Parameter | Value (Mode I, VHF Band III) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Useful symbol duration $T_u$ | **1.000** | ms |
| Number of OFDM carriers | **1536** | carriers |
| Carrier spacing $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ | **1.000** | kHz |
| Total occupied bandwidth | **1.536** | MHz |
| Guard interval options ($\Delta / T_u$) | **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** | dimensionless |
| Maximum $T_g$ (Δ = 1/4) | **246** | μs |
| Speed of light $c$ | **3 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Synchronisation reference | **GPS / GLONASS 1 pps + 10 MHz** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award or deduct marks for using the **Mode I** parameters by default. Always state the *Mode* you are analysing (I, II, III or IV) before plugging numbers into any equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why SFN Is Possible Only in DAB (Not in FM/AM)

In **analog** broadcasting (FM, AM, DVB-T legacy), echoes from distant co-channel transmitters cause **visible/audible distortion** (ghosting, beat-whistles, pilot-tone phase jitter) because the analog detector treats every echo as a real, undesirable copy of the signal. In DAB:

- The **COFDM receiver** uses a **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** over a window of length $T_u$.
- It ignores signal energy arriving **outside the FFT window** provided the delay lies in the **guard interval** $T_g$.
- Therefore, the receiver treats **all useful symbols** the same way, and **multiple SFN transmitters become indistinguishable from a strong multipath echo**, which is naturally tolerated.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important conceptual leap** in DAB SFN. Without the guard interval, the SFN would not function. Therefore, **guard interval = SFN enabler**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic – Step by Step

1. **Reference clock distribution** – Every SFN transmitter locks its **10 MHz reference** and **1 pulse-per-second (1 pps)** to a **GPS/GNSS disciplined oscillator**, ensuring **bit-accurate** frame timing.
2. **Identical ensemble content** – All transmitters receive the **same ETI (Ensemble Transport Interface) stream** (or NI/G.703 equivalent) from a central multiplexer. No transmitter is allowed to insert local programme substitution at the modulator.
3. **Co-located COFDM modulation** – Each transmitter generates the **same COFDM frame** (same Phase Reference Symbol, same time-interleaving, same frequency-interleaving).
4. **Co-channel radiation** – All transmitters emit the ensemble on the **same centre frequency** (a single DAB block, e.g. Block 10B = 211.648 MHz in Band III).
5. **Echo absorption by guard interval** – At the receiver, signals arriving from each transmitter are *echoes* with delays $d_i / c$. If $\max(d_i) - \min(d_i) \le c \cdot T_g$, the FFT discards the inter-symbol transitions and decodes the useful part of the symbol.
6. **Constructive power addition** – Because OFDM carriers from all transmitters are *coherent*, they add in power, giving an **SFN network gain** of up to $10 \log_{10}(M)$ + bonus, where $M$ is the number of effective transmitters in the coverage overlap.

### 2.3 Why Engineers Choose SFN (Real-World Engineering Utility)

- **Spectrum saving** – Only **one frequency block** is needed for a national service, instead of one block per MFN transmitter. This is the single biggest reason regulators (OFCOM, BNetzA, TRAI, etc.) mandate SFN planning.
- **Receiver simplicity** – The listener does **not retune** while driving across the country. A single DAB channel (e.g. *BBC National DAB*) covers the entire UK.
- **Coverage of difficult terrain** – Hills, valleys, and tunnels can be filled with *gap-fillers* that operate on the same frequency.
- **Power efficiency** – Instead of one 10 kW transmitter, the same coverage is achieved by **many low-power transmitters** (~ 1 kW each), saving on electricity, cooling, mast loading and capital cost.
- **Soft-failure** – If one transmitter fails, the rest of the SFN still provides coverage; an MFN fails completely when its single transmitter drops.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are in the **DAB Mode I, VHF Band III** context unless otherwise stated. Use $\vert$ for absolute value (the table cell pipe is the markdown separator, so inside math we use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carrier spacing | $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ | Hz | $T_u$ is useful OFDM symbol duration |
| 2 | Guard interval duration | $T_g = \Delta \cdot T_u$ | s | $\Delta \in \{1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32\}$ |
| 3 | Total symbol duration | $T_s = T_u + T_g = T_u(1 + \Delta)$ | s | — |
| 4 | **Maximum SFN transmitter separation** | $d_{max} = c \cdot T_g$ | m | Capped by guard interval |
| 5 | Bandwidth per DAB block | $B = N \cdot \Delta f$ | Hz | $N = 1536$ for Mode I |
| 6 | Bit rate (Mode I, ¼ GI, EEP-3) | $R_b \approx 1.152$ | Mbit/s | Reference value, ±PL padding |
| 7 | SFN network gain (approx.) | $G_{net} \approx 10\log_{10}(M) + G_{diversity}$ | dB | $G_{diversity}\le 3$ dB |
| 8 | Diversity gain of $M$ transmitters | $G_{div} = 10\log_{10}(M)$ | dB | Power-addition ideal case |
| 9 | Field strength additivity | $E_{SFN} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{M} E_i^2}$ | V/m | Coherent in OFDM symbol interior |
| 10 | Receiver min. usable level (Mode I) | $\approx 35$ | dBμV/m | Indoor, robust protection ratio |
| 11 | Location probability factor | $F_L = 10\log_{10}(P_{cov,\%})$ variant | dB | From ITU-R P.1546 |
| 12 | DAB block centre (Band III) | $f_c = 174.928 + 0.1712 \cdot N_{block}$ | MHz | $N_{block}=5..13$ |

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Equation

- **Eq. 2 (Guard Interval):** Direct ratio of the protection window to the useful symbol. Larger $\Delta$ → larger $T_g$ → bigger transmitter spacing, **but** higher overhead $\rightarrow$ lower useful bitrate.
- **Eq. 4 (Transmitter separation):** The guard interval is a *time budget* during which an echo is harmless. Multiplying by $c$ converts that time to a *distance budget* — the maximum extra path length the echo can travel before it turns into ISI.
- **Eq. 7 (Network Gain):** In the *useful* part of the symbol, all transmitters contribute coherently. The ideal summed field strength is the **vector sum** of the individual $E$-vectors, hence the power-additive $10\log_{10}(M)$ plus a small diversity bonus.

### 2.6 The Critical Trade-Off Table

| $\Delta$ | $T_g$ (Mode I) | $d_{max}$ (km) | Overhead loss (dB) | Useful bitrate (Mode I) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1/4 | 246 μs | **73.8** | -1.25 | lowest |
| 1/8 | 123 μs | 36.9 | -0.67 | medium |
| 1/16 | 61.5 μs | 18.5 | -0.34 | high |
| 1/32 | 30.75 μs | 9.2 | -0.17 | highest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** When asked *"design a national DAB SFN for India/India-like geography"*, the correct answer almost always cites the **1/4 guard interval** with **$d_{max} \approx 74$ km** because the typical inter-city transmitter distance of Indian public broadcasters (AIR) lies in that range.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Maximum Permissible Transmitter Separation in an SFN

We are given a DAB Mode I signal with useful symbol duration $T_u = 1$ ms and chosen guard interval fraction $\Delta$. The receiver uses an FFT window of length $T_u$ that must be filled with **only one symbol's worth of energy**, otherwise ISI appears. An echo from the most distant SFN transmitter must therefore *fully* fit inside the guard interval $T_g$.

**Step 1 – Express the guard interval in seconds**

The guard interval duration is a fixed fraction of the useful symbol:

$$
T_g \;=\; \Delta \cdot T_u
$$

**Step 2 – Convert time budget to distance budget**

The echo travels an extra path length $\Delta d$ relative to the closest transmitter. The delay is $\tau = \Delta d / c$. For the echo to remain inside the guard interval we require:

$$
\frac{\Delta d}{c} \;\le\; T_g
$$

**Step 3 – Solve for the maximum extra path length**

$$
\Delta d_{max} \;=\; c \cdot T_g \;=\; c \cdot \Delta \cdot T_u
$$

**Step 4 – For the worst case (receiver at the edge between two transmitters)**

If the receiver lies exactly on the line joining two transmitters separated by $D$, the difference in path length to each transmitter is $D$ when the receiver is at one transmitter's foot. Hence the inter-transmitter distance must satisfy:

$$
D_{max} \;=\; \Delta d_{max} \;=\; c \cdot \Delta \cdot T_u
$$

**Step 5 – Plug in numerical values for Mode I with $\Delta = 1/4$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{max} &= (3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}) \times \left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right) \times (1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}) \\
&= 3 \times 10^{8} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{m} \\
&= 75\;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

The figure of **74 km** quoted in international literature accounts for the 4 km/symbol-guard alignment tolerance (i.e. the FFT window is centred slightly inside the symbol to maximise the *useful* echo window — see Eq. 6 below).

> [!NOTE]
> Some texts use the **effective** guard interval
> $T_{g,eff} = T_g - T_{margin}$ where $T_{margin} \approx 50$ μs absorbs the receiver's tolerance and SFN jitter. The KTU-acceptable value is **$T_g = 246$ μs**, **$D_{max} \approx 74$ km**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Effective Guard Interval and the Practical $D_{max}$

**Step 1 – Account for the receiver's sampling jitter**

The receiver's sampling clock has a residual uncertainty $\sigma_t$. To keep the echo inside the *useful* part of the FFT window, we reduce the nominal guard interval by a margin $T_{margin}$:

$$
T_{g,eff} \;=\; T_g - T_{margin}
$$

**Step 2 – Choose a margin**

ETSI TS 101 191 implies a margin in the order of **1–2 sample periods at the chip rate**; for Mode I a conservative value is $T_{margin} = 1\,\mu s$ to $5\,\mu s$.

**Step 3 – Compute the corresponding $D_{max}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{g,eff} &= 246\,\mu s - 6\,\mu s = 240\,\mu s \\
D_{max,\,eff} &= c \cdot T_{g,eff} = 3\times 10^{8} \cdot 240 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 72\;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **14-mark KTU question** that asks "design a DAB SFN", quote both the **theoretical 75 km** and the **practical 72–74 km** to demonstrate examiner-friendly depth.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – SFN Network Gain for $M$ Equal-Power Transmitters

We consider $M$ SFN transmitters each of EIRP $P_{tx}$. In the *interior* of the SFN coverage area a receiver sees $M$ useful signals (one from each transmitter) added coherently within the FFT window.

**Step 1 – Sum the received powers**

The received power from the $i$-th transmitter is $P_{r,i} = P_{tx} \cdot G_{tx} G_{rx} \lambda^2 / ((4\pi)^2 d_i^2 L_i)$. In the *equal-power, equal-distance* case all $P_{r,i} = P_0$:

$$
P_{r,total} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{M} P_{r,i} \;=\; M \cdot P_0
$$

**Step 2 – Convert to decibels**

$$
G_{net,\,dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{M \cdot P_0}{P_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(M)
$$

**Step 3 – Add the diversity bonus**

In practice the transmitters are not at exactly equal distance. The resulting statistical distribution yields an additional diversity gain $G_{div} \le 3$ dB. Hence:

$$
G_{net,\,dB}^{total} \;\approx\; 10 \log_{10}(M) + 3\;\text{dB (diversity bonus)}
$$

**Step 4 – Numerical example, $M = 4$**

$$
G_{net} = 10 \log_{10}(4) + 3 = 6.02 + 3 = 9.02\;\text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 3 dB diversity bonus represents the *statistical* improvement of having $M$ uncorrelated fading channels instead of one. It is *not* a hard physical constant but a planning figure of merit (ITU-R BS.1660, EBU R3-7).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 – Spectral Efficiency of SFN vs MFN

**Step 1 – Define the spectral efficiency of a single transmitter**

A DAB block in Mode I occupies $B = 1.536$ MHz and carries $R_b \approx 1.5$ Mbit/s of useful audio capacity.

$$
\eta_{single} = \frac{R_b}{B} \;[\text{bit/s/Hz}]
$$

**Step 2 – Count frequencies used in an MFN covering $M$ cells**

In an **MFN** every cell needs its own DAB block, hence the total spectrum used is $M \cdot B$. The *per-cell* spectral efficiency is unchanged, but the **national** spectrum cost is $M$ times higher.

**Step 3 – Count frequencies used in an SFN covering $M$ cells**

In an **SFN** all cells share **one** block, so the total spectrum is $B$.

**Step 4 – Compute the ratio**

$$
\frac{\text{Spectrum}_{MFN}}{\text{Spectrum}_{SFN}} = M
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For $M=7$ transmitters, the SFN is 7× more spectrally efficient** than an equivalent MFN. This single numerical fact is the most-cited "exam-favourite" reason for SFN adoption in KTU and other university question banks.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | DAB Mode I (¼ GI) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Useful symbol | $T_u$ | 1.000 ms | Sets FFT window |
| Guard interval | $T_g$ | 246 μs | Echo absorption |
| Total symbol | $T_s$ | 1.246 ms | Frame timing |
| Max Tx distance | $D_{max}$ | 74 km | Network cell size |
| Spectral saving vs MFN | $\eta$ | × M | Regulator benefit |
| Network gain (M = 4) | $G_{net}$ | 9 dB | Lower per-Tx EIRP |
| Sync tolerance | $T_{margin}$ | ≈ 1–5 μs | GPS-locked reference |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – National DAB SFN Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The diagram below models the **functional architecture** of a three-transmitter DAB SFN. Each block represents a *function* (not a physical wire); arrows represent **information flow** (ETI, GPS, RF).

```mermaid
graph TD
    studioA[Studio Content Source] --> muxA[Central Ensemble Multiplexer]
    etisrc[ETI Stream NI G 703] --> muxA
    muxA --> distribA[Contribution Network Fiber or Satellite]
    distribA --> tx1[Transmitter Site 1 with GPS Disciplined Oscillator]
    distribA --> tx2[Transmitter Site 2 with GPS Disciplined Oscillator]
    distribA --> tx3[Transmitter Site 3 with GPS Disciplined Oscillator]
    gpsref[GPS Constellation 1 pps and 10 MHz] --> tx1
    gpsref --> tx2
    gpsref --> tx3
    tx1 --> cov1[Coverage Footprint 1 radius approx 50 km]
    tx2 --> cov2[Coverage Footprint 2 radius approx 50 km]
    tx3 --> cov3[Coverage Footprint 3 radius approx 50 km]
    cov1 --> overlapA[Overlap Zone Guard Interval Absorption]
    cov2 --> overlapA
    cov3 --> overlapA
    overlapA --> rxA[Mobile DAB Receiver inside vehicle]
    overlapA --> rxB[Fixed DAB Receiver home rooftop antenna]
    rxA --> audioA[Decoded Audio Output]
    rxB --> audioA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**
> - All three *Transmitter Site* blocks share two common references: the **ETI stream** (carrying the same DAB ensemble bits) and the **GPS 1 pps / 10 MHz** (carrying the same frame timing).
> - The *Overlap Zone* is the **only place** where the SFN behaviour is non-trivial — it is where the guard interval does its job.
> - The mobile receiver block (vehicle) emphasises the **drive-test scenario** that KTU questions frequently describe.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – COFDM Symbol Structure with Guard Interval

Because a time-domain waveform cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the diagram below maps the **functional timeline** of one DAB Mode I OFDM symbol, showing where the **guard interval** protects the receiver from intersymbol interference.

```mermaid
graph LR
    t0[Start of Symbol Index k] --> guard[Guard Interval Length Tg 246 us copy of tail]
    guard --> useful[Useful OFDM Symbol Length Tu 1 ms carries 1534 data plus 1 TII reference carriers]
    useful --> guardnext[Guard Interval of Symbol k plus 1 copy of head of next symbol]
    guardnext --> utilnext[Useful OFDM Symbol k plus 1]
    utilnext --> fftwin[FFT Window of Receiver length Tu 1 ms positioned to avoid ISI]
    fftwin --> decode[FFT Output 1536 subcarrier complex values QPSK to 64 QAM]
    decode --> fec[Channel Deinterleaving and Viterbi Decoding]
    fec --> audio[Source Decoding MPEG 1 Layer II or HE AAC v2]
    audio --> spk[Loudspeaker Output]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The block **Guard Interval** is implemented by copying the **tail** of the useful symbol and prepending it to the front — this is the famous *cyclic prefix*.
> - The block **FFT Window** shows that the receiver deliberately *excludes* the cyclic prefix from the FFT to avoid ISI. The receiver's FFT must therefore be **time-aligned** to within the residual jitter margin.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – SFN vs MFN Spectrum Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph mfnA[MFN Plan 3 Cells use 3 Blocks]
        mfn1[Block 5A 174.928 MHz to Cell 1]
        mfn2[Block 5B 176.640 MHz to Cell 2]
        mfn3[Block 5C 178.352 MHz to Cell 3]
    end
    subgraph sfnA[SFN Plan 3 Cells use 1 Block]
        sfn1[Block 5A 174.928 MHz to Cell 1]
        sfn2[Block 5A 174.928 MHz to Cell 2]
        sfn3[Block 5A 174.928 MHz to Cell 3]
    end
    mfnA -. saves 2 blocks .-> sfnA
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **single most powerful visualisation** for answering the KTU descriptive question *"Explain the spectrum-saving advantage of an SFN over an MFN."*
