## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Two short-answer questions as per KTU ESE pattern. Each carries 3 marks. Answers must be crisp, **definition-first**, and within the prescribed word limit (~80–100 words).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a Single Frequency Network (SFN) in the context of DAB. State two conditions that MUST be satisfied for an SFN to operate correctly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **SFN** in DAB is a network of multiple transmitters that radiate **the same DAB ensemble on the same carrier frequency, at the same instant, with bit-identical content**, so as to extend coverage over a wide area using a single frequency block.
>
> **Mandatory conditions (1.5 marks each):**
> 1. **Time synchronisation** – All transmitters must lock their 1 pps and 10 MHz references to a common source, typically **GPS**, so that OFDM frame edges coincide within the guard-interval tolerance.
> 2. **Content synchronisation** – All transmitters must receive the **same ETI (Ensemble Transport Interface) stream** from a common multiplexer; no local audio substitution is allowed at the modulator input.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the role of the *guard interval* in enabling a DAB SFN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **guard interval** is a copy of the tail of each useful OFDM symbol, prepended to the symbol so that the total symbol length is $T_s = T_u + T_g$.
>
> **Role in an SFN (3 marks):**
> - It provides a **time window** (of duration $T_g$) during which **echoes from neighbouring SFN transmitters** are absorbed as harmless *intra-symbol* energy.
> - The receiver's **FFT window is positioned inside the useful part** of the symbol; energy arriving within the cyclic prefix does not enter the FFT, hence does not create ISI.
> - Hence, the **guard interval is the enabler of SFN**: without it, multiple co-channel transmitters would mutually interfere. The maximum transmitter separation is $d_{max} = c \cdot T_g$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 pattern, Part B is answered with a single 14-mark sub-question pair (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Below are **two alternative questions**; the student answers only one.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a three-transmitter DAB Single Frequency Network** covering a national area. Clearly indicate the role of GPS, the central multiplexer, and the overlap zone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For DAB Mode I operating with a **guard interval fraction of 1/4**:
- (i) Compute the **maximum permitted separation** between two SFN transmitters.
- (ii) If the network has **four identical transmitters** of EIRP 1 kW each, compute the **approximate SFN network gain** in dB in the central overlap region.
- (iii) Comment on the **trade-off** between choosing $\Delta = 1/4$ and $\Delta = 1/32$ for a national SFN design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**(a) Functional architecture of a 3-transmitter DAB SFN (7 marks)**

- **1 mark** – Studio content and central multiplexer: the studio provides audio/data services; the **Ensemble Multiplexer** packs them into a single ETI stream per DAB block.
- **1 mark** – Distribution network: the ETI is carried over **fibre or satellite** to all transmitter sites (contribution network), guaranteeing **bit-identical** content.
- **1 mark** – GPS reference: each site has a **GPS-disciplined oscillator** providing 1 pps and 10 MHz; this ensures all COFDM frames are aligned to **within the guard-interval tolerance** (typically ≤ 1 μs).
- **1 mark** – Three transmitters on the **same frequency block** (e.g. Block 10B = 211.648 MHz in Band III).
- **1 mark** – Each transmitter's coverage footprint is roughly **50–70 km**; the **overlap zone** is where the SFN behaviour becomes critical.
- **1 mark** – In the overlap zone, the **guard interval absorbs the differential delay** between transmitters; inside this zone the receiver decodes a single, summed signal.
- **1 mark** – Mobile and fixed receivers (in vehicles or on rooftops) produce **identical audio** anywhere in the network — no retune required.

(Reference diagram: see **Section 4.1** above; reproducing it scores an additional aesthetic mark.)

---

**(b) Numerical and analytical sub-parts (7 marks)**

**(i) Maximum transmitter separation (2 marks)**

Given: $T_u = 1$ ms, $\Delta = 1/4$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_g &= \Delta \cdot T_u = \dfrac{1}{4} \times 1\;\text{ms} = 250\;\mu s \\
d_{max} &= c \cdot T_g = 3 \times 10^{8} \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{4}\;\text{m} = 75\;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> After applying a 1–5 μs practical margin (e.g. 4 μs): $d_{max,practical} \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \times 246 \times 10^{-6} = 73.8$ km. **Quote 74 km (practical) or 75 km (theoretical).** *(½ mark for stating the assumption, 1½ marks for the calculation.)*

**(ii) SFN network gain (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{net} &= 10\log_{10}(M) + G_{div} \\
&= 10\log_{10}(4) + 3 \\
&= 6.02 + 3 \\
&= 9.02\;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Trade-off (3 marks)**

| Aspect | $\Delta = 1/4$ | $\Delta = 1/32$ |
|---|---|---|
| $T_g$ | 246 μs | 30.75 μs |
| $d_{max}$ | 74 km | 9.2 km |
| Spectral efficiency | Lower (1/4 of symbol is overhead) | Higher (1/32 overhead) |
| Network topology | Wide-area, fewer transmitters | Dense, many gap-fillers |
| Suitable for | National/large-cell SFN | Local/DAB+ simulcast in dense urban |

**Conclusion:** For a **national** SFN (e.g. India, Europe) choose $\Delta = 1/4$. For **dense urban** local coverage choose $\Delta = 1/32$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Supplementary)]*

**(a)** Explain the **COFDM technique** used in DAB. Show how a DAB Mode I symbol is constructed and identify the **function of the guard interval** in the time-domain structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DAB SFN is to be designed for a coastal stretch 220 km long. Each transmitter has a useful symbol duration of 1 ms and a guard interval fraction of 1/4. Determine:
- (i) The **number of transmitters** required (assuming linear placement, each covering up to $d_{max}$).
- (ii) The **total spectrum used** in MHz, comparing it with an equivalent **MFN** that uses the same per-cell coverage but a different frequency block per cell.
- (iii) The **approximate network gain** in the centre of the SFN. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**(a) COFDM in DAB (7 marks)**

- **1 mark** – **COFDM = Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing.** The DAB stream is split into 1536 (Mode I) parallel low-bit-rate sub-streams, each modulating its own sub-carrier.
- **1 mark** – The **sub-carriers are orthogonal** because they are spaced at $\Delta f = 1/T_u = 1$ kHz, exactly the inverse of the useful symbol duration.
- **1 mark** – The composite time-domain signal is built as an **IFFT** of the frequency-domain subcarrier constellation (QPSK to 64-QAM depending on protection profile).
- **1 mark** – **Channel coding:** convolutional coding (mother rate 1/4) with puncturing gives EEP/EEP-2/EEP-3 protection profiles; the Viterbi decoder corrects errors.
- **1 mark** – **Time interleaving** (depth up to 384 ms) and **frequency interleaving** (over all 1536 carriers) are applied to disperse burst errors caused by frequency-selective fading.
- **1 mark** – **Guard interval:** a copy of the tail of each useful symbol of length $T_g = \Delta T_u$ is prepended to make the total symbol $T_s = T_u + T_g$. This is the **cyclic prefix**.
- **1 mark** – **Function of the GI:** it absorbs echoes (multipath *and* SFN co-channel echoes) up to a delay of $T_g$ without causing ISI, because the receiver's FFT window sits entirely within the useful part of the symbol. The cyclic prefix also **converts linear convolution** with the channel into **circular convolution**, allowing single-tap equalisation in the frequency domain.

---

**(b) Coastal SFN design (7 marks)**

**(i) Number of transmitters (2 marks)**

The maximum spacing is $d_{max} = c \cdot T_g = 75$ km (theoretical) or ≈ 74 km (practical). The total length is 220 km. Using $d_{max} = 74$ km:

$$
N_{tx} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{d_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{220}{74} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.97 \right\rceil = 3
$$

**Three transmitters** are required, with the middle one positioned to cover the gap.

> If the examiner uses 75 km, $N = \lceil 220/75 \rceil = 3$ — same answer. *(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the integer.)*

**(ii) Spectrum used (3 marks)**

Each DAB Mode I block occupies $B = 1.536$ MHz.

- **SFN spectrum:** $B_{SFN} = 1 \times 1.536 = 1.536$ MHz.
- **MFN spectrum:** $B_{MFN} = 3 \times 1.536 = 4.608$ MHz.
- **Saving:** $B_{MFN} - B_{SFN} = 3.072$ MHz (a 3× reduction).

**(iii) Network gain (2 marks)**

$$
G_{net} = 10\log_{10}(3) + 3 = 4.77 + 3 = 7.77 \;\text{dB} \approx 7.8\;\text{dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> 1. **Don't confuse Mode I and Mode II parameters.** Mode II has $T_u = 250$ μs, which gives $d_{max} = 18.5$ km — *not* 74 km. The default is **Mode I** unless stated.
> 2. **Don't forget the practical margin** when quoting $d_{max}$. A clean 75 km theoretical answer is acceptable, but quoting 72–74 km shows examiner-friendly engineering maturity.
> 3. **Don't mix up SFN and MFN** in the spectrum question. A common error is multiplying the SFN answer by 3 "to be safe" — that gives the **MFN** answer, not the SFN.
> 4. **Network gain is in dB**; stating "9 dB for 4 transmitters" without the formula loses 1 mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DAB SFN (Module 2)**

- **Definition** – Multiple transmitters, same frequency, same time, same content, GPS-synchronised.
- **Enabler** – COFDM with a **guard interval** (cyclic prefix).
- **DAB Mode I default parameters** – $T_u = 1$ ms, $N = 1536$, $\Delta f = 1$ kHz, $B = 1.536$ MHz.
- **Guard interval options** – 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 → $T_g$ = 246, 123, 61.5, 30.75 μs.
- **Maximum SFN transmitter separation** – $d_{max} = c \cdot T_g$ → 75 / 37 / 18.5 / 9.2 km for the four options.
- **Network gain for $M$ equal-power transmitters** – $G_{net} \approx 10\log_{10}(M) + 3$ dB.
- **Spectrum saving vs MFN** – Factor of $M$ (i.e. 3× for 3 cells, 7× for 7 cells).
- **Synchronisation** – GPS 1 pps + 10 MHz; practical jitter margin ≈ 1–5 μs.
- **Standards to remember** – ETSI EN 300 401 (DAB), ETSI TS 101 191 (SFN synchronisation), ETSI TS 102 563 (DAB+).
- **Eureka-147 family** – DAB (MP2 audio), DAB+ (HE-AAC v2 audio), DMB (video).
- **Three operational "must-haves"** – (i) same ETI stream, (ii) same frequency, (iii) same time.
- **Two biggest advantages** – spectrum efficiency + soft failure / graceful degradation.
- **Two biggest disadvantages** – precise GPS lock required + extra contribution network (fibre/satellite).
- **Receiver behaviour in SFN** – treats all transmitters as one, FFT is single-tap equalised, no retune.
- **KTU 2024 - frequently tested formula** – $d_{max} = c \cdot \Delta \cdot T_u$ (memorise this verbatim).
- **KTU 2024 - frequently asked 7-marker** – "Compare SFN and MFN with a diagram" (use the spectrum block diagram from §4.3).
- **KTU 2024 - frequently asked 14-marker** – "Design a DAB SFN for a given length and compute gain/spectrum" (use the derivation pattern from §3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Broadcasting Using DAB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERPRISE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Broadcasting Using DAB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB)** is a digital radio standard (Eureka 147 / ETSI EN 300 401) designed for terrestrial, satellite, hybrid, and cable delivery of high-quality audio and **multimedia data services** to fixed, portable, and mobile receivers. Beyond conventional audio programming, DAB provides a robust **data broadcasting framework** that carries ancillary text, images, traffic information, electronic program guides (EPG), and IP-based packet data alongside the audio stream.

**Data Broadcasting** in the DAB context refers to the **transmission of non-audio digital information** (text, pictures, multimedia, IP packets, traffic updates) within the DAB multiplex, using defined logical channels that coexist with the audio services in the same RF ensemble.

| DAB Family Member | Audio Codec | Launch Era | Key Capability |
|---|---|---|---|
| DAB (Classic) | MPEG-1 Audio Layer II | 1995 | Stereo @ 192–256 kbps |
| DAB+ | HE-AAC v2 (AAC+ SBR + PS) | 2007 | Stereo @ 48–128 kbps, error correction via Reed-Solomon |
| DAB-IP (T-DMB) | BSAC / HE-AAC | 2005 | Mobile TV / multimedia wrapping |
| HD-Radio (IBOC) | HD Codec | 2002 (US) | Hybrid FM/AM digital extension |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multiplexed Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a multi-lane expressway (the DAB ensemble) where each lane carries a different type of cargo: one lane carries music CD-quality audio (audio sub-channels), another lane carries text captions and song titles (PAD — Programme Associated Data), a third lane carries weather/traffic alerts (TPEG / TMC), and a fourth lane carries internet-style packets (IP packets via MOT/DL). A traffic controller (the **Main Service Multiplexer**) packs all lanes onto the highway, and at the destination, a sorting yard (the **DAB receiver**) splits them again for the listener. The highway is wide because it uses **COFDM** (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing), which makes it immune to multipath echoes from buildings and hills.

### 1.3 Key Performance & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Bands:** VHF Band III (174–240 MHz), L-Band (1452–1492 MHz). L-band largely phased out for broadcasting in favour of Band III.
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **1.536 MHz** per DAB block.
- **FFT Size:** Modes 1, 2, 3, 4 use **2048, 512, 256, 1024** sub-carriers respectively.
- **Bit-rate Capacity of an Ensemble:** ≈ **0.6 – 1.7 Mbit/s** (Mode I, 1.5 MHz channel, depending on protection level and code rate).
- **Audio Codec Latency Target:** DAB+ end-to-end ≈ **2–4 seconds** for seamless hand-over.
- **Single Frequency Network (SFN):** All transmitters in a network transmit on the **same frequency** and **same time-slot**, dramatically improving spectrum efficiency.

### 1.4 Why DAB Data Broadcasting Matters in Entertainment Electronics

- Enables **hybrid radio** (audio + synchronized slide-shows on phone screens).
- Powers **slideshow (SLS)** — album art and presenter images synchronized to the broadcast.
- Provides **Journaline** — a hierarchical, on-demand text service for news/sports scores.
- Supports **TPEG** (Transport Protocol Experts Group) for real-time traffic/weather/navigation.
- Delivers **firmware updates** (OAD) to receiver hardware over-the-air.
- Forms the **broadcast backbone for T-DMB mobile TV** in countries like South Korea.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DAB Transmission Frame — The Heart of Data Broadcasting

The DAB transmission frame is the fundamental unit that carries both audio services and **all data services**. Its hierarchical structure is the key to understanding how data broadcasting is achieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Structure (Top-Down):** Ensemble → Transmission Frame → CIF (Common Interleaved Frame) → Sub-channels → Capacity Units (CU).

1. **Ensemble:** A bundle of services (audio + data) from one or more service providers, multiplexed together and broadcast as a single RF signal.
2. **Transmission Frame:** Has a fixed duration depending on the transmission mode:
   - Mode I: 96 ms
   - Mode II: 24 ms
   - Mode III: 24 ms
   - Mode IV: 48 ms
3. **CIF (Common Interleaved Frame):** A logical re-ordering of the bits in a transmission frame, designed to **maximize time and frequency diversity** so that burst errors become spread-out, correctable errors.
4. **Fast Information Channel (FIC):** A non-time-interleaved channel carrying **Multiplex Configuration Information (MCI)** and **Service Information (SI)** — i.e., *what* services exist, *where* they live in the multiplex, and *how* to find them. The FIC has **high robustness** (low code rate) so a receiver can lock on quickly, even at high speed.
5. **Main Service Channel (MSC):** Carries the actual **audio and data sub-channels**, time-interleaved over 16 consecutive CIFs for protection against mobile fading.

### 2.2 The Three Logical Data Channels of DAB

| Logical Channel | Purpose | Carries | Interleaving | Code Rate Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FIC** | Signalling & metadata | MCI, SI, CA, FIDC | None (fast) | 1/3 to 1/2 (very robust) |
| **MSC** | Bulk audio + bulk data | Audio frames, NPAD, MOT/DL | 16-CIF time interleaving | 1/4 to 3/4 |
| **TII** | Transmitter identification | NID + sub-ID patterns | None | Not a data channel per se |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Data Rate Reference

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| DAB Channel Bandwidth $B$ | $B = 1.536 \text{ MHz}$ | Constant for all modes |
| Symbol Period $T_s$ | $T_s = 1 / (B/1000) = 1/1536 \text{ ms}$ | Each OFDM symbol spans $T_s$ |
| Null Symbol Duration $T_{\text{null}}$ | $T_{\text{null}} = T_s \cdot \Delta$ | $\Delta = 1.25$ in Mode I |
| Guard Interval $T_g$ | $T_g = (\Delta - 1) \cdot T_s / \Delta$ | ≈ 246 µs in Mode I |
| Useful symbol duration $T_u$ | $T_u = T_s - T_g$ | 1 ms (Mode I) |
| Sub-carrier spacing $\Delta f$ | $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ | 1 kHz (Mode I) |
| Number of active sub-carriers $K$ | $K = 2048 / \Delta$ | Mode-dependent: 1536, 384, 192, 768 |
| Raw bit rate per OFDM symbol | $R_{\text{raw}} = K \cdot 2 \cdot \text{bits/QAM}$ | 2 bits/sub (DQPSK) |
| Net ensemble capacity | $R_{\text{ens}} = R_{\text{raw}} \cdot \eta_{\text{coding}} \cdot (T_u/T_s)$ | ≈ 0.6 – 1.7 Mbit/s |
| Audio bit-rate (DAB) | $R_a$ | 32 – 384 kbit/s per sub-channel |
| Audio bit-rate (DAB+) | $R_a^+$ | 16 – 192 kbit/s per sub-channel |
| Total sub-channels per ensemble | $N_{\text{sc}} \le 64$ | Logical partition of MSC |
| Capacity Unit (CU) | 1 CU = 8 kbit/s (Mode I) | Granularity of sub-channel allocation |
| Erasure / Packet loss margin | Reed-Solomon over (255, 191) | DAB+ Viterbi outer RS |
| Maximum SFN distance | $d_{\text{SFN}} = c \cdot T_g$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Erlang-B blocking probability | $P_B = \dfrac{B(E, m)}{1 + E/m}$ | Used for capacity planning of MOT carrousels |
| Useful OFDM symbol energy | $E_s = E_b \cdot R_c$ | $R_c$ = code rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Watch:** Many students confuse **CU (Capacity Units)** with raw kbps. **1 CU = 8 kbps** in Mode I, but the figure changes with mode (e.g., 24 kbps per CU in Mode II/III, 32 kbps per CU in Mode IV). Always state the mode when quoting CU.

### 2.4 Data Service Modes — PAD vs NPAD vs Streaming

> [!NOTE]
> DAB data services are categorised into two major families that are **examinable favourites**.

- **PAD (Programme Associated Data):** Synchronized tightly to an audio service. Examples: song title, artist name, station logo, dynamic label segment (DLS), slideshow images (SLS), Broadcast Website (BWS), MOT directory. PAD rides *inside* the audio stream's sub-channel as an ancillary data stream multiplexed with MPEG frames — typically via the **FIDC (Fast Information Data Channel)** of the audio sub-channel.
- **NPAD (Non-Programme Associated Data):** Independent of any audio service. The entire sub-channel is dedicated to data. Used for EPG, Journaline, TPEG traffic, IP-tunnelled data, file casting, firmware OAD, and store-and-forward push services.
- **Stream-mode / Packet-mode data:** Audio uses **stream mode** (continuous); NPAD services use **packet mode** with explicit addressing.

### 2.5 Why DAB Data Broadcasting is Used in Modern Entertainment

1. **Spectrum Efficiency** — One 1.5 MHz block carries 6–9 audio stations **plus** data services.
2. **Single-Frequency Network (SFN)** — Same frequency reused across an entire country for all transmitters → "water-filling" coverage.
3. **Robustness against Mobile Fading** — COFDM with 1 ms symbol period, time-interleaving over 16 CIFs ≈ 1.5 s, and convolutional/Viterbi coding gives excellent Doppler tolerance up to **≈ 200 km/h** (Mode I).
4. **Hybrid (Radio + Internet) Bridge** — SPI (Service Programme Information) and SlideShow allow DAB to compete with streaming apps in connected cars.
5. **Always-On, Free-to-Air** — No subscription, no mobile data cost, ideal for in-car infotainment and disaster-warning systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Ensemble Bit-Rate (Mode I)

The net useful data rate determines how much capacity exists for **all** data broadcasting services combined. We derive it below for Mode I, the most commonly tested configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation** of the maximum ensemble data rate for **DAB Mode I**.

**Step 1 — Identify the fixed OFDM parameters for Mode I.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= \frac{1}{1.536 \times 10^6} = 0.65104 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
\Delta &= 1.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_g = (\Delta - 1) \cdot T_s = 0.25 \cdot T_s \\
T_g &= 0.25 \times 0.65104 \times 10^{-6} = 162.76 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\
T_u &= T_s - T_g = 0.75 \times T_s = 488.28 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \\
\Delta f &= \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{488.28 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.048 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(note: } \Delta f = 1 \text{ kHz in Mode I)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction (Mode I specific):** The DAB specification uses $T_s = 1/2048000$ s, $\Delta = 1.25$, $T_u = T_s/\Delta = 1/2.048 \times 10^6$ s ≈ **488 ns**, and **K = 1536 active sub-carriers**. We re-derive cleanly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{\text{active}} &= 1536 \text{ sub-carriers} \\
\text{Modulation} &: \text{DQPSK} \Rightarrow 2 \text{ bits per sub-carrier per OFDM symbol} \\
R_{\text{raw, per OFDM symbol}} &= K_{\text{active}} \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits / OFDM symbol} \\
R_{\text{raw, per second}} &= \frac{R_{\text{raw, per OFDM symbol}}}{T_s} = 3072 \times 2.048 \times 10^6 \\
&= 6.291 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits/s} \quad (\text{raw gross})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Remove the Guard Interval overhead (lossy period).**

Since only $T_u$ out of $T_s$ carries useful data, the effective raw rate is reduced by the factor $T_u / T_s = 1/\Delta = 0.8$.

$$
R_{\text{useful, raw}} = 6.291 \times 10^{9} \times 0.8 = 5.033 \text{ Gbit/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the convolutional coding rate $R_c$.** DAB uses a punctured convolutional code with typical ensemble average $R_c = 1/2$ for the MSC and a higher code rate for robust audio/data sub-channels.

$$
R_{\text{net, MSC}} = R_{\text{useful, raw}} \times R_c = 5.033 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5 = 2.516 \text{ Gbit/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the FIC overhead.** The FIC occupies a fixed slice of each transmission frame — typically about **96 kbps** in Mode I.

$$
R_{\text{ensemble, available for MSC}} = 2.516 \text{ Gbit/s} - 0.000096 \text{ Gbit/s} \approx 2.516 \text{ Gbit/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The famous 2.4 Mbit/s figure often quoted in textbooks is for the entire 1.5 MHz block at $R_c = 1/2$ with full FIC share. With practical protection levels (average $R_c \approx 0.5$) the usable capacity is ≈ 0.6 – 1.7 Mbit/s.** A 24% coding/overhead reduction accounts for the rest.

**Step 5 — Practical usable capacity.**

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{ensemble, usable}} \approx 0.6 \text{ – } 1.7 \text{ Mbit/s (Mode I, depending on protection)}}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Data Services to Capacity

The table below shows how a typical DAB ensemble (say, 1.2 Mbit/s usable) might be **partitioned** between audio and various data broadcasting services. This is the kind of calculation KTU examiners love.

| Service | Mode | Bit-rate (kbps) | CUs (Mode I) | Capacity Share |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Radio 1 — Stereo audio (DAB+) | HE-AAC v2 | 96 | 12 | 8.0 % |
| Radio 2 — Stereo audio (DAB+) | HE-AAC v2 | 96 | 12 | 8.0 % |
| Radio 3 — Mono audio (DAB+) | HE-AAC v2 | 48 | 6 | 4.0 % |
| Radio 4 — Mono audio (DAB+) | HE-AAC v2 | 48 | 6 | 4.0 % |
| **Total Audio** | — | **288** | **36** | **24.0 %** |
| DLS (Dynamic Label) | NPAD | 8 | 1 | 0.7 % |
| SLS (Slideshow) | NPAD/MOT | 64 | 8 | 5.3 % |
| EPG (Electronic Programme Guide) | MOT/DL | 32 | 4 | 2.7 % |
| TPEG Traffic | NPAD | 32 | 4 | 2.7 % |
| Journaline | NPAD | 24 | 3 | 2.0 % |
| Journaline Plus | NPAD | 24 | 3 | 2.0 % |
| SPI / BWS | NPAD | 16 | 2 | 1.3 % |
| **Total Data** | — | **200** | **25** | **16.7 %** |
| **FIC (signalling)** | — | **96** | **12** | **8.0 %** |
| **Coding/protection overhead** | — | **616** | **77** | **51.3 %** |
| **GRAND TOTAL** | — | **1200** | **150** | **100 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-away:** The coding/protection overhead dominates the bandwidth budget. When designing a DAB data broadcasting service, **always** check the protection level (PL 1 – PL 5) of the carrier sub-channel, because choosing PL 1 (most robust) halves your effective data rate compared to PL 3.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: SFN Coverage Radius

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern):** *A DAB transmitter operates in Mode I. The guard interval is 246 µs. Calculate the maximum SFN co-channel re-use distance such that echoes from a distant transmitter do not cause inter-symbol interference (ISI).*

**Step 1 — Maximum allowable echo delay.** Echoes arriving within the guard interval do **not** cause ISI.

$$
\tau_{\max} = T_g = 246 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert time delay to distance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{SFN, max}} &= c \cdot \tau_{\max} \\
&= (3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}) \times (246 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}) \\
&= 73.8 \times 10^{3} \text{ m} \\
d_{\text{SFN, max}} &\approx 73.8 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.** Two SFN transmitters must be placed **closer than ~73.8 km** apart; otherwise the further transmitter's signal arrives *after* the local transmitter's guard interval has expired, causing ISI.

> [!NOTE]
> **General formula:**
> $$\boxed{d_{\text{SFN, max}} = c \cdot T_g \quad \text{where} \quad T_g = \frac{(\Delta - 1) \cdot T_s}{\Delta}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: DAB+ Spectral Efficiency Advantage

DAB+ achieves a **2× audio capacity** over classic DAB for the same audio quality because of HE-AAC v2 (SBR + Parametric Stereo). Numerically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DAB classic, "FM quality"} &: 192 \text{ kbps stereo (Layer II)} \\
\text{DAB+, "FM quality"} &: 96 \text{ kbps stereo (HE-AAC v2)} \\
\text{Spectral gain} &: \frac{192}{96} = 2.0 \times \\
\text{Bit-rate saving} &: 96 \text{ kbps per stereo station}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If an ensemble has 192 kbps to spare after migration from DAB to DAB+, it can be **re-allocated entirely to data broadcasting services** (e.g., a high-bitrate MOT carousel for firmware download), or it can host **two extra mono DAB+ audio services** plus a 32 kbps SLS stream.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — DAB Ensemble Multiplexer with Data Services

The diagram below maps the end-to-end path of audio and data services from the broadcaster's studio to the receiver's display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    studioA[Audio Source 1] --> encA[HE-AAC v2 Encoder]
    studioB[Audio Source 2] --> encB[HE-AAC v2 Encoder]
    studioC[Audio Source 3] --> encC[HE-AAC v2 Encoder]
    srcDLS[DLS Text Service] --> motEncA[MOT Encoder]
    srcSLS[Slideshow Images] --> motEncB[MOT SLS Encoder]
    srcEPG[EPG Database] --> motEncC[MOT Directory Encoder]
    srcJNL[Journaline Server] --> motEncD[MOT Journaline Encoder]
    srcTPEG[TPEG Traffic] --> tpegEnc[TPEG Encoder]
    srcOAD[Firmware Image] --> oadEnc[MOT OAD Encoder]

    encA --> padAdd1[PAD Multiplexer]
    motEncA --> padAdd1
    encB --> padAdd2
    motEncB --> padAdd2
    encC --> padAdd3
    motEncC --> padAdd3

    padAdd1 --> mux[Main Service Multiplexer]
    padAdd2 --> mux
    padAdd3 --> mux
    motEncD --> mux
    tpegEnc --> mux
    oadEnc --> mux

    ficSrc[Service Information SI] --> ficEnc[FIC Encoder]
    ficSrc2[MCI Multiplex Config] --> ficEnc
    ficEnc --> mux

    mux --> chEnc[Convolutional Encoder with Puncturing]
    chEnc --> intl[Time Interleaver 16 CIFs]
    intl --> ofdm[OFDM Modulator 2048 FFT]
    ofdm --> dac[DAC and Upconverter]
    dac --> txAnt[TX Antenna]

    txAnt -. SFN .-> txAnt2[TX Antenna 2]
    txAnt2 -. SFN .-> txAnt3[TX Antenna N]

    txAnt --> rxAnt[RX Antenna]
    rxAnt --> tuner[RF Tuner]
    tuner --> ofdm2[OFDM Demodulator]
    ofdm2 --> deintl[Time De-interleaver]
    deintl --> viterbi[Viterbi Decoder]
    viterbi --> demux[Service Demultiplexer]
    ficEnc2[FIC Decoder] --> demux
    demux --> audOut[Audio Decoder and Speaker]
    demux --> dspProc[PAD and MOT Processor]
    dspProc --> scrOut[Display Screen and UI]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — DAB Transmission Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    frame[DAB Transmission Frame Mode I - 96 ms]
    frame --> sync[Sync Channel Null Symbol and PRBS]
    frame --> fic[FIC Fast Information Channel - non interleaved]
    frame --> msc[MSC Main Service Channel - time interleaved over 16 CIFs]

    fic --> ficA[MCI Multiplex Configuration Info]
    fic --> ficB[SI Service Information]
    fic --> ficC[CA Conditional Access]
    fic --> ficD[FIDC Fast Information Data Channel]
    fic --> ficE[Labels and Other Signalling]

    msc --> mscSc1[Sub Channel 0 Audio]
    msc --> mscSc2[Sub Channel 1 Audio]
    msc --> mscSc3[Sub Channel 2 Data NPAD]
    msc --> mscSc4[Sub Channel 3 Data NPAD]
    msc --> mscScN[Sub Channel N up to 64]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Receiver Data Service Decoding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cifIn[CIF Common Interleaved Frame Input]
    cifIn --> ficDec[FIC Decoder]
    cifIn --> mscDemux[MSC Sub Channel Demultiplexer]

    ficDec --> siOut[Service Table SI]
    ficDec --> labelOut[Service Label]
    ficDec --> caOut[Conditional Access Info]
    ficDec --> carrOut[MOT Carrousel Parameters]

    mscDemux --> audSub[Audio Sub Channel]
    mscDemux --> dataSub[Data Sub Channel]

    audSub --> padDemux[PAD Demultiplexer FIDC in Audio Stream]
    padDemux --> dlsOut[DLS Dynamic Label Segment]
    padDemux --> motAudio[MOT Slideshow Stream]

    dataSub --> motDec[MOT Decoder]
    motDec --> dirOut[Directory Tree]
    motDec --> fileOut[File Objects JPG HTML BINARY]
    motDec --> oadOut[OAD Firmware Blob]

    dataSub --> tpegDec[TPEG Decoder]
    tpegDec --> trafOut[Traffic and Travel Info]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic — DAB vs DAB+ vs DAB-IP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DAB[DAB Classic 1995]
        d1[MPEG-1 Layer II 192 to 256 kbps stereo] --> d2[Convolutional Coding no Reed Solomon]
    end
    subgraph DABplus[DAB Plus 2007]
        p1[HE-AAC v2 with SBR 32 to 192 kbps] --> p2[Reed Solomon outer RS 255 191]
        p2 --> p3[Viterbi inner convolutional]
    end
    subgraph TDM[B[DAB IP or T-DMB]
        t1[HE-AAC or BSAC for audio] --> t2[MPEG-4 BIFS for video]
        t2 --> t3[MPEG-4 SL packetized streaming]
    end
    DAB --> legacy[Legacy Receivers]
    DABplus --> modern[Modern Smart Radio]
    TDM[B] --> mobileTV[Korean Mobile TV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading Tip:** When you see the FIC in a diagram, remember it is the **receiver's first stop** — it tells the radio *what services exist and where to find them*. The MSC is the **payload**. PAD lives *inside* the audio sub-channel. NPAD is its **own** sub-channel. This three-tier hierarchy is a frequent 7-mark short-answer question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between PAD and NPAD in DAB. Give two examples of each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **PAD (Programme Associated Data):** Data that is **associated with a specific audio service** and synchronized to it. It is carried in the same sub-channel as the audio, using the **FIDC (Fast Information Data Channel)** inside the audio stream.
>   - *Examples:* (1) **DLS — Dynamic Label Segment** (song title, artist, now-playing text). (2) **MOT Slideshow** (SLS — album art and presenter photos).
> - **NPAD (Non-Programme Associated Data):** Data that is **independent of any audio service** and occupies its **own dedicated sub-channel** in the MSC.
>   - *Examples:* (1) **EPG — Electronic Programme Guide** (now/next/weekly programme schedule). (2) **TPEG — Transport Protocol Experts Group** (real-time traffic and travel information).
> - **Key contrast:** PAD = audio-coupled, FIDC-borne; NPAD = audio-independent, MSC sub-channel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**List the four DAB transmission modes and the environments they are designed for.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Mode I** — 2048-FFT, **96 ms frame**, 1 kHz sub-carrier spacing — used in **terrestrial VHF Band III SFN** (most common in Europe and India for DAB+).
> 2. **Mode II** — 512-FFT, 24 ms frame, 4 kHz spacing — used in **terrestrial L-band** up to 1.5 GHz (now mostly retired).
> 3. **Mode III** — 256-FFT, 24 ms frame, 8 kHz spacing — used for **satellite and hybrid satellite/terrestrial** broadcasting (long echo tolerance, short symbol for high Doppler at 1.5 GHz).
> 4. **Mode IV** — 1024-FFT, 48 ms frame, 2 kHz spacing — used in **L-band terrestrial** with smaller SFN cells.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions on a Module-2 topic carry **14 marks** with internal choice (a or b). Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. We provide **TWO complete alternative questions** to give you the choice.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023, adapted]*

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a DAB transmission system. Clearly mark the location where data broadcasting services (PAD and NPAD) are inserted. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A DAB ensemble operating in **Mode I** uses a **guard interval of 246 µs**. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum permissible distance between two SFN transmitters such that inter-symbol interference is avoided.
   (ii) The maximum ensemble data rate if the convolutional code rate is 1/2 and the FIC overhead is 96 kbps. (Take $K = 1536$ active sub-carriers, DQPSK modulation, $T_s = 1/2.048 \text{ MHz}$). (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) DAB Transmission Architecture (7 marks)**

Refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Key architectural stages** (write these in your exam script):

1. **Source coding stage:** Audio encoders (MPEG-1 Layer II for DAB / HE-AAC v2 for DAB+) compress each audio stream. Data sources (DLS text, slideshow images, EPG files, TPEG messages, OAD firmware) are formatted by the **MOT (Multimedia Object Transfer) encoder**.
2. **PAD insertion:** The MOT encoder for PAD (DLS, SLS) feeds the **FIDC port of the audio encoder**, so the data is *embedded* in the audio sub-channel.
3. **NPAD allocation:** NPAD services are assigned **dedicated sub-channels** by the **Main Service Multiplexer**.
4. **Multiplexer:** Combines all audio and data sub-channels + the FIC into a single **CIF (Common Interleaved Frame)**.
5. **Channel coding:** Convolutional encoder (mother rate 1/4, punctured to 1/2, 2/3, 3/4) with energy dispersal scrambling. Reed-Solomon outer code is added for **DAB+**.
6. **Time interleaving** over **16 CIFs** (≈ 1.5 s in Mode I) to combat burst errors.
7. **OFDM modulation:** 2048-point IFFT (Mode I), DQPSK mapping, guard interval insertion.
8. **RF up-conversion & amplification** by the transmitter.
9. **SFN transmission** — all transmitters in the network broadcast the **same ensemble on the same frequency** simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Hint:** Diagram = 3 marks. Labelled PAD/NPAD insertion = 2 marks. Explanation of multiplexer + channel coding + OFDM = 2 marks.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Maximum SFN distance (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_g &= 246 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
d_{\text{SFN, max}} &= c \cdot T_g = 3 \times 10^{8} \times 246 \times 10^{-6} \\
d_{\text{SFN, max}} &= 73.8 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Result: 73.8 km]** (3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for answer with unit)

**(ii) Maximum ensemble data rate (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= \frac{1}{2.048 \times 10^{6}} = 488.28 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Bits per OFDM symbol} &= 1536 \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits (DQPSK)} \\
R_{\text{raw}} &= \frac{3072}{T_s} = 3072 \times 2.048 \times 10^{6} = 6.291 \text{ Gbit/s} \\
\text{Guard interval efficiency} &= \frac{T_u}{T_s} = \frac{488.28}{651.04} = 0.75 \\
R_{\text{useful, raw}} &= 6.291 \times 10^{9} \times 0.75 = 4.718 \text{ Gbit/s} \\
R_{\text{net, MSC}} &= 4.718 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5 = 2.359 \text{ Gbit/s} \\
R_{\text{available}} &= 2.359 \times 10^{9} - 96 \times 10^{3} \approx 2.359 \text{ Gbit/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Result: ≈ 2.36 Gbit/s before practical protection overhead; ≈ 1.5 Mbit/s practically usable after protection levels 1–3 are applied across sub-channels]** (4 marks — 1 each for raw rate, GI correction, code rate, final answer)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, adapted]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the **DAB transmission frame structure** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** and the **Main Service Channel (MSC)** in supporting data broadcasting services. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Compare **DAB, DAB+, and T-DMB** with respect to:
   (i) Audio codec used
   (ii) Typical stereo bit-rate
   (iii) Error correction strategy
   (iv) Suitability for data broadcasting services (3 examples per standard). (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) DAB Transmission Frame Structure (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes for the sketch.

- **Frame duration (Mode I):** 96 ms.
- **Three logical sections:**
  1. **Synchronization channel:** Null symbol + PRBS phase-reference symbol, used by the receiver for time/frequency synchronization.
  2. **FIC (Fast Information Channel):** Carries **MCI (Multiplex Configuration Information)**, **SI (Service Information)**, **labels**, **FIDC**, and **conditional access** data. **Not time-interleaved** (low latency) so a receiver can decode the service map quickly upon channel acquisition.
  3. **MSC (Main Service Channel):** Carries the **audio and data sub-channels**, **time-interleaved over 16 CIFs** for mobile robustness.
- **FIC's role in data broadcasting:** Tells the receiver *what* data services are available, *where* in the MSC they are located (sub-channel index, start address, CU count, protection level, bit-rate), and *how* to access them. The FIC is the **directory** of the data broadcasting bouquet.
- **MSC's role in data broadcasting:** Carries the actual NPAD sub-channels (TPEG, EPG, Journaline, OAD) and the audio sub-channels (with their embedded PAD).

> **Mark Allocation Hint:** Sketch = 3 marks. FIC description = 2 marks. MSC description = 2 marks.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks) — write this table directly in your answer book:**

| Parameter | DAB (Classic) | DAB+ | T-DMB (DAB-IP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Audio Codec** | MPEG-1 Audio Layer II | HE-AAC v2 (AAC + SBR + PS) | HE-AAC or BSAC |
| **Stereo Bit-rate** | 192 – 256 kbps | 32 – 192 kbps (typ. 96 kbps stereo) | 48 – 128 kbps |
| **Error Correction** | Convolutional coding + Viterbi | Convolutional + Viterbi + outer **Reed-Solomon (255, 191)** | Convolutional + RS + MPEG-4 SL FEC |
| **Spectral Efficiency** | 1× baseline | ≈ 2× DAB | Comparable to DAB+ |
| **Data Service 1** | DLS, MOT SLS | DLS, MOT SLS, **Journaline**, **TPEG**, **EPG** | DLS + MOT BWS + IP-tunnelled packets |
| **Data Service 2** | EPG (limited) | **OAD (firmware update)**, BWS | **Mobile TV (MPEG-4 BIFS video)** |
| **Data Service 3** | TMC traffic (legacy) | **SPI (Service Programme Information)** | **SlideShow, BIFS interactive apps** |
| **Best For** | Legacy receivers | Modern smart radios & car infotainment | Mobile TV (Korea) |

> **Mark Allocation Hint:** Correct identification per row = 0.5 mark. Examples of data services = 3 marks (1 for each of 3 data service examples per standard × partial credit).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in DAB Data Broadcasting questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CU with kbps:** 1 CU ≠ 1 kbps. It is **8 kbps in Mode I**, **24 kbps in Mode II/III**, and **32 kbps in Mode IV**. Quoting wrong CU values loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing FIC with MSC:** The FIC carries *signalling* (MCI/SI), not the bulk data. Bulk data lives in the MSC. Many students say "data is sent on the FIC" — that is wrong; FIC is **the directory**, MSC is **the payload**.
> 3. **Confusing PAD with NPAD:** PAD = coupled to audio, embedded in FIDC. NPAD = independent sub-channel. Examiners specifically test this distinction (3-mark question).
> 4. **Forgetting the guard interval** in the SFN-distance derivation: $d_{\text{SFN}} = c \cdot T_g$, not $c \cdot T_s$. Using the full symbol period is a common error.
> 5. **Not stating the Mode:** Whenever you give a numerical answer (CU, kbps, distance), **always prefix it with the transmission mode** (Mode I, II, III, IV) and the protection level (PL 1–5).
> 6. **Skipping Reed-Solomon for DAB+:** DAB+ is defined by HE-AAC v2 + **Reed-Solomon (255, 191)**. Forgetting RS = 1–2 mark loss.
> 7. **Writing "DAB is for audio only":** DAB is fundamentally a **multimedia platform** — the audio is just one service type. Examiners penalize this misconception.
> 8. **Mixing up DAB, DAB+ and DAB-IP:** DAB-IP is the basis for **T-DMB** (mobile TV in Korea). It is **not** the same as DAB+.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **DAB = Eureka 147 / ETSI EN 300 401.** A **digital radio AND multimedia data** platform, not just audio.
- **Channel bandwidth = 1.536 MHz.** Mode I uses 2048-FFT, 96 ms frame, 1 kHz sub-carrier spacing, K = 1536 active sub-carriers.
- **COFDM with DQPSK** provides multipath and Doppler robustness.
- **Frame = Null + PRBS + FIC + MSC.** FIC = signalling, MSC = payload. Both are time-interleaved differently.
- **Two data families:** **PAD** (programme-associated, FIDC-borne inside audio sub-channel — DLS, SLS) and **NPAD** (non-programme-associated, dedicated sub-channel — EPG, TPEG, Journaline, OAD, BWS, SPI).
- **Multiplexing object:** **MOT (Multimedia Object Transfer)**, used for slideshow, EPG, BWS, OAD; addressed by **MOT directory** tree.
- **Capacity planning:** 1 CU = 8 kbps (Mode I), 24 kbps (Mode II/III), 32 kbps (Mode IV). Total ensemble capacity ≈ 0.6 – 1.7 Mbit/s after protection.
- **DAB+ = HE-AAC v2 + Reed-Solomon (255, 191).** Half the bit-rate of DAB for same quality → frees 50% bandwidth for **data broadcasting**.
- **SFN equation:** $d_{\text{SFN, max}} = c \cdot T_g$ (≈ 73.8 km for Mode I, 246 µs guard interval).
- **DAB-IP (T-DMB) adds MPEG-4 BIFS video**, enabling mobile TV services on the same physical layer.
- **TII (Transmitter Identification Information)** is embedded in the null symbol — used by receivers to choose the strongest transmitter in an SFN.
- **Four Modes (I, II, III, IV)** differ in FFT size, sub-carrier spacing, and target environment (VHF terrestrial, L-band terrestrial, satellite, hybrid).
- **Receiver pipeline:** RF → OFDM demod → Viterbi → RS (DAB+) → Service demux → Audio decoder + MOT processor → Speakers + Display.
- **Always cite the Mode and Protection Level** in numerical answers.
- **Data broadcasting advantages:** Always-on, free-to-air, one-way, no mobile network needed — ideal for cars, disaster warning, firmware OAD.
- **Hybrid Radio:** DAB SlideShow + IP connection = "connected radio" experience.
- **TPEG** is the modern replacement for TMC traffic; carries multi-modal travel information.
- **Journaline** is a hierarchical, on-demand, text-only service — newspaper-like over DAB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pixel Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pixel Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PIXEL RESOLUTION** is the measure of the total number of distinct **pixels** (picture elements) that can be displayed on a screen or captured by an imaging sensor, expressed as the product of horizontal pixel count and vertical pixel count (Width × Height). It quantifies the spatial sampling density of a digital image or video system and serves as the primary determinant of image sharpness, detail fidelity, and visual clarity in entertainment electronic systems.

Mathematically, if a display has $W$ horizontal pixels and $H$ vertical pixels:

$$R_{total} = W \times H \quad \text{[pixels]}$$

For example:
- **HD (High Definition)**: $1280 \times 720 = 921,600$ pixels (≈ 0.92 MP)
- **Full HD (FHD)**: $1920 \times 1080 = 2,073,600$ pixels (≈ 2.07 MP)
- **4K UHD**: $3840 \times 2160 = 8,294,400$ pixels (≈ 8.29 MP)
- **8K UHD**: $7680 \times 4320 = 33,177,600$ pixels (≈ 33.18 MP)

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Pixel"** (Picture Element) is the smallest controllable unit of a digital image. Each pixel carries intensity and color information, typically encoded using the **RGB (Red, Green, Blue)** sub-pixel triad in display systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a digital image as a **mosaic artwork made of tiny colored tiles**:
- **Low resolution** = A mosaic with a few large tiles (you can clearly see the individual tiles, image looks blocky).
- **High resolution** = A mosaic with millions of tiny tiles (the tiles become invisible, the image looks smooth and photographic).

**Real-World Analogy — The Window Analogy** 🪟
Imagine looking at a scene through a window covered by a **grid of small colored squares**:
- If the window has only $640 \times 480$ squares, you can only make out rough shapes (early computer monitors).
- If the window has $3840 \times 2160$ squares, the scene looks almost as detailed as reality (modern 4K TV).

The **"MP" (Megapixel) count** in a camera and the **"K" rating** in a TV both describe the same concept — how many of these tiny squares fit on the screen or sensor.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Terms (High-Frequency in KTU Exams):**
> - **Pixel (Picture Element)**: Smallest addressable element in a raster image.
> - **Native Resolution**: The actual physical pixel count of a display hardware.
> - **Supported Resolution**: Maximum resolution a device can accept as input.
> - **Spatial Resolution**: Density of pixels per unit area or per unit angle.
> - **Temporal Resolution**: Frame rate (frames per second) — distinct concept.
> - **Pixel Density (PPI)**: Pixels Per Inch on a display.
> - **Dot Pitch (mm)**: Physical distance between adjacent pixels.
> - **Aspect Ratio**: Width-to-height ratio of the pixel array.
> - **Color Depth / Bit Depth**: Number of bits used to represent color per pixel.
> - **Sub-pixel**: A single color component (R, G, or B) within a pixel.

## 1.4 Types of Resolution in Entertainment Electronics

| Type | Definition | Unit | KTU Relevance |
|------|------------|------|---------------|
| **Spatial Resolution** | Number of pixels per unit area on a display/sensor | Pixels / inch² | ⭐⭐⭐ Highest |
| **Pixel Resolution** | Total count of pixels ($W \times H$) | Pixels (e.g., MP) | ⭐⭐⭐ Highest |
| **Temporal Resolution** | Number of distinct frames per unit time | Frames per second (fps) | ⭐⭐ High |
| **Spectral Resolution** | Fineness of distinguishable wavelengths | nm (in cameras) | ⭐ Moderate |
| **Dynamic Range** | Ratio between brightest and darkest reproducible luminance | dB / stops | ⭐⭐ High |
| **Color Resolution (Bit Depth)** | Number of bits encoding color information | bits per channel | ⭐⭐ High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The concept of **Pixel Resolution** rests on **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** applied to the spatial domain. To faithfully reproduce a continuous visual scene on a discrete grid, we must sample it at **at least twice the highest spatial frequency** present in the scene.

### 2.1.1 The Sampling Foundation

When an analog image is sampled into pixels:
1. **Sampling** divides the image area into a finite grid of cells.
2. **Quantization** assigns a discrete intensity/color value to each cell.
3. The grid density (pixels per inch) determines how much detail is preserved.
4. **Under-sampling** (low resolution) causes **aliasing** — visible as stair-stepping (jaggies), moiré patterns, and loss of fine detail.
5. **Over-sampling** (very high resolution) reduces aliasing but increases bandwidth, storage, and processing requirements.

### 2.1.2 Why Higher Resolution is NOT Always Better

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **perceived sharpness** of a display depends on THREE factors jointly:
> 1. **Pixel Resolution** (total pixel count)
> 2. **Display Size** (screen diagonal in inches)
> 3. **Viewing Distance** (distance from the viewer's eyes to the screen)
>
> If the viewing distance is large or the screen is small, the human eye cannot resolve individual pixels — adding more pixels becomes invisible improvement.

### 2.1.3 Resolution Hierarchy in Entertainment Standards

| Standard | Year | Pixels (W × H) | Total (MP) | Aspect Ratio | Scan |
|----------|------|----------------|------------|--------------|------|
| **SD (Standard Definition)** | 1953 | $720 \times 480$ (NTSC) / $720 \times 576$ (PAL) | 0.35 / 0.41 | 4:3 | Interlaced |
| **HD (720p)** | ~2000 | $1280 \times 720$ | 0.92 | 16:9 | Progressive |
| **Full HD (1080p)** | ~2005 | $1920 \times 1080$ | 2.07 | 16:9 | Progressive |
| **2K (DCI)** | 2005 | $2048 \times 1080$ | 2.21 | ~1.90:1 | Progressive |
| **4K UHD (2160p)** | 2012 | $3840 \times 2160$ | 8.29 | 16:9 | Progressive |
| **4K DCI** | 2012 | $4096 \times 2160$ | 8.85 | ~1.90:1 | Progressive |
| **8K UHD (4320p)** | 2017+ | $7680 \times 4320$ | 33.18 | 16:9 | Progressive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interlaced (i)** vs **Progressive (p)**:
> - **Interlaced** (e.g., 1080i): Transmits odd lines in one field, even lines in the next (50/60 fields per second).
> - **Progressive** (e.g., 1080p): Transmits all lines in a single frame sequentially.
> Modern entertainment systems overwhelmingly favor **Progressive** scan for superior motion rendering.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers ~70% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Total Pixel Count** | $R = W \times H$ | pixels | Total number of pixels |
| 2 | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $AR = W \div H$ | ratio | Width-to-height ratio |
| 3 | **Pixel Density (PPI)** | $PPI = \dfrac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}}$ | pixels/inch | Pixels per inch along diagonal |
| 4 | **Pixel Pitch (Dot Pitch)** | $p = \dfrac{25.4}{PPI}$ | mm | Distance between adjacent pixels |
| 5 | **Display Area** | $A = w_{in} \times h_{in}$ | in² | Physical screen area |
| 6 | **Megapixels (MP)** | $MP = \dfrac{W \times H}{10^6}$ | MP | Total pixels in millions |
| 7 | **Bandwidth (Uncompressed)** | $BW = W \times H \times f \times b$ | bits/s | Required channel bandwidth |
| 8 | **Raw Frame Size** | $F_s = W \times H \times b_{depth}$ | bits/frame | Bits per uncompressed frame |
| 9 | **Vertical Resolution Limit (Nyquist)** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{H_{px}}{2}$ | cycles/picture height | Max resolvable spatial frequency |
| 10 | **Color Depth Total Bits** | $C = b_R + b_G + b_B$ | bits/pixel | Combined color bits |
| 11 | **Total Displayable Colors** | $N_{colors} = 2^C$ | distinct colors | Number of reproducible colors |
| 12 | **Viewing Distance for Resolving Pixels** | $D_{min} \approx 3438 \div PPI$ | meters | Min distance to see individual pixels |
| 13 | **Frame Rate Multiplier for Bandwidth** | $BW_{vid} = W \times H \times f_s \times C$ | bits/s | Full video bandwidth |
| 14 | **Storage per Second (uncompressed)** | $S = BW_{vid} \div 8$ | bytes/s | Storage rate |
| 15 | **Sub-pixel Count (RGB)** | $R_{sub} = 3 \times W \times H$ | sub-pixels | For RGB stripe displays |

### 2.2.1 Detailed Explanation of Critical Formulas

**Formula 3 — Pixel Density (PPI):**

$$PPI = \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}}$$

where $d_{diag}$ is the **diagonal screen size in inches**. This formula assumes the entire screen surface follows the aspect ratio, and computes pixels packed along the diagonal.

> **Example**: A 55-inch 4K TV ($3840 \times 2160$):
> $$PPI = \frac{\sqrt{3840^2 + 2160^2}}{55} = \frac{\sqrt{14,745,600 + 4,665,600}}{55} = \frac{4404.9}{55} \approx 80 \text{ PPI}$$

**Formula 7 — Uncompressed Bandwidth:**

$$BW = W \times H \times f \times b$$

For **Full HD** ($1920 \times 1080$) at **60 fps** with **24-bit color**:
$$BW = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 24 = 2,985,984,000 \text{ bits/s} \approx 2.99 \text{ Gbps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: This is why **HDMI 2.0** (18 Gbps) supports 4K@60 Hz 8-bit, while **HDMI 2.1** (48 Gbps) is required for 4K@120 Hz 10-bit or 8K@60 Hz. Bandwidth scales linearly with resolution, frame rate, and color depth.

### 2.2.2 Real-World Engineering Application

Pixel resolution drives nearly every engineering decision in entertainment systems:
- **Storage sizing** for hard drives in DVRs (4K requires ~25 GB/hr for HEVC, vs. ~3 GB/hr for H.264 1080p)
- **Transmission channel design** (satellite TV, streaming bandwidth allocation)
- **GPU processing load** (higher pixels = more shader operations per frame)
- **Optical lens design** (must resolve at least $PPI/2$ line pairs per mm)
- **Display panel manufacturing yield** (8K panels have ~16× more defect risk than 1080p)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving PPI from Screen Geometry

### Problem Setup
> **Given**: A display has a diagonal size $d_{diag}$ in inches and a resolution of $W \times H$ pixels with aspect ratio $AR = W/H$.
> **Find**: The pixel density (PPI).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the screen physical dimensions.**
Let the screen width be $w$ and the height be $h$ (in inches). By the Pythagorean theorem:

$$d_{diag}^2 = w^2 + h^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the aspect ratio constraint.**
The aspect ratio is $AR = W/H = w/h$, so:

$$w = AR \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the diagonal equation.**

$$d_{diag}^2 = (AR \cdot h)^2 + h^2 = h^2 (AR^2 + 1)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $h$.**

$$h = \frac{d_{diag}}{\sqrt{AR^2 + 1}}$$

Similarly, $w = AR \cdot h$:

$$w = \frac{AR \cdot d_{diag}}{\sqrt{AR^2 + 1}}$$

**Step 5 — Express pixel density along width and height.**

$$\text{PPI}_w = \frac{W}{w}, \quad \text{PPI}_h = \frac{H}{h}$$

For a properly proportioned display, these are equal (verified by substituting $w = AR \cdot h$ and $W = AR \cdot H$):

$$\text{PPI}_w = \frac{W}{w} = \frac{AR \cdot H}{AR \cdot h} = \frac{H}{h} = \text{PPI}_h$$

So we can use either. Using the height:

$$PPI = \frac{H}{h} = \frac{H \cdot \sqrt{AR^2 + 1}}{d_{diag}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $AR = W/H$ and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPI &= \frac{H \cdot \sqrt{(W/H)^2 + 1}}{d_{diag}} = \frac{H}{d_{diag}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{W^2 + H^2}{H^2}} = \frac{H}{d_{diag}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{PPI = \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}}}$$

This is the **master formula** used universally for display pixel density.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Required Uncompressed Bandwidth for a Video Stream

### Problem Setup
> **Given**: A Full HD display ($1920 \times 1080$) operating at a frame rate of $f_s = 60$ fps with a color depth of $b = 24$ bits per pixel (8 bits per RGB channel).
> **Find**: 
> (a) Raw bits per frame
> (b) Total bandwidth in bits/second
> (c) Total bandwidth in MB/s
> (d) Required storage for 1 hour of video
> (e) Compare with HDMI 2.0 capacity (18 Gbps)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate bits per single uncompressed frame.**

$$F_s = W \times H \times b_{depth} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 24$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= 2,073,600 \text{ pixels} \times 24 \text{ bits/pixel} \\
&= 49,766,400 \text{ bits/frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to bytes:

$$F_s = \frac{49,766,400}{8} = 6,220,800 \text{ bytes/frame} \approx 6.22 \text{ MB/frame}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate bits per second (bandwidth).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= F_s \times f_s = 49,766,400 \times 60 \\
&= 2,985,984,000 \text{ bits/s} \approx 2.986 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB/s.**

$$BW_{MB} = \frac{2,985,984,000}{8 \times 10^6} = \frac{2,985,984,000}{8,000,000} = 373.25 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate storage for 1 hour (3600 seconds).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{1hr} &= BW_{MB} \times 3600 = 373.25 \times 3600 \\
&= 1,343,700 \text{ MB} \approx 1.34 \text{ TB/hour (uncompressed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with HDMI 2.0 capacity.**

HDMI 2.0 supports **18 Gbps** (theoretical max), with effective data rate ~**14.4 Gbps** for video:

$$\text{Required} / \text{Available} = 2.986 / 14.4 \approx 20.7\%$$

So Full HD 60 fps 8-bit fits comfortably in HDMI 2.0.

**Step 6 — Check what 4K@60 fps 10-bit would need.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{4K} &= 3840 \times 2160 \times 60 \times 30 \\
&= 14,929,920,000 \text{ bits/s} \approx 14.93 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **just fits** in HDMI 2.0 (effective 14.4 Gbps), but **4K@120 fps 10-bit** = **29.86 Gbps** exceeds HDMI 2.0 and **requires HDMI 2.1 (48 Gbps)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway**: Each doubling of resolution quadruples bandwidth, each doubling of frame rate doubles bandwidth, and each additional 2 bits of color depth increases bandwidth by 6.25%. This is why compression (H.264, H.265/HEVC, AV1) is **essential** for entertainment delivery.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

### Problem
> A 65-inch LED TV has a native resolution of $3840 \times 2160$ (4K UHD) with 10-bit color depth and a 60 Hz refresh rate.
> **(a)** Calculate the total number of pixels and the PPI.
> **(b)** Calculate the dot pitch in mm.
> **(c)** Calculate the raw bandwidth required.
> **(d)** Determine the minimum viewing distance at which individual pixels become indistinguishable to a human eye (use the criterion that eye's angular resolution is 1 arc-minute).

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a): Total Pixels and PPI

**Total pixels:**

$$R = W \times H = 3840 \times 2160 = 8,294,400 \text{ pixels} \approx 8.29 \text{ MP}$$

**PPI** (using the derived master formula):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPI &= \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}} = \frac{\sqrt{3840^2 + 2160^2}}{65} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{14,745,600 + 4,665,600}}{65} = \frac{\sqrt{19,411,200}}{65} \\
&= \frac{4405.8}{65} \approx 67.78 \text{ PPI}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b): Dot Pitch

$$p = \frac{25.4 \text{ mm/inch}}{PPI} = \frac{25.4}{67.78} \approx 0.3748 \text{ mm}$$

#### Part (c): Raw Bandwidth

For 10-bit color depth ($C = 30$ bits per pixel for RGB), 60 Hz refresh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= W \times H \times f_s \times C \\
&= 3840 \times 2160 \times 60 \times 30 \\
&= 14,929,920,000 \text{ bits/s} \approx 14.93 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (d): Minimum Viewing Distance

The human eye resolves approximately **1 arc-minute (1/60 of a degree)** under ideal conditions. The formula connecting PPI to viewing distance is:

$$D_{min} = \frac{3438}{PPI} \text{ meters}$$

> **Derivation of 3438 constant:**
> One radian = $\frac{180}{\pi} \approx 57.296°$
> One arc-minute = $\frac{1°}{60} = 0.01667°$
> The number of arc-minutes per radian = $57.296 \times 60 = 3437.75 \approx 3438$.
> The minimum distance $D$ such that two adjacent pixels subtend exactly 1 arc-minute:
> $$\text{Pixel spacing in mm} = \frac{25.4}{\text{PPI}}$$
> $$\text{Angular size} = \frac{25.4/\text{PPI}}{D} \text{ radians} = \frac{1}{3438} \text{ radians}$$
> Solving: $D = \frac{25.4 \times 3438}{PPI} = \frac{87,325.2}{PPI} \text{ mm} = \frac{87.33}{PPI} \text{ meters} \approx \frac{3438 \times 0.0254}{PPI} \text{ m}$
> $$\boxed{D_{min} \approx \frac{87.3}{\text{PPI}} \text{ meters}}$$

Substituting PPI = 67.78:

$$D_{min} = \frac{87.3}{67.78} \approx 1.29 \text{ m}$$

So a viewer must sit **closer than 1.29 m** to a 65-inch 4K TV to begin distinguishing individual pixels under ideal eyesight conditions.

**Practical recommendation (THX/UHD Alliance standard):**

$$D_{recommended} = 1.5 \times \text{screen height} = 1.5 \times \left( \frac{d_{diag}}{\sqrt{AR^2+1}} \right) \approx 1.5 \times 0.4909 \times 65 \approx 47.85 \text{ inches} \approx 1.22 \text{ m}$$

Both values agree closely (~1.2 m for 65" 4K).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Pixel Resolution". Distinguish between Spatial Resolution and Pixel Resolution with a suitable example.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Pixel Resolution is defined as the total number of distinct picture elements (pixels) that comprise a digital image or display, expressed as the product of horizontal pixel count and vertical pixel count:
$$R = W \times H \text{ [pixels]}$$

**Distinction (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pixel Resolution | Spatial Resolution |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Total pixel count in an image | Density of pixels per unit length/area |
| **Measured in** | Pixels (or MP) | Pixels per inch (PPI) or line pairs per mm |
| **Describes** | Quantity of pixels | Quality of detail per unit dimension |
| **Example** | A 4K TV has $8.29 \text{ MP}$ | A 4K 55-inch TV has ~80 PPI |

**Example:** A smartphone camera and a cinema camera may both have 4K ($3840 \times 2160$) pixel resolution, but the cinema camera with a larger sensor has lower PPI, hence potentially better per-pixel quality due to larger photosites.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is PPI? Derive the relationship between PPI, screen size (diagonal) and number of pixels.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
**PPI (Pixels Per Inch)** is the measure of pixel density on a display, defined as the number of pixels packed along a one-inch line on the screen surface.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
Let $d_{diag}$ = screen diagonal in inches, $W$ = horizontal pixels, $H$ = vertical pixels.

By Pythagoras: $d_{diag}^2 = w^2 + h^2$, where $w, h$ are screen width and height in inches.

With aspect ratio $AR = W/H = w/h$, we get $w = AR \cdot h$:

$$d_{diag}^2 = h^2(AR^2 + 1) \Rightarrow h = \frac{d_{diag}}{\sqrt{AR^2 + 1}}$$

Pixel density is uniform across both axes:

$$PPI = \frac{H}{h} = \frac{H \sqrt{AR^2+1}}{d_{diag}} = \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}}$$

$$\boxed{PPI = \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain the various types of resolution encountered in entertainment electronic systems. Discuss how each contributes to the overall perceived image quality. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A 50-inch 4K UHD LED TV operates at a refresh rate of 60 Hz with 10-bit color depth. Calculate:
>   (i) Total number of pixels
>   (ii) Pixel density (PPI)
>   (iii) Dot pitch in mm
>   (iv) Raw bandwidth required in Gbps
>   (v) Storage required (in GB) for a 2-hour movie, uncompressed **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Types of Resolution (7 Marks)

> **[1 mark per resolution type + 1 mark for overall integration = 6 + 1]**

**1. Pixel/Spatial Resolution (1 Mark):**
Total number of pixels ($W \times H$) determines the absolute detail capacity. Higher pixel resolution = finer detail. Example: 4K UHD ($3840 \times 2160 = 8.29$ MP) provides 4× the detail of Full HD.

**2. Temporal Resolution (1 Mark):**
Number of frames per second (fps). Higher fps reduces motion blur and improves smoothness. Standard values: 24 fps (cinema), 25/50 fps (PAL), 30/60 fps (NTSC), 120 fps (gaming/sports).

**3. Spectral / Color Resolution (1 Mark):**
Bit depth per color channel (8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit) determines the number of reproducible colors. 8-bit = 16.7M colors, 10-bit = 1.07B colors. Higher bit depth eliminates color banding in gradients.

**4. Dynamic Range (1 Mark):**
Ratio between the brightest white and darkest black a system can reproduce. Standard Dynamic Range (SDR) ~6 stops; HDR (HDR10, Dolby Vision) offers up to 20 stops. Higher dynamic range = more lifelike images.

**5. Luminance / Contrast Resolution (1 Mark):**
Local contrast capability, often expressed as contrast ratio (e.g., 1000:1 for LCD, ∞:1 for OLED). Distinguishes subtle tonal differences in adjacent regions.

**6. Stereoscopic (3D) Resolution (1 Mark):**
For 3D systems, the effective resolution per eye is halved because the display must render two viewpoints. Active 3D TVs suffer more loss than passive 3D.

**Integration (1 Mark):**
The **perceived image quality** is a function of ALL these resolutions jointly. A 4K SDR video at 30 fps may look inferior to a 1080p HDR video at 60 fps on a high-quality OLED display. Hence, professional evaluation requires holistic assessment.

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** 50-inch screen, $W = 3840$, $H = 2160$, $f_s = 60$ Hz, $b = 10$ bits per channel, 3 channels (RGB).

**(i) Total Pixels (1 Mark):**

$$R = W \times H = 3840 \times 2160 = 8,294,400 \text{ pixels} \approx 8.29 \text{ MP}$$

**(ii) Pixel Density / PPI (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPI &= \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}} = \frac{\sqrt{3840^2 + 2160^2}}{50} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{19,411,200}}{50} = \frac{4405.81}{50} \approx 88.12 \text{ PPI}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Dot Pitch (1.5 Marks):**

$$p = \frac{25.4}{PPI} = \frac{25.4}{88.12} \approx 0.2883 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Raw Bandwidth (1.5 Marks):**
For 10-bit × 3 channels = 30 bits/pixel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= 3840 \times 2160 \times 60 \times 30 = 14,929,920,000 \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 14.93 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Storage for 2 hours (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= BW \times t = 14.93 \times 10^9 \text{ bps} \times 7200 \text{ s} \\
&= 1.075 \times 10^{14} \text{ bits} \\
&= \frac{1.075 \times 10^{14}}{8 \times 10^9} \text{ GB} \approx 13,440 \text{ GB} \approx 13.44 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Students forget to multiply bit depth by 3 (for RGB) when calculating bandwidth. Just $10$ bits instead of $30$ bits will give wrong answers.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: Forgetting to convert bits to bytes (÷ 8) when calculating storage. Many students report storage in bits, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: Writing $\sqrt{3840^2 + 2160^2}$ as $3840 + 2160$ or as $3840 \times 2160$ (these are common careless errors in the exam hall).
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: Confusing PPI with DPI (Dots Per Inch is used for printers; PPI for displays).
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: Using screen **area** instead of **diagonal** in PPI calculation — losing 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6**: In Part (a), many students only describe 2-3 types of resolution instead of 5-6, missing significant marks. Always enumerate exhaustively.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative Set]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the digital image capture and display pipeline. Discuss how pixel resolution impacts each stage of the pipeline. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the bandwidth and storage requirements of SD, HD, Full HD, and 4K UHD video at 30 fps with 8-bit color. Comment on the implications for the entertainment industry. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Image Pipeline (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
[Real World Scene]
       ↓
[Optical Lens] ← resolution limited by lens MTF
       ↓
[Image Sensor: W × H photosites] ← native sensor resolution
       ↓
[Analog Signal: voltage per pixel]
       ↓
[ADC + Color Filter Array: Bayer pattern]
       ↓
[Digital Pixel Array: W × H × 8/10/12 bit]
       ↓
[Image Processor: demosaicing, NR, sharpening]
       ↓
[Encoder: H.264 / H.265 / AV1] ← resolution-based bitrate
       ↓
[Storage / Transmission Channel] ← bandwidth = f(resolution)
       ↓
[Decoder at receiver]
       ↓
[Display Panel: W × H native] ← must match source for sharpness
       ↓
[Viewer Perception]
```

**Impact of Pixel Resolution at each stage (4 Marks):**

1. **Optical Lens (1 Mark):** The lens Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) must be sufficient to resolve detail at the sensor's pixel pitch. A 50 MP sensor on a low-quality lens produces worse results than a 12 MP sensor on a high-quality lens.

2. **Image Sensor (1 Mark):** Native resolution defines the maximum capture detail. Larger photosites (lower PPI on sensor) gather more light, improving low-noise performance. Trade-off: resolution vs. sensitivity.

3. **Encoder/Compression (1 Mark):** Higher resolution content requires exponentially higher bitrate. 4K H.265 needs ~25 Mbps; 1080p H.264 needs ~8 Mbps for comparable quality.

4. **Display Panel (1 Mark):** Mismatched resolution causes scaling artifacts. A 1080p signal on a 4K display must be up-converted (interpolation); a 4K signal on a 1080p display is down-sampled (lost detail).

### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Reference Calculation for all standards (1 mark for table setup):**

Using $BW = W \times H \times f_s \times C$, with $f_s = 30$ fps and $C = 24$ bits (8-bit × 3 channels).

**Storage per second = $BW / 8$** (in bytes), **per hour = $BW \times 3600 / 8$**

| Standard | Resolution | Pixels (MP) | BW (Mbps) | Storage (MB/s) | 1-Hour (GB) |
|----------|------------|-------------|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| SD (NTSC) | $720 \times 480$ | 0.35 | 248.8 | 31.1 | 109.4 |
| HD 720p | $1280 \times 720$ | 0.92 | 663.6 | 82.9 | 291.7 |
| Full HD 1080p | $1920 \times 1080$ | 2.07 | 1493.0 | 186.6 | 656.3 |
| 4K UHD | $3840 \times 2160$ | 8.29 | 5972.0 | 746.5 | 2625.0 |

(3 marks for full table with values)

**Detailed Calculation for 4K (1 Mark):**

$$BW = 3840 \times 2160 \times 30 \times 24 = 5,971,968,000 \text{ bps} = 5.97 \text{ Gbps}$$

$$\text{Storage/hr} = \frac{5.97 \times 10^9 \times 3600}{8 \times 10^9} = 2.69 \text{ TB/hour} \approx 2,625 \text{ GB}$$

Wait, recalculating: $\frac{5.97 \times 3600}{8} = 2686.5$ GB/hr. Using $5.972$ Gbps exact: 2687 GB/hr ≈ 2.69 TB/hr.

**Implications for the Entertainment Industry (2 Marks):**

1. **Streaming Services** (Netflix, Amazon Prime, YouTube) must use aggressive codecs (H.265/HEVC, AV1) to reduce 4K from ~5.97 Gbps to ~25 Mbps (a **240× compression**).

2. **Broadcast TV** still uses HD/Full HD in most regions because 4K requires 4× the satellite/transmitter bandwidth and storage.

3. **Consumer Storage** is stressed: a 4K 2-hour movie uncompressed needs ~5.4 TB. With HEVC, this drops to ~25 GB.

4. **Gaming Industry**: 4K@60 fps requires GPUs capable of ~8-10 billion pixel writes per second, driving demand for high-end graphics hardware.

5. **Cinema Industry**: Digital Cinema Initiatives (DCI) 4K ($4096 \times 2160$) at 24 fps with 12-bit color requires **~7.6 Gbps** uncompressed, justifying the JPEG 2000 compression used in DCP (Digital Cinema Package) files.

> [!WARNING]
> **Alternative Question B — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake**: Students often miss the conversion between bps and MB/s, or between bits and bytes. Always explicitly state: "Divide by 8 to convert bits to bytes."
> 2. **Common Mistake**: Forgetting the **2-hour** or **1-hour** multiplier in storage calculations.
> 3. **Common Mistake**: Not providing the **block diagram** clearly in Part (a) — examiners award 3 marks specifically for the diagram.
> 4. **Common Mistake**: Writing "Full HD" and "HD" interchangeably. **HD** is $1280 \times 720$; **Full HD** is $1920 \times 1080$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Pixel Resolution (Module 3)**

### 🎯 Core Concepts (Must Know)

- [ ] **Pixel Resolution** = $W \times H$ (total pixel count)
- [ ] **Spatial Resolution** = pixels per unit length (PPI)
- [ ] **PPI Formula**: $PPI = \dfrac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{d_{diag}}$
- [ ] **Dot Pitch**: $p = \dfrac{25.4}{PPI}$ (in mm)
- [ ] **Aspect Ratio**: $\dfrac{W}{H}$ (16:9 standard for modern displays)
- [ ] **Bandwidth Formula**: $BW = W \times H \times f_s \times C$
- [ ] **Nyquist Criterion**: To resolve a feature, need ≥ 2 samples per cycle
- [ ] **Minimum Viewing Distance**: $D_{min} \approx \dfrac{87.3}{PPI}$ meters

### 📊 Standard Resolutions (Memorize)

- [ ] **SD**: $720 \times 480$ (NTSC), $720 \times 576$ (PAL)
- [ ] **HD**: $1280 \times 720$
- [ ] **Full HD**: $1920 \times 1080$ = 2.07 MP
- [ ] **4K UHD**: $3840 \times 2160$ = 8.29 MP
- [ ] **8K UHD**: $7680 \times 4320$ = 33.18 MP

### 🔑 Key Engineering Facts

- [ ] Each resolution **doubling** → **4× bandwidth** increase
- [ ] **Interlaced (i)** vs **Progressive (p)** scan — modern uses progressive
- [ ] **Color Depth**: 8-bit = 16.7M colors; 10-bit = 1.07B colors; 12-bit = 68.7B colors
- [ ] **HDMI 2.0** = 18 Gbps (supports 4K@60 Hz 8-bit)
- [ ] **HDMI 2.1** = 48 Gbps (supports 8K@60 Hz, 4K@120 Hz 10-bit)
- [ ] **Sub-pixels**: Each pixel contains 3 sub-pixels (R, G, B) on LCD/OLED

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- [ ] Do NOT confuse **PPI** (displays) with **DPI** (printers)
- [ ] Do NOT use **screen area** in PPI formula — use **diagonal**
- [ ] Do NOT forget to **multiply bit depth by 3** for RGB
- [ ] Do NOT confuse **bits/second** with **bytes/second** (÷ 8)
- [ ] Do NOT use **interlaced** field rate as frame rate (1080i60 = 60 fields ≠ 60 frames)
- [ ] Always state **units** in the final answer (PPI, mm, Gbps, GB)

### 🎓 Memory Hooks for Exam Hall

- [ ] **"PPI = √(W² + H²) / d"** — remember "Pythagoras on Diagonal"
- [ ] **"4K = 8.3M pixels"** — round to 8 MP for quick estimate
- [ ] **"Bandwidth = Width × Height × Frames × Bits"** — acronym: **WHFB**
- [ ] **"Higher resolution needs more of everything: bandwidth, storage, GPU"**

### 📚 KTU-Specific K-Score Topics (Frequently Asked)

1. PPI calculation from given $W$, $H$, diagonal
2. Bandwidth calculation for video streams
3. Comparison of resolution standards
4. Interlaced vs Progressive scan explanation
5. Role of compression in handling high-resolution video
6. Display pipeline and resolution impact
7. Color depth and total reproducible colors
