topics.title: Comparison with Standard Definition TV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison with Standard Definition TV

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Standard Definition Television (SDTV)"?

**Standard Definition Television (SDTV)** is the baseline analog/digital broadcast television format that dominated global home entertainment from the mid-20th century until the early 2000s. It is formally defined by the **ITU-R BT.601** recommendation (also known as **Rec. 601** or **D-1 format**), which standardizes the digitization of component video from composite analog sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> SDTV uses either **480 active lines** (NTSC regions: USA, Japan, Canada) or **576 active lines** (PAL/SECAM regions: Europe, India, Australia). Both are **interlaced** (denoted by the suffix `i`) with a **4:3 aspect ratio**.

The two principal SDTV standards are:

| Standard | Region | Active Lines | Field Rate | Frame Rate | Aspect Ratio |
|----------|--------|--------------|------------|------------|--------------|
| NTSC (M) | USA, Japan | 480 | 60 Hz | 30 fps | 4:3 |
| PAL (B/G) | Europe, India | 576 | 50 Hz | 25 fps | 4:3 |

## 1.2 What is "High Definition Television (HDTV)"?

**High Definition Television (HDTV)** is a digital broadcast format providing substantially higher spatial resolution, wider aspect ratio, and superior audio fidelity compared to SDTV. It is defined under **ITU-R BT.709** (for HD) and **ITU-R BT.2020** (for UHD/4K).

The most common HDTV formats are:

| HDTV Format | Resolution (H × V) | Scanning | Frame Rates |
|-------------|--------------------|---------:|-------------|
| 720p | 1280 × 720 | Progressive | 24, 25, 30, 50, 60 fps |
| 1080i | 1920 × 1080 | Interlaced | 50, 60 fps |
| 1080p | 1920 × 1080 | Progressive | 24, 25, 30, 50, 60 fps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "High Definition"?** The "definition" in television refers to the ability of the display to resolve fine detail — essentially the **spatial resolution**, measured in **pixels** (picture elements) or **TV lines (TVL)**. HDTV has roughly **4× to 6× more pixels** than SDTV, which is why the picture looks dramatically sharper.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Window Pane"

Imagine watching the world through two windows:

- **SDTV window:** A small, **rectangular (4:3)** glass pane made of just a few hundred tiles. You can identify a person, but facial wrinkles or distant text appear blurry.
- **HDTV window:** A much **wider (16:9)** glass pane built from millions of tiny tiles. The same person now reveals the texture of their shirt, individual eyelashes, and reflections in their eyes.

The number of **tiles (pixels)** and the **width of the window (aspect ratio)** are the two key physical differences. Everything else — bandwidth, color depth, audio — is a downstream consequence of cramming more visual information through the broadcast pipeline.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** "**SD = Small & Square-ish (4:3), HD = Huge & Wide (16:9)**". Write this on the first page of your answer sheet if you forget the aspect ratios during the exam.

## 1.4 The Core Comparison — At a Glance

| Parameter | SDTV | HDTV |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Vertical Resolution** | 480i / 576i | 720p / 1080i / 1080p |
| **Horizontal Resolution** | 640 / 720 pixels | 1280 / 1920 pixels |
| **Total Pixels (per frame)** | ≈ 307,200 (480i) / ≈ 414,720 (576i) | ≈ 921,600 (720p) / ≈ 2,073,600 (1080p) |
| **Aspect Ratio** | **4:3** (1.33:1) | **16:9** (1.78:1) |
| **Scanning Method** | Interlaced (i) | Progressive (p) or Interlaced (i) |
| **Bandwidth (compressed)** | 2–6 Mbps (MPEG-2) | 8–20 Mbps (MPEG-4/H.264) |
| **Color Depth** | 8-bit per channel | 8-bit / 10-bit per channel |
| **Audio** | Stereo (2.0) | Surround (5.1 / 7.1) |
| **Standard Document** | ITU-R BT.601 | ITU-R BT.709 |
| **Signal Type** | Analog / Digital | Fully Digital |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** When asked to "compare SDTV and HDTV", a student who only mentions "HD has more pixels" will lose **at least 5 of 14 marks**. The examiner expects **8–10 distinct comparison parameters** with technical values.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Parameter-by-Parameter Comparison

### 2.1.1 Spatial Resolution (The Pixel Count)

**Spatial resolution** is the count of distinct pixels that can be displayed in each dimension (horizontal × vertical). It determines the **finest detail** the system can reproduce.

**SDTV (480i, NTSC):**
- Horizontal pixels: **640**
- Vertical active lines: **480**
- Total pixels per frame: $640 \times 480 = 307{,}200$

**SDTV (576i, PAL):**
- Horizontal pixels: **720**
- Vertical active lines: **576**
- Total pixels per frame: $720 \times 576 = 414{,}720$

**HDTV (720p):**
- Horizontal pixels: **1280**
- Vertical active lines: **720**
- Total pixels per frame: $1280 \times 720 = 921{,}600$

**HDTV (1080p) — "Full HD":**
- Horizontal pixels: **1920**
- Vertical active lines: **1080**
- Total pixels per frame: $1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600$

> **Why it matters:** 1080p has **2.25× more pixels** than 720p, and **6.75× more pixels** than 480i. More pixels = finer detail = sharper, more realistic images.

### 2.1.2 Aspect Ratio — Shape of the Frame

**Aspect ratio (AR)** is the ratio of the **width** to the **height** of the displayed image.

$$AR = \frac{W_{pixels}}{H_{pixels}}$$

| System | Aspect Ratio | Decimal | Movie Equivalent |
|--------|--------------|---------|------------------|
| SDTV | 4:3 | 1.33:1 | Academy / Old TV |
| HDTV | 16:9 | 1.78:1 | Modern Cinema / Streaming |

> **The "Why" Behind 16:9:** Psychophysical studies (notably by **Dr. Kerns Powers** in 1984) found that 16:9 is the **compromise aspect ratio** that minimizes the letterboxing (black bars) required for displaying films of all major cinematic aspect ratios (1.85:1, 2.35:1, etc.) on a single screen.

### 2.1.3 Scanning Method — Interlaced vs. Progressive

- **Interlaced (i):** Each frame is split into two **fields** — **odd-numbered lines** (Field 1) and **even-numbered lines** (Field 2). These are transmitted sequentially, exploiting the **persistence of vision** to fool the eye into seeing a full frame.
  - Used in: 480i, 576i, 1080i
  - **Disadvantage:** "**Interline twitter**" (flicker on horizontal edges) and **comb artifacts** during motion.

- **Progressive (p):** The entire frame is scanned line-by-line, top to bottom, in a single pass.
  - Used in: 720p, 1080p
  - **Advantage:** No flicker, cleaner motion, better for stills and computer displays.

> **KTU Insight:** The suffix `i` in `1080i` means **1080 lines, interlaced, 60 fields/sec** = **30 full frames/sec**. The suffix `p` in `1080p` means **1080 lines, progressive, 60 full frames/sec**. Note the frame-rate difference: 1080i60 is **30 fps**, while 1080p60 is **60 fps**.

### 2.1.4 Bandwidth & Data Rate

The **uncompressed digital data rate** of a video stream is given by:

$$R_{bits} = W \times H \times f_{frame} \times C_{depth} \times N_{channels}$$

Where:
- $W \times H$ = Total pixels per frame
- $f_{frame}$ = Frame rate (fps)
- $C_{depth}$ = Color depth (bits per channel)
- $N_{channels}$ = Number of color channels (typically 3: R, G, B, or Y, Cb, Cr with 4:2:2 subsampling)

**Example Calculation (1080p60, 4:2:2, 10-bit):**
$$R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 10 \times 2 = 2.49 \text{ Gbps}$$

**SDTV Example (480i30, 4:2:2, 8-bit):**
$$R = 720 \times 480 \times 30 \times 8 \times 2 = 166 \text{ Mbps}$$

This **15× bandwidth gap** is why SDTV could be broadcast over analog RF channels, while HDTV **mandates digital compression** (MPEG-2 for ATSC, H.264/HEVC for DVB-T2 / ATSC 3.0).

### 2.1.5 Colorimetry — BT.601 vs. BT.709

| Parameter | SDTV (BT.601) | HDTV (BT.709) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Primaries (R, G, B)** | SMPTE-C | Rec. 709 |
| **White Point** | D65 (x=0.3127, y=0.3290) | D65 (same) |
| **Gamma (Transfer Function)** | 0.45 (CRT-derived) | 0.45 (same formula) |
| **Luminance Equation** | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | $Y = 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B$ |
| **Color Gamut** | Smaller (~35% of CIE 1931) | Larger (~72% of CIE 1931) |

> **Engineering Implication:** The BT.709 luminance coefficients are "**flatter**" — the green channel carries more of the perceived brightness weight, matching modern human visual perception more accurately. The wider color gamut of HDTV means **more saturated, lifelike colors**, especially in reds and cyans.

### 2.1.6 Audio — From Stereo to Surround

| Feature | SDTV (Analog) | HDTV (Digital) |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Channels** | 2.0 (Stereo) | 5.1 / 7.1 Surround |
| **Codec** | Analog FM modulation | Dolby Digital (AC-3), Dolby TrueHD, DTS-HD |
| **Bitrate** | N/A (analog) | 384–640 kbps (AC-3) |
| **Sample Rate** | N/A | 48 kHz (standard) |
| **Bit Depth** | N/A | 16/24-bit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Note:** HDTV's 5.1 surround adds a dedicated **Low-Frequency Effects (LFE)** channel (the ".1") — typically a subwoofer for 20–120 Hz bass, crucial for cinema-style immersion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 5 formulas and the table below — they account for ~80% of numerical questions on this topic in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_{px} = W \times H$ | Total pixels per frame | pixels |
| 2 | $AR = W / H$ | Aspect Ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_{frame} = f_{field} / 2$ (interlaced) | Frame rate from field rate | fps |
| 4 | $R_{uncomp} = W \times H \times f \times b \times n$ | Uncompressed bit rate | bits/sec |
| 5 | $CR = R_{uncomp} / R_{comp}$ | Compression Ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_{data} = R \times T$ | Total data volume for duration T | bits |
| 7 | $TVL \approx 0.75 \times H$ | Approx. horizontal TV lines from vertical resolution | TVL |
| 8 | $T_{line} = 1 / (H \times f_{frame})$ | Time per scan line | seconds |
| 9 | $f_{Nyquist} = R_{px} / 2$ | Minimum sampling rate (pixels per second) | samples/sec |
| 10 | $BW_{video} \propto N_{px} \times f_{frame}$ | Bandwidth ∝ pixel-rate | Hz |

> **Where:** $W$ = width, $H$ = height, $f$ = frame rate, $b$ = bit depth per sample, $n$ = number of samples per pixel (1 for 4:2:0, 2 for 4:2:2, 3 for 4:4:4), $R_{px}$ = pixel clock rate.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SDTV → HDTV transition is not just a "sharper picture" — it has profound engineering consequences:

1. **Broadcast Infrastructure:** HDTV required replacement of analog transmitters with digital OFDM-based systems (DVB-T, ATSC, ISDB-T), enabling **Single Frequency Networks (SFN)** and **spectral efficiency** gains of ~3–4×.
2. **Storage & Streaming:** The jump from SD (DVD = 4.7 GB / 2 hr movie) to HD (Blu-ray = 25–50 GB / 2 hr movie) drove the development of **H.264/AVC** and later **HEVC (H.265)** codecs, which achieve the same quality at **half the bitrate** of MPEG-2.
3. **Display Manufacturing:** HDTV's 1920×1080 standard became the baseline for LCD, OLED, and Plasma panels, eventually displacing the bulky **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)** that was physically incapable of reliably displaying more than ~480i in consumer sizes.
4. **Content Production:** Film and TV cameras, editing systems (e.g., Avid, DaVinci Resolve), and graphics pipelines all had to be upgraded to handle **1080p60** and beyond — fueling the digital cinema revolution.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pixel Count Comparison (SDTV vs. HDTV)

### Problem Statement
A KTU ESE question states: *"Calculate the total number of pixels in one frame of 480i SDTV and 1080p HDTV. How many times more detailed is HDTV?"*

### Step 1: Identify the Resolution Parameters
For **SDTV (480i, NTSC Rec. 601):**
- Active horizontal pixels: $W_{SD} = 720$
- Active vertical lines: $H_{SD} = 480$
- (Note: 480i is often quoted as 640×480 for square pixels, but Rec. 601 uses 720×480 with rectangular pixels of DAR 4:3 or 16:9. We will use the **Rec. 601 native** value of 720×480.)

For **HDTV (1080p, Rec. 709):**
- Active horizontal pixels: $W_{HD} = 1920$
- Active vertical lines: $H_{HD} = 1080$

### Step 2: Calculate Total Pixels per Frame (SDTV)

$$N_{SD} = W_{SD} \times H_{SD}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N_{SD} = 720 \times 480$$

$$N_{SD} = 345{,}600 \text{ pixels/frame}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Pixels per Frame (HDTV)

$$N_{HD} = W_{HD} \times H_{HD}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N_{HD} = 1920 \times 1080$$

$$N_{HD} = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels/frame}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Detail Ratio (How Many Times More Detailed?)

$$R_{detail} = \frac{N_{HD}}{N_{SD}}$$

$$R_{detail} = \frac{2{,}073{,}600}{345{,}600}$$

$$R_{detail} = 6.0$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
HDTV (1080p) has **6× more pixels** per frame than SDTV (480i). In other words, the HDTV frame contains **six times** the spatial information, which is why fine details like text, hair strands, and distant objects are clearly visible on HDTV but blurry on SDTV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative SDTV calculation** (if the question uses 640×480 square-pixel convention): $N_{SD} = 640 \times 480 = 307{,}200$ pixels, giving a ratio of $\frac{2{,}073{,}600}{307{,}200} = 6.75\times$. **Always state the assumption** (Rec. 601 vs. square-pixel) at the start of your answer.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Uncompressed Bit Rate Comparison

### Problem Statement
*"Calculate the uncompressed bit rate of a 1080p60 HDTV signal (4:2:2 chroma subsampling, 10-bit per sample) and a 480i30 SDTV signal (4:2:2, 8-bit). By what factor does HDTV's data rate exceed SDTV's?"*

### Step 1: State the General Formula

$$R_{bits} = W \times H \times f_{frame} \times N_{samples/px} \times C_{depth}$$

Where:
- $N_{samples/px}$ = number of samples per pixel (4:2:2 = 2, 4:2:0 = 1.5, 4:4:4 = 3)
- $C_{depth}$ = bit depth per sample

### Step 2: HDTV Calculation
Given: $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $f_{frame} = 60$ fps, $N_{samples/px} = 2$ (4:2:2), $C_{depth} = 10$ bits

$$R_{HD} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 2 \times 10$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$R_{HD} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 20$$

$$R_{HD} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 1200$$

$$R_{HD} = 2{,}488{,}320{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}$$

$$R_{HD} \approx 2.49 \text{ Gbps}$$

### Step 3: SDTV Calculation
Given: $W = 720$, $H = 480$, $f_{frame} = 30$ fps (since 480i60 = 60 fields/s = 30 frames/s), $N_{samples/px} = 2$, $C_{depth} = 8$ bits

$$R_{SD} = 720 \times 480 \times 30 \times 2 \times 8$$

Step-by-step:

$$R_{SD} = 345{,}600 \times 480$$

$$R_{SD} = 165{,}888{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}$$

$$R_{SD} \approx 165.9 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Data Rate Ratio

$$R_{ratio} = \frac{R_{HD}}{R_{SD}} = \frac{2{,}488{,}320{,}000}{165{,}888{,}000}$$

$$R_{ratio} = 15.0$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
HDTV (1080p60, 4:2:2, 10-bit) requires **15× more bandwidth** than SDTV (480i30, 4:2:2, 8-bit) in uncompressed form. This is why:
- SDTV could be carried by **analog 6/7/8 MHz RF channels**
- HDTV **absolutely requires** digital compression (MPEG-2 at ~15–20 Mbps, or H.264 at ~8–12 Mbps for similar perceptual quality)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Display Area Ratio (Why 16:9 Looks Wider)

### Problem Statement
*"Compare the displayable area of a 16:9 HDTV screen to a 4:3 SDTV screen of the same diagonal size (e.g., 42 inches). Express the area gain as a percentage."*

### Step 1: Define Diagonal-to-Side Relationship
For a screen with diagonal $D$ and aspect ratio $AR$:

$$W = D \cdot \frac{AR}{\sqrt{1 + AR^2}}$$

$$H = D \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + AR^2}}$$

### Step 2: SDTV (4:3) — Diagonal = 42 inches
$AR_{SD} = 4/3 = 1.333$

$$W_{SD} = 42 \cdot \frac{1.333}{\sqrt{1 + 1.333^2}} = 42 \cdot \frac{1.333}{\sqrt{2.778}} = 42 \cdot \frac{1.333}{1.667}$$

$$W_{SD} = 42 \times 0.7997 \approx 33.59 \text{ inches}$$

$$H_{SD} = 42 \cdot \frac{1}{1.667} \approx 25.20 \text{ inches}$$

$$A_{SD} = W_{SD} \times H_{SD} = 33.59 \times 25.20 \approx 846.5 \text{ in}^2$$

### Step 3: HDTV (16:9) — Diagonal = 42 inches
$AR_{HD} = 16/9 = 1.778$

$$W_{HD} = 42 \cdot \frac{1.778}{\sqrt{1 + 1.778^2}} = 42 \cdot \frac{1.778}{\sqrt{4.160}} = 42 \cdot \frac{1.778}{2.040}$$

$$W_{HD} = 42 \times 0.8715 \approx 36.60 \text{ inches}$$

$$H_{HD} = 42 \cdot \frac{1}{2.040} \approx 20.59 \text{ inches}$$

$$A_{HD} = W_{HD} \times H_{HD} = 36.60 \times 20.59 \approx 753.6 \text{ in}^2$$

### Step 4: Area Comparison

$$\Delta A = A_{SD} - A_{HD} = 846.5 - 753.6 = 92.9 \text{ in}^2$$

Wait — this shows SDTV has **more area** for the same diagonal. The correct insight is about **width gain**:

$$\Delta W = W_{HD} - W_{SD} = 36.60 - 33.59 = 3.01 \text{ inches}$$

$$\Delta H = H_{SD} - H_{HD} = 25.20 - 20.59 = 4.61 \text{ inches}$$

### Step 5: Final Interpretation
For the same diagonal, a 16:9 HDTV is **3.01 inches wider** and **4.61 inches shorter** than a 4:3 SDTV. The screen is **wider relative to its height** — which matches the human **horizontal field of view** (~200° combined, ~120° binocular). This is why 16:9 feels "**cinematic**" — it mimics the horizontal dominance of human vision.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If the question asks "which has more area", the answer is **4:3 has more square inches for the same diagonal**. If the question asks "which looks wider", the answer is **16:9** (it has a wider aspect ratio). Read the question carefully.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Channel Bandwidth (Compressed)

### Problem Statement
*"An HDTV channel using H.264/AVC compression at 12 Mbps achieves the same perceptual quality as an SDTV channel using MPEG-2 at 4 Mbps. If an 8 MHz UHF channel is allocated, how many SDTV and how many HDTV programs can be carried using a single frequency network?"*

### Step 1: Number of SDTV Programs

$$N_{SD} = \frac{BW_{channel}}{R_{SD}} = \frac{8 \text{ MHz (as bit-rate equivalent)}}{4 \text{ Mbps}}$$

Using a typical DVB-T capacity of ~24 Mbps in an 8 MHz channel (64-QAM, 2/3 FEC, 1/4 guard):

$$N_{SD} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ SDTV programs}$$

### Step 2: Number of HDTV Programs

$$N_{HD} = \frac{24 \text{ Mbps}}{12 \text{ Mbps}} = 2 \text{ HDTV programs}$$

### Step 3: Compression Efficiency Ratio

$$CR_{H.264} = \frac{R_{uncomp,HD}}{R_{comp,HD}} = \frac{2488 \text{ Mbps}}{12 \text{ Mbps}} \approx 207:1$$

$$CR_{MPEG-2} = \frac{R_{uncomp,SD}}{R_{comp,SD}} = \frac{166 \text{ Mbps}}{4 \text{ Mbps}} \approx 41:1$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
H.264/AVC provides roughly **5× better compression** than MPEG-2 for equivalent perceptual quality. This is the technological enabler that made HDTV broadcast over the same 6/7/8 MHz terrestrial channels feasible.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Engineering Metrics

| Metric | SDTV (480i) | HDTV (1080p) | Ratio (HD:SD) |
|--------|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| Pixels/frame | 345,600 | 2,073,600 | **6.0×** |
| Frame rate (max) | 30 fps | 60 fps | **2.0×** |
| Pixel rate (px/sec) | 10.37 M | 124.42 M | **12.0×** |
| Uncompressed bit rate (4:2:2) | 166 Mbps | 2.49 Gbps | **15.0×** |
| Compressed bit rate (typical) | 3–6 Mbps | 12–20 Mbps | **~3–4×** |
| Aspect ratio width/height | 1.33 | 1.78 | 1.34× |
| Color bits/channel | 8 | 8/10 | 1.0–1.25× |
| Audio channels | 2 | 6 (5.1) | 3× |
| Required storage (2 hr movie) | ~5 GB | ~22–40 GB | **~5–8×** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **uncompressed ratio (15×)** is far higher than the **compressed ratio (3–4×)** because compression algorithms exploit the redundancy in the increased pixel count more effectively — the larger the frame, the more spatial and temporal correlation exists to compress.


> **Key HDTV-specific blocks (not present in SDTV):**
> - **De-interlacing Engine** (converts 1080i → 1080p for display)
> - **Scaler** (up-converts legacy SD content to fill the 16:9 HD screen)
> - **H.264/HEVC Decoder** (replaces MPEG-2)
> - **Surround Sound Processor** (replaces stereo demodulator)
> - **BT.709 Color Space Converter** (replaces BT.601)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is meant by 'progressive scanning' in HDTV? How is it different from the interlaced scanning used in SDTV?"**

**Model Answer:**

- **Progressive scanning** displays all lines of a frame sequentially in a single pass, from top to bottom, in one scan cycle. *(1 mark)*
- **Interlaced scanning** (used in SDTV 480i/576i) splits each frame into two fields — odd lines (Field 1) and even lines (Field 2) — which are displayed alternately. *(1 mark)*
- **Differences:** *(1 mark)*
  - Progressive: full frame per scan, no flicker, better for motion and stills
  - Interlaced: half frame per scan, can show flicker (interline twitter) and comb artifacts on motion, but reduces bandwidth by half

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (with KTU-Style Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare SDTV and HDTV with respect to **spatial resolution, aspect ratio, scanning method, and color standard**. State the ITU recommendations applicable to each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 1080p HDTV signal is sampled at 4:2:2 chroma subsampling with 10-bit color depth at 60 fps. Calculate: *(7 marks)*
   (i) Total active pixels per frame  
   (ii) Uncompressed bit rate in Mbps  
   (iii) If the same content is compressed using H.264/AVC to 15 Mbps, calculate the compression ratio and the storage required for a 2-hour movie in GB.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**1. Spatial Resolution (2 marks):**
- SDTV: 720 × 480 (NTSC) or 720 × 576 (PAL) = up to **414,720 pixels/frame**
- HDTV: 1280 × 720 (720p) or 1920 × 1080 (1080p) = up to **2,073,600 pixels/frame**
- HDTV delivers **~5× to 6× more spatial information** than SDTV.

**2. Aspect Ratio (2 marks):**
- SDTV: **4:3** (1.33:1) — square-ish frame
- HDTV: **16:9** (1.78:1) — widescreen, cinematic format
- 16:9 is closer to the human horizontal field of view, reducing letterboxing for films.

**3. Scanning Method (2 marks):**
- SDTV: **Interlaced** (suffix `i`) — odd/even fields alternate, e.g., 480i60 = 60 fields/s = 30 frames/s
- HDTV: **Progressive** (`p`) or Interlaced (`i`) — e.g., 1080p60 = 60 full frames/s; 720p60 = 60 full frames/s
- Progressive eliminates interline twitter and improves motion rendition.

**4. Color Standard (1 mark):**
- SDTV: **ITU-R BT.601** (Rec. 601) — SMPTE-C primaries
- HDTV: **ITU-R BT.709** (Rec. 709) — wider gamut primaries, flatter luminance coefficients

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$, $f = 60$ fps
- 4:2:2 → $N_{samples/px} = 2$ (1 luma + 2 chroma samples, but each chroma is half-rate → equivalent 2 samples/pixel)
- $C_{depth} = 10$ bits/sample
- Compressed rate = 15 Mbps
- Duration $T = 2$ hours = 7200 seconds

**(i) Active Pixels per Frame:**

$$N_{px} = 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Uncompressed Bit Rate:**

$$R_{uncomp} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 2 \times 10$$

$$R_{uncomp} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 1200 = 2{,}488{,}320{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

$$R_{uncomp} = 2488.32 \text{ Mbps} \approx 2.49 \text{ Gbps} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**(iii) Compression Ratio and Storage:**

$$CR = \frac{R_{uncomp}}{R_{comp}} = \frac{2488.32 \text{ Mbps}}{15 \text{ Mbps}} \approx 165.9 : 1 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Storage Volume:**

$$V = R_{comp} \times T = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 7200 \text{ s} = 1.08 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits}$$

$$V = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{11}}{8} = 1.35 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} = 13.5 \text{ GB} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting chroma subsampling factor** (4:2:2 = 2 samples/px, not 3). If you use 3 samples/px, you over-calculate by 50%. Deduct **2 marks**.
> 2. **Converting Mbps → MB incorrectly.** $1 \text{ MB} = 8 \text{ Mb}$, so divide by 8 after multiplying by time. Don't just write "$15 \times 7200 = 108000$ MB" without the b-to-B conversion. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Frame rate confusion for 1080i vs. 1080p.** 1080i60 = 30 frames/s, 1080p60 = 60 frames/s. Read carefully whether the question says `i` or `p`. Deduct **2 marks** for confusion.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **interlaced scanning** technique used in SDTV. With a neat diagram, describe the **two-field** structure and derive the relationship between field rate and frame rate. Why was interlacing chosen for broadcast TV? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A broadcaster replaces an SDTV (576i, 4:2:2, 8-bit) channel with an HDTV (1080i, 4:2:2, 8-bit) channel. Both use MPEG-2 compression at the same perceptual quality, requiring approximately **3× more bits** for HDTV. If the original SDTV channel occupied **6 MHz of RF bandwidth** with analog modulation, calculate: *(7 marks)*
   (i) The compressed bit rate of the SDTV MPEG-2 stream (assume **5 Mbps**)  
   (ii) The compressed bit rate required for the HDTV stream  
   (iii) The minimum RF bandwidth needed for the HDTV digital channel, assuming **4 bits/sec per Hz** spectral efficiency. Comment on whether the existing 6 MHz allocation is sufficient.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**1. Interlaced Scanning Concept (2 marks):**
Interlaced scanning is a technique in which each complete frame (still image) is divided into **two half-frames called fields**. The first field (Field 1 or "odd field") contains all odd-numbered scan lines (1, 3, 5, ..., 575 for 576i). The second field (Field 2 or "even field") contains all even-numbered scan lines (2, 4, 6, ..., 576). These two fields are transmitted sequentially in time, one after the other, with the eye's persistence of vision fusing them into a single perceived frame.

**Diagram (description in text for 1 mark):**
> *Field 1 (Odd):* Line 1 → Line 3 → Line 5 → ... → Line 575 → Flyback to top
> *Field 2 (Even):* Line 2 → Line 4 → Line 6 → ... → Line 576 → Flyback to top
> *(This sequence repeats 25 (PAL) or 30 (NTSC) times per second to display 50 or 60 fields/s respectively.)*

**2. Frame Rate vs. Field Rate Relationship (2 marks):**
For any interlaced system:

$$f_{frame} = \frac{f_{field}}{2}$$

**Derivation:**
A complete frame consists of 2 fields. If the field rate is $f_{field}$ fields per second, then the number of *pairs* of fields (i.e., complete frames) per second is:

$$f_{frame} = \frac{\text{Number of fields per second}}{\text{Fields per frame}} = \frac{f_{field}}{2}$$

**Examples:**
- NTSC: $f_{field} = 60$ Hz → $f_{frame} = 30$ fps
- PAL: $f_{field} = 50$ Hz → $f_{frame} = 25$ fps

**3. Why Interlacing Was Chosen (2 marks):**
- **Bandwidth reduction:** Interlacing effectively **halves the bandwidth** required for a given perceived frame rate because only half the lines are transmitted per field. This was critical for fitting video into the scarce **6/7/8 MHz RF channels** allocated to analog broadcasters in the 20th century.
- **Flicker reduction:** The high field rate (50/60 Hz) reduces large-area flicker perceived by the human eye, even though the frame rate is lower (25/30 fps).
- **Compatibility with CRT displays:** The scanning electron beam of a CRT could be modulated to alternate between odd and even fields with a simple vertical-deflection offset — no digital memory was required.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:**
- SDTV: 576i, 4:2:2, 8-bit, MPEG-2 → **5 Mbps**
- HDTV: 1080i, 4:2:2, 8-bit, MPEG-2 → **3× bit rate of SDTV**
- SDTV original analog RF bandwidth = 6 MHz
- Spectral efficiency for digital = **4 bits/sec per Hz**

**(i) Compressed bit rate of SDTV MPEG-2 stream:**

$$R_{SD,comp} = 5 \text{ Mbps} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Compressed bit rate required for HDTV:**

$$R_{HD,comp} = 3 \times R_{SD,comp} = 3 \times 5 = 15 \text{ Mbps} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Minimum RF bandwidth for HDTV digital channel:**

Using the relation $BW = \frac{R_{comp}}{\eta}$ where $\eta$ is spectral efficiency:

$$BW_{HD} = \frac{R_{HD,comp}}{\eta} = \frac{15 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}}{4 \text{ bits/s/Hz}}$$

$$BW_{HD} = 3.75 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 3.75 \text{ MHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Commentary (1 mark):**
Since the calculated minimum RF bandwidth of **3.75 MHz** is **less than** the original 6 MHz analog allocation, the existing 6 MHz channel is **sufficient** to carry a 15 Mbps HDTV digital stream. In fact, with margin for error-correction (FEC) and transport overhead, a 6 MHz channel can typically carry 19.4 Mbps (ATSC 8-VSB), which is more than enough for 15 Mbps HDTV. This is precisely why broadcasters could **transition from SDTV to HDTV** using the **same RF channel allocations** — only the modulation and compression schemes needed to change.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Frame rate trap for 1080i:** Students often write $f_{frame} = 60$ fps for 1080i. **Correct:** 1080i60 means **60 fields/s = 30 fps**. If the question says "1080i", use 30 fps unless explicitly told otherwise. Deduct **2 marks** if confused.
> 2. **Spectral efficiency units:** The given $\eta = 4$ bits/s/Hz is equivalent to $4$ bits/second/Hertz. Do not confuse it with the symbol rate (Baud). Deduct **1 mark** if the formula is written incorrectly.
> 3. **Saying "the 6 MHz channel is not enough":** This is the most common wrong conclusion. Always do the math — 3.75 MHz < 6 MHz, so the existing allocation **is** sufficient. Deduct **2 marks** for an incorrect conclusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (save this for the night before the exam):**

- ⭐ **SDTV resolutions:** **480i** (NTSC, 720×480) and **576i** (PAL, 720×576). Both are **interlaced, 4:3**.
- ⭐ **HDTV resolutions:** **720p** (1280×720) and **1080i/1080p** (1920×1080). Mostly **16:9 widescreen**.
- ⭐ **Pixel-count landmark numbers:** 480i ≈ **345k**, 720p ≈ **922k**, 1080p ≈ **2.07M** pixels/frame. 1080p = **6× SDTV**.
- ⭐ **Aspect ratio:** SD = **4:3 (1.33)**, HD = **16:9 (1.78)**. 16:9 minimizes letterboxing.
- ⭐ **Frame vs Field rate:** 1080i60 = **30 fps** (60 fields), 1080p60 = **60 fps**. Don't confuse!
- ⭐ **ITU Standards:** SDTV → **ITU-R BT.601**, HDTV → **ITU-R BT.709**, UHD/4K → **ITU-R BT.2020**.
- ⭐ **Bandwidth:** SDTV analog = 6 MHz; SDTV digital = 3–6 Mbps (MPEG-2); HDTV digital = 12–20 Mbps (H.264/HEVC).
- ⭐ **Uncompressed bit rate formula:** $R = W \times H \times f \times N_{samples/px} \times C_{depth}$. **Always include chroma subsampling factor (4:2:2 = 2, 4:2:0 = 1.5, 4:4:4 = 3).**
- ⭐ **Color depth:** SDTV = 8-bit; HDTV = 8/10-bit; UHD = 10/12-bit per channel.
- ⭐ **Audio:** SDTV = 2.0 stereo; HDTV = 5.1/7.1 surround (Dolby AC-3, Dolby TrueHD, DTS-HD).
- ⭐ **Compression standards:** SDTV used **MPEG-2**; HDTV uses **H.264/AVC** (and newer **HEVC/H.265** for 4K).
- ⭐ **H.264 vs MPEG-2 efficiency:** H.264 is ~**2× more efficient** than MPEG-2 (some estimates up to 4× for high motion).
- ⭐ **Scan-line-time formula:** $T_{line} = 1 / (H \times f_{frame})$. Useful for deriving horizontal sync frequencies.
- ⭐ **KTU Board Mantra:** "**More pixels + wider screen + higher frame rate + digital compression = HDTV**." Memorize this hierarchy.
- ⭐ **Real-world data point:** A 2-hour 1080p movie at 15 Mbps ≈ **13.5 GB** (≈ 1 Blu-ray disc layer).
- ⭐ **Luminance coefficients differ:** BT.601 → 0.299/0.587/0.114; BT.709 → **0.2126/0.7152/0.0722** (more weight to green).
- ⭐ **Display technology shift:** SDTV = CRT (bulky, 4:3); HDTV = LCD/LED/OLED (flat, 16:9). CRTs physically could not reliably display 1080p at consumer sizes.

> **Final Exam Mantra (write it on your hand if you must):**
> *"SDTV is 480i/576i, 4:3, BT.601, MPEG-2, stereo, 6 MHz analog. HDTV is 720p/1080i/1080p, 16:9, BT.709, H.264, 5.1, digital compressed. HDTV has 6× the pixels and ~15× the uncompressed data rate of SDTV."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Discrete Cosine Transforms (DCT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERPRISE ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Review of Discrete Cosine Transforms (DCT) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** The **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** is a real-valued, orthogonal transform that converts a finite sequence of equally-spaced real data points into a sum of cosine functions oscillating at different frequencies. It is the most widely used transform in modern **entertainment electronics** for lossy compression of audio, image, and video signals. For a one-dimensional input sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, the **DCT-II** (the de-facto standard, also called simply "the DCT") is mathematically defined as: $$X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right], \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$ where the normalization constant $\alpha(k)$ is: $$\alpha(k) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}}, & k = 0 \\[6pt] \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{N}}, & k = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1 \end{cases}$$ The **Inverse Discrete Cosine Transform (IDCT)** reconstructs the original signal: $$x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \alpha(k)\, X(k) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right], \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Musical Chord Decomposition" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a piece of music playing in a room. Your ear hears a complex, blended sound. If you hand the same recording to a spectrum analyzer, it decomposes the sound into individual **pure notes** (sine/cosine waves) at different pitches, each with its own loudness. The DCT does exactly this for **any signal** — it expresses a complicated waveform as a weighted sum of pure cosine waves of increasing frequency. - The **first coefficient** $X(0)$ = the **DC component** → represents the average brightness/loudness. - Higher-index coefficients $X(k)$ → represent the **fine details, edges, and high-frequency texture**. - Most of the signal's energy collapses into a **few low-frequency coefficients** — this is the **"energy compaction" property** that makes DCT the heart of JPEG, MP3, and MPEG. ## 1.3 Why DCT over DFT? | Feature | DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform) | DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform) | |---|---|---| | Output type | **Complex** (real + imaginary) | **Purely real** | | Boundary handling | Imposes **periodic** extension → causes sharp discontinuities at block edges (Gibbs phenomenon) | Imposes **even (symmetric) extension** → smooth, continuous boundary | | Energy compaction | Moderate | **Excellent** (close to optimal Karhunen-Loève transform for correlated data) | | Real-time speed | Slightly slower (complex arithmetic) | **Faster** (only real multiplications) | | Compression efficiency | Good | **Superior** — industry standard | > [!NOTE] > The **even extension** property of DCT means when we wrap a signal around itself, the two halves meet smoothly (matching values AND matching slopes), eliminating the discontinuities that plague DFT. This is *the* reason DCT is preferred for **block-based compression** in entertainment systems. ## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics in Entertainment Codecs > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Entertainment-Electronics Metrics (highlighted for KTU exam):** > - **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** standard measure of reconstruction quality; typical broadcast quality $\geq 40$ **dB**. > - **Compression Ratio (CR):** ratio of original size to compressed size; JPEG typically 10:1 to 20:1. > - **Bit-rate:** measured in **bits per pixel (bpp)** for images, **kbps** for audio/video. > - **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** average squared pixel-wise difference. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Standard Variants of DCT DCT has eight variants; the four most relevant for entertainment electronics are: | Variant | Formula (Forward) | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **DCT-I** | $X(k) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{N-1}} \sum\limits_{n=0}^{N-1} \beta(n)\beta(k) x(n) \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n k}{N-1}\right)$ | Edge processing, spectral analysis | | **DCT-II** | $X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum\limits_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right)$ | **JPEG, MPEG, MP3, Dolby, HEVC** | | **DCT-III** (Inverse DCT-II) | $x(n) = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{N-1} \alpha(k) X(k) \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right)$ | IDCT in decoders | | **DCT-IV** | $X(k) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{N}} \sum\limits_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi(2n+1)(2k+1)}{4N}\right)$ | MDCT in modern audio codecs (MP3, AAC) | where $\beta(m) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ for $m=0$ or $m=N-1$, else $\beta(m) = 1$. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of DCT-II Operation The DCT-II decomposes an input signal through the following structured pipeline: 1. **Input Sampling:** Read $N$ samples $x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)$ from the digitized entertainment source (audio frame, image block, etc.). 2. **Even Symmetric Extension:** Conceptually extend the sequence to length $2N$ by mirroring: $x(-1)=x(0), x(-2)=x(1)$, etc. This **kills the boundary discontinuity** that DFT would suffer. 3. **Basis-Function Multiplication:** For each frequency index $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, compute the inner product of the input with the cosine basis vector $\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right)$. 4. **Normalization:** Multiply by $\alpha(k)$ so that the basis vectors are unit-energy (orthonormal). 5. **Output:** The vector $X(0), X(1), \ldots, X(N-1)$ represents the signal in the **frequency domain**. The $k^{th}$ coefficient gives the **amplitude** of the $k^{th}$ cosine wave needed to rebuild $x(n)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Why" matters (KTU favourite):** The even symmetric extension makes the **periodized** signal look like a pure cosine series (no imaginary sine terms arise), explaining why DCT is *real-valued* while DFT is *complex-valued*. The choice of frequency samples at the **half-step** $\frac{(2n+1)k}{2N}$ is precisely what makes the $N$ cosine vectors mutually orthogonal over $\{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$. ## 2.3 Two-Dimensional DCT (2D-DCT) for Image/Video Since entertainment content is fundamentally **2-D (images)** and **3-D (video = 2D + time)**, we need the 2D-DCT. It is **separable** — applied first row-wise, then column-wise: $$F(u,v) = \alpha(u)\,\alpha(v) \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2M}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right]$$ The **2D-IDCT** is: $$f(x,y) = \sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1} \alpha(u)\,\alpha(v)\, F(u,v) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2M}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right]$$ For an $8 \times 8$ block (the JPEG standard), $M = N = 8$ and the basis functions are $64$ discrete cosine "patterns" of increasing horizontal and vertical frequency. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Formula Table — must memorize for KTU ESE:** | # | Formula / Property | Expression | |---|---|---| | 1 | **DCT-II Forward** | $X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum\limits_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$ | | 2 | **IDCT (DCT-III)** | $x(n) = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{N-1} \alpha(k)\, X(k) \cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$ | | 3 | **Normalization constant** | $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{1/N}$ if $k=0$, else $\sqrt{2/N}$ | | 4 | **2D-DCT (JPEG)** | $F(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_x \sum_y f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2x+1)u}{16}\right]\cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2y+1)v}{16}\right]$ for $8\times8$ | | 5 | **Parseval's Theorem (energy conservation)** | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n)\vert^2 = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k)\vert^2$ | | 6 | **Energy Compaction Ratio** | $\text{ECR} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \vert X(k)\vert^2}{\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k)\vert^2} \times 100\%$ | | 7 | **PSNR (dB)** | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | | 8 | **MSE** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum (f(x,y)-\hat f(x,y))^2$ | | 9 | **Compression Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | | 10 | **Separability property** | $F(u,v) = \alpha(v) \sum_y \left[\alpha(u) \sum_x f(x,y)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2M}\right)\right]\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right)$ | | 11 | **Symmetry property** | $F(u,v) = F(-u,-v)$ for real $f$ | | 12 | **Periodicity of basis** | $\cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$ has effective period $2N$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **DCT in Industry — Where you'll find it (KTU application-based questions):** > - **JPEG** image compression — $8 \times 8$ block DCT > - **MPEG-1/2/4, H.264, H.265 (HEVC), AV1** — video compression standards > - **MP3, AAC, Dolby Digital, AC-3** — audio compression > - **DVD, Blu-ray, Streaming platforms (Netflix, YouTube, Hotstar)** — playback pipeline > - **Digital TV broadcasting (DVB)** — MPEG-2 video over the air > - **Speech codecs in VoIP, WhatsApp calls, Bluetooth headsets** --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the DCT-II Formula from the DFT of an Even-Extended Signal The cleanest derivation starts with a length-$N$ real sequence $x(n)$ and creates an **even-symmetric extension** of length $2N$: $$y(n) = \begin{cases} x(n), & 0 \le n \le N-1 \\ x(-n-1), & -N \le n \le -1 \end{cases}$$ This guarantees $y(n) = y(-n)$ (true even symmetry) and $y$ has period $2N$. **Step 1: Take the $2N$-point DFT of the extended sequence.** $$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{2N-1} y(n)\, e^{-j2\pi kn/(2N)} = \sum_{n=0}^{2N-1} y(n)\, e^{-j\pi kn/N}$$ **Step 2: Split the sum into two halves** (using $y(n)$ for $n \ge N$ and $y(n) = y(n-2N)$ for that range): $$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\pi kn/N} + \sum_{n=N}^{2N-1} y(n)\, e^{-j\pi kn/N}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the symmetry** $y(n) = y(2N - 1 - n) = x(2N-1-n)$ for $n=N,\ldots,2N-1$. Let $m = 2N-1-n$ so $m$ runs $N-1 \to 0$: $$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\pi kn/N} + \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m)\, e^{-j\pi k(2N-1-m)/N}$$ **Step 4: Simplify the second exponential.** $$e^{-j\pi k(2N-1-m)/N} = e^{-j2\pi k}\, e^{j\pi k/N}\, e^{j\pi km/N} = e^{j\pi k/N}\, e^{j\pi km/N}$$ because $e^{-j2\pi k} = 1$ for integer $k$. **Step 5: Combine the two sums.** $$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[e^{-j\pi kn/N} + e^{j\pi k/N} e^{j\pi kn/N}\right]$$ Factor $e^{j\pi k/(2N)}$: $$Y(k) = e^{j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[e^{-j\pi k(2n+1)/(2N)} + e^{j\pi k(2n+1)/(2N)}\right]$$ **Step 6: Apply Euler's identity** $\cos\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta}+e^{-j\theta}}{2}$: $$Y(k) = 2 e^{j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right]$$ **Step 7: Define the DCT as the real, scaled magnitude** (dropping the pure phase factor $e^{j\pi k/(2N)}$ which is a unit complex number): $$X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$$ where $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{1/N}$ for $k=0$ and $\sqrt{2/N}$ otherwise accounts for the factor of $2$ and energy normalization. **This is the canonical DCT-II.** ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 4-Point DCT Let $x = [8, 4, 8, 4]$ (a typical "striped" block in an image), $N=4$. **Compute the basis frequencies** for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$: $$C(k,n) = \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right] = \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{8}\right]$$ Let us build the matrix row by row. **Row $k=0$:** $\cos(0) = 1$ for all $n$. So $C(0,:) = [1, 1, 1, 1]$. **Row $k=1$:** $\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)}{8}\right]$ for $n=0,1,2,3$: $$\begin{aligned} n=0:&\ \cos(\pi/8) = \cos(22.5°) = 0.9239 \\ n=1:&\ \cos(3\pi/8) = \cos(67.5°) = 0.3827 \\ n=2:&\ \cos(5\pi/8) = \cos(112.5°) = -0.3827 \\ n=3:&\ \cos(7\pi/8) = \cos(157.5°) = -0.9239 \end{aligned}$$ **Row $k=2$:** $\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)}{4}\right] = \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi}{4}(2n+1)\right]$: $$\begin{aligned} n=0:&\ \cos(\pi/4) = 0.7071 \\ n=1:&\ \cos(3\pi/4) = -0.7071 \\ n=2:&\ \cos(5\pi/4) = -0.7071 \\ n=3:&\ \cos(7\pi/4) = 0.7071 \end{aligned}$$ **Row $k=3$:** $\cos\!\left[\frac{3\pi(2n+1)}{8}\right]$: $$\begin{aligned} n=0:&\ \cos(3\pi/8) = 0.3827 \\ n=1:&\ \cos(9\pi/8) = -0.9239 \\ n=2:&\ \cos(15\pi/8) = 0.9239 \\ n=3:&\ \cos(21\pi/8) = -0.3827 \end{aligned}$$ **Form the unnormalized DCT matrix** $C$: $$C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0.9239 & 0.3827 & -0.3827 & -0.9239 \\ 0.7071 & -0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0.7071 \\ 0.3827 & -0.9239 & 0.9239 & -0.3827 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Apply normalization** $\alpha(0) = 1/2$ and $\alpha(1)=\alpha(2)=\alpha(3) = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$. The **DCT matrix** is $T = D \cdot C$ where $D = \text{diag}(0.5,\, 0.7071,\, 0.7071,\, 0.7071)$: $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5000 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 & 0.5000 \\ 0.6533 & 0.2706 & -0.2706 & -0.6533 \\ 0.5000 & -0.5000 & -0.5000 & 0.5000 \\ 0.2706 & -0.6533 & 0.6533 & -0.2706 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute $X = T \cdot x^T$** for $x = [8, 4, 8, 4]$: $$\begin{aligned} X(0) &= 0.5(8) + 0.5(4) + 0.5(8) + 0.5(4) = 12.0 \\ X(1) &= 0.6533(8) + 0.2706(4) - 0.2706(8) - 0.6533(4) \\ &= 5.2264 + 1.0824 - 2.1648 - 2.6132 = 1.5308 \\ X(2) &= 0.5(8) - 0.5(4) - 0.5(8) + 0.5(4) = 0 \\ X(3) &= 0.2706(8) - 0.6533(4) + 0.6533(8) - 0.2706(4) \\ &= 2.1648 - 2.6132 + 5.2264 - 1.0824 = 3.6956 \end{aligned}$$ So $X = [12.0,\ 1.5308,\ 0,\ 3.6956]$. **Verification of energy compaction (Parseval's theorem):** Original energy: $\sum x^2 = 64+16+64+16 = 160$. DCT-domain energy: $144 + 2.343 + 0 + 13.66 = 160.003 \approx 160$. ✓ Notice $X(0)=12$ alone accounts for $144/160 = 90\%$ of the signal energy — perfect compaction for a constant-ish signal. ## 3.3 Reconstruction (IDCT) Verification $$\hat x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{3} \alpha(k) X(k) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{8}\right]$$ For $n=0$: $$\begin{aligned} \hat x(0) &= 0.5(12)(1) + 0.7071(1.5308)(0.9239) \\ &\quad + 0.7071(0)(0.7071) + 0.7071(3.6956)(0.3827) \\ &= 6 + 1.000 + 0 + 1.000 = 8.0 \end{aligned}$$ This exactly matches the input $x(0) = 8$. Similarly, $\hat x(1) = 4, \hat x(2) = 8, \hat x(3) = 4$. **Perfect reconstruction confirmed.** ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DCT Properties vs DFT | Property | DCT-II | DFT | |---|---|---| | **Real-valued output for real input** | ✓ Always | ✗ (complex output) | | **Energy compaction for correlated data** | Near-optimal (close to KLT) | Moderate | | **Boundary discontinuity** | Smooth (even extension) | Sharp (periodic wrap) | | **Computational cost** | $O(N \log N)$ (via FFT-like algorithms) | $O(N \log N)$ | | **Used in JPEG/MPEG/MP3** | ✓ Yes | ✗ No | | **Suitable for real-time broadcast** | ✓ Yes | Less so (due to complex arithmetic) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DCT Processing Pipeline in an Entertainment System The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates how DCT fits into a typical **JPEG image encoder** (the canonical entertainment-electronics application): ```mermaid flowchart TB src["Input Entertainment Source
Image Frame / Audio / Video"] rgb2yuv["Color Space Conversion
RGB to YCbCr
4:2:0 Chroma Subsampling"] block["Block Splitter
Divide into 8x8 pixel blocks"] dct["Forward DCT-II
8x8 Block to 64 Coefficients"] quant["Quantizer
Divide by Q-Table and Round"] zigzag["Zig-Zag Scan
Low-Freq First"] enc["Entropy Encoder
Huffman / Arithmetic"] store["Compressed Bitstream
JPEG .jpg / MPEG .mp4"] src --> rgb2yuv --> block --> dct --> quant --> zigzag --> enc --> store dec["Entropy Decoder"] dez["Inverse Zig-Zag"] deq["De-Quantizer
Multiply by Q-Table"] idct["Inverse DCT-III
64 Coefficients to 8x8 Block"] merge["Block Merger"] yuv2rgb["YCbCr to RGB"] out["Reconstructed Image / Video / Audio"] store -.Compressed Transmission.-> dec dec --> dez --> deq --> idct --> merge --> yuv2rgb --> out ``` ## 4.2 The 64 DCT Basis Functions for 8×8 Block The 2D-DCT of an $8\times 8$ image block can be visualized as a sum of $64$ "basis patterns" indexed by horizontal frequency $u$ and vertical frequency $v$. The pattern intensifies as $u$ or $v$ grows (top-left = DC average, bottom-right = highest-frequency checkerboard): ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BAS["8x8 DCT Basis Functions - Frequency Increases Right and Down"] dir1["DC Component u=0 v=0
Solid Gray Patch"] dir2["Horizontal Low u=1 v=0
Vertical Stripes"] dir3["Vertical Low u=0 v=1
Horizontal Stripes"] dir4["Diagonal u=1 v=1
Tilted Bands"] dir5["High-High u=7 v=7
Fine Checkerboard"] end dir1 --> dir2 --> dir3 --> dir4 --> dir5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** The above is rendered as a **block-level functional flow** because physically drawing all 64 basis functions would exceed Mermaid's safe syntax constraints. In KTU exams, you may draw **any 4** of the 64 patterns manually and label them by $(u,v)$ indices. ## 4.3 Energy Compaction Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB s["Natural Image Pixel Block
8x8 = 64 numbers"] d["DCT Output
64 Coefficients"] e["Energy Distribution
Top-Left Coefficients Dominant"] q["After Quantization
Many High-Freq Coefficients = 0"] r["Compressed Bitstream
Sparse Non-Zero Data"] s --> d --> e --> q --> r note["Most Energy in X(0,0)
DC coefficient = average brightness"] e -.- note ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Discrete Cosine Transform. List any four of its key properties.** **Model Answer:** The Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) of a finite real sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$ is defined as: $$X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right], \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ where $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{1/N}$ for $k=0$ and $\sqrt{2/N}$ otherwise. **Four Key Properties:** 1. **Real-valued output** for real-valued input. 2. **Energy compaction:** concentrates signal energy in low-frequency coefficients. 3. **Separability:** the 2D-DCT can be computed as two successive 1D-DCTs. 4. **Orthogonality:** the DCT basis functions are mutually orthogonal, enabling perfect reconstruction. 5. *(Optional 5th)* **Symmetry:** $F(u,v) = F(-u,-v)$ for real 2D signals. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for properties.)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare DCT and DFT. Why is DCT preferred in entertainment electronics compression?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | DFT | DCT | |---|---|---| | Output | Complex | Real | | Energy compaction | Moderate | Excellent | | Boundary handling | Periodic (sharp wrap) | Even-symmetric (smooth) | | Computational cost | Higher (complex arithmetic) | Lower (real arithmetic) | **DCT is preferred** in entertainment electronics (JPEG, MPEG, MP3) because: - It is **real-valued**, halving memory and computation. - Its **even-symmetric extension** removes block-edge discontinuities, eliminating the Gibbs phenomenon visible in DFT-based compression. - It provides **superior energy compaction**, meaning a few low-frequency coefficients can represent the entire signal — ideal for compression. **(3 marks.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks **Q. (a)** Derive the 1D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT-II) from the Discrete Fourier Transform applied to an even-symmetric extended sequence. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the 4-point DCT of the sequence $x(n) = \{6, 4, 6, 4\}$. Also compute and verify using the IDCT that reconstruction is exact. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.(a): Given real sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$, define an even-symmetric extension of length $2N$: $$y(n) = x(n) \text{ for } 0 \le n \le N-1, \quad y(-n-1) = x(n)$$ so that $y$ is even: $y(n) = y(-n)$. Compute the $2N$-point DFT: $$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{2N-1} y(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/(2N)}$$ Splitting into two halves and using $y(n) = y(2N-1-n)$ leads (after algebra shown in Section 3.1) to: $$Y(k) = 2 e^{j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right]$$ The DCT-II is defined as the real, magnitude-scaled version of $Y(k)$: $$\boxed{X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]}$$ where $\alpha(0) = \sqrt{1/N}$ and $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$ for $k \ne 0$. **Assumptions stated:** input $x(n)$ is real; sequence is even-extended; phase factor $e^{j\pi k/(2N)}$ has unit magnitude and is dropped without loss of generality. #### Model Solution for Q.(b): $N=4$, $x = [6, 4, 6, 4]$. Normalization: $\alpha(0) = 1/2$, $\alpha(1) = \alpha(2) = \alpha(3) = 1/\sqrt{2}$. DCT matrix $T$ (from Section 3.2): $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.6533 & 0.2706 & -0.2706 & -0.6533 \\ 0.5 & -0.5 & -0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.2706 & -0.6533 & 0.6533 & -0.2706 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Compute coefficients:** $$\begin{aligned} X(0) &= 0.5(6+4+6+4) = 10.0 \\ X(1) &= 0.6533(6) + 0.2706(4) - 0.2706(6) - 0.6533(4) \\ &= 3.9198 + 1.0824 - 1.6236 - 2.6132 = 0.7654 \\ X(2) &= 0.5(6) - 0.5(4) - 0.5(6) + 0.5(4) = 0 \\ X(3) &= 0.2706(6) - 0.6533(4) + 0.6533(6) - 0.2706(4) \\ &= 1.6236 - 2.6132 + 3.9198 - 1.0824 = 1.8478 \end{aligned}$$ So $X = [10.0,\ 0.7654,\ 0,\ 1.8478]$. **IDCT verification** — apply $\hat x = T^T \cdot X$ (since $T$ is orthogonal, $T^{-1} = T^T$): $$\begin{aligned} \hat x(0) &= 0.5(10) + 0.6533(0.7654) + 0.5(0) + 0.2706(1.8478) \\ &= 5 + 0.5 + 0 + 0.5 = 6.0 \quad \checkmark \\ \hat x(1) &= 0.5(10) + 0.2706(0.7654) - 0.5(0) - 0.6533(1.8478) \\ &= 5 + 0.2071 - 1.2071 = 4.0 \quad \checkmark \\ \hat x(2) &= 0.5(10) - 0.2706(0.7654) - 0.5(0) + 0.6533(1.8478) \\ &= 5 - 0.2071 + 1.2071 = 6.0 \quad \checkmark \\ \hat x(3) &= 0.5(10) - 0.6533(0.7654) + 0.5(0) - 0.2706(1.8478) \\ &= 5 - 0.5 - 0.5 = 4.0 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ **Reconstruction is exact** ($\hat x = x$). This demonstrates the perfect-reconstruction property of DCT. --- ### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks **Q. (a)** Explain the 2D Discrete Cosine Transform with the help of its mathematical expression. Discuss its application in JPEG image compression. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An $8 \times 8$ image block has a constant pixel value of $128$. Compute its 2D-DCT. Also calculate the PSNR if the reconstruction is perfect. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.(a): The 2D-DCT of an $M \times N$ image block $f(x,y)$ is: $$F(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2M}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right]$$ **Key features:** - **Separable:** $F(u,v) = \alpha(v) \sum_y \left[\alpha(u) \sum_x f(x,y) \cos(\cdot)\right] \cos(\cdot)$ — can be computed as row-DCTs followed by column-DCTs. - **Symmetric:** $F(u,v) = F(-u,-v)$ for real images. - **Energy Compaction:** Most energy concentrates in the upper-left low-frequency region. **JPEG Compression Pipeline:** 1. RGB image is converted to YCbCr and chroma-subsampled (4:2:0). 2. Each channel is split into $8 \times 8$ blocks (so $N=M=8$). 3. Pixel values are shifted by $-128$ to centre around zero. 4. 2D-DCT is applied to each block, producing $64$ coefficients. 5. Coefficients are **quantized** using the standard JPEG quantization table (zig-zag ordered). 6. Quantized values are **Huffman/entropy encoded** into the final bitstream. 7. At the decoder, the inverse operations (IDCT, de-quantize, de-zig-zag) reconstruct the image. **Why DCT works for JPEG:** Natural images have strong low-frequency content (smooth regions, gradients). The 2D-DCT packs this energy into a few coefficients, so quantization can zero out the high-frequency ones (which the human eye is less sensitive to) with minimal perceptual loss. #### Model Solution for Q.(b): For an $8 \times 8$ block with every pixel $f(x,y) = 128$: **Step 1 — Compute the constant term:** $F(0,0)$: $$F(0,0) = \alpha(0)\alpha(0) \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} 128 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{8} \cdot 128 \cdot 64 = 128$$ **Step 2 — All other coefficients:** For $u > 0$ OR $v > 0$, the cosine terms integrate to zero over a uniform block: $$F(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \cdot 128 \sum_{x=0}^{7}\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{16}\right] \sum_{y=0}^{7}\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{16}\right]$$ Each sum is a known identity equal to **zero** for $u, v \in \{1,\ldots,7\}$ (orthogonality of the DCT basis with the constant function). So: $$\boxed{F(0,0) = 128, \quad F(u,v) = 0 \text{ for } (u,v) \ne (0,0)}$$ **Step 3 — Reconstruct via IDCT:** $$f(x,y) = \sum_{u=0}^{7}\sum_{v=0}^{7} \alpha(u)\alpha(v) F(u,v) \cos(\cdot)\cos(\cdot)$$ Only the $(0,0)$ term contributes: $$f(x,y) = \frac{1}{8}\cdot\frac{1}{8}\cdot 128 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 128$$ **Reconstruction is exact — every pixel becomes 128 again.** **Step 4 — PSNR calculation:** With perfect reconstruction, MSE = 0. The PSNR formula is $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}(255^2 / \text{MSE})$. Since MSE $\to 0$, PSNR $\to \infty$ dB. In practice, perfect reconstruction means **infinite PSNR** (lossless for constant blocks). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting normalization constants** $\alpha(k)$ in numerical problems — KTU strictly awards $1$ mark for each correctly applied $\alpha(0) = 1/N$ and $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$. > 2. **Confusing DCT-II with DCT-I or DCT-IV** — examiners may not give marks for the wrong formula variant unless explicitly asked. > 3. **Skipping the "even-symmetric extension" step** in derivation questions — this is the *key conceptual step* worth $2$ marks. > 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (real input, periodic extension, etc.). > 5. **Miscalculating PSNR** by using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{10}$ (PSNR for images is always $10\log_{10}$ since amplitude, not power, is being compared). > 6. **In 2D-DCT problems, forgetting separability** — failing to mention that 2D-DCT = row DCT + column DCT loses $1$ mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):** - ✅ **DCT-II Formula:** $X(k) = \alpha(k)\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$ — **memorize verbatim.** - ✅ **Normalization:** $\alpha(0) = 1/\sqrt{N}$, $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$ for $k\ne 0$. Equivalently, in matrix form: $\alpha(0) = 1/N$, $\alpha(k\ne 0) = \sqrt{2/N}$. - ✅ **IDCT uses the same formula** (DCT-III is the inverse of DCT-II with the same kernel). - ✅ **DCT is real-valued; DFT is complex.** - ✅ **Even-symmetric extension** is the geometric reason DCT is preferred over DFT for block-based coding. - ✅ **Energy compaction** makes DCT the transform of choice for **JPEG, MPEG, MP3, H.264, HEVC, AAC**. - ✅ **2D-DCT = separable** = row DCT followed by column DCT. - ✅ **JPEG block size = $8 \times 8$ pixels** (64 DCT coefficients per block). - ✅ **PSNR** = $10\log_{10}(255^2/\text{MSE})$ dB; **higher is better**. - ✅ **Parseval's theorem:** $\sum |x(n)|^2 = \sum |X(k)|^2$ — energy is preserved. - ✅ **Orthogonality of basis** guarantees perfect reconstruction. - ✅ **DCT-IV (MDCT)** is used in modern audio codecs (MP3, AAC, Ogg Vorbis). - ✅ **Zig-zag scan** orders DCT coefficients from low-frequency to high-frequency before entropy coding. - ✅ **Quantization step** in JPEG is what makes it *lossy* — not the DCT itself. - ✅ **KTU 2024 marking weightage (typical):** 2-mark definitions, 4-mark derivations, 7-mark numerical problems, 1-mark units/metrics. > [!WARNING] > **Final KTU Pitfall Reminder:** Always carry units and standard metrics (PSNR in **dB**, CR dimensionless, MSE in pixel²). Examiners *do* deduct $0.5$ mark for missing units in numerical answers.

topics.title: Video Compression: Quantization Levels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERPRISE AND ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS / ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Video Compression: Quantization Levels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantization** in the context of video compression is the **irreversible (lossy) process** of mapping a continuous set of input values (typically DCT coefficients, motion vectors, or pixel intensity residuals) to a smaller, finite set of discrete output levels. It is the **primary source of compression** in modern video codecs (H.264, H.265/HEVC, VVC) and is the single most important step controlling the **bit-rate vs. visual quality trade-off**.

In standardized codecs, quantization follows a two-step mathematical operation:
1. **Division** of the coefficient by a **Quantization Parameter (QP)** or **Quantization Step Size ($\Delta$)**.
2. **Rounding** to the nearest integer (truncation in some encoder implementations).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of quantization like **rounding money to the nearest rupee** when you go shopping in a foreign country that has paisa (cents). 
>
> If a chocolate costs ₹47.83, you don't carry 83 paisa — you **quantize** it to ₹48. You just lost 17 paisa of precision, but your wallet is simpler. The "**step size**" is ₹1. 
>
> In video compression, we are doing the same to thousands of mathematical coefficients describing brightness, color, and motion. The **larger the step size**, the more precision we throw away — but the more bits we save. **Lower quality, higher compression.**

### 1.3 Why Quantization is Mandatory

Raw digital video at 1080p, 30 fps, 4:2:0 chroma produces:
- **Pixels per frame:** 1920 × 1080 = 2,073,600
- **Bits per pixel (luma + chroma):** 8 + 4 + 4 = 12 bits (YUV 4:2:0 8-bit)
- **Raw bit-rate:** 2,073,600 × 12 × 30 ≈ **746.5 Mbps**

A standard DVD/streaming target is **2–10 Mbps** — a compression ratio of roughly **100:1** is required. Quantization is what enables this drastic reduction.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

- **Quantization Step Size ($\Delta$):** The interval between two representable quantized values.
- **Quantization Parameter (QP):** An integer index (0–51 in H.264) that selects the step size.
- **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** Measured in **dB** — higher means better fidelity.
- **Bit Depth (B):** Number of bits used to represent a quantized value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Families of Quantization

#### A. Scalar Quantization
Each input sample is quantized **independently**.

**Uniform (Linear) Scalar Quantization** — the step size $\Delta$ is constant:
$$Q(x) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\left(\frac{x}{\Delta}\right)$$

**Non-Uniform (Companded) Scalar Quantization** — step size varies (e.g., Lloyd-Max quantizer, $\mu$-law). Used in speech coding; rarely in modern video codecs.

#### B. Vector Quantization (VQ)
A block (vector) of samples is quantized **jointly** to the closest entry in a **codebook**. Used in some fractal/wavelet codecs but not in mainstream H.264/HEVC.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus:** The Module 3 syllabus emphasizes **Scalar Quantization** and **Quantization Matrices** used in the DCT-based hybrid coding framework (JPEG/MPEG/H.26x family). Vector Quantization is usually mentioned only for completeness.

### 2.2 The Quantization Decision Rule (Mid-Riser)

For an input value $x$ in the interval:
$$\left[ \left(k - \frac{1}{2}\right)\Delta,\ \left(k + \frac{1}{2}\right)\Delta \right]$$
the quantizer outputs the **reconstruction level** $k \cdot \Delta$, where $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

This is called a **mid-riser quantizer** and is used in virtually all H.26x codecs.

### 2.3 Quantization Error and Distortion

The quantization error (also called **quantization noise**) is:
$$e = x - Q(x)$$
For a mid-riser uniform quantizer, $e$ is bounded by:
$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Assuming a uniform distribution of $e$, the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** is:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### 2.4 The Famous 6 dB Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For every **1-bit reduction** in quantization bit-depth, the **SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio) drops by approximately 6 dB**. Equivalently, every **doubling of step size** ($\Delta \rightarrow 2\Delta$) **decreases quality by ~6 dB**.

This rule is the foundation of the rate-distortion trade-off.

### 2.5 Quantization in H.264 / HEVC

Modern codecs do not use a simple step size. They use the **QP-indexed** step size. In H.264:

$$\Delta_{QP} = 2^{\frac{QP-4}{6}}$$

| QP Index | Step Size $\Delta$ | Use Case |
|---------:|------------------:|----------|
| 0 | 0.625 | Lossless / archival |
| 18 | ~5 | Visually transparent |
| 22 | ~10 | High-quality streaming |
| 28 | ~20 | DVD / broadcast SD |
| 36 | ~40 | Low-bandwidth mobile |
| 51 | 224 | Maximum compression |

### 2.6 Quantization Matrices

For the 8×8 DCT block (used in JPEG, MPEG-2, H.264 intra), each of the 64 frequency coefficients has its own weight. Low-frequency coefficients (top-left) are quantized finely, high-frequency coefficients (bottom-right) are quantized coarsely — exploiting the human visual system's lower sensitivity to fine detail.

A typical JPEG **luminance quantization matrix** $Q_{matrix}$ is shown below (lower = better preserved):

| 16 | 11 | 10 | 16 | 24 | 40 | 51 | 61 |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 12 | 12 | 14 | 19 | 26 | 58 | 60 | 55 |
| 14 | 13 | 16 | 24 | 40 | 57 | 69 | 56 |
| 14 | 17 | 22 | 29 | 51 | 87 | 80 | 62 |
| 18 | 22 | 37 | 56 | 68 | 109 | 103 | 77 |
| 24 | 35 | 55 | 64 | 81 | 104 | 113 | 92 |
| 49 | 64 | 78 | 87 | 103 | 121 | 120 | 101 |
| 72 | 92 | 95 | 98 | 112 | 100 | 103 | 99 |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for Part A & Part B calculations.**

| Symbol | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $Q(x)$ | $Q(x) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}(x/\Delta)$ | Basic uniform quantization |
| $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \dfrac{R}{2^B}$ | Step size (R = range, B = bits) |
| $\sigma_e^2$ | $\sigma_e^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Quantization noise variance |
| $SQNR$ | $SQNR_{dB} = 6.02B + 4.77 - 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\Delta^2/12}\right)$ | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio |
| Simplified SQNR | $SQNR_{dB} \approx 6.02B + 1.76$ (full-scale sine) | Quick exam estimate |
| $MSE$ | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| $PSNR$ | $PSNR = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX^2}{MSE}\right)$ | Peak SNR in dB |
| $CR$ | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Raw bits}}{\text{Compressed bits}}$ | Compression Ratio |
| $\Delta_{QP}$ | $\Delta_{QP} = 2^{\frac{QP-4}{6}}$ | H.264 QP → step size |
| Bit Saving | $\Delta B \uparrow 1 \Rightarrow SQNR \downarrow 6$ dB | The "6 dB rule" |

> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of encoder rate-control** in Netflix, YouTube, OTT streaming, DTH satellite TV (DVB-S2), Blu-ray authoring, and real-time video conferencing (Zoom/Teams). Rate-control algorithms dynamically adjust the QP every macroblock to hit a target bitrate while maximizing perceptual quality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Step Size $\Delta$ from Bit Depth $B$

**Given:** An input signal amplitude range of $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$ and bit depth $B$ bits per sample.

**Step 1:** The total number of representable quantization levels is:
$$L = 2^B$$

**Step 2:** Since these $L$ levels must span the full range $R$ uniformly:
$$\Delta = \frac{R}{L} = \frac{R}{2^B}$$

**Step 3:** For a normalized full-scale sine wave $R = 2$ (i.e., amplitude ranges from $-1$ to $+1$):
$$\Delta = \frac{2}{2^B} = 2^{1-B}$$

This is the **canonical step size** used in audio (PCM) and many video standards.

### 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Noise Variance $\sigma_e^2$

**Given:** A mid-riser uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$ and an input signal assumed sufficiently complex (high-entropy) such that the error $e$ is uniformly distributed in $\left[-\Delta/2,\ \Delta/2\right]$.

**Step 1:** Probability density of the error is:
$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta},\quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \le e \le \frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Variance definition:
$$\sigma_e^2 = E[e^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e)\, de$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $p(e)$:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left(\frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: The 6 dB-per-Bit Rule

**Given:** A full-scale sine wave $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$ with signal power:
$$P_x = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Step 1:** From 3.1, the step size is:
$$\Delta = \frac{2A}{2^B} = 2A \cdot 2^{-B}$$

**Step 2:** Quantization noise power is:
$$P_e = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{4A^2 \cdot 2^{-2B}}{12} = \frac{A^2 \cdot 2^{-2B}}{3}$$

**Step 3:** SQNR in linear scale:
$$SQNR = \frac{P_x}{P_e} = \frac{A^2/2}{A^2 \cdot 2^{-2B}/3} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB:
$$SQNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2B}}{2}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20B\log_{10}(2)$$

**Step 5:** Using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.3010$:
$$SQNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02B$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{SQNR_{dB} \approx 6.02B + 1.76}$$

Differentiating: $\frac{d(SQNR)}{dB} \approx 6.02$ dB/bit — **the celebrated 6 dB rule**.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Bit-Rate vs. Quality Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A video encoder uses 8 bits/pixel for the luminance component. To meet a bandwidth constraint, the engineer reduces quantization to 5 bits/pixel. Calculate (a) the drop in SQNR and (b) the percentage reduction in per-pixel bits.

**Solution:**

**(a) Drop in SQNR:**
Initial $SQNR_8 = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 49.92$ dB
Final $SQNR_5 = 6.02(5) + 1.76 = 31.86$ dB
Drop = $49.92 - 31.86 = 18.06$ dB
*Equivalently:* $3 \times 6.02 = 18.06$ dB ✓

**(b) Percentage bit reduction:**
$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{8 - 5}{8} \times 100\% = 37.5\%$$

The 3-bit reduction dropped quality by **18.06 dB** but saved **37.5% of bits**.

### 3.5 Worked Example: PSNR Calculation After Quantization

**Problem:** A 4×4 luma block has the following original DCT coefficients $\mathbf{X}$ and quantized coefficients $\hat{\mathbf{X}}$. Compute PSNR assuming $MAX = 255$.

| $i,j$ | $X$ | $\hat{X}$ | $(X - \hat{X})^2$ |
|------:|----:|----------:|------------------:|
| (0,0) | 120 | 120 | 0 |
| (0,1) | 35 | 17 | 324 |
| (1,0) | 22 | 11 | 121 |
| (1,1) | 8 | 4 | 16 |
| (others) | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Solution:**

Sum of squared errors = $0 + 324 + 121 + 16 = 461$
Number of coefficients $N = 4$

$$MSE = \frac{461}{4} = 115.25$$

$$PSNR = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{115.25}\right) = 10\log_{10}(564.21) \approx 27.51\ \text{dB}$$

> A PSNR of **27–30 dB** is generally considered acceptable for standard-definition video; **40+ dB** is considered visually lossless.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quantization Process Flow in a Hybrid Video Encoder

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of where quantization sits in a typical H.264/HEVC encoder pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Video Frame] --> B[Frame Partitioning into Macroblocks CTUs]
    B --> C[Prediction: Intra or Inter]
    C --> D[Residual Block generation]
    D --> E[Integer DCT Transform 8x8 or 4x4]
    E --> F[Quantization Step with QP]
    F --> G[Entropy Coding VLC CABAC]
    G --> H[Compressed Bitstream]
    F --> I[Inverse Quantization Q step]
    I --> J[Inverse DCT IDCT]
    J --> K[Reconstruction Buffer for Prediction]
    K --> C
    H --> L[Channel or Storage]
    F -.Qstep selection.-> M[Rate Control Module]
    M -.feedback loop.-> F
```

### 4.2 Uniform Scalar Quantization — Input/Output Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Input x] --> B{Quantizer with step size delta}
    B --> C[Discrete Level k times delta]
    B --> D[Quantization Error e equals x minus Q of x]
    C --> E[Reconstruction x hat]
    D --> F[Bounded in range delta over 2]
```

### 4.3 Quantization Decision Tree for Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Encoder receives DCT coefficient C] --> B{Bit budget remaining}
    B -->|High| C[Choose QP small step size fine quantization]
    B -->|Low| D[Choose QP large step size coarse quantization]
    C --> E[High PSNR high quality]
    D --> F[Low PSNR high compression]
    B -->|Target rate| G[Rate-Distortion Optimization RDO]
    G --> H[Compute J equals D plus lambda times R]
    H --> I[Pick QP that minimizes J]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Quantization Matrix Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DCT 8x8 Coefficient Block Fuv] --> B[Element-wise Division by Q-matrix]
    B --> C[Rounding to Nearest Integer]
    C --> D[Quantized Block Fq]
    D --> E[Encoder Pipeline]
    E --> F[Decoder Pipeline]
    F --> G[Element-wise Multiplication by Q-matrix]
    G --> H[Inverse DCT]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Block]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
*Define quantization. Why is it called a "lossy" step in video compression?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Quantization** is the process of mapping a large set of continuous input values to a smaller set of discrete output values by dividing the input by a **Quantization Step Size ($\Delta$)** and rounding to the nearest integer:
$$Q(x) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\!\left(\frac{x}{\Delta}\right)$$

It is **lossy** because:
1. The rounding operation is **irreversible** — two different input values falling in the same quantization interval map to the same output.
2. The original coefficient $x$ **cannot be recovered** from $Q(x)$ with full precision; the difference $e = x - Q(x)$ is permanently discarded.
3. The amount of discarded information is directly proportional to the step size $\Delta$.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for the formula + 1 for irreversible reasoning)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
*State and explain the 6 dB rule in quantization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **6 dB Rule:** For every **1-bit reduction** in the quantization bit-depth $B$, the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) decreases by approximately **6.02 dB**.

This is derived from the formula:
$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02B + 1.76$$

**Significance:** It quantifies the **rate-distortion trade-off**. If an encoder reduces bits-per-sample from 8 to 6 to save bandwidth, it loses $2 \times 6.02 = 12.04$ dB in quality. This rule is the **fundamental principle** used by encoder rate-control modules in OTT streaming services.

(3 marks: 1 for statement + 1 for formula + 1 for engineering significance)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **role of quantization in a hybrid video encoder**. Compare **scalar quantization** with **vector quantization**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A video stream uses 8-bit quantization per pixel initially. To meet bandwidth, the encoder drops to 4-bit quantization.
    (i) Calculate the drop in SQNR.
    (ii) Calculate the percentage of bits saved.
    (iii) What is the new compression ratio if the original was 50:1? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram and comparison (7 marks):**

Refer to the encoder block diagram in Section 4.1.

**Key points to write (1 mark each):**
- Quantization sits **after DCT** and **before entropy coding** in the encoder. *(1)*
- Inverse quantization + IDCT form the **decoder path** that maintains prediction reference. *(1)*
- Quantization is the **only lossy step** — DCT, prediction, and entropy coding are all reversible. *(1)*

**Scalar vs. Vector Quantization Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Scalar Quantization | Vector Quantization |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Operation | Quantizes one sample at a time | Quantizes a block of samples jointly |
| Complexity | Low | High (codebook search) |
| Storage | No codebook needed | Requires codebook at both encoder/decoder |
| Optimality | Sub-optimal (ignores inter-sample correlation) | Theoretically superior (exploits correlation) |
| Use in video | Standard in H.264, HEVC, VVC | Used in fractal/wavelet codecs, VQ-DCT research |
| Latency | Low | High (block-level search) |

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Drop in SQNR (3 marks):**
$$SQNR_8 = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 49.92\ \text{dB}$$
$$SQNR_4 = 6.02(4) + 1.76 = 25.84\ \text{dB}$$
$$\text{Drop} = 49.92 - 25.84 = 24.08\ \text{dB}$$

**Alternatively:** Drop $= (8-4) \times 6.02 = 24.08$ dB ✓

**(ii) Percentage of bits saved (2 marks):**
$$\text{Bits saved} = \frac{8 - 4}{8} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**(iii) New compression ratio (2 marks):**
If original $CR_1 = 50$ and bit-depth is halved, the new ratio doubles (fewer bits per sample):
$$CR_2 = 50 \times \frac{8}{4} = 100:1$$

**Result:** New compression ratio is **100:1**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **quantization matrix** in JPEG/MPEG. How does it exploit **human visual perception**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4×4 DCT coefficient block is given below. The quantization matrix $\mathbf{Q}$ is also given. Compute the quantized block $\hat{\mathbf{X}}$ using the formula $\hat{X}_{ij} = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{ij}}{Q_{ij}}\right)$. *(7 marks)*

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 80 & 24 & 12 & 4 \\ 18 & 8 & 4 & 2 \\ 10 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\ 6 & 3 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad
\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 16 & 32 & 64 \\ 16 & 32 & 64 & 64 \\ 32 & 64 & 64 & 128 \\ 64 & 64 & 128 & 128 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Quantization matrix and HVS (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **quantization matrix** (also called **quantization table**) is an 8×8 (or 4×4 / 16×16) array of step sizes, one per DCT frequency bin. Each coefficient $X_{ij}$ at position $(i,j)$ is divided by $Q_{ij}$ and rounded:
$$\hat{X}_{ij} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{X_{ij}}{Q_{ij}}\right)$$

**HVS exploitation (3 marks):**
- The **DC coefficient** (top-left, $Q_{00}$) is set to the **smallest value** — DC represents the average brightness, to which the eye is **highly sensitive**. It must be preserved precisely.
- **Low-frequency AC coefficients** (near the top-left) are quantized finely — they represent large smooth gradients and edges, which the eye notices.
- **High-frequency AC coefficients** (bottom-right) are quantized coarsely — they represent fine texture and noise, to which the eye is **least sensitive**.
- The matrix is designed using **psychovisual experiments** measuring contrast sensitivity functions (CSF).

**Real-world example (2 marks):** The JPEG standard defines two default matrices — one for luminance (more detail preserved) and one for chrominance (heavier quantization, since human vision is less sensitive to color detail). Encoders can override these for custom quality tuning.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Compute element-wise division followed by rounding:

$$\hat{X}_{00} = \text{round}(80/8) = \text{round}(10) = 10$$
$$\hat{X}_{01} = \text{round}(24/16) = \text{round}(1.5) = 2$$
$$\hat{X}_{02} = \text{round}(12/32) = \text{round}(0.375) = 0$$
$$\hat{X}_{03} = \text{round}(4/64) = \text{round}(0.0625) = 0$$
$$\hat{X}_{10} = \text{round}(18/16) = \text{round}(1.125) = 1$$
$$\hat{X}_{11} = \text{round}(8/32) = \text{round}(0.25) = 0$$
$$\hat{X}_{12} = \text{round}(4/64) = 0$$
