<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Active Matrix (AM) and Passive Matrix (PM) LED displays. State one entertainment application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PM-LED | AM-LED |
|---|---|---|
| Drive element | None — row/column scan only | One TFT per sub-pixel |
| Peak brightness | Lower | Higher |
| Refresh rate | Limited | High (broadcast-grade) |
| Power efficiency | Lower (peak current per row) | Higher |

**Application:**
- **PM-LED** → low-cost scoreboards, traffic signs.
- **AM-LED** → professional broadcast studios, cinema, AR/VR micro-displays.

*(Examiner allocates ~1.5 marks for the table and ~1.5 marks for correct applications.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define luminous efficacy of an LED. A blue LED emits at 470 nm with luminous flux 8 lm from 1.2 W electrical input. Calculate its luminous efficacy.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Luminous efficacy $\eta_v$ is the ratio of luminous flux emitted (in lumens) to the electrical power consumed (in watts). Units: **lm/W**.

**Numerical:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{el}} = \frac{8 \text{ lm}}{1.2 \text{ W}} \approx 6.67 \text{ lm/W}$$

This low value is expected for a monochromatic blue LED because $V(470 \text{ nm}) \approx 0.10$, so the maximum theoretical efficacy at that wavelength is only $K_m \cdot V(\lambda) = 683 \times 0.10 \approx 68$ lm/W. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module-level Part-B questions carry an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are given below; the student answers **ONE**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **direct-view RGB LED video wall** used in concerts and stadiums. Discuss the role of each block: video processor, scan controller, row/column drivers, and SMPS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An outdoor LED display uses **SMD 3535 RGB LEDs** with pixel pitch 4 mm. The display is $4 \text{ m} \times 2.5 \text{ m}$. Calculate:
  (i) Total number of pixels.
  (ii) Optimal viewing distance using the 1-arc-minute visual acuity criterion.
  (iii) Required horizontal scan frequency if 1/8 duty-cycle row multiplexing is used at 60 Hz frame rate.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A direct-view LED video wall is **self-emissive**: every pixel is physically an RGB triad of LED chips. The signal chain is:

1. **HDMI / SDI Input:** Receives uncompressed video (1080p or 4K) from cameras, media servers, or PCs.
2. **Video Processor (FPGA/ASIC):** Performs scaling, de-interlacing, color-space conversion (Rec. 709 ↔ Rec. 2020), and gamma correction (typically γ = 2.2).
3. **Scan Controller:** Divides the screen into rows and columns; generates row-select signals and shifts pixel data into column latches.
4. **Row Driver IC:** Sources/sinks the high row current (e.g., 1 A peak) on the active scan line.
5. **Column Driver IC:** Performs 16-bit PWM per sub-pixel for brightness & grayscale.
6. **SMPS Power Supply:** Provides constant current to drivers; usually 5 V DC for logic, 48 V DC for LED rails.
7. **LED Module PCB:** The physical tile; each tile typically $256 \times 256$ pixels, daisy-chained via Cat-6 or fibre.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 should be redrawn in the answer sheet for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Total pixels:**

$$N_x = \frac{4 \text{ m}}{4 \text{ mm}} = \frac{4000}{4} = 1000 \text{ pixels (horizontal)}$$

$$N_y = \frac{2.5 \text{ m}}{4 \text{ mm}} = \frac{2500}{4} = 625 \text{ pixels (vertical)}$$

$$\boxed{N_{total} = 1000 \times 625 = 625\,000 \text{ pixels}}$$

**(ii) Optimal viewing distance:**

$$d_{opt} = \frac{P}{\tan(1')} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{0.000291} \approx 13.74 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Horizontal scan frequency:**

Each row is scanned for $1/8$ of the frame period:

$$T_{row} = \frac{1}{60 \times 8} = 2.083 \text{ ms}$$

The number of rows scanned per second is the *vertical* scan rate:

$$f_v = 60 \times 8 = 480 \text{ Hz (vertical refresh)}$$

$$\boxed{f_{h,\min} \approx 480 \times 625 = 300\,000 \text{ row scans/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — losing 2 marks:** Students often confuse **frame refresh (60 Hz)** with **scan-line frequency**. For 1/8 duty multiplex, the scan rate is $N_{rows} \times f_{frame} = 8 \times 60 = 480$ Hz, NOT 60 Hz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PWM dimming** technique used to realise 16-bit grayscale in LED displays. Why is PWM preferred over analogue current dimming?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A green micro-LED has $E_g = 2.43$ eV. Determine:
  (i) Peak emission wavelength.
  (ii) Energy of a single emitted photon (in joules).
  (iii) Number of photons emitted per second if luminous flux is 12 lm and $V(\lambda) \approx 0.69$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**PWM Dimming — Detailed Explanation:**

In PWM dimming, the LED is switched fully ON or fully OFF at high frequency. The **duty cycle** $D$ controls perceived brightness. For 16-bit grayscale ($L = 2^{16} = 65\,536$ levels):

$$D_n = \frac{n}{2^{16}}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 65535$$

The frame is divided into $2^{16}$ time slices (e.g., 65 536 slices within ~ 16 ms). Bit-plane streaming outputs bit $k$ for the entire frame before moving to bit $k+1$, achieving $1/2^k$ precision per slice.

**Why PWM beats analog dimming:**
1. **Colour shift:** Current dimming shifts dominant wavelength slightly (band-filling effect); PWM keeps $\lambda$ constant.
2. **Efficiency:** LED efficacy is nearly constant at full current; analog dimming drops efficacy dramatically.
3. **Precision:** 16-bit analog current sources are impractical; PWM is digital and exact.
4. **Thermal stability:** Constant $I_{peak}$ keeps junction temperature predictable.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{E_g} = \frac{1240}{2.43} \approx 510.3 \text{ nm (cyan-green)}$$

**(ii) Photon energy:**

$$E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{510.3 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$E_{ph} \approx 3.895 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Verification:** $E_g = 2.43$ eV $= 2.43 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \approx 3.892 \times 10^{-19}$ J ✅ (matches).

**(iii) Number of photons per second:**

$$\dot{N} = \frac{\Phi_v}{V(\lambda) \cdot K_m \cdot E_{ph}}$$

Wait — more directly, the number of photons needed to produce 12 lm at 510 nm:

$$P_{opt} = \frac{\Phi_v}{V(\lambda) \cdot K_m} = \frac{12}{0.69 \times 683} \approx 0.0255 \text{ W}$$

$$\dot{N} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{ph}} = \frac{0.0255}{3.895 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{N} \approx 6.55 \times 10^{16} \text{ photons/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — losing 3 marks:** Students frequently forget to multiply by $V(\lambda)$ when converting lumens → watts. **1 lumen ≠ 1/683 watt** unless $\lambda = 555$ nm. For any other wavelength, the $V(\lambda)$ correction is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on LED Display questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting $V(\lambda)$:** Mixing photometric (lumens) and radiometric (watts) units without applying the photopic luminosity correction. (–2 marks per numerical)
> 2. **Confusing PWM and analog dimming:** Writing "PWM changes the current" — wrong; PWM switches between full ON and full OFF. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Miscounting pixels:** Using the wrong unit (cm vs mm) when converting physical panel dimensions to pixel counts. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Wrong viewing-distance formula:** Using the rule-of-thumb "10 × pitch in metres" when the question explicitly asks for the **arc-minute** criterion. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram:** A 7-mark question without a labelled block diagram loses a minimum of 2 marks even if the explanation is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ LED = **electroluminescent p-n junction** whose photon energy equals its bandgap: $\lambda = 1240/E_g$ nm.
- ✅ RGB primary wavelengths used in displays: **R ≈ 625 nm, G ≈ 525 nm, B ≈ 470 nm**.
- ✅ Photometric constant: **$K_m = 683$ lm/W at $\lambda = 555$ nm**.
- ✅ Luminous efficacy $\eta_v = \Phi_v / P_{el}$ (lm/W).
- ✅ Inverse-square law: $E_v = I_v / d^2$.
- ✅ PWM duty cycle controls brightness: $D = T_{on}/T_{total}$.
- ✅ In multiplexed scan with $N$ rows, peak current per row = $N \cdot I_{DC}$.
- ✅ Refresh rate above the **flicker fusion threshold (~75 Hz)** is essential to avoid visible flicker.
- ✅ Bit depth $n$ gives $2^n$ colour levels per channel; 8-bit/channel = 16.7 M colours.
- ✅ Optimal viewing distance: $d_{opt} = P / \tan(1') \approx P \times 3438$ (with $P$ in metres).
- ✅ Active Matrix (TFT-per-pixel) is preferred for **high-brightness, high-refresh** entertainment applications.
- ✅ LED Displays are the **only emissive technology with unlimited modular size** and the highest daylight readability.
- ✅ Key metrics for entertainment: **pixel pitch, nits (cd/m²), refresh rate (Hz), bit depth, gamut (% Rec.2020), IP rating (for outdoor)**.
- ✅ Common pitfalls: unit errors in lumens↔watts, missing $V(\lambda)$, conflating frame rate and scan-line rate.
- ✅ Block diagram essentials to memorise: **Source → Processor → Scan Controller → Row/Col Drivers → LED Array → Viewer**, plus SMPS for power.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Emission Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Field Emission Displays # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Field Emission Display (FED)** is a flat-panel vacuum electronic display technology that uses an array of sharp micro-tip cathodes (or nano-structured emitters) which emit electrons through quantum-mechanical **tunneling** under a strong local electric field. These electrons are accelerated across a narrow vacuum gap to strike phosphor-coated anodes, producing light via cathodoluminescence — essentially, it is a **flat, matrix-addressed version of a CRT**. The display sandwich typically consists of two glass plates separated by a **vacuum gap of 1–2 mm**, held apart by tiny insulating spacers. The rear plate carries millions of micro-emitters, while the front plate carries red, green, and blue (RGB) phosphors arranged in a pixel matrix. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"Imagine a stadium full of tiny lighters"** 🔥 > > Picture a huge football stadium where, instead of a few large floodlights (as in older stadium designs), every single seat has its own microscopic lighter. When the referee switches on the field, only the lighters in the seats corresponding to the picture elements (pixels) light up. Each tiny lighter is independently triggered by a strong electric "wind." > > - The **field emitters** = the microscopic lighters. > - The **electric field** = the wind that lights them. > - The **phosphor screen** = the canvas where the lit area is "painted." > - The **vacuum gap** = the clean stadium air ensuring no flame goes out. In simpler words: a FED is a CRT that has been **flattened, miniaturized, and pixelated** — combining the picture quality of a CRT with the slim form factor of an LCD. ## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Vacuum gap | **1–2 mm** | | Operating anode voltage | **300 V – 10 kV** | | Pixel emitter count | **1000 – 10,000 micro-tips per pixel** | | Tip radius of curvature | **≤ 50 nm** | | Luminance | **300 – 600 cd/m²** | | Viewing angle | **> 160° (near 180°)** | | Response time | **< 1 ms** | | Operating temperature | **−40 °C to +85 °C** | | Work function (φ) of Mo tips | **≈ 4.5 eV** | | Turn-on field | **~ 5 × 10⁹ V/m** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote anode voltage range as "**hundreds of volts to several kilovolts**" — the question setter expects the wide range because it varies between low-voltage and high-voltage FED architectures. ## 1.4 Classification of FEDs (Syllabus-Relevant) 1. **Spindt-type FEDs** — Molybdenum (Mo) micro-tips fabricated by IC-like processes. 2. **Carbon Nanotube (CNT) FEDs** — Vertically aligned CNT arrays as emitters. 3. **Surface-Conduction Electron-Emitter Displays (SED)** — PdO thin-film strips as emitters. 4. **Carbon-film FEDs** — Diamond-like carbon emitters. 5. **Metal-Insulator-Metal (MIM) / Ballistic Electron Surface-emitting Displays** — Planar emitters (historically). For KTU exams, **Spindt-type** and **CNT-based** FEDs are the most important. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A FED is essentially a **two-electrode vacuum diode** operating in a regime where electron emission is governed by quantum tunneling rather than thermionic emission. **Step 1 — Cathode Preparation** The rear glass substrate is patterned with **row electrodes (cathodes)**, and at each pixel intersection, an array of sharp micro-tips (or CNTs) is grown. A **gate electrode** sits ~1 µm above the tips, separated by an insulating layer. **Step 2 — Field Application** When a voltage $V_g$ is applied between the **gate** and the **cathode**, a very high local electric field $E$ is generated at the tip apex (because the tip radius is on the order of tens of nanometers — the field is concentrated by a geometric field-enhancement factor $\beta$). $$E = \beta \cdot \frac{V_g}{d}$$ where $d$ is the gate-to-cathode distance. **Step 3 — Quantum Tunneling (Field Emission)** At field strengths exceeding ~$5 \times 10^9 \text{ V/m}$, the surface potential barrier becomes thin enough (≤ 1 nm) for electrons to **tunnel** from the cathode's conduction band into the vacuum. This is **cold emission** — no heating is required. **Step 4 — Electron Acceleration** The emitted electrons are accelerated by the much higher **anode voltage** ($V_a$ in the range of hundreds of volts to several kV) across the vacuum gap toward the phosphor-coated front plate. **Step 5 — Cathodoluminescence** High-energy electrons strike the RGB phosphor stripes/dots. The kinetic energy excites phosphor electrons; when they de-excite, photons of the desired wavelength are emitted — red (~625 nm), green (~545 nm), or blue (~450 nm). **Step 6 — Address & Sustain** The row gates are scanned sequentially while column (anode) data voltages are applied simultaneously — a standard **matrix-addressing** scheme. Pixel luminance is controlled by the **pulse-width modulation (PWM)** of the column data. > [!NOTE] > **Why FED looks like CRT picture quality:** Because the light is generated by cathodoluminescence, the **color gamut, viewing angle, and contrast** match those of a CRT, but the form factor is flat. ## 2.2 The Why & How Behind Each Step | Step | Why? | How? | |---|---|---| | 1 | Need dense, addressable electron sources | IC lithography + self-aligned gate | | 2 | Sharp tips concentrate the field | Geometric enhancement $\beta \approx h/r$ | | 3 | Room-temperature emission is mandatory for a flat display | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | | 4 | Electrons must gain energy to excite phosphors | High anode-cathode potential | | 5 | Convert electron KE to visible light | Phosphor cathodoluminescence | | 6 | Display arbitrary images | Active-matrix row-column addressing | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS** | # | Formula | Description / Definition of Terms | |---|---|---| | 1 | $J = \dfrac{A \cdot E^2}{\phi} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B \cdot \phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$ | **Fowler-Nordheim (F-N) equation** — current density $J$ (A/m²) for field emission. $A = 1.54 \times 10^{-6}$, $B = 6.83 \times 10^{9}$ (in SI), $E$ = local field (V/m), $\phi$ = work function (eV). | | 2 | $E = \beta \cdot \dfrac{V_g}{d}$ | Local electric field at emitter tip; $\beta$ = field enhancement factor, $V_g$ = gate voltage, $d$ = gap. | | 3 | $\beta \approx \dfrac{h}{r}$ | Geometric enhancement; $h$ = tip height, $r$ = tip radius. | | 4 | $E_{\text{turn-on}} \approx 5 \times 10^{9} \;\text{V/m}$ | Empirical turn-on field for typical Mo/CNT emitters. | | 5 | $I_{\text{anode}} = n \cdot J \cdot A_{\text{tip}}$ | Total current from one pixel; $n$ = number of tips per pixel, $A_{\text{tip}}$ = effective emitting area. | | 6 | $L = \eta \cdot V_a \cdot I_a$ | Luminance $L$ proportional to anode voltage and current; $\eta$ = luminous efficiency of phosphor. | | 7 | $t_{\text{transit}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m_e d^2}{e V_a}}$ | Electron transit time across gap $d$ under $V_a$ — governs response time. | | 8 | $\text{Contrast Ratio} = \dfrac{L_{\text{on}}}{L_{\text{off}}}$ | Contrast; in FEDs can exceed **100,000 : 1**. | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **High-end medical monitors & avionics** — FEDs offer CRT-quality images without the bulk. - **Military ruggedized displays** — Wide temperature range, no liquid-crystal degradation. - **HDR television prototypes** — Pixel-level emission → true blacks and infinite contrast. - **Vacuum microelectronics research** — FEDs are a flagship of this entire field. - **CRT replacement in legacy aerospace cockpits** — Boeing, Airbus retrofit programs considered FEDs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fowler-Nordheim Equation (Detailed) > [!IMPORTANT] > The F-N equation is the **heart of FED physics** and is examinable in KTU. We derive it qualitatively (the rigorous derivation requires solving the Schrödinger equation for a triangular potential barrier, but the KTU board expects the physical reasoning and final form). ### Setup Consider an electron inside a metal at the Fermi level. Without an external field, the potential energy barrier to escape is the work function $\phi$ (typically 4–5 eV). With no field, only thermal excitation (thermionic emission) can help the electron escape. When a strong external electric field $E$ is applied, the potential outside the metal tilts linearly: $$V(x) = -eEx \quad (x > 0)$$ This creates a **triangular barrier** of finite width. The barrier width $w$ (at the Fermi level) is given by: $$w = \frac{\phi}{eE}$$ ### Quantum Tunneling Argument The transmission probability $T$ through a triangular barrier of width $w$ and height $\phi$ in the WKB (Wentzel–Kramers–Brillouin) approximation is: $$T \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\sqrt{2m_e}}{3\hbar eE}\,\phi^{3/2}\right)$$ where $m_e$ = electron rest mass, $\hbar$ = reduced Planck's constant. ### Current Density The emission current density is the product of: - Electron supply function (number of electrons incident on the barrier per unit area per unit time). - The transmission probability $T$. Carrying out the integration over energy states (assuming the metal is at $T = 0$ K as a simplification), one obtains: $$J = \frac{e^3 E^2}{8\pi h \phi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{8\pi \sqrt{2m_e}}{3heE}\,\phi^{3/2}\right)$$ ### Final Compact Form The two coefficients are grouped as $A$ and $B$: $$A = \frac{e^3}{8\pi h} \approx 1.54 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{A·eV/V}^2$$ $$B = \frac{8\pi \sqrt{2m_e e}}{3h} \approx 6.83 \times 10^{9} \;\text{V/(eV}^{3/2}\text{·m)}$$ leading to the canonical **Fowler-Nordheim (F-N) equation**: $$\boxed{\,J = \frac{A\,E^{2}}{\phi}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)\,}$$ ### Step-by-Step Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Numerical example — typical KTU exam style** **Given:** A Spindt-type FED with Mo emitters, work function $\phi = 4.5$ eV. The gate voltage is $V_g = 80$ V across a gap $d = 1.2\;\mu\text{m}$. The field enhancement factor is $\beta = 600$. The tip area is $A_{\text{tip}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{m}^2$, with 2000 tips per pixel. **Find:** (a) Local field $E$ at tip. (b) F-N current density $J$. (c) Total current per pixel. **Solution (a):** The local field at the tip is: $$E = \beta \cdot \frac{V_g}{d}$$ Substituting the values: $$E = 600 \times \frac{80}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\boxed{E = 4.0 \times 10^{10}\;\text{V/m}}$$ **Solution (b):** Apply the F-N equation. First compute the exponent argument: $$\frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E} = \frac{6.83 \times 10^{9} \times (4.5)^{3/2}}{4.0 \times 10^{10}}$$ $$(4.5)^{3/2} = 4.5 \times \sqrt{4.5} \approx 4.5 \times 2.1213 = 9.546$$ $$\frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E} = \frac{6.83 \times 10^{9} \times 9.546}{4.0 \times 10^{10}}$$ $$= \frac{6.52 \times 10^{10}}{4.0 \times 10^{10}} = 1.630$$ So the exponential term is: $$\exp(-1.630) \approx 0.196$$ Now the prefactor: $$\frac{A\,E^{2}}{\phi} = \frac{1.54 \times 10^{-6} \times (4.0 \times 10^{10})^{2}}{4.5}$$ $$(4.0 \times 10^{10})^{2} = 1.6 \times 10^{21}$$ $$\frac{A\,E^{2}}{\phi} = \frac{1.54 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{21}}{4.5}$$ $$= \frac{2.464 \times 10^{15}}{4.5} = 5.476 \times 10^{14}\;\text{A/m}^2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{J = 5.476 \times 10^{14} \times 0.196 \approx 1.07 \times 10^{14}\;\text{A/m}^2}$$ **Solution (c):** $$I_{\text{pixel}} = n \cdot J \cdot A_{\text{tip}}$$ $$I_{\text{pixel}} = 2000 \times 1.07 \times 10^{14} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-14}$$ $$= 2000 \times 1.605 \approx 3210\;\text{A}$$ > [!WARNING] > The result above seems astronomically large — in real FEDs the F-N current density is much smaller because the *effective* emitting area is far smaller than the geometric tip area (only a few atoms at the apex actually emit). Real per-pixel currents are typically **microamperes**. ### 3.1.1 F-N Plot (Used to Verify Field Emission) A diagnostic tool: plot $\ln(J/E^2)$ vs $1/E$. A straight line with negative slope is the signature of field emission (as opposed to thermionic or Schottky emission). The slope gives $\phi$, the intercept gives $A$. $$\ln\!\left(\frac{J}{E^2}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{A}{\phi}\right) - \frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E}$$ This linear F-N plot is **frequently asked** in KTU viva and short-answer questions. ## 3.2 Electron Transit Time Derivation Electron transit time across the vacuum gap is the fundamental response-time limiter of a FED. Starting from Newton's second law for an electron accelerated from rest across potential difference $V_a$ over distance $d$: $$eE_a = m_e \cdot a, \quad E_a = \frac{V_a}{d}$$ Uniform acceleration gives: $$d = \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \frac{eV_a}{m_e d}\,t^2$$ Solving for $t$: $$t^2 = \frac{2 m_e d^2}{e V_a}$$ $$\boxed{\,t_{\text{transit}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 m_e d^2}{e V_a}}\,}$$ **Numerical example:** $d = 1\;\text{mm}$, $V_a = 5000\;\text{V}$. $$t_{\text{transit}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times (10^{-3})^2}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5000}}$$ $$= \sqrt{\frac{1.822 \times 10^{-36}}{8.0 \times 10^{-16}}} = \sqrt{2.28 \times 10^{-21}}$$ $$\boxed{t_{\text{transit}} \approx 4.77 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{s} \approx 48\;\text{ps}}$$ This picosecond-range transit time is **why FEDs are ultra-fast** — orders of magnitude faster than LCD response times (~5–10 ms). ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: FED vs Competing Display Technologies > [!TIP] > **This table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU module-comparison questions.** | Parameter | FED | CRT | LCD | Plasma | OLED | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Luminance (cd/m²) | 300–600 | 500–1000 | 200–500 | 500–1000 | 200–1000 | | Contrast ratio | 100,000 : 1 | 50,000 : 1 | 1000 : 1 | 30,000 : 1 | 1,000,000 : 1 | | Response time | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | 5–10 ms | < 1 ms | < 0.1 ms | | Viewing angle | ~ 180° | ~ 180° | ~ 170° | ~ 170° | ~ 170° | | Operating T range | −40 to +85 °C | 0 to +50 °C | 0 to +50 °C | 0 to +40 °C | −20 to +70 °C | | Thickness | ~ 5 mm | > 300 mm | ~ 5 mm | ~ 50 mm | ~ 3 mm | | Power consumption | Medium | High | Low | High | Low | | Vacuum required? | **Yes** | Yes | No | No | No | | Spacers visible? | Can be issue | No | N/A | No | N/A | | Manufacturing | Difficult | Mature | Mature | Mature | Growing | | Color gamut | Wide (CRT-like) | Wide | Medium | Wide | Very wide | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a FED Panel ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["REAR PLATE — CATHODE SUBSTRATE"] RowElec["ROW ELECTRODE
Cathode lines"] InsLayer["DIELECTRIC INSULATOR
SiO2 layer"] GateElec["GATE ELECTRODE
Extraction electrode"] EmitterBank["EMITTER BANK
Spindt Mo tips or CNTs"] RowElec --> EmitterBank InsLayer -.separates.-> GateElec GateElec -.controls emission.-> EmitterBank end subgraph B["VACUUM GAP — 1 to 2 mm"] Vacuum["SEALED VACUUM
Pressure less than 10 to power of minus 6 Torr"] Spacers["INSULATING SPACERS
Glass or ceramic pillars"] Spacers -.mechanical support.-> Vacuum end subgraph C["FRONT PLATE — ANODE SUBSTRATE"] AnodeFilm["TRANSPARENT ANODE
ITO thin film"] RGBPhos["RGB PHOSPHOR STRIPES
Red ZnCdS Ag
Green ZnS Cu Al
Blue ZnS Ag"] FrontGlass["FRONT GLASS
Viewing surface"] AnodeFilm --> RGBPhos RGBPhos --> FrontGlass end EmitterBank == Electrons accelerated == Vacuum Vacuum == High KE electrons == RGBPhos RGBPhos == Visible light == FrontGlass ``` **Reading guide for the diagram:** - The **rear plate** generates electrons (the "engine" of the display). - The **vacuum gap** accelerates them (the "highway"). - The **front plate** converts electron energy to light (the "screen"). ## 4.2 Spindt-Type Micro-Tip Fabrication (Process Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1
Deposit Mo cathode row lines
on glass substrate"] --> S2["STEP 2
Deposit SiO2 dielectric layer"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3
Deposit Mo gate layer
on top of dielectric"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4
Photolithography
Open gate holes of 1 micrometer diameter"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5
RIE etch of SiO2
Reveals cathode beneath"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6
Vertical Mo evaporation
with rotating substrate"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7
Self aligned cone forms
in gate hole"] S7 --> S8["STEP 8
Lift off excess Mo
Sharp Mo tip remains"] S8 --> S9["FINAL
Sharp conical Mo emitter
inside gate aperture"] ``` ## 4.3 FED Pixel Equivalent Circuit (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR Vg["GATE VOLTAGE Vg
Row driver"] -->|controls| Cge["Cge
Gate to Cathode"] Cge --> Cathode["CATHODE TIP
Field emitter"] Cathode -->|emits electrons| VacuumRegion["VACUUM DIODE
Variable conductance G"] VacuumRegion --> Anode["ANODE
High voltage Va"] Anode -->|excites| Phosphor["PHOSPHOR PIXEL
with capacitance Cph"] Phosphor --> GND["COMMON GROUND"] Cathode --> GND Vh["HIGH VOLTAGE Va
300 V to 10 kV"] --> Anode ``` **Component meanings:** - $C_{ge}$ = gate-to-cathode capacitance (controls speed). - Vacuum diode = variable conductance representing field emission current. - $C_{ph}$ = phosphor + ITO capacitance (stores charge between scans). ## 4.4 F-N Plot Generation Flow (Diagnostic Procedure) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Apply ramp voltage Vg
from 0 to 100 V"] --> B["Measure emission current I
at each Vg step"] B --> C["Convert Vg to local field E
E equals beta times Vg by d"] C --> D["Compute J equals I by Atip
for each Vg step"] D --> E["Compute Ln of J by E squared
and 1 by E for each point"] E --> F{"Straight line
with negative slope?"} F -->|Yes| G["CONFIRMED FIELD EMISSION
Slope yields work function phi"] F -->|No| H["NOT field emission
Check vacuum or contact issues"] ``` ## 4.5 Color Generation Sub-System ```mermaid flowchart LR ElectronBeam["ELECTRON BEAM
from one pixel tip"] --> Selector["DEFLECTION / SELECTION
controlled by gate"] Selector --> R["RED PHOSPHOR STRIPE
ZnCdS Ag
emits 625 nm"] Selector --> G["GREEN PHOSPHOR STRIPE
ZnS Cu Al
emits 545 nm"] Selector --> B["BLUE PHOSPHOR STRIPE
ZnS Ag
emits 450 nm"] R --> Out["VIEWED IMAGE
True RGB color"] G --> Out B --> Out ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions are modeled strictly on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks follow the official split: **Part A = 3 marks**, **Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice)**. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] **Define Field Emission Display. List any two advantages over LCD.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!TIP] > A FED is a flat-panel vacuum display in which electrons are **emitted via field-induced quantum tunneling** from an array of micro-tips or CNTs and accelerated across a vacuum gap to strike RGB phosphors, producing light by cathodoluminescence. **Two advantages over LCD:** 1. **Wider viewing angle** (~180°) since emission is from each sub-pixel directly, unlike LCDs that depend on the backlight and viewing-angle-dependent liquid-crystal alignment. 2. **Much faster response time** (sub-millisecond) because FEDs use electron transit times in vacuum (~picoseconds), whereas LCDs depend on slow molecular reorientation (~ms). 3. *(Optional third)* Better contrast ratio and color gamut, comparable to CRT. *(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two advantages.)* ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] **What is the Fowler-Nordheim equation? Why is it important for FEDs?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Fowler-Nordheim (F-N) equation gives the **field-emission current density** $J$ from a metallic surface under an applied electric field $E$: $$J = \frac{A\,E^{2}}{\phi}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$$ where $\phi$ is the work function (eV), and $A$, $B$ are constants ($A \approx 1.54 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{A·eV/V}^2$, $B \approx 6.83 \times 10^{9}\;\text{V·eV}^{-3/2}\text{·m}^{-1}$). **Importance for FEDs (1 mark):** - The F-N equation is the **design equation** for the FED cathode. It dictates the required **tip sharpness** (low $r$) and **gate voltage** to achieve a useful emission current. Designers use it to predict turn-on voltage, current per tip, and lifetime (via current-density-driven tip erosion). - An F-N plot (ln$(J/E^2)$ vs $1/E$) is used experimentally to confirm that emission is indeed field emission and to extract the effective work function. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a Spindt-type Field Emission Display**. Discuss the role of the gate electrode. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Derive the **Fowler-Nordheim equation** and explain how a **F-N plot** is used to characterize field emitters. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks **Construction (3 marks):** A Spindt-type FED is built from two glass plates sealed together with a vacuum gap of **1–2 mm** between them. - **Rear (cathode) plate:** A glass substrate is patterned with parallel **molybdenum row electrodes**. Above these, a **SiO₂ dielectric layer** (~1 µm thick) is deposited, and on top of that lies the **Mo gate electrode** with arrays of precisely defined circular apertures (~1 µm diameter). Inside each aperture, a sharp conical **Mo micro-tip** of height ~1.2 µm and tip radius ≤ 50 nm is grown by a self-aligned evaporation/lift-off process. Each pixel contains typically **1000–10000 such micro-tips** in parallel for redundancy. - **Front (anode) plate:** A transparent **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide) layer** is coated with **RGB phosphor stripes** (red: ZnCdS:Ag; green: ZnS:Cu,Al; blue: ZnS:Ag). The ITO acts as the high-voltage anode and is biased at **300 V to several kV**. - **Spacers:** Glass or ceramic pillars ~50 µm wide, periodically placed to resist atmospheric pressure on the vacuum gap. - **Getter:** A small Ba-based getter absorbs residual gas molecules to maintain vacuum over the device's life. **Working Principle (3 marks):** 1. A row voltage $V_g$ (~50–80 V) is applied between the gate and the cathode row of the selected line. 2. The geometry of the sharp Mo tip (small radius $r$) creates an enormous local field enhancement, so even a modest gate voltage produces $E \geq 5 \times 10^9\;\text{V/m}$ at the apex. 3. Electrons **tunnel** out of the tip (Fowler-Nordheim emission) into the vacuum. 4. The high anode voltage $V_a$ accelerates these electrons across the gap; they gain kinetic energy $eV_a$. 5. Electrons strike the phosphor → **cathodoluminescence** → visible light. 6. Column drivers select which RGB stripe is energized (or which sub-pixel), controlling the color. The luminance is set by **pulse-width modulation** of the column voltage. **Role of the gate electrode (1 mark):** The gate is a **non-destructive, low-voltage extraction electrode** that controls emission intensity for each pixel independently. By varying $V_g$ one modulates the local field $E$ and therefore the F-N current exponentially — this is the **addressing mechanism**. The gate also enables **matrix addressing** (rows × columns) without cross-talk. --- #### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks **Derivation (5 marks) — outline:** 1. For a metal with work function $\phi$, the potential energy of an electron just outside the surface is $\phi$ above the Fermi level. Under an external field $E$, the potential outside becomes a linearly decreasing function: $$V(x) = \phi - eEx \quad (x > 0)$$ 2. This produces a **triangular barrier** of width $w = \phi/(eE)$ at the Fermi level. 3. The Schrödinger equation is solved in the WKB approximation for a triangular barrier, giving the transmission coefficient: $$T(E) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\sqrt{2m_e}}{3\hbar eE}\,\phi^{3/2}\right)$$ 4. The current density is obtained by integrating the incident electron flux (supply function) times $T$ over energy. Assuming $T = 0$ K Fermi distribution, the integration yields: $$J = \frac{e^{3} E^{2}}{8\pi h \phi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{8\pi\sqrt{2m_e}}{3 h eE}\,\phi^{3/2}\right)$$ 5. Grouping constants as $A$ and $B$: $$\boxed{\,J = \frac{A\,E^{2}}{\phi}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)\,}$$ with $A \approx 1.54 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{A·eV/V}^2$ and $B \approx 6.83 \times 10^{9}\;\text{V·eV}^{-3/2}\text{·m}^{-1}$. **F-N Plot (2 marks):** Taking the natural log of the F-N equation: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{J}{E^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{A}{\phi}\right) - \frac{B\,\phi^{3/2}}{E}$$ Plotting $\ln(J/E^2)$ on the y-axis vs $1/E$ on the x-axis gives a **straight line with negative slope**. This linearity is the diagnostic signature of true field emission. - The **slope** = $-B\,\phi^{3/2}$ → extract the **effective work function** $\phi$. - The **intercept** = $\ln(A/\phi)$ → verify the prefactor. If the plot is non-linear, the emission mechanism is *not* field emission (could be thermionic, Schottky, or leakage). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] **(a)** Compare **FED with CRT and LCD** in terms of construction, working principle, picture quality, response time, viewing angle, and power consumption. State **two major disadvantages** of FED. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain **Carbon Nanotube (CNT) FEDs**. Why are CNTs preferred over Spindt Mo tips in modern designs? **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks **Comparison table (5 marks):** | Parameter | FED | CRT | LCD | |---|---|---|---| | Construction | Two glass plates with vacuum gap; micro-emitters + phosphor | Single evacuated glass bulb with electron guns | Two polarizers with liquid crystal layer; backlight | | Working principle | Field emission of electrons from tips/CNTs | Thermionic emission from heated cathode, then magnetic deflection | Voltage-controlled molecular reorientation modulates backlight | | Picture quality | CRT-like; wide gamut, high contrast | Excellent; reference standard | Good but limited gamut | | Response time | < 1 ms (electron transit ~ps) | < 1 ms | 5–10 ms (slowest) | | Viewing angle | ~180° | ~180° | 120–170° | | Power consumption | Moderate | High (heater + deflection) | Lowest (backlight dominant) | | Thickness | ~5 mm | > 300 mm | ~5 mm | | Vacuum needed? | Yes | Yes | No | **Two major disadvantages of FED (2 marks):** 1. **Manufacturing complexity & cost:** Producing millions of uniform sharp micro-tips or aligned CNTs with sub-50 nm precision is technologically demanding. Defect densities drastically reduce yield. 2. **Vacuum maintenance & spacer issues:** The thin vacuum gap requires spacers that can sometimes be **visible** as dark lines in the image. Maintaining vacuum over a 10-year lifetime is also challenging — any outgassing destroys the display. *(Optional 3rd disadvantage: Limited screen size — FEDs are hard to scale beyond 50–60 inches economically.)* --- #### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks **What are CNTs? (2 marks):** Carbon nanotubes are cylindrical molecules of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice — either **single-walled (SWCNT)** or **multi-walled (MWCNT)**. Their diameter is on the order of 1–50 nm and length several µm. They possess: - Extremely **high aspect ratio** → very large $\beta$. - Excellent **electrical conductivity** (metallic or semiconducting). - High **mechanical strength** and chemical stability. - Low **effective work function** (~4.5–5.0 eV). **CNT FED construction (2 marks):** - A CNT paste is **screen-printed** onto the cathode lines, OR CNTs are grown by **CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition)** directly on the substrate. - A dielectric layer and gate electrode are then fabricated on top, with the CNT tips protruding through gate holes. - The rest of the panel (vacuum gap, anode, phosphors) is identical to the Spindt FED. **Why CNTs are preferred over Spindt Mo tips (3 marks):** 1. **Lower turn-on voltage:** CNTs achieve emission at $E \sim 1\text{–}3 \;\text{V/µm}$ vs ~$5 \;\text{V/µm}$ for Mo tips — driver ICs can be cheaper and lower-power. 2. **Higher current density & longer life:** CNTs are more resistant to ion bombardment and residual-gas poisoning, giving > 10,000 hours of stable operation. 3. **Cheaper, scalable manufacturing:** Screen-printing CNTs is compatible with large-glass-panel processing, whereas Spindt tips require sub-micron lithography and vacuum evaporation — high cost and low yield. 4. **Mechanical robustness:** CNTs don't blunt as easily as Mo tips under high current, which preserves $\beta$ over time. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing thermionic and field emission:** A common KTU answer that says "the cathode is heated" is **wrong** for FEDs. Mark deduction: 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the gate electrode:** When describing a Spindt FED, many students omit the **gate**. The gate is the *key* addressing element — losing it loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Wrong F-N constants:** Writing $A = 1.54 \times 10^{-6}$ without units, or quoting the equation without specifying that $\phi$ is in **eV** (not Joules) — these are typical 1-mark deductions. > 4. **F-N plot axes:** The plot is $\ln(J/E^2)$ vs $1/E$, **not** $\ln(J)$ vs $E$. This is a recurring KTU viva pitfall. > 5. **Spacers vs Cathode Spacers:** Students often describe the cathode-to-anode gap spacers as if they were on the rear plate — they are **vertical pillars** spanning the gap, placed every few mm. > 6. **Mixing up CNT type:** SWCNT vs MWCNT distinction is rarely required at KTU; mentioning "vertically aligned multi-walled CNTs" is sufficient. > 7. **Forgetting anode voltage range:** Saying "high voltage" without numbers. Always quote **300 V – 10 kV** for FEDs to earn full credit. > 8. **Confusing field enhancement factor β with permittivity ε:** Use $\beta$ (dimensionless), not $\varepsilon$. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 60-second revision before entering the KTU exam hall.** - ☐ **FED = flat CRT** — vacuum + cold electron emission + phosphor. - ☐ Two plates: **cathode plate (emitters + gate)** and **anode plate (ITO + RGB phosphor)**. - ☐ Vacuum gap: **1–2 mm**, sealed with **spacers** and **getter**. - ☐ Spindt FED uses **Mo micro-tips**; modern FEDs prefer **CNTs**. - ☐ Emission mechanism: **Fowler-Nordheim quantum tunneling** (cold emission, no heater). - ☐ Turn-on field: **~5 × 10⁹ V/m**; for CNTs lower. - ☐ Anode voltage: **300 V to 10 kV** (accelerates electrons). - ☐ Light generation: **cathodoluminescence** in **RGB phosphors**. - ☐ Addressing: **row scan (gate) + column data (anode)** with **PWM** for grayscale. - ☐ **F-N equation:** $J = (A E^2/\phi) \exp(-B\phi^{3/2}/E)$ — memorize! - ☐ **F-N plot:** $\ln(J/E^2)$ vs $1/E$ → straight line, slope gives $\phi$. - ☐ **Advantages over LCD:** wider view angle, faster response, better contrast & gamut, wide temperature range. - ☐ **Disadvantages:** manufacturing cost, vacuum maintenance, spacer visibility, scaling difficulty. - ☐ **Key constants to memorize:** $A \approx 1.54 \times 10^{-6}$, $B \approx 6.83 \times 10^{9}$ (SI units, $\phi$ in eV). - ☐ **Field enhancement:** $\beta \approx h/r$; sharp tip ⇒ high $\beta$ ⇒ lower $V_g$. - ☐ **Transit time:** picosecond range — explains ultra-fast response. - ☐ **Phosphor color recipes:** Red ZnCdS:Ag, Green ZnS:Cu,Al, Blue ZnS:Ag. - ☐ **Real-world niche:** military/avionics/medical monitors where CRT-like quality + flat form + wide temperature is needed. - ☐ **Always** mention both **advantages AND disadvantages** in long-answer questions to score full marks.

topics.title: Organic Light Emitting Device Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERTAINENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organic Light Emitting Device Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organic Light Emitting Device (OLED)**: An OLED is a thin-film electroluminescent display technology in which the emissive layer is composed of an organic compound (carbon-based semiconductor) that emits light in response to an applied electric current. Unlike LCDs which require a backlight, OLEDs are **self-emissive** devices, meaning each pixel generates its own light.

According to the KTU 2024 OEECT611 syllabus, OLED technology is classified under **flat-panel emissive displays** and represents the third generation of display technology, succeeding CRTs and LCDs. The organic layers are sandwiched between two electrodes (anode and cathode), and when DC current flows, electrons and holes recombine in the emissive layer producing photons through the process of **electroluminescence**.

The typical KTU-board definition to memorize:

> **OLED = Organic (carbon-based polymer or small molecule) + Light Emitting (electroluminescent recombination) + Device (current-driven thin-film sandwich structure).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Glowing Sandwich" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a sandwich where:
> - The **top bread slice** = Transparent Anode (ITO - Indium Tin Oxide). It "lets light out" like a glass window.
> - The **bottom bread slice** = Metallic Cathode (Al, Ca, Mg:Ag). It acts like a mirror reflector.
> - The **filling layers** = Organic semiconductors. The tomato slice is the **Hole Transport Layer (HTL)**, the cheese slice is the **Emissive Layer (EML)**, and the lettuce is the **Electron Transport Layer (ETL)**.
> - When you apply voltage (a "bite" of electricity), positive charges flow down from the top, negative charges flow up from the bottom. They meet at the **cheese (emissive layer)** and "annihilate" with a tiny flash of light — just like a miniature lightning bolt of photons inside a sandwich!

The total thickness of this "sandwich" is roughly **100–500 nm**, which is about **200 times thinner than a human hair (≈ 100 µm)**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Operating voltage | $V_{op}$ | 2 V – 10 V (typically **3 V – 5 V**) |
| Organic layer thickness | $d$ | 50 nm – 200 nm |
| Luminance | $L$ | 100 – 1000 $\text{cd/m}^2$ (TV: 1000 $\text{cd/m}^2$) |
| Luminous efficacy | $\eta_L$ | 5 – 80 $\text{lm/W}$ |
| Color gamut | NTSC | 100% – 120% (vs LCD 70% – 90%) |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | **−40 °C to +85 °C** |
| Viewing angle | $\theta$ | **≈ 180°** |
| Response time | $t_r$ | < **1 ms** (LCD ≈ 5–10 ms) |

> [!NOTE]
> The energy bandgap $E_g$ of typical OLED emitters lies between **1.8 eV (red) and 3.1 eV (blue)**, dictating the wavelength of emitted light through $\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{E_g}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an OLED

A standard bottom-emission OLED is built as a **multi-layer thin-film stack**. Each layer has a precise HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) and LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) level for efficient charge transport.

| Layer (bottom to top) | Material (typical) | Function | Thickness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | Glass / Flexible Plastic | Mechanical support | 0.5 – 1.1 mm |
| Anode | ITO (work function ≈ **4.7 eV**) | Hole injection | 100 – 200 nm |
| Hole Injection Layer (HIL) | PEDOT:PSS, CuPc | Smooths hole injection | 10 – 50 nm |
| Hole Transport Layer (HTL) | NPB, TPD | Transports holes to EML | 20 – 60 nm |
| Emissive Layer (EML) | Alq3 (green), DCM (red), DPVBi (blue) | **Light generation** | 20 – 50 nm |
| Electron Transport Layer (ETL) | Alq3, BCP, Bphen | Transports electrons | 20 – 50 nm |
| Electron Injection Layer (EIL) | LiF, CsF | Reduces cathode work function | 0.5 – 1 nm |
| Cathode | Al, Ca, Mg:Ag (work function ≈ 2.5–4.2 eV) | Electron injection | 100 – 200 nm |
| Encapsulation | Glass / BariC / Thin-film barrier | Moisture & oxygen protection | — |

## 2.2 Operational Mechanism — The "Five-Step" Process

1. **Charge Injection**: Electrons are injected from the cathode into the LUMO of the ETL; holes are injected from the anode into the HOMO of the HTL.
2. **Charge Transport**: Under applied electric field $E = V/d$, electrons drift toward the anode and holes toward the cathode.
3. **Charge Recombination**: Opposite carriers meet in the EML, forming a neutral bound state called an **exciton** (binding energy ≈ 0.5–1.0 eV).
4. **Radiative Decay**: The exciton relaxes from $S_1$ (singlet) to $S_0$ ground state, emitting a photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = E_g$.
5. **Light Extraction**: Photons exit through the transparent ITO anode (bottom emission) or through a semi-transparent cathode (top emission).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spin Statistics**: Per quantum-mechanical spin selection rules, only **25%** of excitons are **singlets** (radiative, light-producing) and **75%** are **triplets** (non-radiative in conventional fluorescent OLEDs). Phosphorescent OLEDs (PHOLEDs) harvest the triplets using heavy-metal complexes (Ir, Pt), pushing internal quantum efficiency (IQE) toward **100%**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV·nm}}{E_g}$ | Wavelength of emitted photon | nm |
| 2 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | eV / J |
| 3 | $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \times \eta_{out-coupling}$ | External Quantum Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{out} \approx \dfrac{1}{2n^2}$ (Lambertian) | Light extraction efficiency ≈ **20%** for $n = 1.5$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{\Phi_V}{A \cdot \pi}$ (Lambertian) | Luminance from luminous flux | $\text{cd/m}^2$ |
| 6 | $I_{OLED} = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right)$ | OLED diode current (Shockley-like) | A |
| 7 | $P_{consumed} = V \times I$ | DC power consumption | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{power} = \dfrac{\Phi_V}{P_{in}}$ | Power efficiency (luminous efficacy) | $\text{lm/W}$ |
| 9 | $t_{90} \propto \left( L_0 / L \right)^n$ | Lifetime scaling (Weibull) | hours |
| 10 | $V_{th} = \dfrac{E_{LUMO}^{EML} - E_{HOMO}^{EML}}{q}$ | Threshold voltage ≈ bandgap | V |
| 11 | $\gamma_{balance} = \dfrac{J_{holes}}{J_{electrons}}$ | Charge balance factor (ideal = 1) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Color gamut (\%NTSC)} = \dfrac{A_{triangle(RGB)}}{A_{NTSC}}$ | Display color coverage | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Board Tip:** Always remember the **$1/2n^2$ rule**. For glass substrate $n = 1.5$, only $\approx 22.2\%$ of generated photons can escape; the rest are trapped by **total internal reflection (TIR)** at the ITO/glass and glass/air interfaces. This is why KTU questions often ask "why OLEDs need light-extraction enhancement films."

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

OLEDs are used in:
- **Smartphones** (Samsung Galaxy Super AMOLED displays)
- **Televisions** (LG OLED55/65/77/88" panels, 4K & 8K)
- **Wearables** (Apple Watch, fitness bands — flexible OLEDs)
- **Automotive dashboards** (Mercedes MBUX Hyperscreen, Cadillac)
- **AR/VR microdisplays** (eMagin, Sony ECX339A — 0.5" 4K)
- **Solid-state lighting** (OLED panels by LG, Osram, Konica Minolta)

The **commercial relevance** for KTU board: students may be asked to justify OLED's superiority in **contrast ratio** because each pixel can be switched off (true black), giving **infinite contrast (∞ : 1)** — unbeatable by LCD/LED-backlit systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photon Energy from Bandgap (Einstein's Photoemission)

We begin from Planck–Einstein quantization of light.

**Step 1:** Light is emitted in discrete quanta of energy:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu$$

where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $\nu$ is the optical frequency in Hz.

**Step 2:** Express frequency in terms of wavelength $\lambda$ and speed of light $c$:

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the photon energy:

$$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to electron-volts using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J:

$$E_{photon} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot \lambda(\text{m})}$$

**Step 5:** Numerically simplify the constant:

$$hc = 1.989 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m} = 1240 \text{ eV·nm}$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form used in OLED calculations:

$$\boxed{\lambda (\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})}}$$

**Worked Example (Board Style):**
A red OLED emitter has $E_g = 2.0$ eV. Find the peak emission wavelength.

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{2.0} = 620 \text{ nm} \;\; (\text{Deep Red, as expected})$$

For a blue emitter, $E_g = 2.8$ eV → $\lambda = 443$ nm.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — External Quantum Efficiency (EQE)

EQE is the most important figure of merit. It is the product of four microscopic efficiencies.

**Step 1:** Define the four factors:
- $\eta_{rad}$ = fraction of excitons that decay radiatively (singlet or phosphorescent),
- $\gamma$ = charge balance factor (fraction of injected carriers that recombine),
- $q$ = probability that a radiative exciton produces a photon (PL quantum yield),
- $\eta_{out}$ = fraction of photons that escape the device.

**Step 2:** Internal Quantum Efficiency (IQE):

$$\eta_{int} = \gamma \cdot \eta_{rad} \cdot q$$

For phosphorescent OLEDs, $\eta_{rad} \rightarrow 1$ because both singlet and triplet excitons are harvested.

**Step 3:** External Quantum Efficiency:

$$\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \eta_{out}$$

**Step 4:** Lambertian light extraction from a high-index medium:

$$\eta_{out} = \frac{1}{2 n^2}$$

For organic $n \approx 1.7$, $\eta_{out} \approx 0.173$ (i.e., 17.3%).

**Step 5:** Numerical result for an idealized PHOLED:

$$\eta_{ext} = (1)(1)(1)(0.173) = 0.173 = 17.3\%$$

**Step 6:** With micro-lens arrays and out-coupling enhancement films, $\eta_{out}$ can be raised to ~40–50%:

$$\eta_{ext}^{enhanced} \approx (1)(1)(1)(0.5) = 50\%$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — OLED Diode Equation (Current–Voltage Relation)

Unlike inorganic LEDs, OLEDs are **injection-limited** because the organic materials are **amorphous and disordered**. The current follows a **trap-charge-limited (TCL)** or **space-charge-limited (SCLC)** model, not the standard Shockley equation. However, an empirical form commonly used in KTU exam questions is:

**Step 1:** Modified Shockley form for organic diodes:

$$I = I_0 \left( e^{\frac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right)$$

where $n$ is the **ideality factor** (typically 2–6 for OLEDs, compared to 1–2 for inorganic LEDs).

**Step 2:** Define thermal voltage at room temperature $T = 300$ K:

$$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = 0.02585 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** At large forward bias, the "−1" term is negligible:

$$I \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V}{nV_T}}$$

**Step 4:** For a practical green OLED with $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ A, $n = 3$, and $V = 5$ V:

$$I = 10^{-12} \cdot e^{\frac{5}{3 \times 0.02585}} = 10^{-12} \cdot e^{64.4}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the exponent:

$$e^{64.4} \approx 7.5 \times 10^{27}$$

**Step 6:** Final result:

$$I \approx 7.5 \times 10^{15} \text{ A} \quad (\text{clearly an overestimate — actual OLED uses current limit})$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, OLEDs are **current-driven**, not voltage-driven, because luminance $L \propto J$ (current density), not voltage. The KTU board examiner may ask: *"Why is OLED operated in constant-current mode?"* The answer is to **prevent thermal runaway** and ensure **uniform luminance across pixels**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Luminance from Luminous Flux

**Step 1:** Luminance $L$ in $\text{cd/m}^2$ for a Lambertian (perfect diffuser) emitter:

$$L = \frac{I_v}{A_{proj}}$$

**Step 2:** Equivalently, in terms of luminous flux $\Phi_v$:

$$L = \frac{\Phi_v}{\pi \cdot A}$$

**Step 3:** For an OLED with $\Phi_v = 10$ lm emitted into a hemisphere, $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$:

$$L = \frac{10}{\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 3.18 \times 10^4 \text{ cd/m}^2}$$

**Step 4:** Such a high luminance is achievable in pulsed mode; DC mode typically limits to $L \approx 1000$ – $5000 \text{ cd/m}^2$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Lifetime Scaling Law

**Step 1:** OLED lifetime is defined as the time for luminance to drop to 90% of initial value ($t_{90}$), or 50% ($t_{50}$).

**Step 2:** Acceleration law (Weibull / stretched exponential):

$$L(t) = L_0 \cdot e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)^\beta}$$

**Step 3:** Empirical scaling: doubling luminance reduces lifetime by an acceleration factor $A$:

$$\frac{t_{90}(L)}{t_{90}(L_0)} = \left( \frac{L_0}{L} \right)^n$$

where $n \approx 1.5$ – $2.0$ for typical fluorescent emitters.

**Step 4:** Worked example: If $t_{90}$ at 1000 $\text{cd/m}^2$ is 50,000 h, what is it at 2000 $\text{cd/m}^2$ with $n = 2$?

$$t_{90}(2000) = 50{,}000 \times \left(\frac{1000}{2000}\right)^2 = 50{,}000 \times 0.25 = 12{,}500 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5:** This is the **KTU-favorite derivation** — it shows that operating at lower luminance (e.g., dimmer TV) dramatically extends OLED lifetime.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — OLED Operational Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Voltage Source] --> B[Anode ITO Transparent Conductor]
    B --> C[Hole Injection Layer HIL PEDOT PSS]
    C --> D[Hole Transport Layer HTL NPB]
    D --> E[Emissive Layer EML Organic Dye or Phosphor]
    F[Cathode Metal Al or Ca Mg] --> G[Electron Injection Layer EIL LiF]
    G --> H[Electron Transport Layer ETL Alq3]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Exciton Formation S1 Triplet T1]
    I --> J[Radiative Decay S1 to S0 Photon Emission]
    I --> K[Non Radiative Decay T1 to S0 Heat]
    J --> L[Photon Out Coupling]
    L --> M[Substrate Glass or Flexible Film]
    M --> N[Viewer Eye or Light Sensor]
    E -.->|Heat dissipation| O[Substrate Thermal Management]
    O --> P[Encapsulation Barrier Film]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — OLED Display System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDMI or DisplayPort Source] --> B[Video Processor TCON]
    B --> C[Source Driver Column IC]
    B --> D[Gate Driver Row IC]
    C --> E[OLED Pixel Matrix 1920 x 1080 or 3840 x 2160]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Per Pixel TFT Backplane LTPS or IGZO]
    F --> G[Organic Stack Anode HTL EML ETL Cathode]
    G --> H[Light Output to Viewer]
    E --> I[DC DC Converter and VCOM Reference]
    I --> C
    I --> D
    E --> J[Optical Out Coupling Films]
    J --> H
    E --> K[Encapsulation TFE or Cover Glass]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree Diagram — OLED Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OLED[OLED Organic Light Emitting Device]
    OLED --> MAT[Matrix Addressing Type]
    OLED --> MOL[Molecule Type]
    OLED --> EMIS[Emission Type]

    MAT --> PMOLED[PMOLED Passive Matrix]
    MAT --> AMOLED[AMOLED Active Matrix with TFT]
    AMOLED --> LTPS[LTPS Low Temp Poly Silicon]
    AMOLED --> IGZO[IGZO Indium Gallium Zinc Oxide]
    AMOLED --> LTPO[LTPO LTPS plus Oxide hybrid]

    MOL --> SMOLED[SMOLED Small Molecule OLED Vacuum Deposited]
    MOL --> PLED[PLED Polymer LED Solution Processed]
    MOL --> TADF[TADF Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence]

    EMIS --> FLOLED[Fluorescent OLED 25 percent IQE limit]
    EMIS --> PHOLED[Phosphorescent OLED Up to 100 percent IQE]
    EMIS --> HYB[Hyperfluorescent and TADF OLEDs 100 percent stable]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Pixel Operation Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Row as Gate Driver Row
    participant TFT as Pixel TFT Switch
    participant Cap as Storage Capacitor
    participant OLED as OLED Anode to Cathode
    participant Col as Source Driver Column

    Note over Row,Col: One frame period 16.7 ms at 60 Hz
    Row->>TFT: Gate pulse ON select row
    TFT->>Cap: Conduct data current
    Col->>Cap: Program data voltage Vdata
    Cap->>OLED: Hold charge during frame
    OLED->>OLED: Continuous current flow emits photons
    Row->>TFT: Gate pulse OFF next row
    Note over OLED: Emission continues due to storage capacitor hold
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — OLED vs LCD vs LED

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Display Technology] --> B[OLED Self Emissive Organic]
    A --> C[LCD Backlight plus Liquid Crystal Modulator]
    A --> D[LED Inorganic LED Backlit LCD or Micro LED Self Emissive]

    B --> B1[Contrast Infinite 1 to 1000000]
    B --> B2[Response time less than 1 ms]
    B --> B3[True Black Yes]
    B --> B4[Thickness less than 1 mm possible]
    B --> B5[Flexible Yes Plastic Substrate]

    C --> C1[Contrast 1000 to 5000]
    C --> C2[Response time 5 to 10 ms]
    C --> C3[True Black No Backlight Leak]
    C --> C4[Thickness 2 to 5 mm typical]
    C --> C5[Flexible Limited IPS VA]

    D --> D1[Contrast 5000 to 10000]
    D --> D2[Response time 1 to 5 ms]
    D --> D3[True Black Local Dimming only]
    D --> D4[Thickness 3 to 8 mm with Backlight]
    D --> D5[Flexible No Standard LED LCD]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define an OLED. Why is it called a self-emissive display?

**Model Answer:**
An **Organic Light Emitting Device (OLED)** is a thin-film, current-driven electroluminescent display in which the active light-emitting layer is composed of **organic (carbon-based) semiconductor** material, sandwiched between two electrodes. When DC current is passed through the device, electrons and holes injected from the cathode and anode respectively recombine in the emissive layer to form excitons, which decay radiatively to emit visible light.

It is called a **self-emissive display** because the pixel itself **directly generates light** through electroluminescence — no separate backlight, edge-light, or color filter is required, unlike LCD/LED displays. Each sub-pixel (R, G, B) is an independent light source, and can be switched **fully OFF** to produce true black, yielding **infinite contrast ratio**.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the role of ITO in an OLED? Why is it used as the anode?

**Model Answer:**
**ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** is a **wide-bandgap (E_g ≈ 3.5–4.0 eV), highly transparent (transmittance > 85% in visible range) degenerate n-type semiconductor** with a sheet resistance of typically **10–100 Ω/sq** and a work function of **4.5–4.7 eV**.

**Roles:**
1. **Transparent conductor** — it allows emitted photons to escape from the device (bottom-emission OLED).
2. **Hole injector** — its high work function aligns with the HOMO level of common HTL materials (NPB ≈ 5.4 eV), enabling efficient hole injection with low energy barrier.
3. **Current spreading layer** — ensures uniform current distribution across large pixel areas.

For these reasons, ITO is the industry-standard **anode** material for OLEDs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an OLED. Discuss the role of each functional layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance of OLED with LCD and LED displays. Justify why OLED is preferred for high-end televisions and smartphones. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Construction (Cross-sectional description, bottom-emission OLED):**

A standard OLED is built as a layered thin-film stack on a transparent substrate. The layers from bottom to top are:

1. **Substrate (Glass, 0.5–1.1 mm)** — mechanical support, optically transparent.
2. **Anode — ITO (100–200 nm)** — transparent conductive hole injector.
3. **HIL — PEDOT:PSS (10–50 nm)** — smooths hole injection; planarizes the ITO surface.
4. **HTL — NPB (20–60 nm)** — transports holes; blocks electrons.
5. **EML — emissive dopant in host (20–50 nm)** — site of exciton formation and photon emission.
6. **ETL — Alq3 (20–50 nm)** — transports electrons; blocks holes.
7. **EIL — LiF (0.5–1 nm)** — lowers effective cathode work function.
8. **Cathode — Al (100–200 nm)** — reflective electron injector.
9. **Encapsulation** — protects organic layers from moisture/oxygen (water ingress < $10^{-6} \text{ g/m}^2$/day).

**Working Principle (5 steps):**
- **(i) Charge Injection:** Under forward bias, electrons tunnel from cathode → ETL LUMO; holes from anode → HTL HOMO.
- **(ii) Charge Transport:** Under internal field $E \approx 10^5$–$10^6$ V/cm, carriers drift toward the EML.
- **(iii) Recombination:** In the EML, electrons and holes capture each other, forming bound electron-hole pairs called **excitons** (Coulombically bound, binding energy ≈ 0.5 eV).
- **(iv) Radiative Decay:** Exciton transitions from excited state to ground state, releasing photon of energy $E = E_g$ and wavelength $\lambda = 1240 / E_g$.
- **(v) Light Out-coupling:** Photons traverse the transparent ITO and glass substrate to reach the viewer.

**Role of Each Layer:**

| Layer | Role |
|---|---|
| Anode (ITO) | Hole injection, transparent window |
| HIL | Reduces interface traps, improves hole injection |
| HTL | Selective hole transport, electron blocking |
| EML | Electroluminescence (light generation) |
| ETL | Selective electron transport, hole blocking |
| EIL | Reduces cathode interface barrier |
| Cathode | Electron injection, reflector (top-emission: semi-transparent) |
| Encapsulation | Hermetic sealing (moisture/oxygen barrier) |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | OLED | LCD (TFT) | LED-backlit LCD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backlight required | No (self-emissive) | Yes | Yes (LEDs) |
| Contrast ratio | **∞ : 1** (true black) | 1000 : 1 | 3000 : 1 (with local dimming) |
| Response time | **< 1 ms** | 5–10 ms | 5–10 ms |
| Color gamut | **100%–120% NTSC** | 70%–90% NTSC | 80%–95% NTSC |
| Viewing angle | **≈ 180°** | 120°–160° | 140°–170° |
| Thickness | **< 1 mm (flexible)** | 2–5 mm | 3–8 mm |
| Power consumption | Lower (black = 0 power) | Higher (constant backlight) | Higher |
| Lifespan | 30,000–100,000 h | 50,000–100,000 h | 50,000–100,000 h |
| Flexible display | **Yes** (plastic substrate) | No | No |
| Cost | High | Low | Medium |

**Justification for High-End TVs and Smartphones:**

1. **True Black & Infinite Contrast:** Each pixel switches off completely, delivering cinema-grade contrast. Critical for **HDR (High Dynamic Range)** content on Netflix/Amazon Prime.
2. **Wider Color Gamut:** Up to 120% NTSC → more vivid, true-to-life colors. Critical for medical imaging, color-critical graphics.
3. **Faster Response Time:** < 1 ms → no motion blur in fast-paced gaming/sports.
4. **Ultra-Thin & Flexible:** Enables curved TVs, rollable displays (LG Signature OLED R), and foldable phones (Samsung Galaxy Z Fold, Apple foldable).
5. **Lower Power at Dark UIs:** Since black pixels consume zero current, dark-mode smartphone UIs can extend battery life by **30–60%**.
6. **Wide Viewing Angle:** 180° viewing makes OLED ideal for living-room TVs and shared viewing.

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **electroluminescence** in organic materials. Derive an expression for the peak emission wavelength in terms of the energy bandgap. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **PMOLED and AMOLED**? Discuss their construction, addressing schemes, advantages, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Electroluminescence (EL) in Organic Materials:**

Electroluminescence is the **phenomenon of light emission from a material in response to an electric current or strong electric field**. In organic semiconductors, EL occurs through the following microscopic mechanism:

- Organic molecules have **discrete π-electron energy levels** rather than continuous bands, but the HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital ≈ valence band) and LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital ≈ conduction band) can be treated analogously.
- The energy gap $E_g = E_{LUMO} - E_{HOMO}$ is typically **1.5 eV to 3.5 eV** for visible-light emitters.
- When an electron and hole recombine, the excited state (exciton) relaxes to the ground state by emitting a photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = E_g$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Photon energy from Planck's law:

$$E_{ph} = h\nu$$

**Step 2:** Express frequency using speed of light:

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$E_{ph} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 5:** Insert numerical constants ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times E_g \text{(J)}}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify to the standard OLED engineering constant:

$$\boxed{\lambda (\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{E_g \text{(eV)}}}$$

**Worked Example:** For a green emitter with $E_g = 2.4$ eV:

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{2.4} \approx 517 \text{ nm} \quad (\text{green, as expected})$$

**Stokes Shift:** Real emission peaks are slightly red-shifted due to vibrational relaxation (~30–100 nm) and excimer formation in amorphous films.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**PMOLED (Passive Matrix OLED):**

- **Construction:** Simple crossed-stripe anode and cathode lines forming an XY matrix. No TFT (Thin-Film Transistor) per pixel. The ITO anode columns and metal cathode rows intersect at each pixel.
- **Addressing:** Rows are scanned one at a time. Current is driven through the selected row × column intersection.
- **Disadvantages:**  
  - **Scan duty-cycle loss** — each row is OFF most of the frame → must be driven at peak brightness, accelerating degradation.  
  - Limited to **< 200 ppi** resolution.  
  - Limited to **small panels** (≤ 3–5 inch).  
  - High peak current → fast aging.
- **Applications:** Sub-displays in mobile phones, MP3 players, car dashboards, simple indicators.

**AMOLED (Active Matrix OLED):**

- **Construction:** Each sub-pixel has its own **2T1C circuit** (2 TFTs + 1 storage capacitor). The backplane is **LTPS (Low-Temperature Poly-Silicon)** or **IGZO (Indium-Gallium-Zinc-Oxide)** TFT.
- **Addressing:** Each row's TFTs are turned on by a gate pulse; the data voltage charges the storage capacitor, which holds the current through the OLED for the entire frame.
- **Advantages:**  
  - **Continuous driving** (not pulsed) → lower stress, longer lifetime.  
  - High resolution (**> 500 ppi**, up to 800+ ppi in smartphones).  
  - Large panel sizes (**up to 88" 8K TVs**).  
  - **Lower power** for the same luminance.  
  - Enables integration with **LTPO** for variable refresh rate (1–120 Hz).
- **Applications:** Smartphones, tablets, smartwatches, laptops, OLED TVs (LG, Sony, Samsung QD-OLED), AR/VR microdisplays, automotive infotainment.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Aspect | PMOLED | AMOLED |
|---|---|---|
| TFT backplane | No | Yes (LTPS/IGZO) |
| Resolution | < 200 ppi | > 500 ppi |
| Panel size | ≤ 5 inch | 1"–88"+ |
| Power | Higher (peak current) | Lower (continuous) |
| Lifetime | Shorter | Longer |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Use case | Indicators, sub-displays | Smartphones, TVs |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "OLED" with "LED":** OLED = organic (carbon-based, thin film, low voltage 2–10 V). LED = inorganic GaN/GaAs, high current, high luminance. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Missing the ITO purpose:** Always mention **work-function alignment (4.7 eV)** and **transparency > 85%**, not just "transparent conductor."
> 3. **Forgetting the $1/2n^2$ extraction limit:** If asked "why OLED needs an out-coupling film," the answer MUST include the formula $\eta_{out} = 1/2n^2$. Partial marks lost if omitted.
> 4. **Saying OLED is voltage-driven:** WRONG. OLED is **current-driven** because luminance is proportional to current density. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Confusing PMOLED/AMOLED addressing:** Students often forget that PMOLED uses **row-by-row scanning** and AMOLED uses **TFT + storage capacitor hold**.
> 6. **Lifetime law omission:** When asked about lifetime scaling, always quote $t \propto (L_0 / L)^n$ with $n = 1.5$–$2$.
> 7. **No diagram in part (a):** KTU board penalizes **2 marks** if the cross-sectional sketch is missing labels for **all 7 layers**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- ☐ **OLED = Organic (carbon) + Light Emitting (electroluminescence) + Device.** Self-emissive, no backlight.
- ☐ **Standard stack:** Substrate / ITO anode / HIL / HTL / EML / ETL / EIL / Cathode / Encapsulation.
- ☐ **ITO work function ≈ 4.7 eV**, transparency > 85%, sheet resistance 10–100 Ω/sq.
- ☐ **Operating voltage:** 2–10 V (typically 3–5 V). **Current-driven**, not voltage-driven.
- ☐ **Luminance = 100–1000 cd/m²**. Power efficiency 5–80 lm/W.
- ☐ **Key formula:** $\lambda (\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1240}{E_g \text{(eV)}}$
- ☐ **Out-coupling limit:** $\eta_{out} = \dfrac{1}{2n^2} \approx 20\%$. Use micro-lens arrays, scattering layers to enhance.
- ☐ **Exciton spin statistics:** 25% singlet (radiative) + 75% triplet. Phosphorescent OLEDs (Ir, Pt complexes) harvest all 100%.
- ☐ **True black = infinite contrast** because pixel OFF → no current → no light.
- ☐ **PMOLED** = no TFT, row-scanning, small panels (< 5"). **AMOLED** = 2T1C per pixel, large/high-res panels, LTPS/IGZO backplane.
- ☐ **Response time < 1 ms**, viewing angle ≈ 180°, color gamut up to 120% NTSC.
- ☐ **OLED vs LCD:** OLED better in contrast, response, color, viewing angle, flexibility. LCD better in cost, lifespan, peak brightness.
- ☐ **Lifetime scaling:** $t_{90} \propto (L_0 / L)^n$, $n = 1.5$–$2$. Lower brightness = longer life.
- ☐ **Common emitters:** Alq3 (green), DCM (red), DPVBi (blue), FIrpic (blue phosphorescent), Ir(ppy)3 (green phosphorescent).
- ☐ **Common HTL:** NPB, TPD. **Common ETL:** Alq3, BCP, Bphen.
- ☐ **Encapsulation critical:** Water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) must be < $10^{-6} \text{ g/m}^2$/day.
- ☐ **Applications:** Smartphones, OLED TVs, smartwatches, AR/VR microdisplays, automotive dashboards, solid-state lighting.
- ☐ **Derivations to practice:** (1) $\lambda = 1240/E_g$, (2) EQE = $\eta_{int} \times \eta_{out}$, (3) OLED diode current equation, (4) Luminance from flux, (5) Lifetime acceleration law.
- ☐ **Pitfall:** Don't say OLED is voltage-driven; don't confuse OLED with inorganic LED; always draw **labelled cross-section**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Television of the Future: Holographic TV
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT611 - ENTERPRISE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Television of the Future: Holographic TV

# Television of the Future: Holographic TV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** This topic introduces the student to the *next-generation display paradigm* — true 3D autostereoscopic imaging using wavefront reconstruction. It extends the conventional 2D flat-panel display and stereoscopic 3D TV concepts studied earlier in the module into the domain of **holography**, which captures and reproduces the *complete light field* of a scene.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Holographic Television** is a future display technology that records, transmits, and reconstructs the *complete optical wavefront* (both amplitude and phase) of light reflected or emitted by a real or computer-generated 3D scene, thereby producing a true three-dimensional image that exhibits all natural depth cues — *parallax, accommodation, convergence, and occlusion* — without requiring the viewer to wear special glasses.

The technique is rooted in **Dennis Gabor's principle of holography (1948 Nobel Prize in Physics, 1971)**, which records the *interference pattern* (called a **hologram**) between two mutually coherent light beams — a **reference beam** and an **object beam** — and later reconstructs the original object wavefront by illuminating the recorded interference pattern with a replica of the reference beam, exploiting **Fresnel/Kirchhoff diffraction**.

For a television-grade system, the hologram is captured by a *spatial light modulator (SLM)* array of size typically **1920 × 1080** to **4K/8K** pixels, refreshed at standard video frame rates (≥ **24 Hz** for cinema, **50/60 Hz** for broadcast), and reconstructed by an SLM-based display illuminated by a coherent laser or narrow-band LED source.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Box**
> - **Object Beam:** The light scattered by the 3D scene onto the recording medium.
> - **Reference Beam:** A clean, unmodulated copy of the same coherent source, used to interfere with the object beam.
> - **Hologram:** The recorded intensity pattern that *encodes both amplitude and phase* of the object wave.
> - **Reconstruction Beam:** A duplicate of the reference beam that "plays back" the hologram, regenerating the original wavefront.
> - **SLM (Spatial Light Modulator):** An electronically addressable 2D array (e.g., LCoS, DMD) that imposes phase/amplitude modulation on incident light, enabling real-time holographic display.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in front of a **museum window** through which a real, three-dimensional sculpture is visible:
- If you walk left, you see the *right* side of the sculpture.
- If you walk right, you see the *left* side.
- If you focus near, the front edges sharpen; if you focus far, the back sharpens.
- You can even reach out (visually) and see your hand *occlude* part of the sculpture.

A conventional **photograph** preserves only the *intensity* of the light at the camera sensor — it is essentially a 2D shadow. A **stereoscopic 3D TV** tricks the brain using two slightly different views (one per eye), but it still *fails* the focus test and the parallax test when you move your head.

A **hologram**, by contrast, is not a picture of the object — it is a *frozen interference pattern* of the light waves that came from the object. When you re-illuminate this pattern with the right kind of light, the original waves literally *re-emerge* into space. Your eyes and brain then receive *the very same wavefronts* they would have received from the real object. The result: a "ghost" sculpture inside the window that behaves, to your visual system, as if it were physically real.

> **Analogy Summary:** *A photograph is to a painting what a hologram is to a real window into another world.*

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Holographic TV context) |
|---|---|---|
| Visible light wavelength | $\lambda$ | **380 nm – 750 nm** (often **633 nm He-Ne** or **532 nm green laser**) |
| Hologram pixel pitch | $p$ | **~4 – 8 µm** (for SLM-based digital holography) |
| Coherence length of source | $L_c$ | **> 1 m** (laser); **~10 µm** (LED) |
| Required SLM resolution | $N \times M$ | **≥ 1920 × 1080** up to **8K (7680 × 4320)** |
| Display refresh rate | $f_r$ | **≥ 30 Hz**, target **60 – 120 Hz** |
| Viewable 3D angle (parallax) | $\theta_{3D}$ | **±15° to ±30°** for consumer systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Coherence Matters:** Because holography relies on *interference*, the source must be **spatially and temporally coherent**. This is why ordinary room lighting and sunlight *cannot* form holograms directly — they are incoherent. Lasers (or carefully filtered narrow-band LEDs) are mandatory.

---
