# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, OEECT611]**
Define Augmented Reality. How does it differ from Virtual Reality in terms of display technology and user experience?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Augmented Reality (AR)** is a real-time, interactive technology that overlays computer-generated virtual content onto a user's view of the real world, such that the virtual and real elements coexist and are spatially registered in 3D.
>
> **VR replaces** reality using an *opaque* head-mounted display; the user is fully immersed in a synthetic world and cannot see their physical surroundings.
>
> **AR augments** reality using a *see-through* display (optical or video); the user always perceives the real environment, with virtual content added as a contextual layer.
>
> **Key Differences:**
> - **Display:** AR uses transparent combiners; VR uses opaque screens.
> - **Experience:** AR enhances the real world; VR simulates a new world.
> - **Mobility:** AR allows free movement; VR is constrained to a tracked play area.

*(Award 1 mark each for: correct definition of AR, correct display difference, correct user-experience difference.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, OEECT611]**
List any three types of Augmented Reality with one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **Marker-Based AR** — e.g., a 3D character popping out of a printed QR code on a magazine cover.
> 2. **Markerless (SLAM) AR** — e.g., Pokémon GO placing creatures on a real-world footpath via mobile SLAM.
> 3. **Location-Based AR** — e.g., Google Maps Live View superimposing navigation arrows on a real street.

*(Award 1 mark per correct type-with-example pair.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 2 × 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern, Module 4]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of an Optical See-Through (OST) AR display. With the help of a block diagram, describe the function of a waveguide combiner.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **OST Definition (1 mark):** An Optical See-Through AR display is a transparent display system in which the user views the real world directly through an optical combiner, while virtual content is injected into the same optical path using a micro-display and projection optics.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):**
   - A micro-display (LCoS, micro-OLED, or laser-beam-scanning) generates the virtual image.
   - Collimation optics make the virtual rays parallel so the eye perceives them at a comfortable focal distance.
   - An optical combiner (prism, freeform, or waveguide) merges the virtual rays with the real-world rays entering the eye.
   - The user perceives a single, augmented scene with real and virtual elements co-registered.

3. **Waveguide Combiner (3 marks):**
   - A **waveguide** is a thin slab of high-refractive-index glass/polymer.
   - The virtual image light enters at an **in-coupling grating** at one edge.
   - Light propagates inside the slab by **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**, occurring when the incidence angle exceeds the critical angle $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2 / n_1)$.
   - The light is then extracted toward the eye by an **out-coupling grating** (a surface relief grating or holographic optical element).
   - This architecture allows a slim, glasses-like form factor.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Waveguide-based OST is the dominant architecture in modern AR glasses (HoloLens, Magic Leap, Vuzix) due to its compactness, light weight, and ability to support a wide eye-box.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the concept of Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping (SLAM) in the context of AR. How does Visual-Inertial SLAM improve tracking robustness?

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **SLAM Definition (1 mark):** SLAM is a computational process by which a device simultaneously estimates its own position/orientation (localisation) within, and constructs a map of, an unknown environment.

2. **SLAM in AR (2 marks):**
   - Enables markerless AR by tracking the device's 6-DoF pose from camera input alone.
   - Constructs a sparse (point cloud) or dense (mesh) map of the environment, against which virtual content is anchored.
   - Allows virtual objects to remain "locked" to real surfaces (tables, walls) as the user moves.

3. **Visual-Inertial SLAM (VI-SLAM) (2 marks):**
   - **Visual** component: detects and tracks natural features across consecutive camera frames.
   - **Inertial** component: IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope) measures linear accelerations and angular velocities at high rate (1 kHz).
   - The two are **fused** (typically via Extended Kalman Filter or graph-based optimisation) to produce a drift-corrected 6-DoF pose.
   - **Why it is robust:** cameras give accurate relative pose but suffer at high speed/motion blur; IMUs give high-rate motion but drift over time. Fusing both cancels each other's weaknesses.

4. **ARKit / ARCore Use (1 mark):** Apple ARKit and Google ARCore are production-grade VI-SLAM systems, adding plane detection, light estimation, and relocalisation on top of the core VIO.

5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** VI-SLAM is the engineering backbone of modern mobile AR, enabling markerless, robust, real-time 6-DoF tracking on consumer devices.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2025 Pattern, Module 4]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare Optical See-Through (OST) and Video See-Through (VST) AR displays. Discuss their relative merits in entertainment applications.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Optical See-Through (OST) | Video See-Through (VST) |
|---|---|---|
| Real-world view | Direct optical path | Camera-captured and re-rendered |
| Display hardware | Transparent combiner + micro-display | Opaque display + stereo cameras |
| Latency | Lower (no reprocessing) | Higher (capture + composite) |
| Black levels / contrast | Limited by ambient light | Fully controllable |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher (camera + processing) |
| Safety | User always sees reality | User is dependent on camera |
| Best for | Outdoor, mobile, industrial | Indoor, fully synthetic mixing |

**Merits for Entertainment:**

- **OST (e.g., HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2)**: Lets the user see family, friends, and environment during gameplay — ideal for *shared* AR experiences such as board games, AR concerts, and live events. Lower power suits long sessions.
- **VST (e.g., Meta Quest 3 passthrough)**: Enables *full* occlusion (real objects hiding virtual ones), high-contrast graphics, and *relighting* of virtual content. Ideal for immersive single-player games and mixed-reality titles like *Lego Bricktales*.

> *(Award 1 mark per correct comparison point, 1 mark for entertainment-specific conclusion, with at least 2 marks reserved for the entertainment-context discussion.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] With suitable diagrams, explain the marker-based and markerless AR techniques. State one example application of each.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Marker-Based AR (3 marks):**
- A **fiducial marker** (e.g., a high-contrast square like ARToolKit's Hiro marker, or an ArUco / QR code) is placed in the scene.
- The camera detects the marker via **image thresholding and contour detection**.
- A **homography matrix $\mathbf{H}$** is computed from the four known corners of the marker.
- Decomposing $\mathbf{H}$ yields the camera's pose $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$, which anchors the virtual object.
- **Example**: 3D pop-up animations from printed textbooks; AR business cards.

**Markerless AR (3 marks):**
- Uses **natural feature points** (corners, textures) detected in the live camera image (e.g., via ORB or SIFT).
- A **Visual-Inertial SLAM** pipeline estimates the 6-DoF pose in real time.
- Plane detection (horizontal/vertical surfaces) enables content to be anchored on real-world floors and walls.
- **Example**: Pokémon GO (outdoor GPS + SLAM), IKEA Place (indoor SLAM furniture preview).

**Comparison Diagram (1 mark):**
- Marker-based: Camera → Marker detection → Homography → Render → Display.
- Markerless: Camera + IMU → Feature tracking → SLAM → Pose → Render → Display.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** AR with VR — examiners immediately deduct 1–2 marks for mixing definitions. Remember: AR *adds*; VR *replaces*.
> 2. **Avoid stating** "AR uses a VR headset." AR has its own dedicated see-through hardware class.
> 3. **Do not skip** the 3-property Azuma definition in Part A. Examiners give 1 mark for "real + virtual," 1 mark for "real-time interactive," and 1 mark for "3D registered."
> 4. **For 14-mark questions**, the block diagram is **mandatory**. A textual-only answer forfeits 2–3 marks. Use a clean labelled Mermaid/flowchart in pen-paper (sketch neat rectangles + arrows).
> 5. **In derivation questions**, state the *units* of every quantity. Many students lose marks by writing a numeric answer without $\text{mm}$, $\text{cd/m}^2$, or $\text{Hz}$.
> 6. **SLAM ≠ GPS**. Location-based AR uses GPS, but SLAM is *visual-inertial* — do not interchange them.
> 7. **Waveguide TIR**: Examiners expect $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2 / n_1)$ to be written explicitly in derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **AR Definition** (Azuma 1997): Combines real + virtual, **real-time interactive**, **3D-registered**.
- **Milgram Continuum**: Real ← AR → AV → VR (with MR spanning AR and AV).
- **Three key differences from VR**: See-through display, no full immersion, real world always visible.
- **5-Stage AR Pipeline**: Capture → Analyse → Register → Render → Display.
- **7 types of AR**: Marker-Based, Markerless, Projection, Superimposition, Location-Based, Recognition, Outline.
- **OST vs VST**: OST = direct optical path (HoloLens); VST = camera-captured composite (Quest 3 passthrough).
- **Waveguide** is the modern combiner technology; uses **TIR** to propagate light through a thin slab.
- **Field of View**: $\theta = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$ — typical consumer AR ≈ 30°–52°.
- **Resolution metric**: Pixels per Degree (PPD) — target ≥ 25 PPD for comfortable reading.
- **Brightness**: Outdoor AR requires ≥ 5,000 nits.
- **Latency budget**: End-to-end motion-to-photon ≤ 15 ms (each stage ≤ 2 ms).
- **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)**: Caused by fixed-focal-plane displays; solution = varifocal optics.
- **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping; VI-SLAM = Visual-Inertial SLAM.
- **ARKit / ARCore** are the two dominant mobile AR SDKs, both VI-SLAM based.
- **Camera projection equation**: $s \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{P}$ — the geometric foundation of AR.
- **Homography $\mathbf{H}$** underpins marker-based AR; decomposed to recover $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$.
- **Sensor fusion** combines camera + IMU (and optionally GPS, depth) for robust 6-DoF pose.
- **Registration error** must be < 2 mm at typical viewing distances to be visually imperceptible.
- **Key entertainment applications**: Pokémon GO, Snapchat/Instagram filters, AR concerts (ABBA Voyage), theme-park rides, virtual production (LED volumes).
- **Hardest open challenges**: Limited FoV, VAC, outdoor brightness, battery life, privacy.
- **Famous contributors**: Tom Caudell (coined AR, 1990), Ronald Azuma (formalised AR, 1997), Milgram & Kishino (Reality–Virtuality Continuum, 1994).
- **Top-3 high-yield exam terms** to remember verbatim: *Marker-based AR, SLAM, Optical See-Through Display*.
